Preface

		 On 23 February 2005, I, along with a group of colleagues including John W. Welch, John Gee, and others, gathered in the front room of Hugh Nibley’s home. Hugh was there in a hospital bed, but was unconscious. His wife, Phyllis, and some of their children were also present. We were meeting to decide what to do with Hugh’s unfinished book, One Eternal Round. He had been working on it for more than fifteen years before he became too ill to continue. A few months before, we had gathered together all the materials on the book from Hugh’s upstairs rooms. This comprised over thirty boxes of papers, notes, and pictures. There were also over 450 computer files containing sometimes as many as twenty different versions of a given chapter. How could we take all this and make a book out of it? As we talked, it became clear that the only practical way to do this would be for one person to immerse himself in this massive collection of material and condense and distill it into publishable form. This would require much more than simple editing—the person would have to be a coauthor, because there were many parts that were still incomplete. As we all looked uncomfortably at each other, no one, at that point, was willing to commit to doing it, and so we adjourned without making a final decision.

The rest of that day the thought kept going over and over again in my mind, “Mike, you need to do this!” That night I lay awake still struggling with the magnitude of the task and whether I could even accomplish the task. Finally, sometime in the middle of the night, a sense of calm came over me, and I realized that this was something I had to do and could do—I would finish this book for Hugh. I owed him so much for all he had done for me over the years. Because I had been working with him on the book since I first joined the faculty at BYU in 1993, I felt I was in a position to create a cohesive manuscript for the book. Having made the decision, I was finally able to fall asleep.

The next morning I called Shirley Ricks to tell her I would be willing to do it and that I would call Phyllis and let her know. Shirley said that would be great. I hung up and was doing a couple of other things in my office before calling Phyllis when the phone rang. It was Shirley saying that Hugh had passed away earlier that morning. I firmly believe that once I had made the decision, Hugh somehow knew it and felt he could then leave this life, assured that his book would be finished. For the next four years, One Eternal Round would dominate my life.

One Eternal Round is the culmination of Hugh Nibley’s thoughts and research on the Book of Abraham, especially Facsimile 2. The volume is impressive in its penetrating insights into and coverage of the subject. Within its scope Nibley dealt with mathematics, Alexander the Great, the Egyptian pharaohs Sheshonq and Sesostris, medieval Jewish Kabbala, medieval Jewish and Islamic traditions about Abraham, ancient Hermeticism, Greek myths and their relationship to Egyptian and Mesopotamian myths, early Jewish and Christian apocrypha, ancient Chinese jade disks, Indian mandalas, the Aztec calendar stone, shaman drums, ancient Egyptian mirrors, axial times, the great year-rites of ancient civilizations, Paleolithic cave drawings in France, the Tabula Smaragdina, Hopi Indian ceremonies, alchemy, and the relationship of myth, ritual, and history. All these are woven together into a magnificent tapestry of evidence, demonstrating that the Book of Abraham and its facsimiles represent actual ancient materials and traditions that have all come to light long after Joseph Smith published them.

A major challenge in completing this book has been to choose the best of multiple versions of each chapter. This was complicated by the fact that there were often different chapters in which the same passage was found. Over the years Hugh shifted things around not only within chapters, but between chapters. I have tried to combine what he did in as logical and consistent a manner as I could. Inevitably it was necessary to write transitions, additions, and clarifications. At first I thought I would try to indicate what was Hugh’s and what was mine, but I soon realized that that would detract from the overall readability of the book. My approach has been to use what Hugh wrote as much as possible and to keep my own additions to a minimum. In a few places I have added citations of sources published since Hugh’s death that I felt gave especially strong support to his conclusions.

Another problem I faced was the marked difference between my own, rather pedestrian manner of writing and Hugh’s inimitable style. He makes considerable use of hyperbole, he uses humor and satire with delightful results, and he sprinkles his writing with subtle literary quotes—notably Shakespeare. In my contributions, I have avoided trying to match his style (which I could not do anyway) and have stuck to my own manner of writing. It will probably be obvious to many readers which parts I have written or edited. I have, however, tried my best to preserve the spirit of what Hugh was doing.

In all that I have done, I have tried to remain true to Hugh’s purpose. It is my dearest hope that when it comes my turn to cross through the veil, Hugh will approach me with a smile on his face. He was a true friend, a mentor, an inspiration to me in all the scholarly work that I have done. Without his help and influence I would never have accomplished what I have. Even my faculty position at BYU I owe in no small measure to him. Working on this book of his has been for me a labor of love. I know Hugh wanted so much for it to be published and I hope he is pleased with it. To paraphrase Mormon, “And now, if there are faults they are the mistakes of Mike, wherefore condemn not the things of Hugh Nibley.”

I must express gratitude to many people over the years without whose help I could not have done this: To my beloved wife whose angelic disposition and eternal love inspires me in all that I do. To Pat Ward, who for so many years worked as Hugh’s secretary, patiently taking his dictation. To Shirley Ricks, editor for the Neal A. Maxwell Institute for Religious Scholarship, who has been unfailing in her encouragement and support. To the Department of Ancient Scripture at BYU that has been most generous in granting me research time and financial support as I have worked on this book. To a number of source checkers, including Derek Black, Daniel Becerra, Shaun Miller, and Benjamin Rowe. But especially to Jacob Johnson and Jordan Hanavan, who simply accomplished miracles in tracking down difficult and obscure references. To Alison Coutts and John Hardy for reading the manuscript. To Paula Hicken for seeking permission to reproduce images, and to Sophie Barth, Sabrina Clifford, Sarah Seamons, Linda Sheffield, and Sandra Thorne for help in proofreading. To Brenna King for help with images. To Jacob Rawlins for producing a pleasing blend of text and illustrations with his typesetting skills. To Michael Lyon, who not only prepared the beautiful illustrations but who, with his vast knowledge of ancient art, made many observations and suggestions that added significantly to the book. Also to Tyler Moulton, who assisted him. To Derk Koldewyn at Deseret Book. To the Maxwell Institute at BYU for the support and encouragement in this difficult task. To Phyllis Nibley for entrusting me with the task of finishing this book that meant so much to Hugh. To my Father in Heaven, whose guidance and influence I have felt as I labored at this task. And of course to Hugh Nibley.

Introduction

	 Hugh Nibley begins this book with an examination of three major attempts to discredit Joseph Smith and the Book of Abraham: the first in 1860 by Théodule Devéria; the second in 1912 by the Episcopal bishop of Salt Lake City, Franklin S. Spalding; and the third in 1967 by Grant S. Heward and Jerald Tanner. The rest of the book then examines the overwhelming evidence supporting the authenticity and accuracy of Joseph Smith’s work on the Book of Abraham and its facsimiles.

We are put on our guard against too quickly accepting the authoritative pronouncements of Egyptologists in a brief but enlightening look at the development of Egyptology. We find professional jealousy and petty infighting among Egyptologists as well as a general condescension and even contempt for the ancient Egyptians they are studying. Since the mid-twentieth century some Egyptologists have begun to take the Egyptians more seriously, but we still have far to go in our understanding of this remarkable civilization and its contributions.

A central feature of Egyptian religion was an attempt to deal with what Nibley calls “the terrible questions,” questions that have always troubled mankind and continue to do so now. Where did this world come from? What is the purpose of life? What happens after death? These are also questions that the Book of Abraham and the facsimiles deal with in a unique and satisfying manner. The fundamental purpose of both the Book of Abraham and ancient Egyptian religious literature is thus the same.

A major mythological cycle common to ancient civilizations and societies is that of the creation of the world and mankind, the wickedness of mankind that results in the destruction of nearly all mankind, and their subsequent return to wickedness. There are Semitic, Egyptian, and Greek accounts of this cycle, the biblical account being perhaps the most familiar to us. Two Egyptian compositions, the Memphite Theology and the Epic of the Heavenly Cow, deal with the creation and the flood, as does the story of Prometheus in Greek literature. The creation account in the Book of Abraham and the accounts of the wickedness of mankind and the flood given in the Book of Moses expand on this same ancient theme.

But how do we distinguish between ancient myths and real history? What is the relationship between myth, ritual, and history? The surviving writings that have come down to us from ancient times are basically mythological accounts that portray actual historical events in a ritual context. The great year-rite that is found in various forms throughout the ancient world is an example of this. This celebration took place at a sacred location, most often a temple or high place. There were common elements to this ceremony: the dramatic representation of the death and resurrection of the god, a portrayal of the creation, a ritual combat in which the triumph of the god over his enemies was depicted, a triumphal procession where the king or leader played the part of the god followed by a train of lesser gods, an atoning sacrifice by which the people were cleansed of their sins, and festivities and games that recalled a previous Golden Age, now lost. The similarities in these ceremonies throughout the ancient world argue a common origin for them. The Book of Abraham and the Book of Moses in the Pearl of Great Price as well as the Book of Mormon all describe these rites with remarkable accuracy, a fact that argues strongly for their ancient authorship. The attempted sacrifice of Abraham described in the Book of Abraham is a particularly notable example.

After the rediscovery of the Joseph Smith Papyri fragments, critics immediately questioned Joseph Smith’s association of Facsimile 1, which formed the beginning of the ancient Egyptian Book of Breathings, with Abraham. Is there any connection between Abraham and this ancient Egyptian religious document? There are some remarkable parallels based on the ancient perception of history as a repetition of ritual and mythological themes. The full title of the Book of Breathings is the Document of Breathing Which Isis Made for Her Brother, Osiris, and its purpose is “to cause his soul to live.”[1] Just as Abraham was married to his sister Sarah (Genesis 20:12), Osiris was married to his sister Isis. And when Abraham asks Sarah to say that she is his sister, it is so that “my soul shall live because of thee” (Genesis 12:13; Abraham 2:25).

Facsimile 2 is an object Egyptologists call a hypocephalus. Its fundamental purpose was similar to all Egyptian funerary documents—to ensure the resurrection and deification of the dead. It graphically portrays the whole creation of God in a circular or spherical form. It is related in form and function to such objects as the Egyptian prehistoric slate palettes, mirrors buried with the Egyptian dead, drums or tambourines used by the Egyptians and many other ancient peoples, jade disks used by the ancient Chinese, and even the Aztec calendar stone found in the New World. Hypocephali fall into two broad classes. The first class is a prayer to and for a heavenly deliverer, and the second is the answer to that prayer. Facsimile 2 belongs to the second class.

Chapters 7 and 8 deal with the evidence in support of Joseph Smith’s explanation of the figures of Facsimile 2. The correctness and accuracy of these explanations simply cannot be attributed to “lucky guessing” on the part of Joseph Smith. Moreover, virtually all the material that supports Joseph’s explanations was unpublished or undiscovered at the time he wrote these explanations.

A remarkable aspect of the Book of Abraham is how it fits in perfectly with a class of ancient religious literature known as ascension dramas. An ascension drama is an ancient apocryphal text that describes the ascension into heaven or cosmic tour of a patriarch, prophet, apostle, or other religious figure, with his subsequent return to earth to reveal what he has seen and experienced. Two notable ones with significant similarities with the Book of Abraham are the Testament of Abraham and the Apocalypse of Abraham. Others include the Testament of Isaac, the various Enoch texts, 2 and 3 Baruch, the book of Revelation, the Ascension of Isaiah, the tale of Er the Son of Armenios found in Plato’s Republic, Joseph and Asenath, and even the described experiences of ancient shamans. The Book of Breathings and the hypocephalus both have affinities with this ascension text genre. The Book of Abraham (chapter 3) and the Book of Moses (chapter 1 for Moses and chapters 6 and 7 for Enoch) in the Pearl of Great Price are also classic examples of ascension texts. Joseph Smith’s claim that these were all related and were, moreover, translations of ancient texts is certainly compelling evidence that he knew what he was talking about.

Another ancient object that shows parallels in form and function with the hypocephalus is the Hermetic Tabula Smaragdina, which in turn shares similarities with various objects described in Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Chinese, Jewish, early Christian, gnostic, and Arabic sources. These include shining stones, jade disks, the tablets of destiny, and the Urim and Thummim.

Ancient doctrines such as Hermeticism, Gnosticism, and alchemy all claimed to have secret wisdom ultimately derived from Egypt. Their doctrines show affinities with ancient Egyptian texts as well as early Jewish and Christian apocryphal literature—and, of course, with the Book of Abraham.

There are also remarkable similarities between Jewish Kabbala and the Book of Abraham. One of the most well known Kabbalistic books is the Sefer Yetzirah, which claims to have been written by Abraham himself and which shows significant parallels with hypocephali as well. They both deal with a description of the cosmos, God’s place in it, and how mankind can come to use the power of God.

There is a link between hypocephali and Alexander the Great (the most representative symbol of human aspiration); the name of the owner of the hypocephalus portrayed in Facsimile 2, Sheshonq, is a name by which priests, oracles, and common people hailed Alexander during his great campaign of world conquest. In ancient documents the name Sheshonq is often confused with that of Sesostris, the traditional pharaoh of the time of Abraham. There is also an ancient tradition that one of Solomon’s wives was a daughter of the pharaoh Sheshonq. And finally, in Sheshonq’s great inscription at Karnak, the place name Field of Abraham is mentioned.

By far the name most frequently associated with Abraham in Jewish legends is that of his alter ego, reverse image, and mortal enemy Nimrod. These legends show many parallels with the Book of Abraham, including Abraham preaching against idolatry, the subsequent attempt to kill Abraham, and his deliverance by God.

One of the most remarkable aspects of Nibley’s research on the hypocephalus is his groundbreaking chapter on geometry (chapter 15). His discovery of the mathematical relationships depicted on Facsimile 2 such as the golden section or phi proportion, the 1-2- triangle, the Pythagorean 3-4-5 triangle, the Fibonacci series, the phi spiral, the pentagram and the hexagram (star of David), and their relationship with the biological and mineral worlds are remarkable and insightful, providing whole new areas of research for future scholars.

In summary, in One Eternal Round Hugh Nibley has brought together an enormous collection of evidence from a vast number of ancient sources, all of which support and confirm the validity and authenticity of the Book of Abraham and its facsimiles—ancient sources that Joseph Smith simply could not have known about or had access to.

One Eternal Round, Hugh Nibley’s final publication, the culmination of a life dedicated to the gospel of Jesus Christ and to discovering truth wherever it could be found, is a monument to his scholarship, his remarkable ability to see relationships in diverse areas of study and to synthesize them into a comprehensible whole, and his humble willingness to consecrate his work to the glory of the Lord and the furtherance of his kingdom here on earth. I consider it one of the greatest blessings of my life to have known him and to have associated and worked with him. I am deeply honored and grateful for the opportunity I have had to prepare this book for publication. I hope that what I have done is pleasing to Hugh and useful to all those who have waited so long for its publication.

----------------------

^1. Book of Breathings, Louvre P. 3284 1/1.

1

The Critics

The Fatal Mistake?

From time to time various critics of the Prophet Joseph have triumphantly announced the discovery of his most egregious blunder. All proclaim that the supreme indiscretion of Smith was the publication of three Egyptian documents, Facsimiles 1, 2, and 3 of the Book of Abraham (see appendixes 1–2), along with his own supposedly inspired interpretation of them. Even more daring, though attracting less attention, was the accompanying autobiography of Abraham to which the facsimiles were illustrations. And yet every one of these attempts to discredit the Prophet has struck out.

1860—Strike One

The first “scientific” criticism of the Prophet was attempted by Théodule Devéria,[1] a young man working at the Louvre, who set the stage and gave the example when he put the “interpretations of the Egyptologists” in a column parallel to the “interpretations of Joseph Smith.” [2] Devéria proceeded to point out that Facsimile 1, figure 1, a bird, is “the soul of Osiris which should have a human head.” [3] The priest, figure 3, was Anubis and “should have a jackal’s head, carrying out the resurrection of Osiris.” [4] What Smith called the pillars of heaven, figure 11, is simply “artistic ornamentation without any known significance.” [5] Finally, what Smith calls “ ‘Raukeeyang . . . the firmament over our heads’ . . . represents the conventional landscape (terrain) in Egyptian paintings.” [6]

In 1967 the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints acquired some fragments of the original Joseph Smith Papyri; it was at once apparent that the illustration had been accurately copied, although there were missing portions that the engraver had attempted to re-create.[7] As we saw above, Devéria found Facsimile 1, figure 11, “without any known significance.” [8] But the Egyptians did not waste the limited space for sacred scenes in tomb and temple with “meaningless marks” or trivia. The bottom frame in Facsimile 1 resembles the recessed paneling of the serekh structures, called “palace-façade” and going back to the royal residence of predynastic times—representing the pillars flanking the doorway to the royal residence, palace, temple, or pillars of heaven.[9] Figure 11 recalls the fifteen gates in the great enclosure at Saqqara.[10] The Waters of Nun shown in figure 12 bathe the sacred crocodile (fig. 9), who is Sobek,[11] indeed “the idolatrous god of Pharaoh” and the chief patron of the monarchy.[12]

1912—Strike Two

The major campaign to get rid of the Book of Abraham, and with it the Book of Mormon, was undertaken in 1912 by the Episcopal Bishop of Salt Lake City, Franklin S. Spalding,[13] and his aide, the Reverend Samuel A. B. Mercer.[14] The campaign reached its triumphant climax on the pages of the New York Times at the end of 1912.[15] In January of that year the Times had noted with wonder that Bishop Spalding had been speaking with great kindness to and about the Mormons, appealing especially for a more indulgent view of polygamy.[16] Why on earth would the good bishop throw away the ace card of a crusading ministry? In December, the Times frankly explained the stratagem; Spalding had been laying a trap for “the breaking up of Mormonism through the desertion of the intelligent part of its membership.” [17] He would not need polygamy for that because once he exploded his atom bomb, the Egyptian papyri, the results would be assured, and indeed it was announced in the same article that the Pearl of Great Price was finished—“its authenticity has been destroyed.” [18] Nay, “this” (the grand assault), proclaimed the Reverend Mercer, “would signal the end of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.” [19] In fact, the Book of Abraham has always been regarded by critics as the Mormons’ Achilles heel; against the professors, Smith had no case.

In response to the criticism that the scholars had not really spent much time or effort in their examination of Joseph Smith’s interpretation of the facsimiles, Dr. Mercer explained: “The reply of each scholar was brief, very little time being devoted to a study of the Prophet’s work in general,” since “it required only a glance to find out that the interpretation and translation were absolutely wrong . . . the scholars felt that linguistically . . . the subject was not worth much of their valuable time. Hence the brief replies.” [20] No attempt was made to translate the texts because “the hieroglyphics, with the exception of some simple signs, are incorrectly copied . . . the unusual and complicated signs were always wrongly copied.” [21]

The express purpose of Spalding, as he keeps reminding us, was to discredit the Book of Mormon by showing that Joseph Smith had no qualifications as a translator. After trying several approaches, the good bishop decides that his “competency as a translator . . . can be ascertained in but one way. The original texts, together with his interpretations, must be submitted to competent scholars,[22] . . . the opinions of authoritative scholars” being definitive.[23] His collection of statements of eight savants he hailed as the “judgments of the world’s greatest Egyptologists.” [24] The Bishop sent to each one a copy of the three facsimiles along with Smith’s interpretation of them. All he asked of them was eight opinions, yes or no; he announced that one question would settle everything: Was Joseph Smith’s translation correct?

The Art of Stalling

To respond to such a challenge, the only real final answer must be an accurate translation of the passage; the reader is agog to hear just what the inscriptions do say. But they will never get it from Spalding’s panel of scholars! Janne M. Sjodahl summed up the findings of the experts as “practical unanimity” in their conclusions, “but none of them offers an interpretation of the inscriptions. This is all the more remarkable because they all agree that the object (Facsimile 2) is very familiar to Egyptian scholars.” [25] The failure to produce anything but opinions then is the second strikeout.

It so happens that Facsimile 2 was quite well copied, by both the Egyptian and the American craftsmen, and has proven easily legible to the student who takes the trouble to compare it with matching inscriptions from other hypocephali. In 1977, a young Latter-day Saint scholar had no great trouble coming up with a full translation of the text of Facsimile 2, which all the scholars of 1912 and since had fastidiously preferred not to touch.[26]

Among the Giants

The years 1881 to 1914 were designated as the Golden Age of Egyptology.[27] It was also the time of the amateurs.[28] In a world where amateurs aspire to become giants in the eyes of a gullible public, an invidious spirit prevails—a special characteristic of Egyptology to this day. To quote Professor Georges Posener: Egyptology is a science “that shuts itself in, suspicious of the methods of related disciplines, highly skeptical of any possible rapprochement. Its specialists work in a sealed vessel with materials which they like to think of as unique.” [29] Explicitly, this “isolationist tradition goes back to the spiritual father” of Egyptology, none other than Adolf Erman, and it has from the beginning created “a fossé (trench, gap) between Egyptology and other fields which research tends to widen rather than to bridge. The attitude of the Egyptologists has been calculated to cool interest and enthusiasm, and cause people in other disciplines to avoid Egyptology.” [30]

Since professional authority itself affects to bring about the complete and total obliteration of Joseph Smith, a word on the subject is in order. The status of Egyptology, on which the reputation of Joseph Smith depends, has been laid out by their number-one man, the dread and envy of them all: At the forefront, “the Führer not only of German, but all of Egyptologists of all lands,” according to the venerable Georg Steindorff, was Adolf Erman.[31] In his autobiography, Erman gives us his frank appraisal of his colleagues in the profession. And so he goes down the list (fig. 1).

Figure 1. The giants of Egyptology. Though united in a single discipline, the national schools continued in a strong rivalry.

			 Georg Ebers, the true founder of the Berlin School, really knew nothing about Egyptian syntax, according to Erman,[32] and Heinrich Ewald, who studied widely and knew much more than anyone, could be dismissed as a man given to “oracular pronouncements” who also displayed other “eccentricities.” [33] Then there was Johannes Dümichen, the indefatigable copier of inscriptions, so stupid “his friends called him Dümmlichen (dopey), because he was not able to compose a proper sentence.” [34] Heinrich Brugsch, who was a prodigy, was accordingly “frivolous, unscrupulous, intemperate and leichtsinnig (lightminded).” [35] In the end it was Erman who got Richard Lepsius’s job, describing the grand old man as one from whom he was never able to learn a thing because he was hopelessly out-of-date, cold, stuffy, pompous, and inaccessible, never addressing a word to a student unless it was droned across the lectern. Erman’s own modesty is heralded in his Ex libris, which was two lines from Homer:

αἰὲν ἀριστύιν καἱ ὑπἱροχν ἔμμναι ἄλλων,

μηδὲ γὲνος πατὲρων αἰσχυνὲμν, οἳ μὲγ ἄριστοι

Always to be the best and superior to all the others,

And to never shame the lineage of my fathers, who were of the noblest blood.[36]

As to other giants, there was of course W. M. Flinders Petrie, who was “without critical capacity and ignorant of the [Egyptian] language.” [37] Scholars in related fields like E. Bötticher were nothing but dilettantes “who easily overestimated their own learning.” [38] Edward Glaser, the Arabist, was nothing but a fanatic; [39] of Ernst Curtius’s work in comparative philology he writes, “I never could understand his enthusiasm for his subject.” [40] Fritz Hommel was a “Romantic, full of fantasy.” [41] He took his friend Alfred von Sallet to task for not being serious enough in his studies—that made him “a Sunday scholar.” [42] Erman has but one objective; his only purpose in life was to make Egyptology an exact science in which no margin of error was possible. He had little patience for men like Theodor Mommsen, Hermann Diels, and Adolf von Harnack with their quest for ever broader perspectives.[43]

One can imagine how such giants would dismiss Joseph Smith. Bishop Spalding’s dice were loaded; all the jury had to do was to shake their heads and Smith was done for. To cover up any preordained harmony, Spalding adds at the end of his treatise: “The following judgments of the world’s greatest Egyptologists have been ascertained. The opinions were obtained from the scholars themselves, and in no case did one man know the opinion of another.” [44] Thus all was scrupulously fair to make sure that none of these mental giants should contaminate the judgment of another concerning Joseph Smith the Scholar.

The common resort of the local experts in refuting the Prophet has ever been to discourse learnedly on familiar Egyptian figures such as Hathor, Osiris, and Anubis. This was almost the essence of Egyptology a century ago, but today we are told that “it is a waste of time to speculate about the origin of names, attributes, localities of the various gods.” [45] “One talks glibly of fetishes, sun-gods, ram-gods, falcon-gods, and so forth—as if the precision of those terms had any reference to the gods themselves! . . . [which] bars the road to understanding.” [46] To the present day, critics, including a few apostates, of Joseph Smith’s interpretation have pursued that feeble line of demonstration.

The Low-rated Egyptians

But it was the Egyptians that took the brunt of their swaggering. The modern scientific mind had nothing but withering contempt for the intelligence of the Egyptians themselves. As Hermann Kees observed, “There is always a ‘geheimnisvoller nimbus,’ a ‘mysterious aura’ surrounding Egyptian religion.” [47] A fixed conviction was riveted on the Western mind “that the Middle and New Kingdoms at their height concocted their ideas of the next world in a ‘witch’s lab’ (Hexenküche), which has stuck with them until the present day.” [48] Such spooks were an abomination to the scientific mind.

According to his encomiast Georg Steindorff, Erman’s great achievement was to have torn off the veil of romanticism that had given the Egyptians a haunting and alluring quality and showed their authors to be what they were for Erman: perfectly ordinary people doing perfectly ordinary things.[49] Today, now that we can read its inscriptions and study its literature, “every nimbus has vanished,” was Erman’s triumphant cry; in “place of the ‘sacred dawning’ which Goethe still saw in Egypt we have the full unsparing light of history, and the ancient Egyptians have become the people no better and no worse than any other people.” [50] In his ample autobiography, Erman expresses his contempt for all religion and his patronizing tolerance of the low-rated Egyptians. For him the only documents of value are those “from daily life—family and business records, inventories, notebooks, legal documents.” [51] At the University of Chicago, James Breasted, a clone of the Berlin School, “saw religion primarily as a social and ethical phenomenon” and interpreted the ancient Egyptian texts in a way that supported his philosophical creed of “New Humanism” based on naturalism and “a most pronounced meliorism.” [52] Professor John Wilson of the same school delights in the discovery of one Egyptian author who had the “dispassionate scientific state of mind” to recognize that “phenomena were purely physical and not a product of divine or demoniacal force” —reductionism, no less.[53]

Two hundred years ago the poet Herder complained that the “imperfect” hieroglyphs of the Egyptians displayed only “a poverty of ideas” and “stagnation of thought.” [54] The issue here, whether Herder, thirty years before Champollion, was qualified to judge this point, was ignored when the professionals spoke later in the same vein. Most of the papyri, says Erman, “tell us nothing” and that “because of their religious or magical content.” [55] It was religion, naturally equated to magic, that Erman could not stand.

To put it bluntly, the experts felt that they had been let down, and they held the Egyptians responsible. “Decipherment of documents has disappointed centuries of expectation,” wrote Henri Frankfort. “It revealed a remarkable lack of philosophical content; . . . instead, the texts introduce us to an apparent jungle of religious matter, so impenetrable to our understanding that Egyptologists have increasingly shunned the task of interpretation.” [56] The famous wisdom of the Egyptians, according to him, “seems to us at times to descend to entirely trivial matters.” [57] Even for Gardiner their writings are “intolerably arid.” [58]

In a 422-page study of Egyptian culture, Alfred Wiedemann devotes forty pages to dress, thirty-four to housing, thirty-four to trades and crafts, and six pages to religion.[59] Even Breasted insisted that “the Egyptian did not possess the terminology for the expression of a system of abstract thought.” [60] That is why, according to Theodor Hopfner, the Greeks could have got nothing from them, “because such subtlety was beyond the grasp of the Egyptians, who saw in their divinities intensely real beings, and had absolutely no capacity to conceive of spiritual powers (Potenzen).” [61] Though religion was their life, “they lacked any deeper feeling, and showed only an anxious concern for formalities and magic.” [62] The Egyptian’s mind was not like “the modern mind, hampered by its implicit necessity of logical sequence of cause and effect.” [63] It was, writes Professor Wilson, “a myth-making mentality” which lacked the sense of time, relativity, or impersonal causation.[64] For Louis Speleers, the Belgian Egyptologist, “Egyptian thinking was essentially chaotic, . . . an inexhaustible source of confusion and contradiction . . . which defied any logical classification of ideas.” [65]

But, as might be expected, it was in science that these benighted people were found most woefully lacking. The banner-bearer in the field was Otto Neugebauer, specializing in astronomy and mathematics, who remarks on “the utterly primitive framework of Egyptian mathematics.” [66]

Two good reasons justify this emphasis on the contempt of the scholarly giants for Egyptian religion and learning. One reason is that religion was the whole world of the Egyptians, and the other is that the ancient scriptures revealed through Joseph Smith—whether in the Book of Abraham, the Book of Moses, or the Book of Mormon—all begin in the Egyptian setting and share in many points of Egyptian theological speculation.

Egyptians: The “Idiot Savants”

What place would such people have in our sacred books? Professor Hermann Junker is speaking for many when he asks the perennial question, “How does it happen that such an advanced civilization . . . has such a feeble logical development?” [67] The Greeks always laughed at the way the Egyptians got everything backwards and deified animals.[68] On the other hand, Plutarch explains that their rites are not as fantastic or superstitious as people think, but are actually moral, profitable, and consistent with both history and science.[69] Iamblichus asks, if it is just a matter of magical tricks (γοήτων τχνάσματα, goētōn technasmata), how is it that the Egyptian priesthood has commanded the world’s respect for millennia? You can’t fool all the people all the time. How did it happen that their teachings produced demonstrable physical results and that their works have lasted so long?[70] Clement of Alexandria, while ridiculing their gods,[71] points to the great wisdom of their “prophets” who instructed Pythagoras, Thales, Plato, Solon, and Eudoxus.[72] We may well ask with Cornutus why the Greeks themselves, never lacking in φιλαυτἱα (philautia, self-love), always insisted that the Egyptians were the forerunners of Greek science.[73] Professor Neugebauer confesses that he finds the Egyptian way of life by far the pleasantest ever designed by humans. And what else could be the object of all our devising? This must be heaven.

The situation by 1967 has been described by the religious historian Franz Altheim, who asks how it is possible to have any discussion of religion when nobody believes anything or has any firm religious convictions. It is a purely academic affair, and so we are stuck dead center. In 1957, Altheim had offered a way out of the impasse: “We all feel that the coming of a new age, for which we hope and toward which we are ready to strive with our might, must begin with the shattering of conventional forms. . . . This hope must stand or fall with the ability to discover new religious foundations.” [74] This happy state of affairs seems to have emerged with the so-called New School of Egyptology. Altheim’s foresight was providential. Within a decade the original Joseph Smith Papyri had appeared along with the documents; the New School emerged as a result of the new studies in comparative Wisdom literature of the Egyptians and Hebrews. At last things began to offer ground for serious study.

The Morning Breaks

With the so-called New School, Northern European School, or Tübingen School of Egyptology, which emerged after World War II, religion moved from the end to the head of the line, with, of all things, a special zeal for theology! [75] At last they were asking the long-delayed question, “What is the real essence and content of Egyptian religion?” [76] These religious themes were hardly mentioned by Kurt Sethe and his school, who systematically ignored them. But now religion, which is “of fundamental importance for the understanding of ancient Egyptian culture . . . is being studied under new aspects with new investigative methods.” [77]

The New School recognizes both that “a general understanding of Egyptian religion is still to come” and that “a real history of Egyptian religion has not yet been written.” [78] Only now is Egyptian religion beginning to take its place as “a system of human thought which has an important place in the history of the human mind,” offering “an alternative to the Greek tradition.” [79]

Many factors have contributed to the change of attitude expressed by the New School. There have been new archaeological discoveries and texts. “Every year the number of publications continues to grow,” wrote Professor Posener, who kept close check on the situation: “At the same time, however, the publication of new texts is only a feeble trickle, yet while diligently exploiting it students have made almost no attempt to increase it.” [80] To aid them further, we must now have an upgraded vocabulary and rules of grammar [81] and the awareness in the interpretation of the iconography, which “should not be considered as supplements or mere illustrations; . . . rather the iconographical relationships are of a quite mobile nature and point to ad hoc relationships that are dependent on the situation.” [82] This is particularly significant in the case of the Book of Abraham where so much depends upon the interpretation of the facsimiles.

Instead of being the scientific and philosophical cretins deplored by Professors Neugebauer, Erman, and others, we find that the thinking of the Egyptians is “without a trace of mysticism or transcendentalizing; . . . every type of ecstasy seems alien to the Egyptian.” [83] One cannot imagine a more drastic change of view.

The problem of language brings up new problems as steadily as it solves old ones. Since the publication of the Wörterbuch in 1920,[84] “hardly one of its 2,786 pages remains unaltered today.” [85] Professor Gerhard Fecht’s discovery that most of Egyptian literature is written in verse casts new light on their mentality and has opened the way for the metrical reinterpretation of virtually all ancient Egyptian texts.[86] At last the classical sources, so long spurned by the profession, are also being appreciated: It would seem that the Greeks and Egyptians knew far more about each other than we do.[87]

After all this time the old argument still continues as to whether the Egyptians were monotheists or polytheists. Divine epithets are freely passed around among the gods, who fuse with each other just as easily as they fuse with their earthly representative, the king. The standard Egyptian word for god, nṯr, can mean any particular god whom one happens to be dealing with or divinity in general (fig. 2).[88]

Figure 2. The hieroglyph for god, nṯr, is difficult to identify. Early Egyptologists thought it was an ax; it is now understood to be a flag wrapped around a staff. (A) ca. 2900 b.c., (B) ca. 2800 b.c., (C) ca. 2600 b.c., (D) ca. 1500 b.c.

				 As to the primitive animal forms of the Egyptian gods, considered the hallmark of Egyptian religion, “the names of the gods do not provide any support for a hypothetical stage of development in which all gods were animals.” [89] In spite of Smith’s critics, “no thinking Egyptian would have imagined Amun was a man with a ram’s head. . . . Every image is an imperfect means of making a god visible, characterizing his nature, and distinguishing him from other deities.” [90] One is reminded of the opening of Mercer’s text on Egyptian religion (recalling that he was Joseph Smith’s official nemesis): “So the men of Egypt saw heaven as an immense friendly cow standing over them.” [91] Oy!

The Pearl of Great Price—Still More to Come

The Pearl of Great Price is a book of beginnings taking us into the deep past further than any other scriptures; it is also the last of our revealed scriptures and is still awaiting completion. It has received less attention than the other writings and has been studied only superficially. This is neither surprising nor unsettling—it was meant that way; we must wait to the end to hear the whole story.

The Book of Mormon and the Old Testament are tribal histories—we still identify ourselves with the tribes of Israel. The New Testament and the Doctrine and Covenants are theological and doctrinal teachings, everlasting and timeless. But the Pearl of Great Price brings together the contemporary accounts of the seven main dispensations of the world since Adam. Coming last, it sums up the entire history of mankind, filling in many of the gaps in our knowledge that have remained to this day. The records of Adam, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Joseph Smith were given to the Saints as a bonus for their acceptance of the Book of Mormon and are still kept in reserve; we may anticipate the pleasure of more light to come.

Important parts of the Pearl of Great Price which are still being held back include “writings that cannot be revealed unto the world; but is [sic] to be had in the holy Temple of God” (Fac. 2, fig. 8), “ought not to be revealed at the present time” (Fac. 2, fig. 9). Years ago when we cited some passages from what we called an Egyptian endowment,[92] without elaborating, many Latter-day Saints quietly recognized their own temple endowment. Important things are still expressly withheld which “ought not to be revealed at the present time”; these include Facsimile 2, figures 12–21. For some of the secrets there is a standing invitation: “If the world can find out these numbers, so let it be. Amen” (Fac. 2, fig. 11). That was over a century and a half ago, and the invitation to search is still open. Where do we begin?

1967—Strike Three

The third attack was inevitable when in 1967 the Church acquired and immediately published the surviving fragments of the original Joseph Smith Papyri.[93] It was soon established that the purported Egyptian source of the Book of Abraham, chapter 1, was actually from the well-known Egyptian Book of Breathings Made by Isis, and the surviving text was very close to that of Papyrus Louvre 3284. This was a great piece of luck since of the many texts of the Book of Breathings this one has been viewed as the standard. This was also the writing identified as the first chapter of the Book of Abraham, the corresponding pages juxtaposed side-by-side in the Joseph Smith Kirtland papers. It was also the text used in composing the so-called Alphabet and Grammar [94] in which the characters interpreted were supplied by the first page of the Egyptian manuscript. Grant Heward and Jerald Tanner triumphantly announced “The Source of the Book of Abraham Identified” and proposed that the Egyptian text of the Book of Breathings was the text used by Joseph Smith in translating the Book of Abraham, basing this proposal on the use of this text in the Egyptian Alphabet and Grammar.[95] This was supposed to deliver the fatal blow to the Book of Abraham. Instead, it opened astonishing paths of research that vindicate its authenticity.

By His Own Hand

Here was the original Egyptian text waiting for comparison with Joseph Smith’s English translation. The first objections expressed by the critics were to the proposition, which they took for granted, that Smith had published these texts and drawings as an autographic document—the very papyrus and the very handwriting of Abraham himself, “written by his own hand upon papyrus” (introduction to the Book of Abraham). Indeed, the republication of Charles M. Larson’s book on the subject is an attempt to exploit the title as a coup de grâce.[96]

But that all-important title of the book was not Joseph Smith’s designation but the translation of the conventional Egyptian title. This writing was “called [by the Egyptians] ‘The Book of Abraham, written by his own hand, upon papyrus.’ ” They, as well as the Hebrews, attributed a man’s own works after the manner of God’s creation as the “workmanship of his hand,” an expression repeatedly used in the Book of Abraham (Abraham 1:7, 17, 18; 2:7, 8; 3:12). Many Egyptian documents are described as being the “work of a scribe’s fingers”; indeed, we are told that the Book of Breathings was “written by Thoth with his own fingers.” [97] The phrase by the hand is a figure of speech to denote the author or creator of anything—in Hebrew, ביד (bĕyad, by the hand of) is used as an intensified form of the preposition ב (bĕ) to describe the person through whom something is done—for example, “as the Lord had spoken by (ביד, bĕyad) Moses” (Exodus 9:35).

When we read in the heading of one of the oldest Egyptian books that “His Majesty wrote this book down anew,” Professor Sethe explains that this is normal Egyptian usage to express the idea that the king ordered a copy to be made (see pp. 68–69, fig. 6).[98] A noble who announces, “I made this tomb for my old father,” obligingly explains, “I had the sculptor Itju make (it).” [99] Professor Wilson explains that in all such cases the person who claims to have done the work does so “in the sense that he commissioned and paid for it.” [100] Abraham, an important person in Egypt, must be allowed the same liberty. The boast of a nobleman, that he made his father’s tomb “while his arm was still strong,” may suggest the son’s toil and sweat in the quarry—but that was work for others, though still the son’s responsibility.[101] Thus the Book of Abraham that Joseph Smith translated was certainly not the original that Abraham himself made—it was a copy, but Abraham was the author of the text.

The Original Manuscript?

Under no circumstances could Abraham’s original manuscript have survived or been permitted to undergo four hundred years of handling by even the most devout readers. For Abraham, who wrote these things “for the benefit of my posterity that shall come after me” (Abraham 1:31), knew that his descendants would be in the land for at least four hundred more years (Genesis 15:13). The Egyptians were almost fanatical about not letting valued writings become worn and battered. When King Shabako founded the Twenty-fifth Dynasty, he wanted the same rites as those of the First Dynasty, the inauguration of Menes, the first king. While renovating the shrine used on that first occasion, the workers found a copy of the original ceremony. It was in bad condition, with parts eaten by worms, so “His Majesty had it copied anew, fairer than before, that his name might endure and his monument be preserved in the house of his father Ptah . . . throughout all eternity.” [102] Nest-Amun, the daughter of the Prophet Nebwen, states that she “renewed this book (the famous Metternich Stele) after she had found it removed from the house (temple) of Osiris-Mnevis, so that her name might be preserved.” [103] It is notable that both these very important people expected a special reward for their meritorious act—the renewing process would immortalize them as well as the books. The phrase that his name might endure was actually the title given to the first Books of Breathings discovered.[104]

It was a strict rule in Israel as in Egypt that a sacred text, when it became worn out, had to be reproduced character by character or letter by letter, the scribe never trusting to his memory, but meticulously copying from the document before him; the teaching of the Kabbala is that each letter has a special message of its own. The old worn-out copy was never preserved in a library but had to be cut up, hidden away, burned, or even buried, containing as it did the ineffable name of God. When the transfer was completed, the old, worn-out copy was then concealed in a genizah, a secret place hollowed out in the wall of a synagogue or shrine, awaiting its final fate (fig. 3).[105] The original book lived on “renewed,” and it was still the work of the author’s “own hand upon papyrus.”

Figure 3. In the venerable Ben Ezra synagogue in Old Cairo, there is a small, second-story genizah, where for centuries the people reverently placed many worn-out written texts. In 1897 Solomon Schechter removed thirty large bags filled with 140,000 texts and fragments. Their translation and publication by Cambridge University continues to this day.

The book of Jubilees reports that when Jacob died and was buried in Canaan, “he gave all his books and the books of the Fathers to Levi his son that he might preserve and renew them for his children until this day.” [106] This would include his Egyptian library, for we also learn in Jubilees that Joseph, while living in Egypt, used to read to his sons “the words which his father Jacob used to read from among the words of Abraham.” [107] These were written while Abraham was living in Egypt at the court. Here is a clear statement that “the words of Abraham” were handed down in written form from generation to generation in Egypt. The Egyptian tradition is nowhere more expressly confirmed than in the Book of Abraham: “I shall endeavor, hereafter, to delineate the chronology running back from myself to the beginning of the creation, for the records have come into my hands, which I hold unto this present time” (Abraham 1:28).

We have seen that the writings of Abraham were diligently read, studied, and renewed by his descendants in the land.[108] Are we to suppose that his story was handed around for centuries among thousands of people, passing from hand to hand for diligent study and comment, and the only copy available all that time was one poor battered piece of papyrus which now reposes in the Church Historian’s Office? “By his own hand,” indeed! It was thanks to the handing down of the books of the Fathers in “the language of the Egyptians” that Nephi in a later generation gave us the Book of Mormon (1 Nephi 1:2).

Why Egyptian?—Special Delivery

There is another aspect to the text which establishes the bona fides of Abraham’s Egyptian writings. As Theodor Böhl informs us, the one chance the original patriarchal literature had of surviving would be to have it written down on papyrus and preserved in the dry Egyptian sand.[109] Abraham’s purpose in writing was “for the benefit of my posterity that shall come after me” (Abraham 1:31); if that was what he wanted, he knew that the Egyptians were the most reliable custodians of documents over long periods, and the climate there was one conducive to the survival of written documents.

Both the king and the princess knew that in copying old worn-out books, they were acquiring merit and securing their own immortality. The great importance of keeping written records, as Erik Hornung has recently observed, was first realized by the Egyptians who, moreover, were the first to illustrate their documents, thus putting “idea into image.” [110] Moreover, most of the findings contain written texts or pictures, “and very often a combination of the two, so word and image together provide a key to understanding ancient Egyptian sources.” [111] Again the Prophet Joseph Smith shows great insight by stating that the Books of Abraham and Mormon were preserved in Egyptian, and one of them vividly illustrated!

Alphabet and Grammar

Right next to JSP I and joined directly to it on the same strip of papyrus (the matching of the fibers of the two pieces proves this) is a short hieratic text—the Book of Breathings Made by Isis. Since the name of Hor, the owner of this Book of Breathings papyrus, appears in three different places in the hieratic text of Facsimile 3, we know that it was also an illustration on this papyrus and likely followed the text of the Book of Breathings, which was thus bracketed by these two illustrations. In view of its position, what else can it be but the Book of Abraham, ask the critics. As noted above, Heward and Tanner claimed that this text is the one Joseph Smith used to translate the Book of Abraham.[112]

How would he “use” it? These critics maintain that since he admittedly knew no Egyptian and would need an alphabet, grammar, and dictionary to help him out in his translation, he would have to procure them. Where? He would write them himself! How? By translating the first chapter of the Book of Abraham into English, of course, and then using his English text to discover the meaning of the Egyptian.

After the monumental production of the Book of Mormon and the great narratives of Moses and Abraham in the Pearl of Great Price—all of them Egyptian books—why on earth would anyone contrive a convoluted and tortuous device such as the Alphabet and Grammar to continue the work? Joseph would hardly need such clumsy devices to assist him. It was the brethren with him who craved to read ancient records in unknown ancient languages, especially the glamorous prospect of revealing the secrets of Egypt, the dream of generations of romantics, to draw aside the Veil of Isis. The young men, especially W. W. Phelps who took the lead,[113] were particularly beguiled by the project and soon were competing with each other as well as with their leader. Joseph had given them a free hand, following the principle of studying it out in their own minds and then asking God for confirmation (D&C 9:8). Their jealous motivation soon appeared when they presently turned against the Prophet.[114]

There are six manuscripts of the Alphabet and Grammar in six different handwritings, those of W. W. Phelps, Frederick G. Williams, Warren Parrish, Oliver Cowdery, Willard Richards, and Joseph Smith. The Prophet worked with Cowdery and Phelps at first but only for twelve days.[115] Fortunately we are spared the difficult and dubious business of accurately identifying handwriting, since the whole project got nowhere: the longest version of anybody’s grammar is only one-and-a-half pages long,[116] and the great work on the Alphabet and Grammar only got through the first six letters with the interpretation of only one letter completed.[117] The whole thing was dropped like a hot potato after six weeks.[118] None of the Kirtland Egyptian Papers were ever published in any form before 1967.

These brethren, with the exception of Willard Richards,[119] all turned against the Prophet, bearing their false affidavits and reports, which they later renounced. The project was dead. More was promised after 1835, but the subject is never mentioned again in Church publications until 1842. Moreover, there is no mention of any translating of the Book of Abraham before October 1840. Most of the work on translation was still to be done long after Smith’s coworkers had left the Church.

It was never announced that the work on the Alphabet and Grammar in 1837 was official, let alone inspired. Of the brethren who turned against the Prophet with the wildest accusations, none ever laid the Alphabet and Grammar fiasco at his door. And though he was clear and emphatic in taking responsibility for the revealed writings of the Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price, the Prophet Joseph never claimed that the Alphabet and Grammar was his work. Various of the brethren put their names to the Alphabet and Grammar but never Joseph Smith.

Summary

Thus three major attempts by scholars (in 1860, 1912, and 1967) to discredit Joseph Smith and the Book of Abraham have failed. But what evidence is there in support of the Book of Abraham and its authenticity? That is the subject of the remaining chapters of this book.
