2

What Is the Problem?

Smart and Honest

We have seen how those Egyptologists of the Old School, professing proprietary insight into the minds of the Egyptians, viewed them with their childish fantasies and gross contradictions as not too smart and not too honest. As a matter of fact, according to the New School, the Egyptians confound their critics on both points—they were both smarter and more honest than the moderns. Smart, because they recognized the number one problem confronting the human race, and honest in never assuming, as the learned do today, that the terrible question about death has been solved when it has not.

With them, as with all people, death was the problem. To the living the thought of annihilation is not acceptable, and the Egyptian reveled in life, joyously celebrating its homely blessings as divine dispensations. The Bremner-Rhind Papyrus gives an impressive list of things which the Evil One, Apophis, is denied, the things which make life worthwhile: posterity, one’s own house and grounds, soul, intelligence, body, shades, words of power, healthy members, proper tombs, fields, trees, plants, water, bread, fire, warmth, children, relatives, head, arms, legs, ears, seed, seeing the sun every day. All this as against being chained in the darkness of hell, confined, consuming one’s self like the Ouroboros-serpent, in utter futility.[1] Top priority would be given to the great eternal “gifts of first intent”: They are proclaimed in the ʿnḫ, wḏ, snb formula which follows every Pharaoh’s name: “life, prosperity, health.” [2] Often the words ws (power) and wʿb (purity) are included, and to cap it all, nḥḥ (ever in motion, ever-seeking) and ḏ.t (everlasting, never ending).[3] Plutarch noted that nothing brings greater happiness than reasoning, especially about the gods.[4] The main object of it all is joy (w.t ἰb), “expansion of the heart.” A common appeal broadcast from the tombs and stelae is “to all who love life and hate death.”

The obsession of the Egyptians with death has been played down by the Egyptologists as something blown out of proportion by us: “The Egyptians have been accused of an obsession with ritual and death, and this popular notion (despite evidence to the contrary) is not easily quelled.” [5] Don’t blame the Egyptians for that, Alan H. Gardiner tells us, “nine-tenths of all Egyptian excavations have been conducted on the high desert; . . . to this circumstance is due the overwhelming funerary character of most of the finds.” [6]

Facing Up to It

But let us not lay this flattering unction to our soul that it is the cheap and expendable land in the desert and not our own fear of death that leads us to deflate an alarming proposition. The fact is that the Egyptians really were wrapped up in the awareness of death—and so are we! If philosophy begins in Egypt, as Erik Hornung maintains, its substance is “the still unanswered questions of the meaning of death and the nature of Cosmos and man in it.” [7] Lest it be thought that God created in mankind a dull and naturally indifferent or irreligious nature, the fact that we are incapable of forgetting death proves that religion was implanted in the human heart.[8]

But for Ourselves?

The one subject which is the raison d’être for religion is studiously avoided by the clergy, who seek refuge and comfort in the formalities.[9] The Neo-Freudians have finally recognized “the rediscovery of modern psychology: that death is man’s peculiar and greatest anxiety,” outranking even sex.[10] In his prize-winning book, Ernest Becker finds that “All historical religions addressed themselves to this same problem of how to bear the end of life. Religions like Hinduism and Buddhism performed the ingenious trick of pretending not to want to be reborn.” [11] Not so our Egyptians; Siegfried Morenz has pointed out the complete contrast between the Egyptians’ “eternity of the individual” and the Indians’ “transmigration of the soul,” the one determined to be himself forever and the other resigned to becoming anything you please—a drop of water in an Ocean of Being.[12]

If modern scholars are depressed by the mortuary atmosphere of Egyptian culture, our modern world has an even more demoralizing message—the absolute scientific certainty that man “goes back into the ground a few feet in order blindly and dumbly to rot and disappear forever,” taking with him his vast unrealized potential.[13] No wonder “a full apprehension of man’s condition would drive him insane.” [14] Mircea Eliade concludes his book Cosmos and History with the observation that only in the freedom found in faith can we find “a new formula for man’s collaboration with the creation; . . . only such a freedom . . . is able to defend modern man from the terror of history.” [15] The Egyptians did not look upon the face of death with any more complacency than other people do. The terror is always there, from the Pyramid Texts down to Coptic hagiography.[16] “O Re-Atum . . . save me from that god whose face is that of a hound but whose skin is human, who lives by butchery, who is in charge of the windings of the Lake of Fire, who swallows corpses, who controls hearts, who inflicts injury unseen.” [17] These are no figures of speech; it is all too real.

The Egyptians frankly express their hatred of going into the long night. Though all men have to suffer death, like children at a party we all hate going to bed; “the King hates sleep and detests inertness.” [18] Kings and commoners plead passionately for an extension of time: “O you who take away years and bring days to an end, do not take away my years or bring my days to an end, for I am Horus. . . . I will not die in the abyss. . . . I will not die a second time!” [19]

The final act is too awful. The sand thrown in the face; [20] the cold and motionless obstruction; [21] the rotting, putrefaction, stench, and decay; [22] and finally, the foul and choking dust of the tomb—the Egyptians spare us no detail: “You shall have no putrefaction, O King; you shall have no sweat; you shall have no efflux, O King; you shall have no dust, O King!” [23] The final response to these horrors was flat denial—the whole thing was simply unacceptable: “I will not be seized. My corpse will not be constrained!” [24] “I died, but I have returned alive.” [25] “I disintegrated yesterday but I returned today. . . . I died yesterday, I raised myself today, I returned today.” [26] These people are thinking in terms of millions of years, repeated millions of times.[27] But even that is not enough; they want to go on living “like Re, from eternity in the past to eternity in the future.” [28] What they want is life eternal: “I will not die; I have no end!” [29]

How did these ideas get started? There is no need for speculation about man’s first concern with his place in the cosmos. Let the revered Clement of Rome tell his story. He recounts how as a schoolboy in Rome he started to worry about such questions as, “Whether there be for me any life after death, or whether I am to be wholly annihilated: whether I did not exist before I was born, and whether there shall be no remembrance of this life after death, and so the boundlessness of time shall consign all things to oblivion and silence; so that not only we shall cease to be, but there shall be no remembrance that we have ever been.” [30] The interesting thing about this is that such ideas came to the boy spontaneously, as they have to countless adolescents like Huckleberry Finn; we do not have to look for a school of philosophers in Persia, Alexandria, or Athens to come up with these questions.

No one can give a positive answer to the terrible question until other matters are settled; and so Clement went on to the cosmos: “when the world was made, or what was before it was made, or whether it has existed from eternity. For it seemed certain, that if it had been made, it must be doomed to dissolution; and if it be dissolved, what is to be afterwards?—unless, perhaps, all things shall be buried in oblivion and silence, or something shall be, which the mind of man cannot now conceive.” [31] After much searching, Clement decided that the only solution to his problem was to be found in Egypt.[32] But he did not go to Egypt; he sailed to Palestine instead, where he met Peter, who converted him.[33] Of interest to Latter-day Saints: When Clement asked Peter whether his dead parents who had never heard the gospel were doomed to damnation, Peter replied: “You are forcing me, Clement, to make public certain things which cannot be discussed,” but he assured him that his parents were not damned.[34] Clement had found the answer to all the questions, though they were soon effaced by the allegorists of Alexandria.

That adolescent boys are worried by such questions—and many of them are—is sufficient evidence that they are not the product of thinking by professional clergy, philosophers, or scientists. Most significant is that Joseph Smith at the same age started asking the same questions, which ended up getting him and his family in big trouble (see Joseph Smith—History 1:10–26). There is a rich stock of stories about Abraham asking the same questions at the same age and so alarming his family and the neighbors.[35] It should not be necessary to remind us of the boy Jesus in the temple, astonishing the elders with his wisdom, which was later to get him into trouble with the scribes and Pharisees.

Everybody Worries

But death is not the only worry; a malaise almost as serious as fear of facing death, namely the problem of facing life, is Hamlet’s “weary, flat, stale and unprofitable” prospect.[36] It is a malaise from which we are never completely free. Let us recall how the Old School, including the giants (1895–1914), were bemused by “a mysterious aura” surrounding the Egyptian religion,[37] an impression of sadness and weariness (de tristesse et d’ennui), felt in an Egyptian museum. [38] There was the conviction “that the Middle and New Kingdoms at their height concocted their ideas of the next world in a ‘witch’s lab’ (Hexenküche).” [39]

The Egyptian was pressingly aware of his “commitments to the fragile cosmic order against surrounding and encroaching disorder.” [40] Philippe Derchain in particular has called attention to “this fear of the end of the world (which) is constant right through the history of Egyptian religion, from the Pyramid Texts on down.” [41] “It was the great fear of the Egyptians that cessation of thought would mark ipso facto, the end of the universe.” [42] Just so, the quantum physicists tell us that certain particles exist only when we think of them.[43] Whatever happens, one must keep on thinking, stimulating thought by action in the form of ordinances which could not be neglected.[44] The ultimate purpose of it all was “to avoid a world cataclysm.” [45] Meanwhile, we in our day prefer to repress the whole thing by constantly, as Søren Kierkegaard says, tranquilizing ourselves with the trivial, [46] or becoming grim workaholics like Adolf Erman. As Becker noted, Freud himself “suffered his whole life from phobias and from death-anxiety. I don’t believe the complex symbol of death is ever absent, no matter how much inner sustainment a person has.” [47] For Freud “Egypt stood for the whole mysterious and dark past of mankind that psychoanalysis was chosen to decipher.” [48]

Intellectual Solutions

After stating the terrible questions both at the beginning and ending of his Brief History of Time, Stephen Hawking notes that “to try to answer these questions, we adopt some ‘world picture.’ ” [49] This is exactly what the ancients did; we assume that their picture is mythological while ours is scientific, but as Hawking assures us, “Both theories lack observational evidence.” [50] To this day they both remain theories. But they are much worth considering.

Clement’s question, “Is this all there is?” has never been settled either way. The Egyptians returned to the issue again and again. Today’s Egyptologists credit the Egyptians with real scientific insight; they congratulate them on the realistic thinking which replaced that obsessive mysticism with which they were so long credited. “What not long ago was called religion,” wrote Alexandre Piankoff, “was not a mystery religion but physical science, strangely reminiscent of the ideas of Heraclitus.” [51]

Ever concerned with the main problem, they come up with an astonishing variety of theories and scenarios to show that getting to heaven may be a scientific possibility. They wanted to “enter in the cyclical ebb and flow of the universe in order to overcome death, as illustrated in the desire to accompany the sun-god in his boat.” [52] The dead body was taken below to regenerate itself, “not to remain permanently, but to emerge again from the depths that had been opened.” [53] This theory requires another theory to explain it. It means that the mummy-wrapping is preparation not for eternal sleep but for a journey, a temporary protective sheathe, to be cast off, so to speak, once the body reaches the afterlife.[54] The tomb was the proper place for resurrection because that is where the spirit and the body are to be united into a resurrected body in an eternal individual.

How Is It Done?

The objective is clear enough and rational enough, but the problem is “Lord, how is it done?” (Enos 1:7). The first step and favorite solution to difficult problems, like that of the atom bomb, is to show that it actually can be solved. That, we are told, was half the battle at Los Alamos. The Egyptian is constantly demonstrating that his great problems have been solved by others: If A can demonstrate that a thing can be done, then B has a right to give it a try; and if it works for B, why not for C? “If Osiris lives, Wenis lives! If Osiris does not die, Wenis does not die! If Osiris does not perish, Wenis does not perish,” etc.; the king gives 24 such examples.[55] “If Osiris’s bones come together, then mine will; his bread is my bread,” etc.[56] This is the subject matter of Egyptian funerary literature from the oldest Pyramid Texts, the Coffin Texts, the Book of the Dead, the New Kingdom Books of the Netherworld such as the Amduat, the Litany of Re, down to the hypocephali and the Book of Breathings (see p. 53, fig. 5).[57]

The supreme example of what can be done in the way of eternal life and constant rejuvenation, of setting only to rise again, is of course the sun. Every dead person wishes to become a follower of Re, a member of his cortege as he passes to heaven, and as such take on his character, as Osiris did—he being a new Osiris.[58] “When Petosiris is strong, Re is strong, and vice versa. The hair of Petosiris is the hair of Nun, his face is the face of Re, his eye the eye of Hathor, . . . his nose of Khent-Amentit, his lips of Anubis, his teeth of Serkhet, his neck of Isis, his arms of (Osiris) the Ram of Mendes” (who in turn fuses with Re).[59] “I daily come into being as Re, and I live after life as Re does.” [60] If a god is defined by his activity, and if in a given situation I do exactly as the god does, then in that context I am identical with him. As already noted, one of the first discovered Book of Breathings, which sometimes accompanied the hypocephalus and at times served in its place, bore the title, “May My Name Flourish, Even as the Name of Re Flourishes.” [61] Security lies in being close to Re.

Re/Osiris Formula

A formula that is often referred to by Egyptologists today is the Re/Osiris formula.[62] It is the key to the great rational attack on the terrible question about death. In the story in its simplest form, the spirit comes down from above as a being of light and joins the body in the dark tomb below, and the two emerge together, mounting up to heaven. One of the many aspects of the sun-god Re is Osiris.[63] The story invites many embellishments and is constantly repeated as the sun-god appears in various aspects and under many names. The basic idea, like the questions of Clement, is universal. Death takes us down below, resurrection can only be an upward journey: “O Sun-disc . . . may you shine in [his] face . . . may [his] soul go up with you to the sky . . . may he mix with the Unwearying Stars in the sky.” [64]

The most dramatic exposition, the Mendes ceremony, recounts how Re is invited to come down (he yearns for the body), is shown the way to the tomb, stands at the door, knocks, and when the door is open floods the place with his rays as he studies the Osiris figure on the bier. Finally, he goes to it and either stretches out his hand and raises it up, or makes an incision and actually enters into it. The two beings thus united proceed to the horizon where Re continues on his way to the everlasting stars, while Osiris goes back to bed until evening.[65] Since nightly reunion is assured, the event is a permanent one: “Raise yourself Osiris, that you may go round about the sky with Re. . . . I will not be turned away from you, Osiris!” [66]

Winfried Barta noted that the main theme, even of the Pyramid Texts, is “the exaltation (Verklärung, Vergöttlichung) of Osiris through unity with the sun-god.” [67] The Ram of Mendes embraces and infuses with his double to form the “Double-Ba.” [68] Hermann Grapow, who devoted more study to this situation than any other scholar, confessed that the whole thing escaped him.[69] The embracing of the two, body and spirit, is the supreme moment of resurrection.

Sometimes it is Horus, the son of Re, who goes to see Osiris; they meet as the embalming is completed, but then Re takes over, first contemplating Osiris and then embracing him.[70] In this uniting of spirit and element through an intermediary principle, we see the essence of the alchemist’s quest, the subject of the Sefer Yetzirah, a famous book attributed to Abraham.

It is most singular that the hypocephalus emphatically shows us as an actual physical reality, Re and Osiris, past and present, yesterday and tomorrow, as one and the same entity and yet distinctly two beings, as explained by the Egyptian scribe. Such a conjunction is only possible, of course, when both meet as Harakhty—Horus on the Horizon, the place of transition with the sun-disk exactly on the line between the upper and lower worlds, being in both at once for the usual magical moment.[71] The Egyptian scribe of Book of the Dead 17 explains that the one Janus head is Yesterday, Osiris, and the other is Tomorrow, Re at his rising.[72]

To the Stars

The sun of course brings the moon and stars in with him; one of the real marks of progress in the study of ancient theology is the recognition of the whole cosmos as the setting which we must take into account for the future glory of the deceased. A word that often comes up in studies of Egyptian theology is Verklärung (Egyptian ḫ), “glorification, exaltation,” leading on to infinite expansion. If one is looking for an assured hereafter, the natural place to look is among the Undying Stars, the northern stars that never set (fig. 4). We refer those who protest that the hypocephalus, to which Joseph Smith ascribes a cosmic significance, was only a funerary fixture to Wolfhart Westendorf’s conclusion that “everywhere in the funeral cult, images from the cosmos are employed as a model and utilized in the cult.” [73]

Figure 4. The carefully constructed ten-inch square shafts within the Great Pyramid of Khufu point to important stars. They seem to be launching ramps for the pharaoh to join the star-gods. The shafts on the north point to the North Pole and its circumpolar stars, those Undying Ones that never set. The remaining stars, the Unwearying Ones, are born and then die to spend seventy days in the underworld until they are seen again.

As he fares over various waters with the immortal stars to guide him,[74] or moves on a tour of inspection among them,[75] the king is in his element—if you can get into his entourage, you are on your way home. “I shall not be turned back at the gates of the Netherworld. I ascend to the sky with Orion; . . . my abode is at the high portals of the entourage of Re.” [76] “As for anyone who is in his suite or who lives in the suite of Thoth, . . . I am a follower of Re.” [77]

On to Abraham

Why have we gone to such lengths to expose the theology of the funerary literature? It is because the Book of Abraham and the papyri joined to it are not only examples of the literature but the most outstanding examples of all.

Aut Cosmos Aut Nihil

Though medieval and modern theologians vigorously condemn “cosmism” [78]—the view that the universe is self-contained and uncreated—there is, to quote the eminent Egyptologist Gerardus van der Leeuw, “a human inclination, general as well as Christian, . . . to base trust on one’s salvation on the cosmos; . . . only when the human passion of a divine Savior has a cosmic background does salvation seem sufficiently assured.” [79] Hence Arthur O. Lovejoy can conclude that in religious writings of any period “the language of acosmism . . . is never to be taken too literally.” [80] So Origen, first and best-informed of Christian theologians, declares triumphantly, “When finally, for example, the Saints shall reach the celestial place, then shall they discern in detail the systematic knowledge (ratio) of the stars; . . . indeed they will also understand other principles of the works of God, which he himself will reveal to them.” [81] This is the teaching of the early brethren for which he is an authority; but Origen’s own Alexandrian training breaks through at the end of the passage when he appeals for “perfect knowledge (scientia perfecta) . . . purged of all that is physical and corporeal.” [82] This cosmism is one of the most conspicuous and distinguishing features of Mormonism among modern religions, and its main support is the Pearl of Great Price, especially the Book of Abraham.

“The soul’s journey through the heavenly spheres is a central subject in the religion of the New Kingdom,” writes László Kákosy, who goes on to note how the tradition carries on through Gnostic, Jewish, and early Christian apocalyptic writing, while the idea is already perfectly at home in the Pyramid Texts.[83] And it is a real rather than fanciful realm; the more recent interpretation of Egyptian religion emphasizes the remarkable “lack of any ‘mystical’ aspects” to the picture,[84] which we must not confuse with the later speculations of the mystics. Still, the Egyptians are trusting as much as the mystics to imagination and speculation. “It was Shesemetet who bore me, a star brilliant and far-traveling, who brings distant products to Re daily.” [85] The principal concern of the Egyptians is with the lower regions of the universe into which the stars descend and move from west to east, that being necessary if they are to rise with Re’s resurrection on the morrow.

Even now we have the singular privilege of gazing with our natural eyes into the depths of infinity and beholding there worlds without number, to any of which we may retire. “You shall take your ease on the firmament; you shall be more starry than the stars of the horizon as that Lone Star which is in the west of the sky and the east of the earth.” [86] The stars are more than a backdrop; they are our destination.[87] “O Morning Star, make a path for me.” [88] “May N be encircled by Orion, by Sothis [Sirius] and by the Morning Star, may they set you within the arms of your mother Nut [the heavens].” [89] This takes us to the edge of a grand canyon, of an awful abyss.

The Great and Awful Gulf

Our eternal home may lie out there, but how are we going to get there? Modern science is not the only one to ask; the Egyptians were just as exercised about that practical problem as NASA is today. They worked on the project in dead earnest. Great things are going on in the cosmos, but for me the big question is, how do I get in on it all?

As Barta puts it, the purpose of the rites and ordinances that king and commoner alike must attend to is to achieve the passage from this side to that side (Übergang vom Diesseits ins Jenseits), and to be “glorified” (Egyptian sḫw). In this process the deceased is “revivified (wiederbelebt) and made divine (vergöttlicht)—that is, to be endowed with the nature and attributes of a divine existence.” [90] That those people should even think of something so outrageously bold has ever suggested to later ages that they must have had some special knowledge that we no longer possess.

If they were serious about it, careful preparation was needed for the fearful passage into the unimaginable. The bridging of the gulf is an attempt “to put mankind into contact with the world of the Ganz-Anderen (totally other).” [91] Hornung compares the operation to the outfitting “of a more ancient ‘cosmonaut’ to the stars of the other side,” for which he must have a “restrictive but protective mummy-casing” until “the rays of the sun shall awaken him.” [92] The seventy days of embalming prepare him “for the journey to the stars,” providing “not a new body . . . but a protective covering for the long and dangerous transport into the realm of the dead.” [93] The constant reference to Orion, Sirius, the Pleiades, and Ursa Major (and to them only) again suggests a familiarity with those “star factories” (see p. 42, fig. 4).

We seem to have moved into the sphere of science fiction. How is one to cross the great gulf once the pod is provided? Simply to spiritualize the event or to enlist the all-too-convenient sources of magic will not do—if that were allowed there would be no problem. The king works on the puzzle from all angles, trying one thing after another. There is a note of urgency and excitement in his ingenious mental experiments; he thinks of every possible device; after one quite plausible formula, he goes on to suggest distinctly inferior devices, unwilling to run the risk of overlooking the true solution. This open-mindedness clearly suggests that they do not consider the problem solved. The priests and scribes never engage in heated argument about the true explanation of things; their tolerance is anything but the impassioned religious zeal that marks the disputations of the doctors. The search for a scientific answer never reached a dogmatic solution. All we have is the assurance that “the doors of the firmament are thrown open to me . . . he has given me the sacred images of the Old Ones who preceded me.” [94] The scientific explanation remains on the level of speculation, one explanation following another; it is all theory. Consider some examples of this.

Hitching a Ride

There are two main steps in getting there. The first is to find someone or something that is demonstrably capable of traveling through the void on its own power. The second is to attach oneself to that same entity and enjoy the ride. The most direct approach is to lay some legitimate claim to the carrier’s assistance. First of all, there is the sun, who strides across vast distances in a moment, according to the Solar Hymn, visible evidence that it is possible to pass through the void. Since “the characteristics of the dead correspond exactly to those of the sun-god,” [95] the deceased should be able to do what the sun-god can do—in particular span the space to heaven. This suggests that his intimate ties with the sun may enable him to make use of the sun’s beams for ascent.[96] The readiest approach would be simply to take his hand and walk across the Milky Way with him as he makes his daily trip on foot with Khepri.[97]

Nature Assists

Man is an organic part of nature, and in Egypt we always find “a basic blending of religion and overt aspects of nature.” [98] Thus, we can let the air itself give us a boost, “rise as air.” [99] “I shine in the sky; I climb on the sunbeams.” [100] “The king is Osiris in a whirlwind . . . he will not enter into Geb (the Earth) lest he perish and lest he sleep in his mansion on earth.” [101] He rises with the updrafts of the cumulo-nimbus with the hail,[102] riding on the four winds,[103] as a dust-devil or twisted rope,[104] on the wildfire, a flame before the wind, or fire in the sunlight; [105] as smoke [106] or incense.[107] We can identify with little things that ride upon the wind—the birds, insects, and beetles [108]—or with the amazing propulsion of the grasshopper.[109] All this seems rather far-fetched, but the idea is not to overlook any possibility—because one of them might work. Being surrounded by miracles, it is quite possible that we may sometime take advantage of some of them—which is what the Wright brothers did.

Technology, High and Low

The great crossing is a problem in physics; the king tries one thing after another.[110] The Egyptians thought of a surprising number of mechanical means of mounting to the sky, beginning with a knotted rope ladder, which became a great favorite: “A ladder is knotted together by Re in the presence of Osiris; a ladder is knotted together by Horus before his father Osiris when he goes to his spirit; one of them being on one side and one on the other while I am between them.” [111] “A ladder is set up for him that he may ascend on it in its name of ‘Ascent to the Sky.’ ” [112] “I set up the ladder, and those who are in the West grasp my hand.” [113] The metaphor shows that all of this is figurative, yet there must always be a workable model to copy. Stairways are more practical, but they belong to a more grandiose conception which is below.

Masters of Space

Above all, the Egyptians were dedicated bird-watchers. The king ascends to heaven as a falcon or hawk, as a duck, and as a kite.[114] A classical writer tells us that for them “the hawk is the symbol for light and spirit, because of its quickness of motion (ὀξυκινησἱα, oxykinēsia) and the great heights to which it mounts, disappearing into the light.” [115] Recently, Alfred Grimm has called attention to the combination of hawk and sun-disk in royal ornaments going clear back to predynastic times to show that the king is moving through the sky in a glory represented by both the bird and the sun. He also notes that Pharaoh can be represented by different birds in different capacities, but always with the idea of motion through the heavens.[116]

The migratory birds have an especially strong appeal since they “travelled to places beyond the familiar world yet also returned regularly to that world.” [117] The swallow and the goose gave such earnest of the resurrection.[118] Glorified spirits are represented by the akh-bird, a crested ibis , and the ba by the jabiru-stork figure found conspicuously on many of the hypocephali. But it is especially the phoenix which draws our attention. The phoenix was a fabled bird of Arabia which every five hundred years returned to its nest in Heliopolis to sacrifice itself and arise anew from its ashes. The story was told in the First Epistle of Clement (perhaps the earliest authentic Christian writing after the New Testament) as definite proof of the resurrection[119] and, following the Latin of Lactantius, first of the Latin fathers, became an Old English classic in the translation of King Alfred the Great. What interests us here is its commanding position in the rim of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, where the shrine of the phoenix (benben) in Heliopolis is twice, and perhaps three times, depicted (see appendix 2B). At the top of the circle, it takes the form of the blunt shrine seen next to the feather.

Water Transport

If any people should know how important it is to get from one side of the great expanse to the other, it is the dwellers on the Nile. Without a need for complete identification, Re offers to all his family ample accommodations on that ship with which he has cruised the heavens since prehistoric times.[120] “Go aboard the Bark of Re . . . aboard which the Gods desire to go, as Re rows to the horizon,” into the next world.[121] You can claim a place in the ship as a member of the family.[122] If that is too much, one can volunteer as a member of the crew, working an oar,[123] or one may simply appeal to the natural good-heartedness of the Egyptian, who feels for anyone in the dire condition of being “boatless” (ἰwἰ) in a land of waters. The great noble Pa-Hery counts among his meritorious deeds, “I ferried the boatless.” [124]

The great waters offer a means of access as well as a barrier—the two banks “kiss” when the river is at flood. One can get across by swimming,[125] using a raft of reeds,[126] canoeing,[127] or sensibly taking the ferry.[128]

But of course Re’s own boat is the best, and on most of the hypocephali it is depicted (see appendixes 5–6), sometimes carrying his whole family of gods. Re always takes the king along,[129] and by far the most successful and commonest approach is to be taken on as one of the royal entourage, “the followers (šms.w) of Re.” [130] This is what one explicitly asks for on the rim of some hypocephali.[131] Calls of “Take me with you!” [132] resound throughout the funeral literature.

Summary

Professor Barta has established in detail the evolution in the use of funerary texts (fig. 5). The Pyramid Texts, which in the Old Kingdom were reserved exclusively for the king, began, in the Middle Kingdom, to be used by private individuals in their coffins;[133] with additions these became Coffin Texts.[134] In the New Kingdom, the Book of the Dead utilized some of the older texts as well as adding new ones.[135] Also in the New Kingdom new funerary texts, such as the Amduat and the Litany of the Sun were composed and in turn reserved for the king.[136] Finally, in the Late Period we find the Book of Breathings, a sort of abbreviated and condensed version of the Book of the Dead,[137] and the hypocephalus. But it is all a sort of play, every device being followed up with another, as if they were not sure which was the best, but all with the ultimate goal of overcoming death.

Figure 5. From the earliest times, the religious texts were available to those who could pay for them. The timelines are general and approximate.
