3

Dispensations and Axial Times

The Great Dispensations

According to the Oxford English Dictionary, dispensation is “the action of dispensing or dealing out; distribution or administration to others; expenditure, spending, or disbursement (of money); economical use or disposal (of anything).” [1] Specifically, a dispensation is “a special dealing of Providence with a community, family, or person, dispensing blessing, affliction, or other event.” [2] For the Latter-day Saints, a dispensation of the gospel is a time when the fulness of the gospel is being made available to men on earth by direct visitation of heavenly beings. All the knowledge that humans possess about their earthly condition comes to them by way of dispensations, handouts if you will, from a more than human source of wisdom. This runs counter to the standard picture of the slow, steady, patient, strictly objective, and impartial collecting of facts in the irresistible forward march of science. However, we hear more today about inspired individuals and quantum leaps.

About the year 600 b.c. the whole thinking community—philosophy, math, physics, even religion—suddenly took a new direction.[3] In the Melchizedek Priesthood manual for 1957, this writer noted that Joseph Smith in the Book of Mormon had chosen the best possible moment for the founding of a new civilization in the New World. It was the year 600 b.c. when the greatest Greek colonies—Massilia (Marseilles) in the West and Olbia in the East—were founded. Everyone was taking part in new settlement projects or forming companies to finance them. The search for new resources and new horizons was everybody’s business. It was a time of unparalleled exploration and colonization, of inventions and ideas, of trade and commerce. In 600 b.c. Hanno the navigator sailed clear around Africa. It was the year that Solon, the founder of Athenian democracy, left Athens to visit Phoenicia, where Thales at the same time accurately predicted the eclipse of 585 b.c. He shares honors with the great Heraclitus, the “physicists” of the Miletian School, and the immortal Pythagoras in the West. Many of the great religious founders of history were living then—Gautama Buddha, Confucius, Lao Tze, Vardhaman Mahavira (the founder of Jainism), and Zarathustra. The Seven Wise Men were wandering through the world to give, seek, and exchange wisdom with each other.[4]

This was what Karl Jaspers in the 1940s called an “Achsenzeit,” or axial period.[5] The “axial” phenomenon is that sudden and abrupt turning of thinking men everywhere like a military line on parade, wheeling on its axis, to face in a new direction. Today it is receiving more attention than ever before from philosophers and historians. This great axial period is defined as the move from ancient to modern thinking, from the mythic and mystic thinking that had prevailed right down to the time of Descartes, to be replaced by logical, rational, positivist thought.

But, as we have noted, the same decades that produced the great scientists also produced that great and most influential group of religious founders. This created an enormous gap between the two modes of thought, which far from being bridged is now wider than ever.

There has been, of course, more than one axial time. Professor Jan Assmann sees the First Dynasty of Egypt as the great axial period because that is as far back as human memory can reach, making it “the fresh morning of the First Time.” [6] Erik Hornung sees in the Amarna revolution a most distinctive axial period which got rid of the long prevailing ideas of underworld, hereafter, and judgment.[7] Stefan Breuer claims that Israel produced “the first breakthrough in history to a new political order that institutionalized the freedom of man and the responsibility of the ruler.” [8] “It is thus God himself and not, as is otherwise usual in the ancient Orient, the king who functions as the lawgiver.” [9] This in turn prepared the way for the great axial period of the sixth century b.c., and so on.

The idea of abrupt and radical axial changes in the course of history was ever repugnant to the smooth, steady, even imperceptible flow of evolution. Only since the 1960s have we undergone another axial change in accepting the heretofore unthinkable doctrine of real axial shifts in galaxies, the planet, the species, and human history. The Big Bang is an axial event if there ever was one; a minimum of at least five major upheavals changed the geology and of course the biology of the earth. Thus the axial period in history is not only minor in comparison but directly dependent on all the others.

Axial Dispensations

The idea is not hard to grasp. Chrétien de Troyes in the Middle Ages began one of his poems by recalling how Greece had the first chivalry, laws, and clergy; these then passed to Rome “and have now come to France. God grant that they may stay there!” [10] The Mormon term for an axial event is a dispensation. There is no better textbook on the subject than the Pearl of Great Price with its systematic, chronological order of one dispensation leading to the next, just as every axial time begins at the moment a new order arises from the ruins of the old. There are seven dispensations, and they are real breakthroughs, for the book shows how each dispensation, corrupt and exhausted, ends like a burnt-out star with a bang, scattering the world’s population in the scramble of migrations. To list them briefly:

1. Adam received everything at the outset, but his children “loved Satan more than God” (Moses 5:13), and the Cainites prevailed over the Sethians; they took the human race down into the plains with them to live in the land of Nod—that is, of Nomadism.

2. A special crash program sent the Watchers on to the scene, but they, the Sons of God, when they were enticed by the daughters of men, fell and perverted and corrupted everything. Enoch, who preached to the pastoral nomads on the mountains, led his righteous city away, and the others fled in vain.

3. By all accounts the greatest axial event of all was the flood. Noah started a whole new world, his children migrating in all directions. But they too sinned and the earth was blighted by drought and famine right down to the time of the next dispensation.

4. Abraham was a nomad among nomads as “the famine [waxed] sore in the land” (Abraham 2:1).

5. Next Moses handed on to us the whole story from Abraham back through Enoch to Adam. He too was a perpetual migrant and promised the same fate to his own people if they were lax in their covenants, which they soon were.

6. They (whose two thousand years of wandering, their axial calamity, was “only the beginning of the sorrows,” Joseph Smith—Matthew 1:19) rejected Jesus Christ.

7. And then finally, with Joseph Smith, ours is frankly “the last dispensation” as the world winds down to another sudden ending—they all come suddenly on a day of business-as-usual (Joseph Smith—Matthew 1:41–43).

This is the idea of “constant rebirth,” an axial time, which Hornung says has been sorely neglected and is being rediscovered.[11] This Wiederholbarkeit (“repeatability” ), as Horst Klengel calls it, can take place at various times, affect various areas, and follow various tempos; but the really special one is the rise of new ideologies in the axial period of 650–450 b.c.[12] We are reminded that the Egyptians, [13] like the Romans, [14] thought in terms of preordained renewal. A fixed idea with the early Christians was the alternate Summertime and Wintertime of the Just,[15] or the procession of the periods of “black water” and “white water” in the Jewish apocrypha in 2nd Baruch.[16] There are also other “dark ages” attested by the horrendous lamentation literature of Egypt, describing with minute reportorial accuracy the miseries attending the collapse of civilization at the end of the Old Kingdom.[17]

What makes these calamities so long-lived is the weather; the climate is bound to change sometimes, and when it does the whole world is forced to get on the move. Thus in the “dark ages,” from the twelfth to the ninth century b.c., following the Mycenean, Ugaritic, and Hittite collapse, Anatolia was left to be resettled by nomads, the later Phrygians and Lydians who occupied the empty land during the “Neoglacial” cold period, when primitive cultures took over from “the Pacific to the Atlantic.” [18]

An axial time is an apocalyptic time. It is important to emphasize that each axial period ends almost at the moment when the next begins—like the circle of the year. That is the apocalyptic pattern. The idea is strikingly expressed by Ezra the scribe: “I answered and said, ‘What will be the dividing of the times? Or when will be the end of the first age and the beginning of the age that follows?’ He said to me, ‘From Abraham to Isaac, because from him were born Jacob and Esau, for Jacob’s hand held Esau’s heel from the beginning.’ . . . ‘And they shall see the men who were taken up, who from their birth have not tasted death; and the heart of the earth’s inhabitants shall be changed and converted to a different spirit.’ ” [19] The Jewish apocrypha attributes the teaching to Abraham: “This is the city of which I said: ‘On the palms of my hand have I graven thee . . . that which was prepared beforehand, or from the time I took counsel to create Paradise, and showed it to Adam before he sinned . . . and after that I showed it to my servant Abraham by night . . . and again I showed it to Moses on Mount Sinai.’ ” [20]

As mentioned above, the Pearl of Great Price contains seven such dispensations under the names of the mortals who were chosen to lead forth in their establishment—Adam, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Christ, and the Prophet Joseph. Each one is a straightforward historical account. Taking such a position invites the most rigorous critical examination of the record, but the Pearl of Great Price has never enjoyed such because Egyptologists have not thought it worth their time, as they made clear in the great campaign of 1912. What the Egyptologists saw before them was from first to last mythological figures, which Joseph Smith interpreted as real historical characters. What point is there to discussing Abraham’s autobiography with men who firmly believed that Abraham never existed except as a myth? This unbridgeable gap between those who persisted in accepting the reality of revelation and visitations from heaven, and those who cannot under any circumstances seriously imagine or tolerate the reality of such things, is the result of an axial shift.

The Great Triangle

Of the three great cultures of the ancient world—China, India, and the Near East—it would seem that only the last achieved a real axial breakthrough. Isolated from each other,[21] China and India have their own breakthroughs—that is, a great one in China in the fourth century and in India in the sixth. But they got stuck on the threshold of an axial period, their high technical achievements lacking the great spiritual achievement of a true breakthrough.[22] On the other hand, the Near Eastern axial shift produced a single civilization which persisted “entirely in the Roman-antique sense.” [23] “With the axial time of the sixth century,” wrote Jaspers, “arose the man with which we live today.” [24]

The Near Eastern section in turn falls into three main subdivisions—the Egyptian, Greek, and Chaldean (or Semitic).[25] Iamblichus, the great champion of Hellenism, attributes that civilization to those three great cultures of equal age and importance.[26] The three cultures are perfectly fused in the Egyptian Thoth, who is the Greek Hermes as well as the Semitic Enoch. Josephus reports that Abraham greatly enjoyed his stay in Egypt, attending the lectures of the priests and learning their views about the gods.[27] He gave them in return the latest developments in math and astronomy, “these things coming to Egypt from the Chaldeans, and so from the Egyptians passed on to the Greeks.” [28] The youthful Clement of Rome looked for the answers to the “terrible questions,” first among Greek philosophers in the Roman schools. Failing there, he planned to go to Egypt but instead ended up finding his answers in Caesarea.[29] Another model case would be the great high priest Petosiris of the fifth century whose lavishly inscribed and painted tomb shares Egyptian, Greek, and Hebrew motifs and wisdom almost equally.[30]

Three Witnesses

Why are we so concerned with these three? Because they have all recorded the so-called great breakthrough in grandiose epic style. They tell identical stories. The Egyptian and the Greek versions are closely tied together, but no more closely than to the Semitic. This is surprising because the fullest account of the Semitic version is the account of the great axial calamity in the Pearl of Great Price. What is significant is how closely it matches the two classic tales to which we now address ourselves. The Egyptians and the Greeks provide two witnesses for the story and thus confirm the reports of it given to us by the Prophet Joseph.

At all times throughout the ancient Near East the mobility of religious personnel, artisans, builders, minstrels, healers, and military experts was facilitated by the guilds (δημιοργοἱ, dēmioergoi) to which they belonged, which would take them in wherever they were.[31] The Egyptians describe Osiris as one of those benefactors of mankind who wanted “to visit all the inhabited earth and teach the race of men how to cultivate the vine and sow wheat and barley.” [32] The three always hosted a wandering civilization of craftsmen and philosophers (the Seven Wise Men).[33] An Egyptian scribe of the New Kingdom was expected to know the geography of Syria and Palestine.[34] Diplomatic relations among the three areas were “astonishingly intimate and lively.” [35] As it is summed by Professor Siegfried Herrmann, “On Syrian soil Egyptian tradition meets the Greek mind with which it enters into a happy symbiosis.” [36]

According to Pseudo-Eupolemus, “Abram lived in Heliopolis where he instructed the Egyptians in the discoveries made by Enoch and himself,” [37] thus recognizing the Triad—the Egyptian, Greek, and Semitic influences. The Brooklyn Papyrus shows the names of the patriarchs flourishing in Egypt in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries b.c.[38] Describing the Osirian mystery-drama, Theodoretus tells how Orpheus took it to Greece and how the Egyptian and Hebrew lore were mixed in the rites.[39] We learn in the book of 2nd Enoch (The Secrets of Enoch), written by one equally instructed in Egyptian and Hebrew theology, that the ancient cosmogony of Heliopolis was the common property of Egyptians and Hebrews.[40] In the great Fourth and Fifth Dynasties, “Egypt was wide-open to foreigners.” [41] Arthur Keith asked why scientists should spend time comparing Egyptian and Palestinian skulls, which it so happens are “surprisingly similar.” “Why go to Egypt?” he asks. Are those people not the common Mediterranean type? [42] And many have pointed out that the road from Egypt to Palestine, one of the oldest roads in the world, often traversed by invading armies from Egypt into Asia and vice versa, was only a flat stretch of road one hundred miles long. Sinuhe, an Egyptian official, became a Canaanite desert chief; [43] and the unfortunate Wenamun, another important official, spent years as the unwilling guest of the king of Byblos and ended up as a suppliant of the royal mistress of a Minoan island.[44]

While the cultures of Egypt and Greece are monumentally distinctive, the northeast corner of the triangle is an area in which there is an overlapping and mixture of various conquering nations: Hurrian, Hittite, Mittanian, Amoraean, Phoenician, and of course Canaanite and Chaldean occupants. In the Middle Bronze Age, William F. Albright reports, “the civilization of Phoenicia, southern Syria and Palestine was quite homogeneous.” [45] Between the fifteenth and thirteenth centuries b.c., this civilization used a common epic language, and its gods were worshipped all over Palestine and Syria, as well as in Egypt.[46] The connection with Egypt was traditional—it was the custom for Syrian kings to be replaced by their own sons who had been carefully educated in Egypt.[47] We have already noted the exchange of Wisdom literature between Palestine and Egypt.[48] The rabbis of later days were strongly averse to having Abraham in Egypt at all and cut his time there as short as possible, limiting his stay to three months,[49] while the older writers have him there seven years,[50] two of which Sarah spent at the palace, and some even extended it to twenty.[51] The Book of Abraham explicitly states that great religious celebrations were a joint Egyptian and Canaanite undertaking with the four great gods of the region joining in homage to “the god of Pharaoh, king of Egypt” (Abraham 1:6). Hornung points out that, at least in the New Kingdom, “foreign peoples were also included in the sphere of action (Wirkungskreis) of the Egyptian creator-god; he had created them as well as the whole world and kept them alive.” [52]

We have yet to consider relationships between the northeastern angle of the triangle and the other two. Franz Altheim, the religious historian, shows us how Asia presents great expanses, overpowering oppressive distances, cruel extremes of climate, and catastrophic storms. People live more and more on the edge the deeper you penetrate into Asia.[53] But a strange thing happens in Syria; as we go west the ambience changes completely because “most of Syria belongs to the Mediterranean lands” of Greece, Italy (Magna Graeca), and Egypt. It is another world: the colors are brighter, the air is fresher and clearer, the light is exciting, and the landscape is made for human habitation, for a different type of human. The division between the two worlds runs the length of Syria. On the seaside we get the cultivated cities of Antioch, Seleucia, Apamaeia, and Laodicaea, where the Bedouin and the city dweller meet in the places of business and exchange.[54]

There are ample sources connecting the big three with each other. Why do we bring this up? Because in the dramatic Triad the Egyptians and Greeks seem to have got there first with the most; they have the greatest and oldest of the plays. Surely something is missing here. The vast northeast angle has yielded some Ugaritic plays on the flood, notably the Epic of Baal and Yam.[55] The Semitic corner would be left out were it not for the Pearl of Great Price, which presents us with the same magnificent dramas transmitted to us by Abraham and Moses in their respective books. Moreover, their contribution has direct bearing on the hypocephalus.

The Oldest Play?

When Shabako, the second pharaoh of the Twenty-fifth (Nubian) Dynasty began his reign, he planned to celebrate the launching of the new age and the new dynasty with the same rites as were celebrated at the founding of the kingdom itself, of the First Dynasty by Menes, the first pharaoh. Menes had built a special shrine for celebrating the occasion, and Shabako set about renovating this building for a repeat performance for his own glorious inauguration (fig. 6). In the process the workers discovered a manuscript, a leather scroll that had been hidden in the wall; it was the libretto of the play staged for the original dedication. Nothing could be more appropriate than to produce the sacred drama again.

Do we need to note that it was the drama of the great New Year–New Age commemoration of the creation? Erik Iversen has attempted to show that the text is no earlier than the Nineteenth Dynasty. But the important thing is that it was accepted by everybody in high places as going back to the beginning. More important is Iversen’s conclusion that it is not meant to be pure myth or ritual but real history: “a typical example of what might be called mythical historiography. By the ancient mythographer the mythical events chronicled in the text were obviously considered factual historical events; . . . we are therefore faced with the first deliberate efforts to develop and apply an established historical methodology.” [56] The text of the play had been eaten by worms, as the king explains in an inscription on the top of the panel, and what is worse, the stone on which Shabako had the text inscribed was subsequently used as a millstone by farmers in postpharaonic times, and so most of the central part of the writing was obliterated.

The play opens with the summoning of all the gods to the great council at the creation. “Great and mighty is Ptah who grants his power to his heirs the gods and the spirits through his heart and his tongue. By it Horus (his son) had his being; by it Thoth (his lieutenant) had his being in Ptah.” [57] “Power (authority, sḫm) was in the heart and in the tongue, investing all living members.” [58] At once we think of the creation by the “work” and the “words” of God: “There is no end to my works, neither to my words” (Moses 1:38). First it is the plan and then it is made known, shared with his children: “This is my work and my glory—to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man” (Moses 1:39). In the same spirit our text continues. “The teaching is that he dwells, as it were, in every body and in every mouth, in all gods, in all men, in all creeping things, in whatsoever has life. For his heart conceives and his word commands whatever things he wills to be.” [59] “The council of the gods is in his presence as his own teeth and lips (i.e., his words) . . . the lips of that god which gave all things their names and out of which Shu and Tefnet [the first divine couple] came to be.” [60]

Figure 6. Shabako’s workmen found an ancient, worm-eaten scroll recording the theology of the priests of Ptah at Memphis in the form of a creation drama complete with stage directions. The pharaoh, recognizing its value, had the text carved onto a hard stone to preserve it. Ca. 700 b.c. The British Museum.

				 The agents of creation are constantly mentioned by the Egyptians as sἰ, “perception, knowledge, intelligence,” [61] and ḥw, “authoritative utterance.” [62] This is the power of creation according to Joseph Smith as well, “The glory of God is intelligence” (D&C 93:36) who created all things “by the word of [his] power” (D&C 29:30).

Initiatory

Cooperating with the Creator, “the council made the eyes that they might see, the ears that they might hear, and the breath of the nostrils that these might convey information to the heart or mind,” which in turn considers, or “repeats what is duly conveyed by the tongue.” [63] The words must always precede the works if this is to be shared by all of God’s children: “But every (creative) word of God arose first in the heart and mind, for which the tongue uttered command.” [64] “Thus all the gods were born and his Ennead was completed.” [65] The new world being thus prepared, through the same word “all food and nourishment were provided for the same, even through the Word which was conceived in the heart and brought forth by the tongue.” [66] Now that everything was ready and the earth prepared to receive its inhabitants, “things being thus ordered, a law was given to all things.” [67]

Here we must pause, for this is what the world has long been seeking in vain—the explanation of why we are here. “He who does righteously to him shall be given a life of bliss. He who does wickedness shall be given the death of the wrongdoer.” [68] Here we are given the clearest definition of what is “right” and what is “wrong” —namely, what everyone loves or what everyone hates when it is done to himself, the Golden Rule. The reward or punishment is according to one who is pleasant (ḥr ḥtp) or who spoils and cheats (ḥr ḫbn.t, who distorts everything).

All the works of men, all the doings of their arms and walking of their legs, every movement of all their members are subject to this law, which was conceived in the mind and proclaimed by the tongue of God, which shall be the measure of all things, “which has given all things their significance.” [69] “Such is the doctrine; . . . and after all things were created by his word, Ptah was pleased with it all.” [70] “So all the gods and spirits came together before the great throne where the hearts of the gods rejoiced in the temple of Ptah, the source of all life and sustenance for the Two Lands.” [71]

Next is a description of Osiris passing through the waters being rescued by Isis and Nephthys,[72] after which he “passed through the secret doors in the glory of the Lord of eternity, following in the footsteps . . . on the way to the great throne. He entered the heavenly court and mingled with the gods, with Ptah, the Ancient of Days (the Lord of Years).” [73] Finally we have the acclamation of the king as he is embraced by his father and is thereby received into the company “of the gods who were before him and after him.” [74]

The Ramesseum Drama

Usually mentioned with the Shabako stone is the Ramesseum Drama.[75] It was performed from place to place by the king in the course of his royal progress throughout the land, laying claim and giving judgment as he moved from one station to another, as kings have always done.[76] It is the clearest demonstration of the play-acting nature of the temple or creation drama. In it the actors plainly explain that they are letting one person or thing represent another: various precious stones, carnelian beads, are brought in as the Horus-eye,[77] the “blood-red-eye”; [78] faience beads are the green eye of Horus, the cheerful one.[79] The royal ship represents the Eye “because the ship opens the lake as the Eye is opened.” [80] Jugs are brought, representing Thoth, Horus, and Seth. The hieroglyphs graphically and realistically present twins fighting—the combat between Osiris and Seth.[81] The treading of the barley is the killing of Osiris, which Horus avenges by the treading of Seth and his followers.[82] Seth is chained—that is, the djed-pillar is lowered.[83] The contest for the king’s jubilee is drawn as a single combat between Horus and Seth; [84] their followers also fight, and then they are all ordered to forget the fight.[85] And so it goes, all frankly metaphorical, “minding true things by what their mockeries be.” [86]

The Antediluvian Dramatic Triad

The plays of all the Triad are centered around the Cow and the flood. The first play for each of them tells of conditions before the flood; the next describes conditions after. We begin with the antediluvian situation in all of them; men had become so bad that they must be destroyed, and the earth be returned to the watery abyss of Nun that prevailed before the creation. First comes the Egyptian version.

The Book of the Heavenly Cow

We are not picking convenient parallels at random. The drama of the Heavenly Cow is unique in its immense importance.[87] In the greatest sanctity and secrecy the text is found only on the walls of the tombs of six pharaohs, from Tutankhamun to Seti I, in the inaccessible and forbidden depths of the Valley of the Kings. It is so holy that those participating in its production must purify themselves daily for nine days preceding the presentation. After many publications, Hornung has at last given us a definitive edition with all six texts reproduced.[88]

The play was produced, according to Hornung, in “an extremely critical phase in the history of Egyptian thought,” the time of transition from the Amarna to the Ramesside periods, and is an attempt to explain the present imperfect condition of this world, “the prevailing insurrection and violence . . . along with the problem of power and its limitations,” and the disturbing reminders of the “inevitable aging of the world.” [89] These same problems are found, as Hornung observes, in the Epic of Gilgamesh and the Old Testament.[90] They are also the theme of the Greek and Chaldean dramas that follow. These three problems—the desperate state of the world, the apparent failure of God to put things right, and the cruel brevity of life—are the favorite arguments of Omar Khayyam and the atheists. “What kind of a Creator would make such a bungle of creation? What just and loving God would allow things to be as bad as they are?” they ask. But the ultimate argument is the cruelty of the flood, with its indiscriminate slaughter of animals and children.

The Book of the Cow (its modern title) begins, as all true epics begin, with civilization in collapse and the world’s population uprooted and lost in a Völkerwanderungszeit or time of world migrations.[91] A council of all the gods is called in heaven, as it was at the creation, to discuss the new world crisis under the presidency of “Re who created himself.” [92]

We soon realize that the title is another way of saying there was no beginning; he could not create himself unless he was already present to act, and his self-appointment took place only “after he had been king in a time when men and gods lived together.” [93] Moreover, if he could beget himself, why could he not perpetuate himself? The immediate reason for calling the meeting was to anticipate the results of his rapidly approaching demise. He was declining in his old age; he had begun to mineralize, his bones turning to silver, his flesh to gold, and his hair to “real lapis lazuli.” [94] So he summons “the fathers and mothers that were with him in a previous existence in the ‘Waters of Nun’ ” and orders his secretary Thoth to “summon my god Nun and have him bring his attendants with him to the council.” [95]

When all are assembled in separate groups, male and female, all bow down before Re, but the meeting must be held up pending the arrival of another ruler. Re can only present his case “in the presence of the oldest father, the One who created the human race and is the king of the people (Rekhit),” the people whose destruction Re is now considering—Nun’s feelings must certainly be consulted.[96]

It begins to appear that this is by no means the first such assembly to be held, nor is it the last—it would seem that this sort of thing has been going on indefinitely, as if we were looking at the endless reflection of one mirror in another, a corridor of mirrors. Every such meeting, like our own conferences, is not, however, a mere repetition but a reconvening. We are constantly being reminded today of the Egyptians’ dedication to the idea of “the eternal return,” the passing away of the world and everything as we know it, and its renewal in the revolutions of time at the New Year. But also the king, who brings a new age of the world with his coronation, often reports that his works are a definite improvement on all that went before; he has excelled all others in their conquests and their buildings. The kingdom does not simply repeat; it progresses. Professor Hornung tells us that the point of the whole thing is progress (Fortschritt). [97] The passing away of the old world is necessary to the emergence of a new and better one.

In the Book of the Cow we read that the assembly is opened by giving the leader Re not only a vote of confidence but permission for him to speak as each defers to the other: “Speak to us that we may hear!” says the congregation.[98] The celestial order is anything but autocratic; when Re does speak it is with deference to yet a higher authority: “O thou oldest god, from whom I am descended, and all ye assembled gods and ancestors!” [99] And then to the business at hand: “Behold mankind, established by my authority (the word used is wedjat-eye), have murmured against me and are plotting rebellion. I cannot destroy them until I have heard what you have to say.” [100] Nun, the eldest present, speaks in reply: “My son Re (can this be Re, the “self-begotten?” ), who is greater than his begetter and older than those who created him.” [101] Thus by a paradox we escape the invidious bitterness of a Christological controversy: Must the Son be placed below the Father, or the Father divide his singular glory with another? There is no point to arguing about who came first since, as the Doctrine and Covenants says, “Man was also in the beginning with God”; what is more, “Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be” (D&C 93:29).

Being thus acknowledged as presiding at the meeting, Nun invites Re to take charge: “Take over as you please!” [102] Re then states the case: hearing of his displeasure and aware of their guilt, mankind is fleeing to the mountains and the deserts. “They have foresworn themselves and become evil.” [103]

It is agreed in the meeting that the race must be punished, but men must not know of the plan to destroy them, for they must be given their agency, play out their part, and deserve their punishment.[104] Thus in Joseph Smith—Matthew in the Pearl of Great Price, the Lord deliberately “delays his coming” so that when he does come, “as a thief in the night,” he will find his rascally servants acting their true selves as they beat up on the rest of the household and so deserve condign punishment (Joseph Smith—Matthew 1:51–55).

The plan is to send down Hathor in the capacity of the Eye of Re, the one who sees, reports, and executes.[105] The reverse of one hypocephalus is dominated by a picture of a cow carrying the mummy of Re up to heaven on her back.[106] The owners of those sacred objects, all of them high religious dignitaries who drew the pictures, would know at a glance the whole message of the hypocephalus since it would clue them in to this classic tale.

Then Hathor goes down and presides at the destruction of the human race.[107] This is an unpleasant episode which the author, it would seem, prefers to pass over summarily, since with hardly a breather Hathor is back again and reporting her mission accomplished; what is more, she adds that she thoroughly enjoyed it.[108] Was this to be the end of the world? Re has other ideas; Hathor, carried away, must be stopped or we wouldn’t be here. So Re plans what Hornung calls a “gnädig-listige Rettung,” a benevolent but cunning scheme to save the world; he would convert the operation into a ritual or party.[109] He has Thoth order the maidens to make beer, seven thousand jugs of it, mix it with ochre to make it look like blood, and pour it out over the land, covering the fields to a depth of three spans.[110] When Hathor and the ladies behold the sight next morning, they drink the stuff with glee. The wine and music make them merry, and since it was spiked with a strong soporific, all go into a daze and forget all the terrible things they had just been through.[111]

New Year Celebration

Every celebration of a New Year starts the world out on a new dispensation, the “unimaginably mighty works” of the Creator, as Goethe reminds us, with everything “herrlich wie am ersten Tag” (as glorious as on the day of creation), so the angel chorus sang in the New Hymn of the Creation.[112] This calls for a new Garden of Eden. To launch the new age of the world, Hathor parties with the people in peaceful fields, “I will plant vegetation in it . . . and I will supply it with all manner of things.” [113]

But the new world is neither paradise nor that golden age when men and gods lived together. It is a dangerous place, as Hornung points out.[114] The fall is not forgotten; men must still fall if they are to rise again and progress.[115] “Every man has power to turn to God and with his help go to the other side in death and in time become a god.” [116] Re instructs Thoth to bring Geb, the earthly one, to take charge, but he warns him to beware of the snakes; even Re is afraid of them! He is instructed to set up a signboard designating the snakes’ special areas with a warning, “Do not disturb!” [117] The serpent has his legitimate place and function under the charge of Osiris, but snakes and magicians are to be avoided.[118] This recalls the snake-god Nehebkau (see pp. 306–7, fig. 34) of figure 7 of Facsimile 2, who while inspiring the utmost dread must be indulged in his important office, which is putting man to the test.

Hardly had the destruction ended and the world grown light again but men were back to their old ways: “The people came forth with their bows,” and warfare became the normal order of things. The “days of wickedness and vengeance” promptly returned. The first rebellion of men was forgiven them—after all, the sun must go down if it is to come up again, and there must be change and improvement.[119] But the new age is far from paradise. The great problem still remains—namely, Murphy’s law, the problem of Gebrechlichkeit (“defectiveness” ). That is the theme of all the plays. Why is this life such a mess? The flood didn’t solve the problem. Then why does the world have to go through one such catastrophe after another, seven great axial shifts recounted in the Pearl of Great Price, and things end up still as bad as ever—“Behold, the world at this time lieth in sin, and there is none that doeth good, no not one.” Such were the words of the Lord to Joseph Smith in the first vision.[120]

The modern editors of the plays are quite aware of this dismal failure of humanity to get off the ground. The epic of the Heavenly Cow has three main parts: (1) the story, which seems to have been a recitation, though the episode of the great council lends itself naturally to dramatic treatment; (2) instructions for painting the figures to accompany the story; and (3) ordinances to be observed by all participants. It was specified that the individual on earth who is to take part in the presentation must be washed with the water of the flood (which cleansed the earth of its filthiness, Moses 7:48), with purifying natron added (sodium carbonate and bicarbonate used by the Egyptians as soap), anointed with olive oil (bk.t), incensed with myrrh, his ears and hands being rubbed with natron and a natron pellet in his mouth, and clothed in a two-piece white linen garment.[121] Having been anointed with oil and cleansed by the waters as the earth had been in the flood (Moses 7:48), the initiate was to put on white sandals so as never to defile the earth again, or be defiled by it. He receives the maat-symbol on his tongue in white ink that he may never speak guile.[122]

Finally, by these initiatory ordinances, the eye receives the power to see, and all the members of the body are restored, “that he may walk and not faint.” Then he is greeted by his fellows who declare that “he is like Re on the day of his birth.” He has been born again like a little child and has been given a chance of eternal progression with a promise, “His possessions will never be less, nor his way ever be blocked.” [123] As a son he receives all the powers of the Father. Then Nun says, as he is embraced by the Ancient of Days: “I am the one who formed and established heaven as a place where the souls (bas) of the gods may dwell. I am with them even until the end of time, even they who were begotten by the years.” [124]

Prometheus Story: The Japhetic Play

Now we must turn to another archaic production of the same story, told with the same purpose—to explain the horrible things that go on in this world.[125] The outrageous wrong of the flood itself has ever been the strongest argument of the atheist against an all-powerful and righteous god. It was so even in ancient times.[126]

The Prometheus story is intertwined with the story of the flood. He shares his prehistoric shrine in Locris with Protogeneia, the “firstborn” daughter of Deucalion, the Greek Noah. His role as stealer of fire was commemorated at the prehistoric center of the need-fire cult of Ἰαπτιονὶς φύτλα (Iapetionis phytla), the tribe of Iapetos (Japeth), the son of Deucalion.[127] According to Pindar, Prometheus was the grandson of Deucalion and son of Iapetos.[128]

One poet said that the giant Eurymeda violated Hera and begot Prometheus, who in turn begot Deucalion, the Greek Noah, the founder of the first city, the first temple, and the first kingdom—a follow-up of the ark.[129] On the other hand, Hesiod says that Prometheus was the son of the Titan Iapetos and the Oceanid Klymenes.[130] The proprietary claims of the earliest poets show that this was a native tradition, for it was Prometheus’s son who taught Demeter the mysteries of initiation—we are never far from the temple.[131]

Our play comes from its own prehistoric sources but begins with the same council in heaven, depicted in flashbacks.[132] As in the Egyptian production, the Most High God is displeased with his human creations and is determined to destroy them. We learn that this is not the first such council and that there have been other and older gods before. There had been a war in heaven, and the former rulers, the Titans, who thought only of power, had been bested by the present ruler, Zeus. Zeus, who wanted to introduce a glimmer of intelligence into human affairs, owed his victory over the Titans largely to the wise counsel of his right-hand man Prometheus, he who foresees.[133] But Prometheus will not go along with the planned destruction of mankind; Zeus is disgusted with their hopeless stumbling mediocrity, but Prometheus, who helped create them, wants to give them a chance. He feels sorry for them; his crime, φιλανθροπἱα (philanthropia), led him to defy Zeus by secretly giving men the technological advantage they needed to survive, stealing fire from heaven and smuggling it to them in a hollow reed. This is the energy that fuels all the technical achievements of the human race, which they owe largely to Prometheus.[134]

For this defiance of his power and authority, Zeus condemns Prometheus to the greatest punishment he can devise. The power of an absolute monarch has gone to his head, and from being a benevolent father he has become a dangerous tyrant. Like the authors of the Book of the Heavenly Cow, Aeschylus is trying to explain the evil state of the world. Why does God permit it? The purpose of this play, according to Professor Walther Krauss, is to relieve the sheer exasperation (Ärgernis) of facing the “insoluble contradiction” (unüberbrückbare Gegensatz) of having the loving Creator and Father of All guilty of horrendous acts against his children.[135]

Prometheus is condemned to the ultimate punishment. As a god he cannot die, but he can become a nonperson in the most desolate spot on earth, the barren top of the Caucasus, a place where no mortal ever sets foot (there are no human characters in this play). He is to be chained to a rock, visited only by the eagle which comes to lunch on his liver every day (fig. 7).

In the opening scene we are treated to a conversation between two goons who are bringing Prometheus so that Hephaestus can rivet him to a rock in the manner of a crucifixion. Their names are significant: They are Βἱα (Bia), which means force or violence, and Κράτος (Kratos), which means strength or political power. In the Bible and the Book of Moses, we read that the flood was sent upon the earth because “the earth was corrupt before God, and it was filled with violence . . . for all flesh had corrupted its way upon the earth” (Genesis 6:11–12; Moses 8:28–29). “And God said to Noah: The end of all flesh is come before me, for the earth is filled with violence” (Genesis 6:13; Moses 8:30).

Figure 7. (A) Zeus punished Prometheus for daring to help mankind by having him bound on a mountain top, his liver eaten daily by an eagle. Laconian cup from Cervetri, ca. 560 b.c., Vatican Museum, Greg. Etr. 16592. (B) Some two centuries later another vase painting shows him crucified to an arch. Apulian vase, ca. 350 b.c. Staatliches Museum Berlin 1969.9.

We Are Visited

Soon Prometheus has visitors from above, as a spaceship appears and hovers over the desolate rock. It is a space cruiser, called not a boat but an ὄχος (ochos), a carriage with wings. It comes bearing a crew of Oceanids, the daughters of Oceanus—for everything now is building up to the flood. As the girls pass over Prometheus on his rock, their eyes overflow, shedding uncontrollable tears of pity.[136]

Figure 8. Io, as a human-headed cow, is being driven by Hera’s vigilant guard, Argos, and Hermes, identified by his caduceus, arrives with a sword to slay him and deliver Io. Lucanian vase painting, ca. 440 b.c., enhanced rollout drawing. Museum of Fine Arts, Boston.

				 Oceanus, the father of the maidens, now appears and tells Prometheus (as Christ told Paul), “It is hard to kick against the pricks.” [137] What is Oceanus doing high up in the mountains? It is because his presence presages the flood. As Prometheus continues his discussion with the Oceanids, he explains that when he first came to mankind, they “had eyes but saw to no purpose; they had ears but did not hear,” which echoes the words of Christ to Paul.[138] This is a reminder that these ancient writings, whether Egyptian, Greek, or Canaanite, contained many of the same eternal truths we find in the scriptures.

Enter the Cow

But the first step in the deliverance of the hero takes place at this point with the appearance of Io the Cow (fig. 8).[139] She is a beautiful maiden whom Zeus lusted after and his jealous wife Hera turned into a heifer.[140] Incidentally, Homer calls Hera, Zeus’s wife and queen of the gods, βοῶπις (boōpis), Cow-eyed or Cow-faced.[141] Hera then sends a gadfly to torment Io across the world.[142]

Prometheus tells Io what her fate is to be. After her wanderings, one of her male descendants will beget a son who will finally free Prometheus from his bonds [143]—it is Hercules, whose wanderings over the heat-blighted earth on his twelve philanthropic labors [144] more than suggests the ten trials of Abraham, traveling under the same conditions and doing good wherever he goes.

Books of Moses and Enoch

But what of the third angle of the triangle—the Semitic? This is why we are concerned with all this, because the third play of the Triad is supplied in full by Joseph Smith in the Pearl of Great Price. It is in the Book of Moses that we find accounts of the creation, of five of the seven dispensations, and of the great tragedy preceding the flood—that is the book of Enoch as found in Moses chapters 6 and 7. In the writings of Abraham are found Abraham’s creation drama and an account of his own life, including many details missing from the account in Genesis.[145]

If Re was aged, ailing, and disgusted with the human race and determined to destroy them, Zeus was in a like condition, having lost his former benevolence and turned into a destructive tyrant set on wiping out mankind. In Aeschylus’s play, he is foolish and self-destructive. In both instances it was a case of aporia, of God failing to help when people needed him. But what of Enoch’s God? Can he possibly be guilty of such delinquency? Enoch is dumbfounded when he finds God himself weeping, angry, and to all appearances in a state of aporia: “And Enoch said unto the Lord: How is it that thou canst weep, seeing thou art holy, and from all eternity to all eternity?” (Moses 7:29). The weeping of God was likewise a great puzzle to the rabbis, who did not deny it but had no explanation for it save the unsearchable mind of God: “If thou [Enoch] will not suffer me to weep, I God will go whither thou canst not come, and there I will lament.” [146]

God explained that he had given mankind knowledge, agency, in specific commandments to be carried out in a spirit of love (Moses 7:32–33). “But behold, they are without affection, and they hate their own blood; And the fire of mine indignation is kindled against them; and in my hot displeasure will I send in the floods upon them” (Moses 7:33–34). Even so Enoch “wept and stretched forth his arms, and his heart swelled wide as eternity, and his bowels yearned; and all eternity shook” (Moses 7:41). When he saw the God of heaven weep, he said, “How is it that the heavens weep, and shed forth their tears as rain upon the mountains?” (Moses 7:28). This is a figure of speech, so it is not improper to compare it with the weeping of the maidens in their winged chariot, when their eyes brimmed with tears as they saw the suffering of Prometheus in the Caucasus.[147] It is the same terrifying approach of the end with the appalling wail of the hero in one case and the devil’s “weeping, wailing, and gnashing of teeth” in the other, which accompanies the trembling of the earth and the confounding of the elements as everything sinks into the abyss (see Moses 1:19–22).

But even after the great destruction there must be survivors for the next dispensation. As Re stops the slaughter, and Zeus relents and turns benign, so the Lord shows Enoch a future time when all the wicked will have a chance at salvation, even the “the spirits in prison; Which sometimes were disobedient . . . in the days of Noah” (Moses 7:44–45; 1 Peter 3:19–20). What seemed ancient echoes of the gospel appear shared by that triangle of the human race.

Moses Joins the Company

Moses’ own story shows points of similarity with the Aeschylus story of the dawn of the human race. Both begin with a prologue in heaven. In the Greek it is a flashback. In the Book of Moses it begins with God appearing to Moses on a high mountain where he shows him “the world and the ends thereof, and all the children of men which are, and which were created” (Moses 1:8). After this vision, Moses collapses, lying senseless for many hours. Both men are smitten and utterly helpless, Moses barely beginning to pull himself together and Prometheus riveted with iron bonds to a stone. Satan takes advantage of Moses’ enfeebled condition and imperiously orders him to worship him. In the same way Hermes, the messenger of Zeus, arrives and commands Prometheus to worship his master. Both heroes give exactly the same response—instead of arguing they simply make fun of the tyrant’s pretenses, making both Zeus and Satan look ridiculous. This absolutely infuriates them, and they carry the attack on their victim to the absolute limit. Moses, quite overcome, goes into the depths and sees “the bitterness of hell” (Moses 1:20). And Prometheus suffers beyond all description—but neither can he be killed.

Here we find Moses, now the head of his own dispensation, founder of a new world order, appearing in the role of a full-fledged year-king! In his mandatory contest with the Destroyer he finally wins when Satan “began to tremble, and the earth shook” (Moses 1:21). Upon his victory he is hailed by God, “Blessed art thou, Moses, for I, the Almighty, have chosen thee, and thou shalt be made stronger than many waters; for they shall obey thy command as if thou wert God. And lo, I am with thee, . . . for thou shalt deliver my people from bondage, even Israel my chosen” (Moses 1:25–26). Moses was later to command the waters of the Red Sea as well as to heal the waters of Meribah. He is definitely the holder of divine kingship.

After the Flood

The flood ended when “God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged; The fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained; And the waters returned from off the earth continually” (Genesis 8:1–3). “Continually” indeed—their trouble was that both the winds and the drying up continued until the earth was sorely plagued by drought and heat. The record is full and specific [148] and clearly illustrated in the career of Abraham, always on the move, looking for grass and water, planting trees, and digging wells for those who were to follow, never able to settle down because “there was a continuation of a famine in the land” (Abraham 2:21).

As it had before, world climate changed, bringing an axial shift in history. Many have noted that along with the threat of the flood was the threat of fire.[149] It is clear in the Prometheus legends and in the prophecy of the flood: “And the fire of mine indignation is kindled against them; and in my hot displeasure will I send in the floods upon them, for my fierce anger is kindled against them” (Moses 7:34). The “hot displeasure,” “the fire of his indignation,” “the kindling wrath,” of the Lord, like the tears of rain upon the mountain, are more than metaphors. The worldwide upheaval that accompanied the flood continued as heat spells. As Eugène Lefébure pointed out, the Turin Papyrus shows the world damned between the extremes of too much water in the first instance and too much fire from the Sun’s Eye in the other.[150] Methyr, the flood-cow (see p. 324, fig. 38), returned to heaven after the flood, but the danger had not ended; her shaky legs must still be supported by gods, which, as Hornung notes, indicates the unstable condition of the elements ever since.[151]

These postdiluvian trials and tribulations equal those leading to the flood and are also described in another Egyptian epic.

The Epic of Re and the Sun’s Eye

Wilhelm Spiegelberg calls the story of the journey of Tefnut, the daughter and the Eye of Re, through the desert to Egypt a nature-myth of cosmic origin based on the winter solstice.[152] Thus she is greeted as one “like Sothis who created those who created us.” [153] “Like Nut . . . who brought him, who is Sothis, who is also the year.” [154] This is the Sirius of the mixed Egyptian-Canaanitish rites, called Shagreel.[155]

The leading character is the daughter of Re, Tefnut, in the form of her holy animal, the cat, or at times a lion.[156] She is “a form of the Sun’s Eye, which is portrayed at various times and places as a uraeus serpent on the head of the sun-god Re or his earthly representative, the Pharaoh, or as a lion and daughter of Re (Tefnut), as a Cow-goddess (Hathor) with the sun-disk between her horns, or also in the form of other female divinities.” [157] The text is richly marked with rubrics giving stage directions—for example, when the cat speaks in the story, the rubric tells the performer to imitate the voice of a cat.[158]

There are various versions of the tale, two opposing ones in particular: that which has the princess moving through the desert from eastern Nubia to the Nile—that is, the Sonnenauge (Sun’s Eye), and the other from Canaan or Asia—that is, “She who came from Afar.” [159]

There is one version in which Tefnut has left her father Re because of a disagreement and left Egypt to go to Bwgm, but now her father yearns to have her in Egypt again.[160] The other is that she had forgotten all about Egypt and was now finding it for the first time.[161] The northern school has her coming preeminently as “the Lady from Afar.” [162] Her escort is Onuris (ἰn-ḥr.t), meaning “He who brought the Lady from Afar.” [163] Even the southern route has her coming from the East.[164]

Coming from the “Land of the Gods” in the East, she “turned her back on St.t.” [165] The trouble is that St.t can mean either Nubia or Asia.[166] The women of Thebes, her special congregation, characterized Hathor, the Sun’s Eye, as Qudšu, the great hierodule of Canaan.[167] Kurt Sethe and others have her toiling in the deserts of Arabia, which was necessary to explain her entrance to the valley through the Wadi Hammamat, on which there is general agreement.[168]

There is another aspect of the story, and that is its concern with the flood. Her trip to Egypt corresponded with the final defeat of the sea, which was threatening all the lands. This is a Ugaritic drama of Baal and the sea: “To what can the allusion be,” asks Gardiner, “except to a threatened submergence of the world by the Sea?” [169] The Canaanite drama “accords perfectly” with “the general situation in the Egyptian tale,” according to Theodor H. Gaster.[170] With the arrival of the Lady in Egypt, “an end was finally put to the sea’s interminable exactions.” [171]

Plutarch tells how Isis, when her mysteries were compromised, angrily left Palestine for Egypt, taking with her the oldest son of the king, who became Maneros, the first heir to the throne of Egypt.[172] So we have the great goddess of Canaan, the sacred hierodule Qudšu—whom the women of Thebes identified with Hathor of Punt, the Sun’s Eye—all the same, remembering the possibility of Punt being in Palestine, as long argued by Cyrus Gordon. Indeed, in the Sonnenauge myth it is the North Wind that brings the fragrance of Punt to Egypt.[173]

The Queen emerged from the desert on to the valley of the Nile at the canopic side of the Delta, “at the House of the Sycamores in Memphis,” called Momemphis.[174] Momemphis was where the desert meets the delta, where stood the temple of Hathor-Sekhmet and the ancient grove after which the place was named, also called the House of the Lady of the Delightful Trees. Momemphis adjoined Gynaikopolis, the Women’s Town, near the Wadi Natrun on the canopic mouth of the Nile. The cult-place of the Holy Cows was called the House of Hathor, Queen of Malachite.[175] It was here that Hathor-Sekhmet changed for the party, along with her name, from her depressing Sekhmet-garments of the desert to the green civilized raiment of the queen, Hathor.[176] Every year the wives of the place [named Imau] would get drunk at a feast of Hathor, celebrating the end of the flood.[177]

The enlightening vignette to the 186th chapter of the Book of the Dead shows us the cow, who has left the sandy desert, graphically depicted behind her as she breaks through the dense papyrus growth of the river bank (fig. 9).[178] Her Hathor-horns, with the sun-disk between them and the menat-necklace which she wears, show her to be pregnant with the new king. Her crown is topped by two enormous maat-feathers, confirming the authenticity of everything. Immediately before her with stately dignity stands Opet, the patroness of childbirth, a combination of lion, hippopotamus, and crocodile; she rests her paw on a huge sa-symbol of security against her distended womb, along with a giant ankh-symbol. In her right hand she holds a burning torch with which she is about to apply to a lavish heap of festival foods to inaugurate the great feast. She, like the Cow, is crowned with disk and horns. To omit no good omen, the Cow’s Eye is the wḏ.t, and her son is to be the true Pharaoh. The 17th chapter of the Book of the Dead makes it clear that “Meh-Weret [the Heavenly Cow identified with Hathor] is the Wedjat-eye.” [179] The inscription included with the picture in Book of the Dead 186 leaves us in no doubt: “Hathor, Lady of the West; She of the West; Lady of the Sacred Land; Eye of Re which is on his forehead; kindly of countenance in the Bark of Millions of Years; a resting place for him who has done right within the boat of the blessed; who built the Great Bark of Osiris in order to cross the water of truth.” [180] She has come to establish a true foundation or dynasty. The boat refers to the royal progress, cruising the length of the river on a tour of inspection by the monarch upon assuming or renewing his rule over the land. The lotus bouquets in the picture welcome the illustrious arrival.

Figure 9. The customary final vignette of many copies of the Book of the Dead has the Hathor-Cow emerging from a papyrus thicket while the goddess Opet receives the offerings of Ani and his wife. On the right, their pyramid-capped funerary chapel and tomb rest at the foot of the mountain. P. Ani, BD 186, ca. 1250 b.c., BM 10470. The British Museum.

The purpose of the dramas and recitations is to bring comfort and cheer in bad times. The Book of the Sun’s Eye contains an extensive commentary on the true state of the world and what can be done about it. To cheer the Lady on her way to Egypt and beguile the long hot days on the desert, the wise ape, who is really Thoth, and the clever cat, who is the Sun’s Eye herself, a more civilized form of Sekhmet the lioness, rival each other in a continual stream of both edifying and depressing rhetorical displays on the condition of the world and mankind. The bestiaries from protohistoric Egypt and Mesopotamia to the Middle Ages supply just such commentaries on the way of the world. In this world, they agree, everything preys on everything else; power and gain are the name of the game. So as they travel, the ape says, “for hills of malachite are not like a stem of grain growing in your green field.” [181] “Come to Egypt you gazelle of the desert . . . wouldn’t you rather come to Egypt than [have] many things of green stone?” [182] The most valuable of stones is the serpent-colored turquoise, says the ape. But “a green stone amulet cannot grow in water (like the papyrus).” [183]

When she enters the land out of smoke, dust, flies, and darkness, the queen wears a necklace of turquoise and faience as well as a wreath of flowers,[184] and she puts on a crown of fresh hibite-leaves for Hathor-Mut, the Lady of Karnak, and celebrates holding palm branches.[185] She has changed from the mineral world to the vegetable world, and from a savage beast to a lovely lady.

Summary

Each of the three major cultures of the ancient Near East—the Egyptian, the Greek, and the Semitic—have epic accounts of the creation, the wickedness of men that results in a destruction of nearly all mankind, and the subsequent return of wickedness. All three accounts are extant, but the Semitic versions are found in their most complete form in the writings of Moses and Abraham in the Pearl of Great Price.
