4

Myth, Ritual, and History

			 Critics of the Book of Abraham cannot avoid an issue which has exercised scholars for more than a century—that is, how does one tell when an ancient text or other object is conveying mythology, ritual, or history? As we noted above, Théodule Devéria, the first and almost the only critic willing to offer specific objections to the facsimiles, placed in parallel columns “the interpretation of the Mormon prophet” —that is, the facsimiles in the Book of Abraham—opposed to “the interpretations of Egyptologists” of the same.[1] What the Egyptologists saw before them was Egyptian mythology; what Joseph Smith saw was real historical characters. Which was right?

Richard L. Bushman has stated the case: “The greatest error would be to mistake these narratives from ancient times as mere objects of curiosity, revealing a Mormon taste with the mysteries of antiquity. . . . What distinguished Mormonism was not so much the gospel the Mormons taught . . . but what they believed had happened; . . . the core of Mormon belief was a conviction about actual events. . . . Mormonism was history, not philosophy. . . . The strength of the church, the vigor of the Mormon missionary movement, and the staying power of the Latter-day Saints from 1830 to the present rest on belief in the reality of those events.” [2]

The Age of Myth

The study of ancient religion started with mythology enthroned as its true source. At the turn of the twentieth century, Peter L. Renouf announced that “the myths of Egypt, like those of all other nations, arose (it cannot too often be repeated) from the spontaneous and often homely utterances of men in the presence of nature.” [3] The case was strongly put by Wilhelm Spiegelberg, who explains that these Egyptian myths of the gods derived from the “unconstrained fantasy of the common people,” which the priests reworked to produce the final literary redaction.[4] The students who followed Max Müller gloried in the scientific certainty of their discipline—philology, the queen of sciences. That the “tales common to all the nations of Europe” have “a common origin between the East and the West . . . we know it to be a fact . . . from the evidence of philology, which cannot lie,” wrote one of them, and the “infallible evidence of a common origin” is proved by “the primal form of speech still in the main preserved in the Sanskrit.” [5] Adolf Erman too, from his Stube (apartment) in Berlin, assures us that Egyptian religion began with “the fantasy of simple people” as they observed the working of natural surroundings.[6] Evolution would not have it otherwise. No wonder the Book of Abraham, claiming as it does to be accurate history, has received short shrift from the profession.

Samuel A. B. Mercer, the eager critic of the Prophet Joseph Smith, tells us from his exalted scientific vantage point how “just as the imagination of children is less restrained than that of grown-ups, so the imagination of primitive men was vastly more active than is our imagination. So the men of Egypt saw heaven as an immense and friendly cow standing over them, upheld by gods, and with the morning boat of the sun sailing under her star-spangled belly” (see p. 324, fig. 38).[7] What a waste of erudition to have Professor Erik Hornung tell us that the Egyptians never for a moment thought of Hathor as a real cow and that “every such representation can only be an attempt to show something of the complex nature of divinity.” [8]

But there was always something disappointing about Egyptian mythology: “The great majority of our knowledge [of Egyptian mythology] is derived from rather vague and fragmentary allusions and implications” in texts that are “cryptic and unintelligible.” [9] “For complete accounts of the myths we must, as a rule, turn to the Greek authors; . . . for a complete account of the Osiris-myth, Plutarch is our only source.” [10] The Egyptian writers refer to the great drama of Osiris with language that is “as indefinite as possible (so unbestimmt wie möglich).” [11] Finally, “we should hesitate to think in terms of myths among the earliest Egyptians,” wrote Rudolf Anthes. “If we do so, the fact appears that Egyptian religion first of all is a religion of revelation.” [12] There was for a time consolation in the idea of the Egyptians as a people of myth-making mentality.[13] But today it has few takers.[14] Eberhard Otto sees pure mythology in the Pyramid Texts, taken over by the Coffin Texts and used as “funerary texts of a completely secondary nature.” [15] Heike Sternberg also favors mythological beginnings of things, but only in the classical adaptation.[16] Some Egyptologists started to drop the primacy of myths even before they had another theory to put in its place.[17]

The Ritual Argument

When James Frazer and the Cambridge School started pointing out the astonishing resemblances among the rites and ceremonies of primitives all over the world, confirming earlier finds of Adolf Bastian, Baron Erland Nordenskjöld, Leo Frobenius, and so forth, the more tangible and objective evidence than mere mythology was taken as proof that the rites had priority—the myths were merely invented to explain them. This was especially the case in Egypt where mythology was weak. Anthes and Siegfried Morenz agreed that “recent studies of the earliest documents of Egyptian religion have revealed that mythical ideas grew out of ceremonies; . . . the origin of the main myths can be traced back to rites centering on the idea of divine kingship as centered around 3000 b.c.” [18]

Instead of myths, many of the Pyramid Texts were, according to Joachim Spiegel and Anthes, originally the ritual texts [19] as well as hymns of the Fifth Dynasty, whereas “the Coffin Texts were reworked by people with a literary bent” so that they “were no longer based on and limited by the cult, but were freely reformed by individual fantasy.” [20] However, from the rites of Amun-Re, according to Alan H. Gardiner, “mythological traits are sternly excluded.” [21] And so we have either no mythology at all in the Pyramid Texts or nothing but mythology. Some favor “the later overlaying of rites by myths” without destroying “the unity of ritual and myth.” [22] And Elke Blumenthal, while insisting that myth came before ritual, also finds it “clear that the two originally had nothing to do with each other, while one ritual scene can have several interpretations, all valid.” [23]

If the myths came first, what is their origin? John Baines concludes that “attempts to define the date of origin of myths in Egypt have been unsuccessful.” [24] Indeed, myths are “not available for direct investigation.” [25] The myths, according to Mircea Eliade, were for archaic societies “ ‘revealed’ at the beginning of time; . . . they are regarded as having a superhuman and ‘transcendental’ origin.” [26]

And what is the origin of rituals? Scholars have been forced to turn to Carl G. Jung’s “primordial images” or “archetypes” to explain them, though Jung himself confessed that he had no idea of how the images began.[27] The diffusionists had a historical explanation for the phenomenon—namely, that the order was established at one time and place on earth and spread from there. Established by whom? From above, was the ancient reply, either by revelation, visitation, or both. The theory was unacceptable, unscientific. The spread of uniform ritual and myth must have another explanation. One explanation is that the main rites have “the aspect of a many-colored mosaic and were no doubt quite arbitrarily put together.” [28] H. J. Rose found that Greek myth and ritual do not form “parts of a single pattern; . . . the ritual comes into being for reasons often beyond our recovery, often to be guessed at by analogy with the known feelings and customs of non-European peoples.” [29] One specialist concludes: “We have not been able to adequately resolve the problem of myth and ritual; . . . we may be asking the wrong questions,”[30] as is suggested by Claude Lévi-Strauss’s observation that “mythology has no obvious practical function; . . . each myth [refers] to other myths,” and so on.[31]

And Now History

Never once in his long discourse on the psychological and philosophical foundations of Egyptian religion does Philippe Derchain ever hint that some kind of real experience might lie behind both myth and ritual, since they themselves are hopelessly elusive, while everybody’s ideas and images come from actual experience.[32] Indeed it was claimed in the beginning that all myths sprang from an imaginative observance of nature, but of real phenomena just the same. Today some Egyptologists see the point. A myth, Siegfried Schott observes, is a sort of thing that actually happens, and he recommends Kurt Sethe’s method of matching “mythical events” with corresponding “archaeological findings,” from which we can discern that “not only the form in which the myth is told, but also that which the myth tells has its origin in historical time and mirrors historical events.” It is in fact erlebte Geschichte (real historical experience), which has undergone many changes in its formation.[33] Can we discern the real historical content of a myth? What makes ritual the source of history, as Hornung notes, is that “the overall historical picture . . . is heavily determined by recurring typical events”; history goes on through the centuries describing events using “the same basic scenario with the same prescribed roles.” [34] Are not the great rites marking the birth, marriage, coronation, victories, campaigns, and burial of the king all historical realities, recalled in ritual repetition? All the pharaohs, no matter how elusive, whose names appear in Egyptian literature are to be regarded as historical personages, according to Jürgen von Beckerath, no matter how invested with legend and mystery.[35] This gives us “history as celebration,” where “history is played out in the form of a fixed ritual in accordance with the Annals.” [36]

All this is peculiarly relevant to Abraham 1 and the facsimiles of the Book of Abraham. As an autobiography, it is, of course, real history; yet we are constantly reminded that the whole story is in a ritual setting, from Abraham on the altar to Abraham on the throne. What is more, his story comes down through the centuries as folk mythology, not encouraged by the rabbis, but preserved in the Midrash. It deserves the serious study it has never received because everything that happens in his account can be carefully checked in many ancient sources.

The Great Event

So we turn first of all to the great year-rite, which was when everybody, in theory the whole human race, gathered at a particular time and place to renew the corporate and individual life of the society for a new age. The rituals performed there express “the unity and the existence of the universe.” [37] “In a universe which at any instant is on the point of dissolving, rites assure its preservation.” [38]

The great year-rite in one form or another seems to be found throughout the ancient world. What we are talking about is what the Greeks call

the panegyris, the great assembly of the entire race to participate in solemn rites essential to the continuance of its corporate and individual well-being. . . . At hundreds of holy shrines, each believed to mark the exact center of the universe and represented as the point at which the four quarters of the earth converged—“the navel of the earth”—one might have seen assembled at the New Year—the moment of creation, the beginning and ending of time—vast concourses of people, each thought to represent the entire human race in the presence of all its ancestors and gods. [39]

There have always been two standard explanations for the worldwide sameness of surprisingly elaborate and uniform year-rites. The one is Marie-Luise von Franz’s explanation, best expressed by Jung’s “archetypes,” which appear spontaneously when human beings reach a certain stage of evolution no matter where they live: “All men have common inherited patterns of emotional and mental behavior (which Jung called archetypes).” [40] Against this is the “diffusionist” theory, which argues that such a complicated and ingenious devising can only, like the alphabet, have been invented once and spread from a common center throughout the world. This had the advantage of the linguistic evidence. Philology in the nineteenth century aspired by “inquiry . . . into all the languages of one family . . . to reduce them to one common centre, from which they spread like the rays of the sun.” [41]

But both diffusionists and spontaneous generationists confess that they have not the remotest idea of how either the archetypes or the basic scenario began. “They are without known origin; . . . we do not even know how to go about investigating the problem,” writes Jung,[42] while Lord Raglan, a passionate defender of diffusionism, announces that “all the leading features of all existing religions are of immemorial antiquity.” [43]

Giorgio de Santillana finds “mythical” thinking of archaic man “worthy of the greatest modern theorists,” being both rational and cosmic.[44] “As long as a distinct set of answers was satisfactory, the distinctive aspects of a culture remained constant and offered a degree of continuity which made for stability.” [45] The charm worked whether the answers were correct or not, Jung assures us, since “man . . . never perceives anything fully or comprehends anything completely.” [46] “Why, then, should we deprive ourselves of views that would prove helpful in crises and would give meaning to our existence?” he asks. More to the point, “How do we know that such ideas are not true?” [47]

Man’s Immortality

What was the knowledge that gave those people such an advantage? It was the assurance of their own immortality. Since death cannot be denied, the only chance for a continuation of life must necessarily take place “by continuous renewal and rejuvenation,” the sun being the prime example.[48] The rites required what Jan Assmann calls “a consistent cycle of liturgical, cosmographical, and cult-theological elements,” which put man into the order of eternity, a cosmic götterweltlichen (divine) sphere.[49] And this in turn called for the participation of all living things.[50] It was based on the Re/Osiris principle—when Osiris revives, all life revives. This enlists the cooperation of man in the rituals “that express the unity of the universe.”[51]

Behind these impressive conformities, Eliade found a basic scenario which represents archaic man’s image of himself and his place in the cosmos.[52] He also has his list of the fundamental plots and props to the play: The temple is “pre-eminently the sacred place . . . a celestial prototype . . . the transformation of chaos into cosmos by the divine act of Creation”it is the scene of the sacred marriage and the ritual confrontation with evil appearing as the dragon or serpent, and ends in and the victory of the king, whose triumphant coronation inaugurates the new age of the world and the cosmos.” There is an atoning sacrifice to “restore the primal unity between god and man” and to enable the latter to regain the divine presence. The whole, according to Eliade, is suffused with “memories of Paradise,” the loss of which as a result of sin converted this world into a testing ground in which suffering also has meaning.[53] This basic scenario, first recognized in our own time, is the sum and substance of the first chapter of the Book of Abraham.

The Wholeness of Things

At the hierocentric center all things were brought together. “Reality is a function of the imitation of a celestial archetype” which is “the ‘center of the world.’ ” [54] It is the exact reflection or down-projection of what goes on in heaven; in the words of a famous Hermetic saying, “Egypt is an image of heaven; . . . our land is a temple of the whole world.” [55] Accordingly, as Wolfhart Westendorf puts it, “Ancient Egyptian cultic structures are replicas of the cosmos, underworld, supports of heaven, and heaven itself.” [56] Finally, “the bustling temple life . . . mirrors the human body in every detail.” [57]

The Egyptians did not view the various parts of the universe in isolation, but attempted to see the universe as a single whole.[58] The rituals express the unity of the universe, without which the whole structure would vanish.[59] Even the natures of individual gods “overlap and can at any time expand or differentiate, can entirely or in part fuse (verschmeltzen) with others, acquiring the character of a fluid, which is never completely isolated (abgeschlossen).” [60] We can compare this with the holistic trends of modern science in which men can declare with a straight face that “the whole [is] in every part”; [61] “an effect could have an infinite number of causes; . . . no single cause-and-effect relationship was ever really separate from the universe as a whole”;[62] “all particles are nonlocally interconnected”; [63] “the only time quanta ever manifest as particles is when we are looking at them”;[64] “everything we touch turns to matter.” [65] Bell’s theorem “tells us that there is no such thing as ‘separate parts.’ All the ‘parts’ of the universe are connected in an intimate and an immediate way previously claimed only by mystics and other scientifically objectionable people.” [66]

The Time

The timing is all-important to the great event. The occasion was, of course, the end of one age or saeculum and the beginning of what the Greeks called the ἐνιαυτός (eniautos)—“here we are again,” the ending of one cycle and the beginning of the next. The time of the meeting was never in doubt. All life depends on the sun, and the sun begins a new life every year at the winter solstice. It was the time when the sun reaches its lowest point, when heaven, earth, and the underworld come together, when “earthly existence comes into contact with the afterlife, and the sun moves from one realm to another.” [67] As the name (sol-stitium) indicates, it is that moment at which the sun must stand still. It must because at one moment it is going in one direction down as far as it can go, and at the next it is going in the opposite direction. Like the point on the rim of a speeding wheel, which must go down and then come up, it must stand dead still when it touches the earth. It is that elusive moment at which time ends and begins, that instantaneous interval called the pointmort, that magical moment when everything stops: when the leaping fish stands stock still in the air, when the arrow arrests its flight and the flame is frozen in place—all for so short a time that no one notices until it has passed. The whole universe and all that is in it must be “jump-started” for a new round of existence.

Figure 10. Under the pt-sign of heaven , the sun between the two mountains of the horizon rests on the backs of the two lions of yesterday and tomorrow. P. Ani, BD 17, ca. 1250 b.c., BM 10470. The British Museum.

Why must there be a dead stop if the wheels still keep moving? Any point on the rim of a speeding wheel is going either up or down, and no matter how fast the vehicle is moving, that point must stand dead still when it touches the earth—or else the wheel would be skidding. One might call it a singularity, moving up and down at the same time. Yet it is clearly displayed in the important seventeenth chapter of the Book of the Dead (fig. 10). There we see the sun on the horizon, but it is neither yesterday nor tomorrow; Re-Harakhty is exactly at the point of leaving the underworld and moving to heaven. The same idea is expressed in Facsimile 2, figure 2, the two-faced god that represents the sun at the zenith and symbolizes the joining together of Re and Osiris: “As for yesterday, that is Osiris. As for tomorrow, that is Re.” [68] The two come together at, and only at, the indefinable instant of time when the sun reverses its course from a southerly to northerly direction. The Egyptian text explains that this is indeed the combined oneness of Re (who looks forward to the day) with Osiris (who is looking back on it).[69] At that moment his two faces are in both worlds at once, and on some hypocephali the figure is drawn with a double body as well (see appendixes 4–5). But it is only for an unthinkable instant, the passing of time from past to future, the fatal paradox or the moment in which we are all living.

Culmen Omnium (Summit of All Things)

With all eyes fixed on the sun (or the shadow of the obelisk that marked its progress), we have a singleness of heart and mind which was all to the king’s advantage, inevitably identified with the sun. He is “rising on his throne according to the desire of his heart. He gained dominion over all that existed by his [power?]. He assumed the kingship of eternity . . . established as Sole Lord. His forms shone at the First Occasion. All beings were struck dumb at his glory. . . . He opened all eyes and caused them to behold. . . . He began to cry aloud. . . . He gave birth to all that exists; he caused them to live. He made all men to know the path to walk in.” [70]

This reads almost like a paraphrase of chapter 140 of the Theban Recension of the Book of the Dead: “The Mighty One appears, the horizon shines, Atum appears on the smell of his censing, the Sunshine-god has risen in the sky, the Mansion of the Pyramidion is in joy and all its inmates are assembled, a voice calls out within the shrine, shouting reverberates around the Netherworld, obeisance is done at the utterance of Atum-Harakhty. His Majesty gives a command to the Ennead. . . . His Majesty’s utterance goes forth, . . . and the land is happy in the second month of winter, last day. . . . His Majesty shines as on the First Occasion.” The roll call of all the principal gods and goddesses shows all present and accounted for. “These gods are joyful on this day.” The mighty acclamation of the king follows.[71] “After his rebirth, the sun-god together with the bas of those who have been glorified make their ascension to heaven.” [72] It is the universal natalia or birthday. The neighbors of the Egyptians were all impressed by the extreme care paid to the calendar by the priests who, according to Diodorus, gave more attention to astronomy than any other people.[73] The Egyptians, like the Babylonians, saw in the stars before all else the timing of events. It was the great complex of the year-rite which put man into the cosmic götterweltlichen existence in which there is literally only one conceivable theater of being—and it embraces everything.[74]

The Action

The activity follows what Hornung calls the “basic scenario”: The great council of the gods has assembled in the presence of the Most High God to discuss the creation of the earth; of course, the subject of kingship—who is to be the Father’s heir and representative—comes up. There is a dispute between rival claimants. The rightful heir to the throne is overcome and is at the point of extinction before he prevails over the power of death. He is then acclaimed victor and mounts the throne as king of the new age. In other words, we have a full-fledged drama, as Eliade notes: “The ‘origin’ of the drama (both of Greek tragedy and of the dramatic spectacles of the ancient East and of Europe) has been traced back to certain seasonal rituals which, broadly speaking, presented the following sequence: conflict between two antagonistic principles (Life and Death, God and the Dragon, etc.), the tragic suffering of the God, lamentation at his death and jubilation to greet his ‘resurrection.’ ” [75] It is these elements, which Marija Gimbutas finds so abundantly strewn among the remnants of the Old European civilization (6500–1500 b.c.), which forcibly suggest the same scenario.[76]

Creation goes on and on in a living civilization: “History for the Egyptian is the festive . . . periodic renewal of what happened in the beginning, . . . celebrating ritual based on what is necessary and prescribed, . . . a constant ‘renewing’ of the constructions and campaigns of the Pharaoh.” [77] Elements of the creation drama turn up in every temple, tomb, or Coffin Text.[78] Spiegel notes that the Pyramid Texts prescribe the same rites as those of the temples.[79] The founding ceremonies of the temple rehearsed the creation of the world, beginning with the prologue in heaven, the deliberations of the great council of the gods, the dispute between the rival sons concerning the inheritance, the casting out of Seth, etc. All of this the present writer has treated at length elsewhere.[80] What is most noteworthy for us is that this peculiar institution, received with skepticism in the early part of the century as the “patternism” of the Cambridge School, is now taken for granted. Yet it receives full treatment in the Book of Abraham and the Book of Moses and is described in detail in the Book of Mormon as the coronation of King Mosiah.[81] It leads us straight to the hypocephalus.

By far the best view of the big picture can be had from Egypt and Mesopotamia, where the greatest number of records and archaeological remains have survived and been studied.[82] The purpose of the year-rite was to bring all things together in one clear revelation setting forth man’s condition. The Egyptian must view the universe as a single whole, one elementary structure.[83] He has a passion for bringing things together, beginning with the close-knit family which meets us all throughout the literature. On a larger scale, things are held together by festivals that keep life from getting dull or meaningless and guide us through life.

Throughout his life the Egyptian is both a spectator and a participant in everything that is going on. He doesn’t want to miss a thing. He journeys to the holy places where the coming together of absolutely everything—men and gods, heaven and earth, the past, present, and future ages—is the order of the day. Hundreds of such pilgrimages are listed in Papyrus Leiden T 32.[84] Wherever we read, there is a constant joy of getting together. Professor Hornung has shown how this passion dissolves boundaries. All twelve scenes in the Amduat are really one scene; [85] the One God is any God you please; [86] every akh is a god; [87] the all-powerful Re, in order to accomplish his work, requires the aid of all living things to help him; [88] the Ennead is not just nine gods—they are innumerable; [89] and in the beyond there is no distinction between gods and men.[90] But be of good cheer, for when Osiris revives, all those who have died revive. [91] It is a return to the golden age of the Saturnalia—libertas reverentiam remisit (“license waves the rules” ). [92]

Eliade sums up the situation by noting that “for archaic man, reality is a function of the imitation of a celestial archetype.” [93] What happens here is what happens above. In this, “reality is conferred through participation in the ‘symbolism of the Center’: cities, temples, houses become real by the fact of being assimilated to the ‘center of the world’ ”; [94] “the Temple in particular—preeminently the sacred place—had a celestial prototype,” namely the holy mountain. [95] It is this assimilation that saves the day for earthlings: If A survives, B survives, etc.

Professor Derchain has presented a line of reasoning for the Egyptians which explains their religious words and gestures. It begins with the universe in constant danger of dissolution. [96] The setbacks and reverses of life are enough to give anyone that idea—evil is present always and everywhere. [97] This condition inspires a preoccupation with “infinite and eternal stability,” which Derchain considers the very essence of Egyptian religion. [98] Man’s parlous state inspires “a sense of responsibility vis à vis nature” in perceptive souls[99]—can’t something be done about it? Egyptian religion is “a psychological and philosophical system” to meet this need.[100] It calls for a coming together of all the people, a natural occurrence when societies feel threatened, both for mutual reassurance and strength of numbers.

The Meeting Place

The great ceremonial complexes were considered to be the center of the world, the omphalos or navel of the earth, the primal mount, and at the same time the entrance to the underworld (orcus mundi).[101] It was marked by standing stones (ἰwn.w, the sunstones of Heliopolis), which suggest continuity with the ten-thousand-odd megalithic remains marking prehistoric places of assembly all over Europe and the East.[102]

How can every consecrated space coincide with the center of the world, just as any rite coincides with its beginning? The great high priest Petosiris declared that in the garden of his shrine was the very “place where Re was born in the beginning of the world, when the earth was still surrounded by Nun (chaos). It is the house of the cradle of all the gods. . . . It is the place where every creature was born.” [103] He is proud of having renovated the Holy Place to prevent its being trodden under the feet of the populace: “for vulgar people were walking about it, eating the fruit of the trees and carrying off the rushes, . . . thus bringing affliction upon the land of Egypt.” [104]

The center of the universe in his backyard? In some mystical or symbolic sense perhaps? Not at all. This writer loves nothing more than his visits to Hotevilla, a small Hopi village which is believed literally by its elders, and even some others, to be the center of the world and the last place on earth where the complete cycle of the year must be celebrated to keep the human race in contact with heaven. Even though it may go overboard, the title of a recent large and ambitious study Hotevilla: Hopi Shrine of the Covenant, Microcosm of the World gives the idea.[105] It has often been noted that the temple is a scale model of the universe.[106]

The Holy Mountain

The place of the meeting was made absolutely sure by a bold and immovable marker, a symbol of the domain of the tribal god, visible from afar to announce his power and direct the pilgrim to the Holy Place—it was the sacred mountain, “the mountain of the Lord’s House,” the territorial marker—such places as Mount Sinai and the Dome of the Rock. In flat country it was necessary to raise an artificial mound like the ziggurats of Mesopotamia or the great mound in England called Silbury Hill—130 feet high and built up of 8.75 million cubic feet of the local soil. Further to the west was the þing-hill, the Icelandic assembly hill, where the goði (priest/chief) judged law cases for the people who gathered during Althing festivals.[107] The prize, however, goes to the massive pyramids of Egypt, celebrated at their dedication as being built to last forever.[108]

Further insight into how ancient these assembly places are comes from the recent work of a German archaeologist, Klaus Schmidt, who discovered a huge megalithic complex at Göbekli Tepe, Turkey, which dates from about eleven thousand years ago (fig. 11; see map on p. 542, fig. 66).[109] There are several rings of megalithic stones as tall as sixteen feet, weighing between seven and ten tons, some of which are “elaborately carved: foxes, lions, scorpions and vultures abound, twisting and crawling on the pillars’ broad sides.” [110] Interestingly the excavators have found no evidence that people permanently resided on the summit of the hill itself; Schmidt “believes this was a place of worship on an unprecedented scale—humanity’s first ‘cathedral on a hill.’ ” [111] A huge number of animal bones—gazelle, boar, sheep, red deer, vultures, cranes, ducks, and geese, many of which have cut marks and splintered edges, indicating that they were butchered and cooked there—suggests a gathering together and feasting at the ceremonial complex.[112]

Figure 11. Six thousand years before Stonehenge, Neolithic hunter-gatherers in modern-day southern Turkey quarried and erected circles of standing stones believed to have supported a wooden roof. After a period of use, they were carefully buried and new rings constructed above them. The excavator believes it to be the world’s oldest temple and the source of civilization. Tenth millennium b.c. Adapted from a computer-generated model by Klaus Schmidt.

It is often noted that “ancient Egyptian cultic structures are replicas of the cosmos, underworld, supports of heaven, and heaven itself.” [113] Best known was the Urhügel (mound of creation) of Heliopolis: “Hail Atum! Hail Khepre! you have arisen as the hill (or on the hill), you have arisen as the benben-stone (or on the benben-stone), in the House of the Benben in Heliopolis” (see pp. 342–43, fig. 41).[114] That shrine is depicted at least twice in our Facsimile 2, figure 18. A greatly reduced form of the year-rite was in fact recited by a priest in every Egyptian temple every morning.[115]

Thinking Big

It would seem that none of the great centers existed in splendid isolation but that each was part of a larger grid or system: “Grant that I may become ancient in the Duat, like Osiris in Thebes. My throne is Heliopolis, my seat is the White Wall, my house is Hermopolis.” [116] The White Wall of the Memphis complex was duplicated with the Djoser complex at Saqqara, transferred from temple to funeral service.[117] Revelations from heaven were received in the temple of Heliopolis, repeated in Memphis (to Ptah) and written down by Thoth, and then sent to Thebes to be deposited; from there it was sent forth as the law by which all must live.[118] At the victories of Horus over Seth, “Heliopolis rejoices, Thebes is in jubilation, Memphis is glad, Busiris and Abydos are renewed. . . . Siut is joyful, its shrine is renewed. . . . Horus and Isis are in the places of their justification.” [119] One joins with one’s family, friends, and associates assembled together from wherever they are “in heaven, earth, in the necropolis and the underworld, in the place of mourning, in the Nile and the flood, in the Mansion of the Great Bulls, in Busiris, in Mendes, in Heliopolis, in Letopolis, in Pe of the Great One, in Kher’aha, and in Abydos.” [120]

One of the greatest and oldest of all these cult centers was Heliopolis, “City of the Sun.” [121] Ernesto Schiaparelli found that Djoser had built at Heliopolis “a gigantic circular structure (diameter 600 meters, 40 meters thick) of unknown nature.” [122] The writer thinks of the months he spent near Avebury, especially considering that the ideogram for Heliopolis was a standing stone , which gives it its Hebrew name, On (ʾIwnw). Other such ceremonial centers were ʾIwnw šmʿw, Thebes; ʾIwnἰ, Hermonthis; ʾIwn.t, Denderah; ʾIwn.yt, Letopolis. Each ἰ.t (mound) is represented by the ideogram of a grave mound surrounded by standing stones. The dual ἰwn.wy signifies a double line of columns, a hall of columns, lines of standing stones.[123] It is notable that the temple of Apollo at Corinth, built about 540 b.c., is made of forty-two columns, each a single monolith.[124] An archaic sacred place is described in Papyrus Salt 825: “Geb is its floor, Nut is its ceiling. It is the Great God who sits hidden in the interior of it. The four surrounding structures on the outside are of stone on all sides. The groundwork is of sand. Externally on every side are two doors in each of the four directions.” [125]

The scale of the arrangement was sometimes colossal, pyramids and obelisks tall enough to catch the first rays of the sun while the world was in shadow; pits of appalling depth draw the mind into the underworld. Finally, the temple “mirrors the human body in every detail.” [126] The point was to make everything as real as possible, or as the Book of Abraham says, “seeking earnestly to imitate” the real thing (Abraham 1:26).

The most impressive of progresses is certainly that in the Papyrus Leiden T 32, which takes us to almost seven hundred different shrines and festivals, celebrating a set event at a set time at each particular place. This seems to mark out the parameters of a “cone of time,” for each crucial event, in order to be effective, must occur at a specific time and place and in a fixed order of progression.[127] The royal progress is found all over the “archaic” world.[128] It can be the king circumambulating the walls or “beating the bounds” of his capital or kingdom, as depicted on archaic Egyptian seals and mace heads.[129]

Sky Terminals

The neglect of serious study of the cosmic significance of the great ceremonial centers of Egypt has been receiving belated attention.[130] This indifference has kept us from realizing, as François Daumas does, that “there was between the prescriptions of the cult and the rhythm of the universe a profound and essential relationship, which the texts and pictures have, for once, generously preserved, so that we do not have to resort to dangerous personal fantasy to interpret them.” [131] In the temple complexes there are numerous gates, doors, processional ways, passages, and tunnels; all bring the worlds into contact with each other.[132] They were both message centers and observatories. May we not think of the mounds (ἰ.wt) as terminals or bases, space ports or loading zones? They were designed as such. The real ships, wharves, docks, outlets to the Nile, ramps,[133] landing platforms, inclined ways, etc., are to be taken seriously.

It all sounds very fanciful, but were the great ships found buried around the Great Pyramid at Giza purely symbolic? The Beautiful Festival of the Valley had gods and men cruising in real boats, like heavenly visitors, from the temple on one side of the river to the temple on the other, landing like the heavenly visitors to the ziggurats of Babylon, with glad acclamations after their long voyage.

Evidence?

That this fabulous extravaganza could be played out all over the world for thousands of years strains our credulity. What is the evidence for it? The picture of the great annual activity did not emerge until the late nineteenth century with the philological studies that started when the founding of the Bengal Asiatic Society in 1873 set out to make the world aware of Sanskrit as “the Mother of all languages,” and philology as the “Queen of Sciences.” This led to “inquiry . . . into all the languages . . . to reduce them to one common center, from which they spread like the rays of the sun.” [134] Jacob Grimm went so far as to declare that “amongst all languages on earth points of contact are to be found.” [135] The progress of the science is marked by the writings of the great Max Müller, who moved from philology in his monumental edition of The Hymns of the Rigveda, with Sayana’s Commentary (1849–75), to the broader Lectures on the Origin and Growth of Religion (1879), and finally to his wide-ranging studies on Contributions to the Science of Mythology (1897). At the same time Jacob Grimm, recognizing that if valuable linguistic information was to be found, for example, among the peasants of Germany, “a like proceeding must be justifiable in mythology too.” [136] And as the next step, “if the heathens already possessed a finely articulated language, and if we concede to them an abundant stock of religious myths, then the song and story could not fail to . . . interweave themselves with rites and customs.” [137] Hence, “in addition to the fairy-tale and folk-tale, which to this day . . . the common people . . . will not give up . . . we must take account of Rites and Customs.” [138]

The Lists Testify

Beginning earlier, world traveler and major ethnologist of the nineteenth century, Adolf Bastian, “recognized in the myths and ceremonial customs of mankind a significant number of essential themes and motifs that were apparently universal.” [139] Grimm’s Deutsche Mythologie in the 1840s had supplied a list of five features found worldwide in the great rites which closely match Samuel H. Hooke’s list of a hundred years later. The main elements “constitute the underlying skeleton . . . not only of the great New Year Festivals, but also of coronation rituals, and initiation ceremonies.” [140] These five elements are (1) a dramatic representation of the death and resurrection of the god, (2) a recitation or symbolic representation of the myth of creation, (3) a ritual combat, in which the triumph of the god over his enemies was depicted, (4) the sacred marriage, and (5) the triumphal procession, in which the king played the part of the god followed by a train of lesser gods or visiting deities.[141] This includes eight affinities among the people of the North, namely (1) speech, (2) religious worship, (3) mythic notions and nomenclature, (4) heroic legends, (5) names of plants and constellations, (6) gods, demons, wise women and witches, (7) folktales, games, saws, songs, and curses, and (8) very similar systems of law.[142] Also, he notes the same common elements in the Northern cultural triads—that is, Gothic, Frankish, and Norse, or Celtic, Slavic, and Finnish.[143]

Eliade strings together the sacred place, the celestial prototype, the act of creation, the sacred marriage, the confrontation with evil, the victory of the king, and the coronation. To these he adds the atoning sacrifice (cleansing the people of their sins) and memories of paradise, festivities wistfully but happily recalling the golden age.[144]

It became a game for some. A. M. Hocart listed 26 features which characterized the coronation ceremonies of kings in all parts of the world.[145] The present author, many years ago, in a doctoral dissertation, compared a score of well-documented descriptions of ancient year-rites from all over the world.[146]

The Spinoffs

The reality, as well as uniformity, of these rites may be confirmed by the institutions to which they gave birth. It is a long list and impressively consistent wherever we look. In fact, the “spinoffs” which the year-rite generated in various parts of the world would seem to be well nigh inevitable. In the first place, bringing together large numbers of people from widely scattered areas would inevitably produce a trade center for exchange of their various local products, as well as a bank to make such exchange possible and especially further the economy of the temple. These are the cornerstones of civilization throughout the world. For example, the shrine on the Capitol Mons at Rome, to which all the world repaired, belonged to Juno Moneta,[147] the guardian (with her geese), from which we have our sacred word money. There one traded the animals driven from one’s home for tokens acceptable as offerings in the temple—hence the “banks” or benches of the money changers in the outer court of the temple. There must be hostels for the pilgrim, hospitals for the ailing, markets and fairs, athletic contests demonstrating local pride and prowess, songs, dances, dramatic recitations and plays by traveling troupes, bards, and mountebanks. The practices of religion required a huge personnel and included sacrifice, processions, preaching, purification, charitable works, chants and hymns, and so forth.

Most of all, the temple was the home of the arts and sciences. Vividly lifelike portraits of ancestors required real artistry, [148] as did the making of images and idols. The temple itself was a sacred edifice built according to strict rules and sacred dimensions of architecture and art as well as mathematics.[149] The all-important timing of rites and festivals required close observation of the heavens. The study of animals for sacrifice entailed biology and the heavenly garden; the Eden that surrounded the temple was a botanical paradise. The dividing of fields required geometry. The bringing together of people resulted in contracts that were made at the fair and concluded a year later. Law courts as well as banks are still designed to look like temples. The earliest New Year’s celebration was the cattle count, which, like the other business deals, required bookkeeping of which numerous documents still remain. Only at the New Year’s celebration could servants be hired or properties rented, and when their “year and a day” had passed, the contract expired and had to be renewed for the next year.[150] All who attended must be registered in “The Book of Life Opened at the Beginning of the World.” [151] The Book of Life contained the names of all those who were to live through the coming year—that is, as citizens.

In short, one may well ask what civilized institution did not originate with the year-rite.[152] “Do you not know, Asclepius, that Egypt is an image of heaven (imago caeli), or to be more precise: . . . If the truth were told, our land is the temple of the whole world.” [153] At least a hundred great national ceremonial centers could put forth the same claims.

The Ruins

Such an activity must have left its mark in extensive physical remains. It was in fact the awesome size and extent of the ceremonial centers now designated as megalithic that suggested the fantastic economy of the worldwide assemblies.

After the appearance in 1872 of James Ferguson’s Rude Stone Monuments, others began reporting great stone monuments all over the world, in Algeria, Palestine, Ethiopia, the Sudan, the Caucasus, Persia, Baluchistan, Kashmir, central and southern India, Assam, Sumatra, some Pacific islands, Japan, and pre-Columbian America.[154] They may have been used as temples, and “in Spain and Brittany a few megalithic monuments have been incorporated into functioning modern Christian churches.” [155] The vast extent of time and space covered by these establishments is staggering. The megaliths were spread over the northern European plain following the “loess corridor” from the east in the mid-sixth millennium b.c. They were more than tombs or habitations; they were the sites of “the major ceremonies connected with the remembrance of ancestors.” [156] They provide “an appropriate cultural and social framework for a farming existence,” which has left some ten thousand megaliths in Europe.[157]

The grandest and one of the oldest of these centers is Avebury in Wiltshire, southern England. Though Aubrey Burl in his standard work on Avebury denies any possible connection between Avebury and the New World, three thousand years and four thousand miles apart, he cannot resist pointing out a parallel between Avebury and the Mayan ceremonial centers of Central America.[158] Surprisingly, the most impressive resemblance between artifacts of the two worlds is presented by the famous Aztec calendar stone (see p. 198, fig. 18) with Facsimile 2.[159]

The author was stationed near Avebury in 1945; the place was deserted then, and one had ample time to inspect it closely both from the ground and from the air. On leaving the military, your informant went directly to Utah where, at the urging of Brother Virgil Bushman (who after a life spent in Tusayan, Arizona, was the most effective missionary to the Hopi people),[160] he began frequent visits to the Hopi. Here we find the hierocentric state functioning on exactly the ancient pattern.[161]

The presence in northwest Arizona for at least the last thousand years of exactly the same seasonal rites as those we have been describing in the Old World fairly affirms the astonishing extent of the institution, but the question of time still seems baffling. How is it possible that they could have kept it up so long without any major alterations? In our bored and overstimulated society, we cannot imagine how those people could go on with the same routines for millennia without ever being fed up. Archaeologists tell us that the life of Paleolithic and Neolithic must have been “unimaginably” dull, an ageless rut, scandalously unprogressive, empty. They are speaking for their times. To the visitor to a Hopi mesa, life in a crumbling village on a table rock about three city blocks long and one wide, with nothing in sight in all directions but empty brown sand and a few bumps on the horizon, must be worse than Alcatraz, a banishment to oblivion.

Yet these people to this day reject all the conveniences of modern civilization and have suffered severe persecution rather than give up their primitive homes and customs. Why is it always fun to visit them? Because they are having the time of their lives! The cycle of festivals, obligatory and delightful, brings them together through the year. Exciting, colorful, demanding, absorbing, sacred, and regular as clockwork, the weekly rites, secret and public, are sponsored by each of the twelve villages in turn hosting all the others. It should keep them busy and happy all the time, and it does. The Australian Aborigines, who are believed to have inhabited Australia for forty thousand years, have in their corroborees something like it, illuminated by rock paintings displaying the same cosmic, supernatural, and festive objects[162]—forty thousand years and still not bored?[163] The psychologist Jung declares that “most rituals are so old that no one can trace their origin,” and as an example, in the American Indian “we see how a ritual or religious custom can spring directly from an unconscious revelation experienced by a single individual.” [164] For example, “When and where such a motif [as the hero myth] originated nobody knows. We do not even know how to go about investigating the problem. The one apparent certainty is that every generation seems to have known it as a tradition handed down from some preceding time. . . . The hero figure is an archetype, which has existed since time immemorial.” [165]

Lord Raglan, the doughty defender of diffusion, declares that “all the leading features of all existing religions are of immemorial antiquity” and goes so far as to suggest that the original rites, like the original language, must have been “in some centre of civilization . . . [where] there arose a class of people who believed that virtue lay in the elaboration of forms, both verbal and ceremonial, and who devised these complex grammatical forms for liturgical purposes.” [166] Eliade can only conclude that the pattern and ceremony were “ ‘revealed’ at the beginning of time” and are “of superhuman and ‘transcendental’ origin.” [167] It was the timeless Dreamtime of the Aborigines.[168] The trouble is that the concept is asking too much. The whole world? The whole of humanity? Since long before memory? Not until today have the worldwide scope and vast antiquity become believable as platoons of scholars everywhere look for bones and imprints and discover the same hominids and industries at home from South Africa to the Far East.[169]

A Note on Sources

As we have seen, the impressive resemblances between large and elaborate New Year celebrations throughout the ancient world are too conspicuous to be denied, and scholars are divided in their explanation of why this is so—either the “primal images” of Jung or the diffusionists’ explanation that the order was established at one time and place on earth and spread from there.

Nothing raises the question more emphatically than the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. We have seen how it touches on the New Year’s rites at many points; it is the final epitome of the long tradition of funerary literature, containing the whole plan in itself. When first discovered, it excited great interest as scholars felt instinctively that there must be something big behind it, but the idea that it might suggest some sort of higher knowledge was soon dropped, and today it is regarded as one of the innumerable superstitious talismans spawned by the decadent religion of the Roman period in Egypt. It fittingly adorns the Pearl of Great Price, a scripture which strives to bring all the dispensations together following a single pattern. As noted, the Books of Adam, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Christ, and Joseph Smith are all quoted to testify to those repeated cycles of human righteousness and folly known as dispensations of the gospel. Each moves through a cycle from revelation to annihilation.

This strangely arrested progress which questioned the basic accepted view of continual improvement through evolution has since the 1960s been introduced into geology as neocatastrophism[170] to explain sudden and massive extinctions of life forms on the earth, to be succeeded by a huge catalogue of new species: “And as one earth shall pass away, and the heavens thereof even so shall another come; and there is no end to my works, neither to my words” (Moses 1:38). As if not to be outdone, during the same brief period astronomy has introduced the same cycles of destruction and renewal as stars explode to provide the minerals necessary for the formation of other stars. We are likewise back to the rule of dispensations for the human race, which was taught in the Jewish apocrypha as the alternate periods of “black water and white water,” [171] and the “Summertime of the Just and Wintertime of the Just.” [172] Thanks to the “rediscovery of apocalyptic” in the 1960s, we know now how basic was the doctrine of dispensations to the early Jews and Christians.[173]

In the scholarly literature the Book of Mormon is all that is missing; the New World must be brought into the picture. It begins with the rich cultural mix of Palestine in the Twenty-sixth Dynasty, timed to match Karl Jaspers’s “axial century” in history. The coronation of Mosiah 475 years later shows the vitality of the tradition, having not only all the elements of the coronation of the Prince of the Captivity in Babylonia at the same time but resembling in detail rites all over the ancient world.[174s] This suggests a rich infusion into the New World of Jaredite culture, and this in turn harks back to another migration, moving at the same time in the opposite direction from the same place in the region of “the tower” to find another promised land—Egypt. This migration is described, of all places, in the Book of Abraham.

It is admittedly a great leap from the Old World to the New. But still nobody denies that such a leap was made; only the how, when, and where are still disputed. But whatever the connection, the most impressive relic ever found in the New World has a most striking family resemblance to the hypocephalus. We are referring to the great calendar stone of Mexico.[175]

Summary

Whatever the connection between these many reports, the point we wish to make is that the author of the Latter-day Saint scriptures seems to know very well what is going on. In the next chapter we will examine the Book of Abraham description of these rites and compare it with two detailed Egyptian texts dealing with this subject.
