5

Abraham and the Great Year-Rite

Fun at Court: A Domesticated Year-Rite

The Re/Osiris situation has been fully dramatized in some very early texts. For the grand full-scale production of the show, we are fortunate in possessing two documents, Coffin Text 312 and Book of the Dead chapter 78, which have been the study of half a dozen of the most eminent scholars.[1] Most significant is that this noble drama is a rowdy comedy. The sanctity of the subject need not turn the Egyptian off—after all, Aristophanes spoofs the Eleusinian mysteries.[2]

Acting It Out

It was Adriaan de Buck who, while editing the Coffin Texts in 1949, noticed that Coffin Text 312 turns up almost word for word in the 78th chapter of the Book of the Dead. Since Coffin Text 312 goes back to the Twelfth and possibly the Eleventh Dynasty,[3] it was assumed that Book of the Dead 78 is possibly the oldest text in all the Book of the Dead, indeed that “the contents of the spell were already enigmatic and obscure to writers and readers of the Book of the Dead.” [4] What is more, according to the same authority, the Coffin Texts themselves had already wandered far from the ancient purity of the original.[5] But if we place the almost identical Book of the Dead 78 and Coffin Text 312 side by side in parallel columns with Book of the Dead 162 and Berlin Papyrus 3031, which is seven hundred years older and provides us with full instructions on how to make a hypocephalus, the essential identity of all becomes at once apparent: the desperate plea de profundis,[6] the exchange of the miseries of earth for the glories of heaven, the secret names by which the initiate and the god establish their relationship—all refer to the same rites, only in this case the event is presented as a comedy! The 162nd chapter was thrown out by the experts as being too late and alien for consideration. Both the earliest and latest editions of the Book of the Dead, those of Edouard Naville and Erik Hornung, dispense with it. That the funeral rites should take dramatic form in the earliest time is not surprising; Siegfried Schott has traced the whole literature back to a prehistoric Festspiel of processions and games devoted especially to the king’s jubilee, seasonal or year-rites in which all the actors were gods and from which Schott believes the mythology was later derived.[7] The king, of course, is Osiris.[8]

Five years after de Buck’s study, Professor Étienne Drioton noticed that while Coffin Text 312 seemed to be a text that had survived a slapdash and uncritical selection made merely to fill a customer’s demand for a funeral text, the scribe had “by inadvertence” included in the papyrus “a few designations of persons and specific indications” which were enough to show that the piece was originally both a dramatic composition and a script for a temple.[9] Professor Helmut Brunner then found that “the literary character of the text has suffered frightfully in being taken over into the corpus of funerary literature,” which is to say that “our Coffin Text was originally no funerary text at all,” but only “clumsily adapted as such.” [10]

But funeral text or not, it was certainly a drama and even a lively one. As Professor Drioton describes it, the play opens with “Osiris lying helpless in the darkness, mortally smitten by Seth, hiding out in Busiris.” [11] He has been defeated in their ritual combat. “O Horus,” he cries, “come to Busiris (Mendes, where Osiris fuses with Re) to open the ways for me and make the rounds of my places (shrines)! Come that you may look upon my condition and raise me up!” [12]

This is the situation in the hypocephali as well. In response to the prayer comes the akhu, as on the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. But here it is not Re but Horus, the faithful go-between, performing the office of Re for his father Osiris. He appears as a radiant bird of light; de Buck characterizes the akhu as “the one in the radiance,” Brunner called it a Lichtgeist, Hornung a Lichtfunke, others “an angel of light, a Lichtengel.” In the play this radiant, dazzling bird wears a hawk’s mask, for he is Horus come to deliver his father. He appeals to the name of Maat (justice), and promises that the sufferer shall go to join Isis for healing and rejuvenation.[13] In this capacity Isis is the Hathor-Cow, the very Ahat with which the suppliant identifies himself and who is to rescue him in Book of the Dead 162.[14] He urges the sufferer to make an effort to bestir himself (as the lady Isis does in so many funeral texts), but declares that he must go to heaven to get authorization to raise the dead from the Most High God.[15] The afflicted one is further assured that the gods of the gates of the Duat will give way to him in all of his divinity.[16] Compare this with Book of the Dead 162, col. 10: “He shall be divine in ẖr.t-nṯr, he shall not be turned back at any gate.” [17]

Here we have our basic plot set forth in the Pyramid Texts, the Coffin Texts, the Book of the Dead, the Book of Breathings, and finally on the disk of the hypocephalus. The situation is familiar from most of the books of the netherworld or the beyond, but this version goes on with the story and takes the direction which puts it in the category reserved only for hypocephalus heroes. What are we referring to? Well, instead of raising his father on the spot, the Messenger of Light (who to achieve his purpose must be both Horus and Re) departs from the scene—he must go back to heaven and get authorization from the courts on high.

After he has gone off to get clearance another messenger appears and declares himself ready to take over as Horus, claiming that Horus has endowed him with his own image and ba, to act as mediator between Re and Osiris.[18] Some scholars (Brunner and Drioton) consider this intruder a comedian, yet he is the most conspicuous character in the play and is given the most lines. He wears a falcon-mask and boasts of his splendor as an akhu, “one who dwells in the radiance,” [19] or “Spirit of Light (Lichtgeist).” [20] An outrageous imposter, he boasts that he “was created by Atum himself from the root of his Eye,” when that God was all alone in the Waters of Nun.[21] We think here of the hypocephalus formula “concealing himself from his children in his Eye (Wedjat-Eye),” [22] recalling that the hypocephalus itself is the iris of his Eye surrounded by the rim as the Waters of Nun and that the theme of the hypocephalus is the creation drama. The famous 17th chapter of the Book of the Dead states that the Cow is the Eye of Re itself.[23]

The false messenger further asserts that he is announced at the horizon by the akhu-spirits and the Four Powers [24] and that he is held in awe by the Four Akhus and the Four Sekhemu (powers), and that his own four members (ḥʿ.w) are composed by Atum. This calls to our attention, if a bit prematurely, the four ram’s heads on one neck of figure 1 in most hypocephali (see appendixes 4–6), as well as to the four creative canopic figures found on almost all hypocephali (Fac. 2, fig. 6). The rascal also claims that he is one of the serpents created by (in or from) the Eye of Atum before Isis was on hand to bear Horus.[25] The serpents appear flanking the central figure on Facsimile 2 (fig. 1) and are discussed below.

The Mysterious Nemes

But the Guardian of the Gate, Ruty the double lion,[26] stops the messenger in his tracks; he may not pass on to the “Most High” without the nemes: “Despair of reaching the ends of heaven,” says the lion, “even though you have the forms of Horus, for you do not have the nemes to enable you to comprehend (study, discuss) the limits of the universe.” [27] But what was it? It has become the custom to call anything the king wore on his head a nemes. For Brunner it is “die Nemes-Krone” (the nemes-crown),[28] for de Buck it is “the nemes-crown,” for Drioton “le cache-perruque qui est l’insigne des dieux et des rois,” for Raymond O. Faulkner it is “the royal wig-cover,” for Thomas G. Allen a “turban.” But how could that enable one to contemplate the limits of the universe? The ideogram in the Coffin Text version is the white crown on the festival ḥb-basket which is the regular determinant for “hypocephalus” in Book of the Dead 162. This is the white wrr.t-crown which “fitted the head in various ways,” according to Kurt Sethe, “as a sort of linen cap which had no fixed shape, but had to constantly be readapted for the occasion.” [29] In other versions of the drama it is the hypocephalus which gets one from the Duat to heaven. As we learn in the very early Ramesseum Drama, in plays where everything is make-believe, they pass around all sorts of objects to represent all sorts of other objects. Apparently there is no reason why the Facsimile 2 hypocephalus should not enjoy such freewheeling. It has been designated by various scholars as serving various but related purposes—as a passport, certificate, travel guide, road map, ticket, and so forth.

The instructions in the hypocephalus handbook (Book of the Dead 162) say to place the golden cow on the breast (“at the throat” ) of the deceased, while the written text was to go under his head.[30] In just such a way the Book of Breathings served in its intimate ties with the deceased as a guidebook, map, and passport to the beyond, clutched to the breast of the mummy.[31] Whatever it was, the function of the nemes now becomes apparent in the play.

Challenged by the lion, the messenger proposes to prove his ties with the All-Highest by uttering the secret word: “Horus has repeated to me what his father said to him at the ty.t (the partition or veil—a woven screen) on the day of his burial. . . . So give me the nemes!” [32] This is similar to what the suppliant does in Book of the Dead 162. Next we are told how the nemes expedites the journey of its holder through the heavens, observing on the way all who dwell beyond the horizon; [33] he is able to prevail over all the gods and gates of the lower world that might challenge him. When Ruty the lion finally gives the nemes to the messenger, the bird breaks into an ecstatic song on the thrill of moving through the void, even through the Waters of Nun, without fear or hindrance.[34] Not even Seth, the mad storm-bull, can daunt the possessor of the nemes.[35] In the first line of the hypocephalus book, Book of the Dead 162, but also at the very top of every hypocephalus, we see the Lord of the Two Tall Feathers (see appendixes 2, 4–7A), who is addressed as the “Mighty Lion,” while in the rim inscription the suppliant appeals to the “Victorious Bull” for deliverance.

Armed with the nemes, the messenger now descends to the sleeper, who lies amidst a “horror of great darkness” (Genesis 15:12).[36] This underworld mission includes a visit to the house of Ruty [37] and then to the house of Isis to view the ultimate mystery, the primordial stirring of life in the gods themselves.[38] Few have failed to notice, along with Philippe J. de Horrack, that the under part of the hypocephalus, a third or even a half of the area is chthonian, or earthly, while the upper half is male and celestial. The lower part belongs to the Hathor-Cow, who was Isis, and the figures around her denote the womb or depths of the underworld where conception and resurrection both begin.

The scene now shifts to heaven where the real Horus sits properly enthroned in glory on his high ḏb.[39] Let us recall that the rim inscriptions of hypocephali, including the Joseph Smith one, begin by saluting the “Djebabty [ḏbb.ty] in the sun temple [ḥw.t bnbn.t] in Heliopolis” who is “most exalted and very glorious.” [40] De Buck describes the present scene as “Horus upon his seats and thrones”; [41] Brunner, “auf seinem Thron”;[42] while Drioton sees “Atoum, imaginé comme dominant les eaux célestes sur le flotteur” [43]—that is, the raft, the hieroglyph showing the inflated goat’s skins for use on the river, especially in the ritual hunting of hippopotami. The word can mean many things, and de Buck warns that “there are various words ḏb which may be intended, see Wb. v, 560.” [44]

The messenger survives all the terrors of the underworld,[45] thanks to the power of the Eye of Horus to which all do obeisance,[46] “because Horus has sealed upon him his own ba . . . as a divine falcon.” [47] This recalls Book of the Dead 162: “surrounded by fire . . . he will be divine in ẖr.t-nṯr; he shall not be rebuffed at any gate.” [48] We also have the “sealing” in Book of the Dead 162: “He is thine own son, do not disown him!” [49] When the messenger finally reaches Osiris, the ways of heaven and earth are clear for him to leave the mournful lion couch and mount to a throne on high,[50] completely restored in spirit and body.[51] This is of course the momentous union of Osiris with Re, with Horus the Son as intermediary, so the whole thing can end in the usual culminating triumph of the coronation: “Thy Son is placed upon thy throne of glory over all the living,” etc.[52] As our story began with the dismal scene of Facsimile 1, it ends in glory and triumph with Facsimile 3. Plainly Facsimile 2 in some way expedites travel between the two.[53]

The full splendor of the coronation scene is most apparent in the oldest version,[54] with universal acclamation and the Creation Hymn: The heavenly order is restored (rwḏ, flourishing), and Nut rejoices when they all see what Atum has created in the council.[55] Great is the intelligence of Horus, etc.; he rules Egypt, and the gods hearken unto him. Thus Faulkner renders Coffin Text 312: “You may jubilate concerning him; he has been initiated in the words of these gods, the Lords of All, who are at the side of the Sole Lord—so said he who is on high on his dais (ḏb), who dwells in holiness, concerning me.” [56] Surprisingly, the whole epic production ends with a shout of praise for the mother-goddess, the “Mistress of the Enneads, Lady of All.” [57] This is a reminder that the hypocephalus itself is a talisman of the Hathor-Cow, the Lady of Heaven; as Isis she is the Eye of Re. And so the matriarchal balance is restored; to achieve universal rebirth, the Mother-Cow must play the principal role.

Abraham and the Book of Breathings

The Book of Breathings, or sensen papyrus, is the last of a long series of Egyptian funerary texts. One of its titles, May My Ba Flourish (even) as the Ba of Re Flourishes,[58] takes it straight back to the Re/Osiris resurrection literature. We may expect its content to sound quite familiar since this Book of Breathings is a later and briefer version of the Book of the Dead, which in turn is a later presentation of the material in the Coffin Texts, which in their turn are a later presentation of the Pyramid Texts.[59] Indeed, the Book of Breathings “is connected with all the ‘orthodox’ documents whether found in the Book of the Dead or in the texts of the tombs or the coffins.” [60]

According to Marc Coenen the Egyptians divided the various Books of Breathings into three categories: (1) The Book of Breathings Made by Isis, (2) The First Book of Breathings, and (3) The Second Book of Breathings. The Joseph Smith text falls into the first category. There are many manuscripts of the Book of Breathings Made by Isis with minor variations in the Egyptian freewheeling manner, according to the individual owner’s taste; but the main points are always the same. Hence the Joseph Smith text, though badly damaged, can be matched at every point by others. One of them is a text very similar to the Joseph Smith text, a beautifully written hieratic text, the de Horrack papyrus, Papyrus Louvre N. 3284. Fortunately, everything in the short and badly damaged Joseph Smith manuscript falls neatly into place when it is compared with this version of the long sensen.[61]

In the tomb of the family of the priest Soter [62] was found a Book of Breathings, on the back of which were written the words in Greek, ὑπὸ τὴν κφαλήν (hypo tēn kephalēn), directing the mortuary workers to put this Book of Breathings under the head of the deceased. Accordingly the Book of Breathings could be placed under the head of the mummy and thus “could sometimes serve as the hypocephalus.” [63] It is interesting that the tomb of this same Soter family contained three astronomical papyri.[64]

But what is the Book of Breathings? The text itself states that it was the “Document of Breathing which Isis made for her brother, Osiris,” [65] and that Thoth himself wrote it “with his own fingers.” [66] And what are its contents? As Émile Chassinat observed, “The ancient writer has gathered together everything which he could imagine as being most likely to provide means after death of reaching another life.” [67] This is, as we have seen, just what every Egyptian wants and just the special way he goes about finding it, leaving no stone unturned. The opening lines tell us that these instructions are to cause Osiris’s “soul to live, to cause his body to live, to rejuvenate all his limbs again so that he might join the horizon with his father, Re; to cause his soul to appear in heaven as the disk of the moon so that his body might shine as Orion in the body of Nut.” [68] It is our well-known Re/Osiris scenario with its cosmic emphasis derived from the old star religion. The owner of the text, who is identified with Osiris, simply wants resurrection and life eternal. First of all, as we might suppose, resurrection is the theme.

In the first column we learn that this book was to assist in bringing together the body and spirit in a process of eternal rejuvenation.[69] There is an admonishment that the book be sealed and carefully hidden so that no one but the owner may ever see it; it is for his individual benefit hereafter and has proven effective millions of times.[70] By means of the book the deceased will be able to carry on an eternal line of kingship amid the glory of the celestial realms.[71] The prime condition for this is purity—freedom from all corruption.[72] The deceased is told, “Stone of Truth is your name!” [73] This is the stone of washing, the maat-stone upon which a candidate stands for his purification.[74] When Christ gave Simon a new name, it was the Aramaic כיפא (kēpā, the rock, stone), which was translated into Greek as Πέτρος (Petros), which also means “rock or stone.” The Joseph Smith Translation of John 1:42 reads: “Thou shalt be called Cephas, which is, by interpretation, a seer, or a stone” —a Stone of Truth indeed!

This is no mere ritual purity, as we have been so often told, but requires the highest spiritual qualities—charity toward one’s fellows is as necessary as ritual ablutions; justice and honor are tested in passing through the Hall of the Two Maats.[75] In transition from earth to heaven one is washed in the Hall of Geb and Shu.[76] Going to the next world repeats the experience of entering this one. The deceased reverses the order and becomes glorified; his face shines like that of Re in achieving the physical resurrection.[77]

In the next section, the original grand initiatory rite, the Opening of the Mouth, becomes effective. The purpose of that rite was to seal the various members of the body against destruction and in anticipation of the resurrection; and now that time has come, so that the ears hear, the eyes see, the tongue speaks, the legs walk, etc.[78]Amun puts the breath of life into the body which Ptah has formed.[79] These gods establish him in his new life as they did in this one; his body is now immortal. You are exactly as you were on earth, but now immortal, the flesh being joined to the bones and the acts of eating and drinking resumed in the hereafter.[80] It is significant that though the uniting of bones and members is described countless times, nowhere is any mention whatever made of a renewing or refreshing of the blood—that is the corruptible element, for this life only. Carefully written records must be kept to validate all this.[81] To be immortal is to be divine, to pursue an everlasting progression—“the Way.” The initiatory rites having served their purpose, you now “see with your eyes, hear with your ears, walk with your legs,” etc., but now it is all forever. This is the whole point of the ordinance; asleep or awake your breath will never cease; you are an Osiris, a god,[82] with all his rights and privileges.[83] You are instructed and promoted among your fellow gods.[84] Glorious garments bespeak the holiness and everlasting endurance of the wearer.[85] You enter into the presence of Osiris to become one of the gods.[86] The gods in attendance on Re invite you to accompany Re and Osiris together with them.[87] The Re/Osiris formula is completed. Offering allotments from the king are then described.[88]

“May your spirit be healthy. May your body live, enduring at the command of Re himself, and like Re it shall neither perish nor be sick forever and ever.” [89] The moral character of the deceased has been tested for any kind of wrongdoing, including light-mindedness, theft, cheating, trickery, greed, hardness of heart, and unchastity.[90] Note that a list of specific sins is not evaluated here in the manner of the Jesuit casuistry—one is judged by the spirit or state of mind in which one’s actions were performed. Though pettiness and moral shallowness are stock charges against the Egyptians, the “mint and rue” (Luke 11:42) are never the main concern in the judgment. More to the point are the positive performances: “He lives by righteousness” (maat, truth and honesty, that virtue that holds society together, above all else).[91] He has given bread to the hungry, drink to the thirsty, clothing to the naked, etc.[92] This is the climax of virtue. With no specific charges against him he is now qualified for any place he chooses to go or any form he chooses to take. This oft-repeated condition is the consummation of desire to be, as Brigham Young puts it, “as independent as the Gods.” [93] This book is placed by the heart instead of under the head,[94] but the instructions in the other documents make the same distinction. In all these texts we are dealing with the same fundamental principles, leading us ever toward the works of Abraham, ever “a follower of righteousness” (maat) (Abraham 1:2).

The Book of Breathings Bears Witness

But what has the Book of Breathings to do with Abraham? The critics of 1967–68, rushing to judgment, constantly professing their pure total commitment to the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth—objective, cool-headed, detached, and scientific—apparently had no interest or even idle curiosity for what the document had to say. “The Egyptian has been translated,” crowed Grant S. Heward and Jerald Tanner, “and has no recognizable connection with the subject matter of the Book of Abraham.” [95] That is as far as they went or felt they needed to go.

Not “recognizable” to whom? The very first line of the hieratic text bears a remarkable resemblance to Abraham’s words in both Genesis and the Book of Abraham: “Here begins the writing which Isis made for her brother Osiris to cause his ba to live.” [96] In the Book of Abraham and the Bible, Abraham says to his wife (and sister), Sarah, “and my soul shall live because of thee.” In Genesis 12:13 and Abraham 2:25: “Therefore say unto them, I pray thee, thou art my sister, that it may be well with me for thy sake, and my soul shall live because of thee.” Why not simply, “I shall live” ? Why the awkward Egyptian idiom, “My ba shall live” ? That is an Egyptian doctrine, and the expression ba defies translation: but if it “cannot be translated, its meaning can certainly be explained,” says Professor Louis V. Žabkar.[97] Among the many explanations offered, he gives his own definition as the “totality of [a person’s] physical and psychic attributes and functions.” [98] Many Egyptologists leave the word ba untranslated.

Diodorus seems to be quoting an Egyptian source when he tells how Isis rescued her brother-husband from death, “giving him his soul” —δοῦσαν τὴν ψυχήν (dousan tēn psychēn)—enabling him to enjoy (to share in) immortality.[99] Soul here is ψυχή (psychē) in Greek and נפש (nepeš) in Hebrew, both meaning “breath,” a fitting introduction to the Book of Breathings.

Routine Makes Ritual

How do Abraham and Sarah relate to Isis and Osiris? The answer is, ritually. Isis can take the name of more other women, human or divine, than any other character. She is Ceres, Venus, Diana of the Ephesians, the triple-form Proserpine; in Athens she is Athena; in Cyprus the Paphian Aphrodite, etc.[100] Osiris, though he comes a poor second, takes the place of more Egyptians than any other god—at his or her funeral every Egyptian can be an Osiris. Here in the two most venerable and sacred marriages in ancient literature (and the most romantic)—Abraham and Sarah, Isis and Osiris—husband and wife are also brother and sister. In both cases it is the woman who takes the lead to rescue her husband. The next step in Abraham’s story shows that we are dealing with a ritual situation, with a conscious repetition of a significant action which not only makes it ritual but also makes it history. A repeated ritual is a historical event literally, and the repetition confirms its historical possibility and reality.

When Abraham cleared up the misunderstanding with Pharaoh, he went to the adjoining kingdom of Gerar, where the identical mix-up of siblings and mates, incidentally the stock-in-trade of ancient comedy, took place again. To the men of Gerar he said “of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah” (Genesis 20:2). Then when the king learned the truth and confronted Abraham, the patriarch stated, “And yet indeed she is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife” (Genesis 20:12). He further explained that Sarah was only complying with his request, for he had told her, “At every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother” (Genesis 20:13, emphasis added). That guaranteed its repetition and made a ritual of it.

In the next generation during a famine, “Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar” (Genesis 26:1), and again “the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister” (Genesis 26:7) to save his life. Again King Abimelech complained and got the same explanation as from Abraham, because “I said, Lest I die for her” (Genesis 26:9).

Isis is further responsible for legalizing Sarah’s claim to be Abraham’s sister: “The Egyptians passed a law permitting men to marry their sisters, following the example of Isis who married her brother Osiris. When he died she ruled Egypt alone with great success so that a law was passed that the queen should have greater power and authority than the king, and that a wife should rule over her husband and husbands always obey their wives.” [101] The priests applied desperate damage control by making this myth a top-secret mystery with ferocious penalties for divulging it.[102] In Egyptian love poems “the lovers refer to each other as ‘brother’ and ‘sister,’ these words being the normal terms of endearment in ancient Egyptian usage.” [103] Solomon addresses his Song of Songs to “my sister, my spouse” (Song of Solomon 4:9, 10, 12). In the Genesis Apocryphon of the Dead Sea Scrolls, the mother of Noah repeatedly addresses Lamech his father as “my brother and my lord.” [104]

What is going on here? Abraham and Sarah identified with Isis and Osiris? That is just the beginning of the parallels that affirm their identity. Since the woman takes the lead in both stories, we ask Are they playing the same role? The solution lies with Isis. Who is this Isis? “There is a great disagreement over these gods. For the same goddess is called by some Isis, by others Demeter, by others Thesmophorus, by others Hera, while still others apply to her all these names.” [105] Her names multiply as she “hides her nature in a bewildering number of attributes and forms.” [106] She is “the Divine One, the Great of Divinity.” [107] She is also everything good. More than any other divinity she brings comfort and cheer to a miserable world. “She was the discoverer of many health-giving drugs and was greatly versed in the science of healing. . . . She discovered the drug which gives immortality, by means of which she not only raised from the dead her son Horus . . . but also made him immortal.” [108] All of these identify her with the highest, greatest, and first of goddesses.

In the Egyptian funerary rites which are taken from the temple rituals, every man is an Osiris. In a splendid vignette in the Book of the Dead, we see Ani and his wife Tutu confronting the majestic pair Osiris and Isis like a mirror image (fig. 12).[109] In the text Ani is called the Osiris Ani, for, as Hornung states, “Every deceased person of the Middle Kingdom and later [periods] became an ‘Osiris.’ ” [110] Identity with Osiris was strictly a legal identity guaranteeing the dead the privileges of Osiris.[111] To enjoy the blessings of Osiris, eternal life and exaltation, one must do the works and suffer the trials of Osiris.[112] The embalming itself “was an imitation of a treatment which the dead god Osiris was believed to have been the first to receive; the dead person, therefore, became Osiris.” [113] The Osiris name, which is assumed by all the initiates into the ordinances of the temple, as Hornung explains, “does not betoken a genuine identity with the ruler of the dead; rather, it means that through his own efforts the human being takes on a previously determined role that bears the name Osiris.” [114] It is a dramatic role, not a “mystic” identification. The title is only temporary and the candidate sheds it upon leaving the ritual “underworld.” [115]

			 	Figure 12. With hands lifted in adoration, Ani and his wife—who shakes a sistrum with her right hand and brings offerings—greet Osiris, who is tenderly embraced by Isis. P. Ani, BD 15, ca. 1250 b.c., BM 10470. The British Museum.

				 Ani was an Osiris—and his wife? Here she bears the large and conspicuous emblems of Isis, all signifying stages of rebirth—the huge lotus, menat-necklace or pectoral of pregnancy, and the large sistrum which soothed Isis at the delivery. If Hathor is the womb of Horus, then she must be Isis, the mother of Horus. She is “the king’s goddess and goddess of coronation (Königsgöttin, Krönungsgöttin)” [116] or the throne itself (Göttin ‘Thron’).[117] Her name ʾIs.t , which also means “seat” or “throne,” is nothing but a sketch of the throne itself, while her crown is the same throne worn on her head.

Fortunately, Isis has left us a wealth of eight areteologies—that is, inscriptions cataloging her virtues and achievements, widely scattered in her temples and shrines. In the famous Areteology of Cyme at Memphis, she lists 57 of her virtues (ἀρταἱ, aretai), among which she states: “I am Isis, the mistress of every land. And I was taught by Hermes, and with Hermes I devised letters. . . . I gave and ordained laws for men, which no one is able to change. I am the eldest daughter of Kronos, I am the wife and sister of King Osiris. I am she who findeth fruit for men. I am the mother of King Horus. I am she that riseth in the Dog star. I am she who is called goddess by women. . . . I brought together woman and man. . . . I revealed the mysteries unto men. . . . I devised marriage contracts. . . . I decreed mercy to suppliants. . . . . I set free those in bonds.”[118] Her glory gains steadily in late antiquity and persists into Christian times.[119] It is best known in the ecstatic gushings of Apuleius in book XI of the Metamorphoses.

Isis and Sarah

If Isis was first of all the great princesses, Sarah’s name shows her to be the same—a great important royal person—“kings of people shall be of her” (Genesis 17:16). Sarah’s name in Hebrew 	שדה (śārāh) means “princess.” [120] In Egyptian the noun sr means “prince” [121] and the verb sr “to be a prince.” [122] When Abraham went to Egypt, Sarah “was taken into Pharaoh’s house” (Genesis 12:15). Like Isis, she too was the wife of a pharaoh and queen of Egypt. Isis was the wisest of oracles; Sarah was identified by Rashi with יסכה (yiśkāh) of Genesis 11:29.[123] If Isis was the most beautiful of goddesses,[124] Sarah was also “fairer than all other women.” [125] Compared with Sarah, “all other beauties were like apes compared with men.” [126] If Isis bestows the throne, so does Sarah. “The name of the goddess Isis is written as if it simply meant ‘throne,’ while she is also depicted with the throne as her distinctive attribute. . . . The throne ‘makes’ the king . . . and so the throne, Isis, is the ‘mother’ of the king. . . . The throne . . . may be the object of profound and complex feelings.” [127] At the pharaoh’s coronation it is announced that “The king who sits on his throne is the king of all the earth,” whereupon Horus says, “He on the great throne is the Lord of the Throne, King of the Gods, who takes all lands under his power.” [128]

Here let us glance at Facsimile 3, figure 3 (see appendix 1). Here Osiris, king of the dead, is seated upon a throne. Standing behind him, with upraised hand in the official ritual position of support, is Isis, his wife and sister. She sustains and confirms his right to the kingship. In the Late Period, Amun, the king of the gods, comes to be identified as Isis.[129] In Egypt “the actual coronation took place inside the temple.” [130]

Sarapis, the mysterious god of the Late Period, was derived, according to Firmicus Maternus, by the Egyptians from Σάρρας παῖς (Sarras-pais), the child of Sarah.[131] Sarah, like Isis, is the ageless mother and perennial bride; with the birth of Isaac she becomes young again—“Is any thing too hard for the Lord?” (Genesis 18:14). The woman who stands behind Osiris on the throne is Isis, sustaining him in his office with uplifted hand; it is Isis, “fused” with Hathor as the Königmacherin, “the king-maker,” as Jan Assmann puts it.[132]

In a series of shrewd and bold actions, Isis saves Re her father by healing him of the bite of a deadly serpent which she had placed in his way, promising to cure him only if he gives her the secret name, so she can hand it on to her son Horus.[133] Thus it is again Isis to the rescue—but in her own interest.

Isis saves the life of Horus when she finds him lying under a tree, almost dead from the blows of his rival, Seth, who has torn out his eyes, his power and authority, his perfection—that is, the perfectly restored wedjat-eye.[134] She heals him and sponsors him before the great council of the gods as candidate for the throne.[135] In the council she champions his claim to the throne against Seth’s claim, “I am older than he!” They contend for the throne as hippopotami in the water, where Isis rescues Horus from the harpoon of Seth.[136] They contend with boats; Horus’s cedar boat floats, but Seth’s stone boat sinks.[137] In the end Isis orders Seth bound and brought in, and Horus sits on the throne of his father, Osiris, by order of Isis. To celebrate, “all have wreaths for the Festival of Horus, son of Isis in the office of his father Osiris of Busiris.” [138]

“Isis the Mighty, Protectress of Her Brother, . . . seeks for [her son Horus] tirelessly, wandering through the land in mourning, not resting until she has found him.” [139] The court of the gods convenes on an island, and they instruct the ferryman to keep an eye out for Isis. She arrives disguised as a weeping widow seeking for her lost child who has been robbed of his throne by bandits and is now lost and crying somewhere on the island.[140] This is typical of the poems in which the frantic Isis searches for her lost child: “I am Isis. . . . I wandered as a beggar in fear of the evil one. I looked for the child to feed him. . . . I found him . . . helpless and fatherless, weeping on the bank, . . . too weak to answer . . . or even to nurse.” [141] Assmann says the Horus child is here “the image of all suffering patients.” [142]

Perhaps the most significant, and certainly the most striking, aspect of Isis’s calling is the throne. Although she is responsible for putting the king on it, she herself never sits on it. She procures it for her son, glories in his exaltation, defends him against all challengers, but never claims the throne for herself.

Again, we have the repetition which suggests ritual. In the next generation, Rebekah, by clever tricks and deceptions, saves the inheritance for her son Jacob, even though Father Isaac favored Esau as his firstborn (see Genesis 27).

If Isis was the throne and the authority of her husband, Sarah is no less “the Princess,” “the Great One.” As Abraham’s sister, she got him a favorable introduction into the court, where he was well treated and obtained great riches (Genesis 12:16; 13:1–2). Among the gifts that the pharaoh gave Abraham was one of his daughters, Hagar, who became Sarah’s maid.[143]

Within the last fifty years Abraham has been cast in a third great love story. In the Genesis Apocryphon of the Dead Sea Scrolls he reports, “I, Abram, dreamed a dream in the night of our going up into the land of Egypt, and what I beheld in my dream was a Cedar tree . . . [words missing] and men came and tried to cut down and uproot the Cedar while leaving the Palm standing alone. And the Palm tree called out and said, ‘Do not cut down the Cedar! Cursed and shamed whoever’ [words missing]. So the Cedar was spared in the shelter of the Palm.” [144]

This shows some parallels with Homer’s delightful love story in the Odyssey. What turns our attention to Greece is not only the great age of the story but that it was none other than Abraham who dreamed it all as he was crossing the water into Egypt. Did he find Sarah on the shore waiting for him? One account has it that when they were about to cross a waterway in the Delta, an official boundary, he warned her to play the part of his sister because he saw her astonishing beauty uncovered for the first time as she removed some of her clothes before entering the water.[145] In our Greek parallel, again we find a renowned wandering hero depending for his life on the cooperation of a princess. Odysseus, almost drowned, cast ashore on the island of the Phaeacians, falls into a deep sleep in the bushes on the beach. He is awakened by the shrill cry of maidens, who, under supervision of the Princess Nausicaa, had been doing the royal family’s laundry on the riverside, and while it was drying on the shingle were playing ball on the beach. Somebody missed, and the ball went into the river and was being borne out to the angry deep, hence the wailing girls. Odysseus quickly covered himself with a branch (cedar or cypress) and approached the girls. They all scattered except the princess, whose poise and beauty quite overcame the stranger as he recited an eloquent and diplomatic speech comparing the damsel with a tall sacred palm in the courtyard of the temple of Delos.[146]

So the Palm at once took the evergreen-clad stranger into her protection, gave him some clothes, and bade him follow the procession of the big laundry wagon back to the palace. There he was faced with dangerous competition, challenged and taunted by the young dandies of the court, but he redeemed himself by an exhibition of prodigal athletic prowess. After they heard his story, the Phaeacians loaded him with royal gifts, the best of which was a ship to take him back to his own wife and kingdom. Here Odysseus’s real soul mate was the faithful Penelope, his wife back in Ithaca. So Homer, with his usual tact, treats the whole affair with Nausicaa as light comedy, the banter between the wise Odysseus and the clever girl turns serious only in their last conversation. She jokes about saving the life of the greatest hero of the time, but he tells her it was no joke, “for you did save me, Lady, and I will never forget it!” [147]

There is a lesson in these romances; elements of the story turn up in many places. Thus in the Songs of Isis and Nephthys in the Bremner-Rhind Papyrus, there is an episode where the newborn child Horus is hidden in the bushes on the banks of the Chemmis marsh lest Seth should find and destroy him (fig. 13). The child appears on the shore, lost and weeping, hiding in the bushes from Seth. The mother is desperate, but there is none willing to help from the nearby village until a fair princess comes down and takes the child into her care, and he is saved by his leafy disguise.[148]

Humiliation of the Hero

The once popular interpretation of all such tales was the “Dying God Myth” —that is, the time when the Sun, the King, Tammuz, Adonis goes into his annual nadir. One thing certain is that it belongs to a solar year-rite. In all the instances in which the hero is saved by a woman, there is an element of embarrassment for male chauvinists—the strong man saved by a weak woman. Even worse, there is another variation on the theme in which the mightiest man, the cosmocrat of the day, is outwitted and dispatched by a woman. Such is the case of Samson and Delilah; Solomon and Queen Bilqis; or the Shulamite, Cyrus, the world conqueror, and Tomyris, queen of the Massagetae; Sisera and Jael; Esther and Haman; Judith and Holofernes; Alexander and the Nubian Queen Candace. All of these turnabout stories take place as a seasonal celebration and a banquet between parties of different nationalities and race but of equal rank.

Figure 13. Isis nurses her son Horus as they hide among the papyri. On the right, Amun-Re offers the ankh, symbol of life, to her nostrils, while Thoth, Twice-Great, “Lord of the City of Eight,” presents the sa-knot of protection . Sanctuary rear wall, Birth House, Isis Temple, Philae, ca. 145 b.c.

The long and involved Story of the Two Brothers brings together a rich mix of what sounds like folktales or nursery rhymes: the wife of his brother, like Potiphar’s wife, slandering the innocent youth, and the older brother, who accordingly flees to the Valley of the Cedars. There he places his heart in the top of a cedar tree, and the gods create a beautiful woman, his wife. Meantime, a lock of the woman’s hair is borne over the sea and up the Nile to a beach where Pharaoh’s washers are doing the royal laundry. By the wondrous odor of the hair the lady is identified and wooed by Pharaoh’s servants, who give her rich jewelry. She agrees to go to Pharaoh but has the cedar tree cut down so her husband dies. He comes back to life as a bull, which she has Pharaoh kill. Then he is reborn as two sacred persea trees. When at her request the two trees standing at the gate of the temple are cut down, a chip flies into her mouth, and she becomes pregnant with the younger brother, who grows up to become Pharaoh and punishes her.[149]

What is the point of all this? Pharaoh was a good man, but his authority lacked the holy priesthood—he would “fain claim” it and, as a desperate resort, diligently imitate the rites and ordinances that went with it (Abraham 1:27). He claimed patriarchal succession, but according to the order of the Egyptians, it was by his mother’s line that he received it. The true patriarchal authority was claimed by Abraham, and he had the documents to prove it: “But I shall endeavor, hereafter, to delineate the chronology running back from myself to the beginning of the creation, for the records have come into my hands, which I hold unto this present time” (Abraham 1:28).

Dramatizing the Facsimiles

Facsimiles 1, 2, and 3 were included as illustrations of the Book of Abraham. In Facsimile 1, we see the hero on the altar, praying for his life, even as the angel comes to deliver him. Facsimile 3 shows Abraham sitting on an exalted throne. What has happened in the meantime? That is the subject of Facsimile 2 and should cast some light on the hypocephalus, which, as we have just noted, assists the subject in getting from the one world to the other. Abraham’s story tells how it happened and can be matched by some of the richest rabbinical haggadah, Abraham apocrypha, and Muslim written traditions, most of which have only come to light in the twentieth century.[150]

When Abraham was twenty years old, his father instructed him and his brother Haran to go into the market place at Ur and sell some of the family idols in order to raise money for household expenses.[151] An old woman approached Abraham and asked to buy one of the idols he was selling because thieves had stolen the one she just purchased from Haran. Abraham then asked her how she could worship an idol that could not even deliver itself from thieves.[152] He then began to teach her about “the God of gods and Lord of lords, creator of heaven and earth, the sea and all that is in it.” [153] She was converted and acknowledged Abraham as a prophet of the Lord God.[154] This woman then daily went about in the streets of the city preaching the God of Abraham and calling people to repentance.[155] Because many people began to believe, Nimrod summoned her and asked what she was doing and why she did not worship him. She responded, “You are a liar, and you are the greatest unbeliever in the one unchangeable God.” [156] Nimrod promptly had her put to death.[157] Nimrod then counseled with his princes concerning what to do about Abraham, and they suggested that the king decree a great seven-day feast (the great year-rite of the Babylonians), so that Abraham, impressed with the wealth and power of Nimrod, would be converted to his faith.[158] When Terah took the royal invitation to his son, the latter refused to go.[159] The family went to the festivities without him, and, being left alone, Abraham proceeded to smash all the idols in the shop, including the statues of the king.[160] For his iconoclasm Abraham was sentenced by the king to be shut up in prison and starved to death.[161] Abraham was kept in prison for a year but was provided with food and water by an angel.[162] Meanwhile, the king and courtesans made great preparations for the forthcoming sacrifice of Abraham at the year-rite, devoting forty days to the construction of a pyre to support the altar.[163]

The rites of the New Year, being the death of the old, had to begin with a funeral. “The old prehistoric mysteries of Abydos,” wrote Wolfgang Helck, “necessarily include both the funeral of the dead king and the installation of his successor.” [164] The old king must be buried so that the “new king” can mount the throne. The two come right together; things can be arranged so that the old king, who does not want to die, simply stages a mock funeral and burial, followed by a reawakening ceremony, taking place after the king had entered the tomb. This is done by having a substitute take the king’s place, as is illustrated in the Vandier Papyrus.[165] The custom is also attested in Mesopotamia.[166]

When the time of sacrifice arrived, Nimrod commanded that Abraham be cast into the fire, and one of the princes attempted to do so, but fire came out of the furnace and burned him up.[167] Several others attempted to cast Abraham into the fire, and they too were burned up.[168] Satan then appeared as a man bearing wood, nails, and a rope, and proceeded to place Abraham on a catapult to throw him into the flames from a safe distance.[169] Strongly bound and about to be propelled, “at that moment Abraham raised his eyes toward the heavens and said: ‘O Lord, my God, thou seest what this wicked one is doing to me.’ ” [170] While he was thus praying, angels interceded with God to spare Abraham, and he assured them that he would rescue Abraham.[171] Satan then appeared to Abraham in the form of a man and said, “If you wish to be saved from the fire of Nimrod, bow down to me and believe in me!” [172] Whereupon, Abraham recognized the devil and denounced him.[173] Abraham’s mother then pled with Abraham to submit to Nimrod to save himself, but he refused.[174] Then the angel Gabriel came and asked, “What, Abraham, shall free you from this fire?” [175] And Abraham responded, “The God in whom I trust, the God of heaven and the God of the earth, he shall rescue me.” [176] In another version they erected a platform and put Abraham on the top and praised him; but he refused the honor—there is only one king and that is God.[177] Just at the moment when a priest was about to sacrifice Abraham, there were violent convulsions of the earth as flames burst from it and consumed all the bystanders.[178]

Another version of the story reports that Pharaoh and the court, instead of beholding Abraham in the flames, saw him walking in a delightful garden with an angel.[179] Nimrod then offered to worship God and make sacrifices to him.[180] An even greater surprise followed when Abraham refused the astonishing proposal, saying, “God will not accept from thee after the manner of thy religion.” [181] To this Nimrod replied, “O Abraham, I cannot lay down my kingship but I will contribute oxen to the sacrifice,” and after that time, he left Abraham in peace.[182] Or, as the Book of Abraham puts it, the king [i.e., Pharaoh] was “blessed . . . with the blessings of the earth, and with the blessings of wisdom, but cursed . . . as pertaining to the Priesthood” (Abraham 1:26).

More surprises. After Abraham grievously insulted Nimrod by breaking up idols of his gods, Nimrod had his servants gather wood for a pyre forty-four cubits wide and six cubits high to burn him on.[183] Then all his court (365 kings) “bowed themselves at the feet of Abraham our father! . . . And they cut down cedars and made for him a great platform and made him sit atop it, and they brought their sons and placed them on his lap and said, ‘Teach us your ways for permanent eternal security.’ ” [184]

On Target

Why such an obsession with the year-rite? It is because Abraham is a prime example of the tradition in literature, while Joseph Smith, long before the phenomenon emerged, provided us with at least five splendid examples of the great assembly. There is the celebration before the throne of God (1 Nephi 1:8–11); then there is the gathering of the righteous posterity of Adam at Adam-ondi-Ahman just before Adam’s death (D&C 107:53); the future gathering of the righteous at Adam-ondi-Ahman before the second coming of the Savior (D&C 116:1); and the gathering at the temple after Christ’s resurrection (3 Nephi 11–26). But the most striking of all is the coronation of King Mosiah, which we are explicitly told took place at the beginning of a new age when the people are given a new name. The remarkably detailed event can be compared with the description of another ancient Jewish coronation, the crowning of the Prince of the Captivity, or Exilarch, described by Nathan the Babylonian in the tenth century.[185] There is no description of a coronation in the Old Testament, so this one shows impressive resemblance to the Egyptian model.

First, a proclamation was sent out that none should be absent from the coronation, that each should bring with him the most costly thing he had—presents of gold, silver, and textiles.[186] The day before the affair, a wooden pulpit, was erected as a speaker’s platform for the Exilarch whose seat was placed on top of it,[187] exactly as in the story of Abraham at court. From there, like Abraham, he addressed the multitude on the principles of righteousness and the seasonal significance of the event.[188] Then the prayer leader pronounced a blessing on the Exilarch, the heads of the academies, and all the people of the cities and villages.[189] To match this King Benjamin declared, “This day he hath spiritually begotten you” (Mosiah 5:7). It was the universal birthday, also the day of creation, to celebrate which the people all brought the firstfruits of the New Year and animals for sacrifice. For their birthday, King Benjamin gives to the people a new name, etc., and the names of all those present are entered into the national register. There is also a sumptuous feast; Nathan lays special emphasis on the dessert.[190]

The Locale

Abraham’s Ur of the Chaldees was most likely located in northern Syria, called by the Egyptians Naharina, where Egyptian influence was at times strong.[191] The Book of Abraham explicitly states that the festivities were a joint Egyptian and Canaanite undertaking, with the four great gods of the region joining with “the god of Pharaoh, king of Egypt” (Abraham 1:6). The term is a significant one; William F. Albright has proposed that the people of Syria-Canaan sacrificed their children neither to a god nor to the king, but specifically “to the god of the King or the royal god.” [192] In the reverse direction we later behold the idol of Khnum of Thebes going to Naharina to heal the daughter of a local ruler, the king of Bakhtan.[193] In this story we are told how Pharaoh visited Naharina each year, when all the local princes came “bowing in peace to the might of his majesty.” [194] He married the daughter of the king of Bakhtan and during the great festival at Thebes, “the place of the beginning,” a messenger arrived from Bakhtan reporting that her younger sister, Bentresh, was very ill—would Pharaoh send a physician to treat her?[195] The doctor found the girl possessed of an evil spirit beyond his control. So the image of Khnum was sent from Thebes to take over, and the princess was healed.[196] During the long trip to Naharina, the idol was greeted with festivities all along the way, the local idols all coming to pay it homage.[197] The people wanted to keep the idol in Bakhtan, and it took a special dream to get the image back to Egypt again.[198] The story is full of details making vivid reality of the close religious ties between Egypt and Chaldea.

Long before that, in the time usually attributed to Abraham, the famous Sinuhe, fleeing as a political outcast to Canaan, became a chief and a celebrity in Canaan. He preached the gospel of Amun, the Almighty, and the glory of his servant Pharaoh. In a letter which Sinuhe sends to the Pharaoh Sesostris I, the names of Asiatic gods are freely mingled with those of the gods of Egypt, as in our Book of Abraham.[199] A thousand years later a priest of Amun, the famous Wenamun, while on temple business, ran into trouble in Canaan—money trouble, from which he tries to extricate himself by reminding the king of Byblos to show due respect to Amun, the god of the universe.[200] In the Story of the Two Brothers, the younger brother seeks refuge in Canaan, “the Valley of the Pine,” but returns to Memphis where the Queen tries to make a sacrifice of him.[201]

Of particular interest is the Tale of the Doomed Prince; that hero flees like Sinuhe from the court of Egypt to Naharina where the princes of the land come from the four directions to pay homage to the visiting son of Pharaoh, just as the four idols of the four regions stand with the “god of Pharaoh” in Facsimile 1. Joseph Smith identifies these four canopic figures with the four quarters of the earth on the hypocephalus, Facsimile 2, figure 6.[202] All the youth of the land are summoned to the palace when the prince of Naharina himself appears to attend (after opposing) the wedding of the man who has reached the window of a high tower where the princess of Naharina awaits [203]—as Joseph later meets Asenath who is in a high tower in the pseudepigraphical tale of Joseph and Asenath.[204]

The four regional idols appear again in the al-Arish text,[205] recounting the beginnings of civilization on earth; it ends with Pharaoh summoning all the Asiatics to Memphis to be present before the throne of the universe where stand the four idols of Pharaoh—that is, the four canopic figures.[206]

The Mise-en-scène

Abraham chapter 1 begins the story with a lucid picture of the setting (Abraham 1:9–11). It was a vast plain. All the great centers had such spaces, the maidan or field, for contests, processions, campgrounds for pilgrims, and space for the big market.[207] The Midrash tells of the Plain of Sava, where every year all the star-worshippers gather before the king of Sodom for a great celebration; that is where Abraham was put to the test.

Such places were marked from a distance by a sacred mountain or Urhügel of the Egyptians (see pp. 342–43, fig. 41), marking the first land to emerge from the great waters and the place where the sun first rose on the day of creation.[208] The primal mountain dominated all the great centers, whether as pyramid, ziggurat,[209] megalithic mountain like Silbury Hill in England,[210] superbarrow like Newgrange in Ireland,[211] or the Lögberg (or Law Rock) at the Althing in Iceland.[212] They served as “territorial markers,” celebrating the greatness of a ruler or tribe and drawing the world to them from a distance. “Hail Atum! Hail Khepri! Thou hast risen up as the hill (or on the hill)! Thou hast arisen as the benben-stone in the House of the Benben in Heliopolis.” [213] The benben-stone originally sat atop a mountain; it is represented at least twice on the rim of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus (see appendix 2B). Gertrud Thausing puts it well—the pyramid is “the architectural manifestation of the idea of emanation and the symbol of the uniting of the heavenly and earthly. The name itself . . . mr . . . is the way that leads down and also the way that leads up.” [214]

The giant structures are always combined with standing stones. The hieroglyph for a sacred mound ἰ.t is a mound surrounded by standing stones. The famous complex of Niuserre at Abu Gurob has its sun-stone, a huge squat obelisk, standing upon a mound before which is the great altar.[215] The names of the greatest sacred centers in Egypt are all designated as places of standing stones. ʾIwnw, is On (Hebrew) or Heliopolis; ʾIwnw šmʿw, Thebes, the On of the South; ʾIwny, Hermonthes/Erment, twin stones; ʾIwn.t, Tenetyra/Denderah; ʾIwny.t, Letopolis/Esne. In the temple at Denderah there are lists of such mounds.[216]

According to Abraham’s account, the mound was called “Potiphar’s Hill” (Abraham 1:10)—that is, “the Hill of the One-whom-Re-has-given or appointed” (p-dἰ-p-Rʿ), and the plain was called the Plain of Olishem. An inscription of the king of Akkad, Naram-Sin, dating to around 2250 b.c., states that “Naram-Sin the strong defeated Arman and Ebla and from the banks of the Euphrates as far as Ulisum.” [217] It is quite possible that this Ulisum is none other than the Olishem of Abraham 1. The “u” and “o” are phonetic variants of each other in Semitic languages.[218] Moreover texts from the time of Naram-Sin regularly use the “s” to represent the “sh” sound.[219] Also discovered at Ebla are several images of Egyptian gods stylistically datable to the Middle Kingdom and dated by the archaeologists to Middle Bronze II (1750–1650 b.c.),[220] approximately the time of Abraham. So we have both Egyptian and West Semitic names used in the geographical area described by the Book of Abraham (sly Joseph Smith!).

The Time

Roy A. Rosenberg has argued that the Suffering Servant passage of Isaiah 52:13–53:12 was “a portion of a ritual drama . . . celebrated in both Persia and Babylonia in connection with the acronichal rising of Sirius” (sometimes identified with Saturn), “who demanded human sacrifice,” [221]and notes that “in Jewish tradition Isaac is the prototype of the ‘Suffering Servant,’ bound upon the altar as a sacrifice, and that “both the Jewish and the Christian traditions stem ultimately from the ancient Canaanite cult of Jerusalem, in which periodically the king, or a substitute for the king, had to be offered for a sacrifice.” [222] Abraham tells us that on the occasion of his sacrifice, which anticipated Isaac’s, “the priest made an offering unto the god of Pharaoh, and also unto the god of Shagreel, even after the manner of the Egyptians. Now the god of Shagreel was the sun” (Abraham 1:9).[223] The oldest desert tribes, who supplied the lore of Canaan and among which Abraham moved,[224] designated the two bright stars Sirius and Procyon with the name Shighre, or Shagre.[225] Shighre, according to Lane’s dictionary, designates whatever star is at the moment the brightest object in the heavens.[226] Raymond O. Faulkner has emphasized the identity of the king (the original roi soleil) with Sirius.[227] The king of Egypt is able, “with the Dog Star [Sirius] as guide,” to find the place of resurrection at “the Primeval Hill, an island . . . pre-eminently suitable for a resurrection from death.” [228] The most important event in the history of the universe, according to the Egyptians, was the heliacal rising of Sirius, when Sirius, the sun, and the Nile all rose together in the morning of the New Year, the day of creation, as officially proclaimed from the great observatory of Heliopolis.[229] Without expanding on the theme, it will be enough here to note that the sun, the hill, and Sirius are inseparably connected in the rites, as they are in the Book of Abraham, where we find “the god of Pharaoh, and also . . . the god of Shagreel, . . . the sun” receiving sacrifices side by side at Potiphar’s Hill (Abraham 1:9).

As to the name Shagreel, nothing is more familiar to the student of the Near East than theophoric names ending in -el, hence, Shaghre-el, “Shaghre is God.” The name Shigr (vowel speculative) has turned up in a sixth-century Aramaic inscription as a local god that received sacrifices in Canaan.[230] That is exactly where we would like to find it: “Mighty acts” are attributed to the deity, “who may be linked with Astar and . . . Ashtarte” —that is, Venus, indisputably the brightest star in the sky.[231]

In determining the time of the greatest of festivals, Sirius for the Egyptians was all-in-all. The lifetime of the world was set by the “Sothic Cycle”: “It is Sothis (Sirius) . . . who prepares yearly sustenance for you in this her name of ‘Year’ and who guides me when I come to you.” [232] “O Sothis my soul, make my path ready; set up a stairway to the great plain.” [233] Again, the hill and the plain. Sirius guides the king to the primeval hill for his resurrection.[234] On the horizon Sirius joins the Sun for the heliacal rising of Sirius from which the beginning of everything was dated; it was the quintessential New Year: “Now the god of Shagreel was the sun” (Abraham 1:9). A stunning star mural from El-Ghassul is supposed to represent the Sun and Sirius united (see color plate 3 and map on p. 542, fig. 66); it was found at “the meeting point between the two great empires of Egypt and Chaldea,” in one of Abraham’s “cities of the plain” (Genesis 13:12).[235] The odd-sounding name of Shagreel both fixes the time and nature of the celebration for the most important day of the Egyptian year.

The Case of the Virgins

The virgins have a special place in the story. Abraham tells us that three virgins “of the royal descent . . . were offered up because of their virtue” on the same altar and in the same manner—that is, in the same rites as Abraham himself (Abraham 1:11). The striking thing about the Joseph Smith version is the clarity of the picture: It was (a) virgins, (b) namely three virgins, (c) three virgins of royal descent, from Ham, (d) sacrificed on the same altar and in the same manner as Abraham, (e) because of their virtue, and this was (f) in Chaldea, (g) on a special kind of altar like a bed, (h) with knife and fire, and it was done even “after the manner of the Egyptians” (Abraham 1:9–13). All these points and more are confirmed in many places by the record.

Closest to Abraham is the case of the caused his other daughters of Lot, Abraham’s nephew, who were made candidates for sacrifice in Sodom; the eldest was burned on a pyre because she would not follow the wicked practices of the greedy people of Sodom.[236] This story is not found in the Bible, nor do many people know that the name of the damsel was Paltit,[237] showing what was expected of her, for it is the same name as the Greek Palakis, a ritual hierodule or harlot.

There are also the three daughters of Minyas, not far from Sodom, who were driven mad for refusing to participate in licentious Dionysian revels.[238] We should also mention the “prudish cycle” of males who refused to misbehave; such were Hippolytus and even Bata, the younger of the famous two brothers of Egypt, who resisted temptation, like Joseph in Egypt, and suffered accordingly.[239]

Figure 14. Brightly painted wooden case masks and a uniform costume make the performers anonymous and thus able to impersonate the gods before they are sent home to the San Francisco Peaks. Shungopovi Pueblo. Detail of 1901 photograph. National Anthropological Archives, Smithsonian Institution (NAA INV 02152100).

				 There is a grim paradox in the rites. Since fertility is the object, both the bride and the mother must play highly incompatible leading roles, and that in one and the same person. In the rites of Babylon, “the divine bride . . . [was] washed . . . with water and soda . . . rendering her ritually clean and restoring her virginity” while “the bridegroom Marduk wore a garment of ears of corn” —a dead giveaway.[240]

Some time ago the writer attended the great Home Dance of the Hopi hemis kachinas of Arizona (fig. 14). It was a splendid affair, lasting all day. The dancers were men wearing wooden masks and cedar collars and carrying green corn sheaves, and a small company of corn-maidens marked time as they ground the corn.[241] In conclusion the maidens repeatedly circled the corn-dancers, while the presiding elders constantly sprinkled all parties with fertilizing cornmeal. Toward evening some virgins were brought out from the kiva to participate in a wedding ceremony. The “catch” to all this was that the “virgins” were false virgins, men dressed in maidens’ attire. The resemblance to the ancient order of things is unmistakable.

By sacrificing his virgin daughter to Poseidon (her name was Chthonia), Erechtheus, the god of fountains and fields, caused three virgins to commit suicide, for which Zeus consumed him with a flaming thunderbolt.[242] The Egyptians too sacrificed harlots to promote the fertility of the land, “an adulteress [being] one in whom this spirit [of fertility] is emphatically incarnate.” [243] The rites seem to have survived longest in the far south; Heliodorus describes huge throngs gathered for the celebration of sacrificing virgins.[244] Nimrod’s courtesans “advised him to hold a great year-feast . . . to impress Abraham by its splendor and richness.” [245] But when Abraham feigned sickness and stayed home the next year, “all the women held a holiday gathering wood for a holocaust for Abraham” —this time he was the recalcitrant and upright one, like Joseph in Egypt.[246]

In the story of the Egyptian hero Mery-Re, only Hathor is in command in affairs concerning visits to the under- and upper-worlds.[247] From the Twenty-first Dynasty on, the title of “God’s Wife” was transferred from the wife of Pharaoh to his daughter, “who became the consecrated wife of the Theban god and to whom human intercourse was strictly forbidden.” [248] Strabo tells us that the Egyptians assigned the fairest princess, a virgin of the royal line, to be a hierodule until her physical purification, after which she could marry.[249] But some virgins insisted on remaining virgins and so lost their lives. Even in the oldest Egyptian year-rites it is to Hathor that the king announces, “I have slain thine enemies who were massacred by the knife . . . slain upon thine altar!” To which the Lady replies, “Your Majesty! I have burnt . . . thine enemies.” [250]

A Bed of Pain

Another compelling clue to confirm the Book of Abraham is the ingenious mechanical arrangement made for the sacrifice. “If we believe in present revelation, as published in ‘Times and Seasons’ last spring,” said Joseph Smith, “Abraham, the prophet of the Lord, was laid upon the iron bedstead for slaughter.” [251] The tradition is that all the cities of the plain had such beds in Abraham’s time. The judges of the other cities—Gomorrah, Admah, and Menon—had all taken counsel together and advised the other people “to set up beds on their commons. When a stranger arrived, three men seized him by his head, and three by his feet, and they forced him upon one of those beds. There they stretched or contracted him violently to make him fit the exact length of the bed.[252] The story goes that when Abraham’s servant Eliezer, being in the exact image of his master, once visited Lot in Sodom on business for Abraham (whose unauthorized hospitality had got him banished from the town), he was seized and taken to the marketplace to be thrown down upon one of those special beds.[253]

This, of course, is the famous bed of Procrustes,[254] and Bernhard Beer has noted that Procrustes’ other name or epithet, Damastes, means “the Forcer or the Violater,” which is also the root meaning of the word Sodom.[255] The Book of Abraham tells us that the altar “was made after the form of a bedstead, such as was had among the Chaldeans” (Abraham 1:13). It is the well-known lion couch, which is carefully reproduced in Egyptian beds, altars, embalming tables, and royal birth scenes—the all-purpose convenience for rites of passage.[256] In the Mery-Re story, and Midrash alike, it is a great brazier (actually a bed converted to a furnace) like the firewood which was meant to serve as both altar and pyre for Abraham and later for Isaac.

Ungracious Hosts

At a time when the world was blasted with drought and famine, a situation even more heavily emphasized in the Book of Abraham than in the Bible (Abraham 2:17, 21), many a youthful lord was forced to leave home and join the scattered populace seeking for better conditions and trying to alleviate the sufferings of others. There is a whole cycle of such heroes such as Odysseus, Pollux, Menelaus, Paris, Hermes, Jason, Bellerophon, and Cytisorus.[257] Each of them was entertained by a local ruler who in his own desperation needed a human sacrifice to restore the prosperity of the crops. After receiving the king’s hospitality, a guest would be invited, or forced, to duel with his host—wrestling, boxing, running, or with weapons—in which the guest was expected to lose his life and have his skull nailed to the temple roof and his remains scattered over the fields to fertilize them. Some of the heroes, however, turned the table on their hosts. This shows us time and again that the terrible doings we hear about in the Abraham legends actually could have taken place.

The most celebrated of all these heroes was Hercules, whose twelve labors correspond to Abraham’s ten trials, as both journeyed through a heat-blighted world performing their beneficent tasks—Abraham dug wells, planted trees, built hospices, and saved crops as he traveled through the world. After ridding Crete of bears, wolves, and serpents, Hercules went to Libya where the tyrant King Antaeus, the son of Mother Earth, would force all strangers to wrestle with him and end up nailing their skulls to the roof of the temple of his father Poseidon to bring water to the land.[258] Hercules, however, by a trick of his own, won the match and killed Antaeus and brought bumper crops to the land. Then he went next door to Egypt where Antaeus’s wicked brother, Busiris, was Pharaoh. Every year to overcome the drought in his kingdom, Busiris would sacrifice a noble stranger on an altar of Zeus.[259] When Hercules allowed himself to be led to the altar (the city Busiris was from prehistoric times the main center of human sacrifice in Egypt), he prayed for power and at the last moment burst his bonds and killed the cruel king and his priests.[260] This made his priestly victims the substitute sacrifice. It all began when a rascally itinerate seer, Phrasius, told Busiris that the chronic blight on the land would end if a stranger were sacrificed annually.[261] The story is vividly illustrated in the famous Busiris hydria in the Athens Museum. On this painted vase we see Hercules flinging the wicked priests of Busiris in all directions, while the high priest kneels on top of the altar pleading for his life and the miserable Pharaoh lies bound and helpless on the step of the altar.[262] All of this puts us into the picture of the wicked, disorganized, and suffering world of Abraham.

The Real Field of Abraham

Here is an edict of the Emperor Theodosius who was faced with an environmental problem. The Field of Abraham was a sacred place, yet it was also the site of the greatest year-rite in the world, which meant to him the abomination of the pagans. This is his edict describing the situation:

Ch. 4. An account about those things which were done by Constantine the Great concerning the oak of Mamre, and about the church which he built there.

I have long considered the necessity of a report on the business of the Emperor Constantine concerning the oak which is called Mamre. The place today is called Terebinth. It is about 15 stades south of Hebron and about 240 stades from Jerusalem. In this place the Son of God, along with the two angels sent against Sodom, appeared to Abraham and prophesied that a son would be born to him, according to the true report.

There down to the present day the brilliant yearly panegyris is celebrated in the summer by the people living in the region, which includes principally Palestinians, Phoenicians, and Arabs. Most of them come for trade, buying and selling. For it is the most popular of fairs (literally from the Latin: Of all fairs in the world, the greatest number attend this one). The Jews attend it because they boast Abraham as their ancestor; the Greeks because of the visitation of the angels; the Christians because at that place was the manifestation of that blessed man that clearly indicated the salvation of the human race in times to come. The individuals also revere the place with various cultic practices, some pouring out prayers to God, the Ruler of All, others invoking the angels with libations of wine and burning of incense, or the sacrifice of a bullock or a goat, or an egg or a cock. For each one is accustomed to offer the most precious thing he possesses in that year, nourishing it throughout the year to make it fit for sacrifice at the feast for himself and those with him. For everyone, whether for the veneration of the place or in fear of divine judgment lest something terrible happen, abstains from sexual intercourse, even though the festival is attended by women who make themselves as beautiful as possible and parade themselves in public, and they have tents or booths where they engage in general intercourse. The place is all open fields and plowed land without any buildings except around the oak where Abraham dwelt, by which is a well. At other times no merchant drinks water from that well, for according to gentile custom lamps are lit at the well. Some pour wine into it; others throw cakes in; others coins, or ointment, or sufflitus (fumigation).

When the brother-in-law of Constantine, who had been there as a worshipper, reported it to the Emperor, he roundly rebuked the bishop of Palestine for neglecting his duty and permitting that most sacred place to be polluted by impure sacrifice and libations. This is testified in the letter which he wrote to Macarius, the bishop of Jerusalem, and to Eusebius, bishop of Pamphilios, and to the other bishops of Palestine whom he commanded forthwith to meet with the Phoenician bishops to overthrow, destroy, and purge the altar that stood there, burn the statues and arrange for a church to be built for meeting at this most ancient and holy place. They were to take care that thereafter that place should be free of all libations and sacrifices so that no other religion would stand next to the Church of God . . . all this on pain of the most severe punishment he could inflict. Along with the imperial letter, he ordered leaders and priests of Christ to carry out the work.[263]

Figure 15. Pharaoh Sheshonq I had himself depicted in the traditional pose of the mighty warrior smiting his enemies while his father Amun-Re presents him with over 150 captive peoples bound with ropes. The name of the people who lived in the Field of Abraham appears in the list. Heavily restored. About 38’ wide, Karnak Temple, Luxor, ca. 930 b.c.

This, in our opinion, can only be the much disputed Field of Abraham (fig. 15) (p ḥqr brm) mentioned fourteen hundred years earlier in the great Karnak inscription of Sheshonq I.[264] It was occupied by his army on their way to Jerusalem during his expedition in Palestine. The baffling question was why it should be called the field. This is clear from the use of the same term in various languages to designate the site of a major ceremonial center as the scene of contests and games. Thus we have the maidan of the Persians and Arabs, and the Field of Honor or the Field of the Cloth of Gold in Europe.[265]

The Field of Burnings

The oldest known surviving synagogue is that of Dura Europos.[266] It was called anciently the Field of the Burning (πδἱον ἐμπρήσως, pedion emprēseōs), the place of Nebuchadnezzar’s fiery furnace (Daniel 3:6, 11). This was where the universal compulsory gathering of all “people, nations and languages” bowed down to the image of the king (Daniel 3:3–5). As in the case of Abraham, the fire killed those who tended it (Daniel 3:22), and all the people and the king looking into the fire saw the three young friends of Daniel unharmed, pleasantly talking with a fourth person, “like the Son of God” (Daniel 3:25), whom Nebuchadnezzar identified as an angel sent from God (Daniel 3:28). We seem to be on familiar ground. The three young men were sacrificed just as the three virgins were for refusing to participate in idolatrous rites. In other words, we are dealing with a typical national assembly at the New Year. What is worth noting is that in the case of Abraham it is not pure mythology; the places and names are specific and real, and the celebration is clearly recorded. It all goes back to actual history.
