6

What Is a Hypocephalus?

Definition and Description

The short paragraph on the hypocephalus in the Lexikon der Ägyptologie tells us only that the hypocephalus is “a round disk, usually of cartonnage or papyrus . . . [used] as an amulet placed under the head of the mummy ‘to experience the flame of Re,’ . . . often containing passages from the 162nd chapter of the Book of the Dead. . . . In addition there are representations of various gods, animals, and forms of the sun-god. . . . They appear starting in the Twenty-fifth Dynasty . . . especially in Thebes and Achmim.” [1] A final note of interest: “Curiously enough, a hypocephalus is also found among the three holy books of the Mormons.” [2] The disk was usually four to seven inches in diameter, made of wood, linen, gold foil, bronze, copper, clay, stone, cartonnage, faience, leather, papyrus, and even bread dough.

The physical composition of hypocephali Edith Varga finds to be “without any profound religious significance.” [3] These discs have been the subject of intense but brief fits of interest among Egyptologists; the first to come to public notice was the Joseph Smith hypocephalus in 1835, published with an interpretation in the Times and Seasons, March 1842. It attracted prolonged public attention, in both Kirtland and Nauvoo, and has been occasionally mentioned in discussions of hypocephali.

Discoveries and Studies

In the first major study of a hypocephalus in 1855, Samuel Birch noted that “the Mormon Joseph Smith . . . has engraved another of these hypocephali, which, in the arrangement of its subjects and the figures represented, is like [British Museum] No. 8445. The inscription is so badly engraved that it is not possible to make out its meaning, and Smith’s interpretation throws no light on it.” [4]

While BM 8445 is similar in the arrangement of its subjects and figures, its text is not. However, the text of BM 35875 (formerly 8445c), which is in the same collection, is very similar to the text of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. So over 150 years ago there were published hypocephali that were like the Joseph Smith hypocephalus and could have been used to evaluate and translate it, but were ignored. Half a century later, Arthur E. Weigall made the same lame excuse for not reading what is on any of the hypocephali: The “inscriptions . . . are hopelessly confused; many of the groups of signs bearing but a faint resemblance, if any at all, to known words. Although there are some thirty specimens in the various museums, a comparison of these with the present ones does not help much in their decipherment; and it would therefore be very undesirable to offer even a conditional translation.” [5]

Anyone comparing the Joseph Smith hypocephalus with the others will note immediately not only that they are mostly very much alike but that the Joseph Smith one is no more badly engraved than most hypocephali; if one could read them, the Joseph Smith hypocephalus should be no problem, and for all these years the experts to a man have been without excuse. Birch even refers the reader to chapter 71 in the Book of the Dead, which has illustrations similar to the hypocephali.[6] This immediately alerts us to the main purpose of the hypocephalus, which is to deliver the owner from death and oblivion; it is the common yearning of the human race. Book of the Dead chapter 71 reads “O Horus, son of Isis, make me hale just as you made yourself hale. Release him, loose him, put him on earth, cause him to be loved: so says the one-faced one. . . . May you destine me to life annually; may you grant to me many years of life over and above my years of life; many months over and above my months of life; many days over and above my days of life; many nights over and above my nights of life, until I depart. May I rise to be a likeness of myself.” [7] He wants to be both “safe above with Re and splendidly buried with Osiris.” [8] This should be ample evidence that the hypocephalus has a real function of transcendent importance to perform.

After Théodule Devéria’s criticism of Joseph Smith in 1860[9] and Philippe J. de Horrack’s brief note in 1862,[10] it was nineteen years until the next study of hypocephali.[11] In 1883 at the Sixth International Congress of Orientalists meeting in Leiden, Egyptologists decided to share their knowledge of this mysterious object. Conrad Leemans, the Dutch scholar, reported that twenty-one hypocephali were known, “to which we would add a 22nd published by the Mormon Joseph Smith.” [12] Some definitive studies were done by Birch, Leemans, and E. A. Wallis Budge, but after Leeman’s thorough study of the Leiden hypocephalus and Budge’s interpretation of the beautiful example in the Henry Meux collection,[13] interest vanished, and from 1883 to 1944 no serious study was undertaken.

Though de Horrack had declared in 1862 that the hypocephalus proclaims the sublime belief of the Egyptians in the fundamental doctrine of the immortality of the soul and saw in it nothing less than “le fond et le substance de la doctrine égyptienne,” resting on the fundamental belief in “un Dieu unique,” [14] the subject enjoyed almost total neglect thereafter. Only a brief argument between Louis Speleers and Jean Capart in 1942–44 disturbed the silence.[15] And just six new hypocephali were published in the sixty years between 1900 and the appearance of Varga’s study in 1961. Since then she has been able to examine some eighty-nine hypocephali.[16] About one hundred hypocephali are accessible today, most of them in nine museums (Cairo, British Museum, Paris, Turin, Berlin, Boston Fine Arts, University of Pennsylvania, Hermitage, Zagreb).

High Society

For all its poor showing, today the claims of the hypocephalus on our respect are not unimpressive. Varga has found that their production was limited to priests and priestesses of Min and Amun-Re, a closed aristocratic group, “experts in the myths and abstract representations of the gods,” who made their own highly personalized hypocephali. Their production, as we learn from the inscriptions, was a deep secret passed on from father and mother to son and daughter; Varga has established one line of four generations.[17] The centers of production were Thebes and the Fayyum; only a few, especially those made of clay, come from the north, from Tell el-Yahudieh, the place of the Jews, near the Palestine border where the cemetery is filled with “completely Egyptianized Jews.” [18]

German archaeologists in 1970 and 1971, excavating in the cemeteries of the West Bank of the Nile near to the place where the Joseph Smith Papyri were found, discovered that the whole area from Deir el-Bahri to the Valley of the Kings had been one vast sacred complex, the scene of dazzling processions at the Great Festival of the Valley, when Amun would cross the river to visit his temples there, “a sort of extension of the Amon cult of Karnak.” The great “ceremonial center (Kernstück) of the necropolis of the old imperial capital of Thebes” —that is, the complex on the Western Bank—saw the great processions in which not only the kings and gods but “the relatives of the dead commoners marched along in the parade.” [19] So everyone gets into the act. At the very center of the action was the temple-tomb of one Ankh-Hor, who held the highest civil and ecclesiastical offices in Thebes, including tutor to the royal children.[20] German scholars were able to construct genealogical charts of the names of 166 individuals in the cemetery, thus joining members of the family. All date from the Twenty-sixth to the Thirtieth Dynasties.[21]

In this Ankh-Hor family was found a lady of very high degree, Wah-ib-Re, her head reposing on a hypocephalus. Though meticulously thorough in everything else, the excavators give this object the brief brush-off that a hypocephalus can expect: “At the head a hypocephalus of gold foil lay like a halo pasted to the floorboard.” [22]

From the same Theban cemetery came another surprise, long delayed because people failed to notice what was under their noses. In 1828 Giovanni Anastasi found in the area a gnostic sketch matching Facsimile 1 of the Book of Abraham, the companion piece to the hypocephalus. The supremely incompetent draftsman has shown us a man or a mummy supine on an altar with the priest of Anubis standing over him. A single line of text below startles us with the name of Abraham.[23] How could anyone have overlooked it?

General Aspect and Forms

Adolf Erman described the hypocephalus as a disk placed under the head of a mummy that was painted with peculiar (seltsamen) figures.[24] Yet the small space on the hypocephalus is limited to a number of stock themes. Budge concluded that “the texts and vignettes found on hypocephali are to all intents and purposes the same, though in some they are fuller than in others.” [25] Varga notes that, far from being random, the same figures recur in the same order on nearly all the hypocephali; in general, eight scenes are presented in panels which consistently follow the same arrangement (fig. 16).[26] For example, the Joseph Smith hypocephalus is almost identical with the Ws.t-wr.t hypocephalus in the Kunsthistorisches Museum of Vienna (see appendix 4) [27] and one belonging to Ḥr in the British Museum.[28]

Figure 16. The most complete hypocephali have eight panels.

			 				 If we arrange the hypocephali in order of their completeness, certain symbols remain while others fall away; what remains in the end is what is most vital and fundamental. First of all, it is round, the universal concept of completeness. Specifically, it represents what is generally agreed upon as the pupil of an eye, a theme deserving special attention. Almost all hypocephali are divided into three horizontal divisions, recognized by the figures in them as solar, lunar, and chthonian. Even on first inspection certain aspects of the hypocephalus suggest the obvious situation stated by de Horrack in 1862 and 1884 and never seriously challenged: The circle is divided “to represent the two celestial hemispheres. . . . The human cycle is thus continually compared to that of the solar star, the various phases being personified by divine types.” [29]

“The scenes portrayed on these disks relate, in all their details, to the resurrection and the renewed birth after death . . . symbolized by the course of the sun, the living image of divine generation. . . . All these different symbols represent on the one side the female, and on the other the male element, to express the idea of eternal generative power.” The lower part of the circle “represents the lower or dark hemisphere from whence the sun was supposed to have come forth to mark the beginning of time; . . . the mystical eye, called Uza, conveys the idea of the renewal of a period, like the full moon, the solstice, the equinoxes, etc., and it designates here the accomplishment of the period of resurrection, always assimilated with the daily and annual revival of the Sun.” [30]

The hypocephalus brings the cosmos down to earth, especially as Joseph Smith describes it. Alan H. Gardiner called attention to a hypocephalus-like disk containing a nome list from a late sarcophagus (fig. 17), now in the Metropolitan Museum, on which the nomes, or administrative districts, of Egypt occupy the rim of the disk; the solar hawk with outspread wings fills the upper and lower thirds of the circle, which are inverse to each other, as they should be on a globe rather than a disk, while the winged sun-disk occupies the center position. The cosmic nature of the chart is indicated by a line of well-drawn stars dividing the two top sections, directly over the heads of a line of fifteen figures.[31] As we have previously noted, there is a famous passage from the hermetic writing Asclepius which says, “Do you not know, Asclepius, that Egypt is an image of heaven or, to be more precise, that everything governed and moved in heaven came down to Egypt and was transferred there? If truth were told, our land is the temple of the whole world.” [32] Here we have that association of the hypocephalus and the temple which prevails throughout the study of Facsimile 2. But before proceeding we should note that a number of such cosmic charts are found in the ancient world—one thinks of the enormous star ceiling at Denderah and the great Hopi seasonal glyphs, but none is more challenging than the famous Aztec calendar stone, too closely resembling the Joseph Smith hypocephalus to be ignored, as it has been described by Zelia Nuttall.[33]

Figure 17. On the rim we see the nome standards of Upper and Lower Egypt. Sarcophagus of Wereshnefer, ca. 350 b.c. Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York.

The Calendar Stone

A hundred years ago Nuttall summed up the conclusions of scholars through three centuries of study of the great Mexican calendar stone (fig. 18). Naturally, she regards it as a product of evolution, “the high-water mark reached in the evolution of a set of ideas, which were suggested to primitive man by long-continued observation of the phenomena of Nature.” [34]

First she notes that it is both an astronomical and an earthly document; it is “an image of the nocturnal heaven as it is of a vast terrestrial state which . . . had been established as a reproduction upon earth of the harmonious order and fixed laws which apparently governed the heavens.” [35] At the same time, the stone “serves as a chart of the territory of the State, its capital and its four provinces.” [36] “These signs . . . designated . . . four chiefs and . . . 16 minor tribal groups or divisions of men.” [37] It is a map of the nation and a chart of the stars at the same time.[38] At once we recall the Egyptian cosmic geographic maps from the walls and ceilings of tombs and temples which show the nomes of the Delta, arranged in an order corresponding to the relative positions of the stars above them. As noted above, these great charts show similarities to a hypocephalus.

Figure 18. The famous calendar stone is actually a depiction of Aztec cosmology. It is made of olivine basalt, an extremely difficult stone to carve, and was brightly painted. The constellations carved around the border show its heavenly context, and the complete stone probably formed part of the altar complex for sun worship in the Aztec capital city, Tenochtitlan. 24.5 tons, 11’10” in diameter. Commissioned by Axayácatl, sixth Mexica ruler, ca. 1480 a.d. Museo Nacional de Antropología.

			 The Mexican stone is a calendar with stars as indicators, marking time and space together, typifying “light and the heaven itself with its two eyes; the sun and moon and darkness and the earth.” [39] They are joined in “a ‘complete count’ . . . expressive of a great era of time” with special emphasis on the four thirteen-year periods which gave the basic epoch of fifty-two years.[40] These were the sacred numbers of Aztecs and Mongols, representing the weeks of the year and the numbers of the tribes in each nation. It was also the official number of dancers in the old Greek spring drama, the Dionysia. The astral numbers 7, 12, and 50, 51, and 52 recur among “primitive” tribes everywhere,[41] and one can spot them on the surfaces of temples and tombs throughout the Mediterranean. The first families of Israel were represented by the 52 arrow-shafts 	(שנטים šbāṭîm) that were kept in the ark of the covenant, each representing a family or phratry of Israel.[42] The writer was never more impressed than when he counted 52 dancers emerging in the first Hopi dance of spring—their Dionysiac, at Hotevilla in 1948.

The face in the center of the calendar stone supplies “the motive power, . . . which emanated from the central luminary.” [43] It shows “the conception of a divine power who ruled heaven and earth from a changeless and fixed centre in the heaven.” [44] Compare with Doctrine and Covenants 88:12–13, “which light proceedeth forth from the presence of God to fill the immensity of space—The light which is in all things, which giveth life to all things, which is the law by which all things are governed, even the power of God who sitteth upon his throne, who is in the bosom of eternity, who is in the midst of all things.” It is also “the quadruple lord, ‘He who looks in four directions.’ ” [45] Recollect that the corresponding figure 1 in Facsimile 2 was called by the Egyptians, “He with four faces on one neck” (fig. 19; see appendix 4). A ray of light directly above him connects him with the top of the circle, corresponding to the zenith, solstice, or noon, which is figure 2 of Facsimile 2, while the face that supports it “conveys the idea of duality, being masked and double-faced,” again just as figure 2 of Facsimile 2 (see appendixes 4, 5).

Like the hypocephalus, the calendar stone is conspicuously divided into opposite parts. In the upper half, “between the symbols for air and fire, is the conventionalized ‘ray of the Sun,’ or pyramid which typifies ‘that which ascends or is above’ the upper elements and the Above. As its opposite we find below, situated between the symbols of earth and water, a ring with a concentric circle representing the drop of water = ‘that which descends.’ ” [46] Around the center are placed “symbols of the four elements, the union of which was believed by the native philosophers to be essential for the production and maintenance of life.” [47] This is exactly the function of the four canopic figures that comprise figure 6 of Facsimile 2.

			 Figure 19. At the entrance to his tomb, Ramses IX makes offerings to Amun-Re Harakhty, shown with four ram’s heads, each with two kinds of horns, as in fig. 28 (p. 262). The composite scepters are similar to those held in Fac. 2, fig. 1. About 9’ wide, restored. Valley of the Kings, ca. 1120 b.c.

			 The geometry of the stone is equally significant: “It is impossible not to realize that, in ancient Mexico, the pyramid constituted an image of the entire system.” [48] Here on the calendar stone is a circle of eight triangles, each showing the sacred angle of 36° found on the pentagram marking the great circle, suggesting the repeated image of the city of Heliopolis on the rim of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. The encompassing of all in the sacred geometry of the pyramid is equally evident in the dominance of the golden section in the pyramids of Egypt and significantly in the phi proportion which is unique to the Joseph Smith hypocephalus (see pp. 606–7, fig. 72).[49]

The great assembly of the New Year is not neglected. The design on the stone “determined once and for all time the exact position to be taken up in all public assemblages, in councils, sacred dances. . . . What is more: each division of the people . . . had its own season during which it led in ceremonial observances.” [50] We are also reminded of the careful arrangements by which dancers and onlookers were assigned positions in the Greek theater or the Hopi dance plaza. The same care is displayed in the Temple Scroll from the Dead Sea Scrolls, for the perfect temple of Zion to come, where spaces are set apart along the outer wall to accommodate the booths or tents of each of the leaders of the tribes of Israel.[51] The massive circle of the calendar stone “was conceived as looking down from above on the spectator” [52] and to be in rotation like the great zodiacal ceiling of the temple of Hathor at Denderah (see pp. 310–11, fig. 35).

The great complex would not be complete without displaying the order of the dispensations. Like the Hopi, these people believed that three dispensations of men on earth have already passed away, each time the race being “almost completely destroyed” [53] because of wickedness, and they believed they were “living in a fourth age which was doomed to perish by fire.” [54] Based on the native Zodiac, “an intimate relation was thus established and maintained between star-groups and human beings,” [55] as we find in Facsimile 2, according to Joseph Smith (Fac. 2, figs. 5–7).

Animals are also fully represented in the Zodiac, as in archaic astronomy everywhere. But nothing is more striking than the report of the ancient Ixtlilxochitl “that it was an ancient custom of his people on taking possession of new territory ‘to shoot with utmost force four arrows, in the direction of the four regions of the world.’ ” [56] From the earliest times it was the practice of Pharaoh, in taking possession of the land at his coronation, to shoot the four arrows in the four directions and release four birds. The Emperor Titus is said to have shot the four arrows from the top of the temple at Jerusalem to lay claim to world empire. He was following the example of Nimrod, who established the arrow rite and shot into the heavens to challenge the God of Abraham and claim for himself the dominion of all the earth.[57] As Nuttall sums it up, “engraved upon it were the Great Plan and its laws of organization and rotation. It was clearly determined . . . the relation of all classes of the population to each other and to the whole.” [58] The whole thing was “beyond the capacity of all but an initiated minority, consisting of the elders, chiefs, and priests” [59]—that is, it belonged to the mysteries. The same holds true of the hypocephalus and of the great astronomical ceiling of the temple of Hathor at Denderah. At this point we cannot resist calling attention to another composition closely resembling the one just described.

The Hypocephalus Eye

For Budge, the hypocephalus originally “represents the Eye of Horus . . . which superseded the gold figure of the cow.” [60] The central figure is Amun, described in a Hymn to Amun-Re as “Amun who art hidden in the pupil of thine Eye, Soul who dost shine forth from thine own Eye, wondrous God of transformations, Sublime One who cannot be known.” [61] As we have seen, it was for the Egyptians the creative Eye that gives form and hence existence to things: “I am Shu, the image of Re, seated in the interior of the Eye of his Father.” [62] The Eye creates and nourishes; “the Eye of Hor is usually the equivalent of food.” [63] The Egyptians called Egypt itself the Pupil of the Eye, Chemia, because it is black.[64] The idea was ancient; an inscription of Thuthmosis III reads, “the Upper Egyptian Heliopolis is his shining eye in this land.” [65] Significantly, it was not the reception of the royal insignia that bestowed his ba on the king but “the handing over of the Eye of Horus which filled the recipient anew with divine powers, including the Ba.” [66]

In the mysteries, including the Orphic mysteries, both πόθος (pothos), or yearning for that which is afar, and ἵμρος (himeros), yearning for what is near, “like Eros, are associated with the eye, which was popularly regarded as the channel through which the image or phantom (ἴδωλον, eidōlon) of the desired object entered to inspire love in the soul.” [67] In the Memphite theology, one of the oldest Egyptian creation dramas, the tongue (or word) and the heart (or mind) of the God Ptah are the source of creation (see pp. 68–69, fig. 6).[68] But the Eye itself has creative power: “My Eye meditated upon the divine tear which I shed on myself, and men and women came into being from it.” [69]

Placing the Hypocephalus

In a most important note on the placing of the hypocephalus, Emmanuel de Rougé points out that the purpose of the thing can be achieved in various ways. Thus when the mother vulture spreads her wings, “this life-giving action, directed towards the head, was, in effect, localized at the occiput, at the spot where the hypocephalus was supposed to conserve for the mummy the bes or source of the vital fire.” [70] As already noted, the Book of Breathings, which contains instructions for passing through the underworld as so many other funerary texts do, could also be placed under the head of the mummy, exactly like a hypocephalus.[71] The point here is that the Book of Breathings could do service for a hypocephalus, which contains the same instructions.

As the name indicates, the hypocephalus belongs under the head. The instructions in Book of the Dead chapter 162 are to place a figure of the cow-goddess Ihet around the neck of the deceased and to draw a picture of the same on new papyrus and place it under the head.[72] Protection is most needed behind one, as when Hathor or Isis and Nephthys stand behind the king, or when the red crown of Hadrian has written on the back of it, “All life and power are behind him as Re forever.” [73]

The classic crown of flowers worn in military or religious rites attests one who has triumphed over great dangers and offers protection hereafter; it was of Egyptian origin.[74] On festive occasions such crowns could be made of bread.[75] Moreover, the imperial crown est circulus orbis—represents the circle of the universe.[76]

The Function of the Hypocephalus

The official instructions for making a hypocephalus in the Book of the Dead chapter 162 tell us that it is “a spell for causing flame to be under the head of the blessed one.” [77] The rim inscriptions of some hypocephali contain that information. The word translated “flame” is bs, which the Wörterbuch defines as “flame, fire, etc.” It also is used for a ray or beam of light from the Moon.[78] It probably derives from the verb bsἰ, the basic meaning of which is to burst forth from confinement, an emanation, like water from a hole or plants from the ground.[79] Another verb bs means “to introduce,” [80] and its derived noun bs is a secret or mystery, and in the combination bs št, the mysteries.[81]

Why a fire? First, for protection, of course. Erman, with magisterial finality, has given a practical no-nonsense explanation that leaves religion out of the picture: “Under the head of the mummy was placed a round disk of linen, which was painted with peculiar figures; it gives the dead person warmth beneath his head, so that he would not get cold in his sleep—to this day it is the head which is that part of the body which the Egyptian most carefully wraps up at night.” [82] Then why is the thing so rarely used in graves, and why only by very special persons, and in a very limited geographical area? To fit his no-nonsense theory, he mentions only linen and only a pillow—the other things can’t be wrapped.

Varga considered the warming of the head to be only a secondary function introduced in the New Kingdom (Twentieth Dynasty) to strengthen the original object, which was protection.[83] Protection is often declared to be the express purpose of the flame.[84] But the two functions are inseparable: every sleeper is in a defenseless position and must have protection. The Pharaoh Amenemhet I, who was murdered in his sleep, could have used such protection.[85]

Fire has always been the natural protection against the creatures of the night. A number of funerary inscriptions make this very clear. Thus Coffin Text 49 explains: “Light a torch, you wardens of the chambers, you gods who are in the darkness, set a protection about your lord . . . until Horus comes from Heliopolis.” [86] That the heating is no later addition, as Varga supposes, is plain from the Middle Kingdom tomb of the eminent Pa-Heri: “May you go up from the tomb every morning and return every evening. May a torch be lit for you in the night until Shu (the sunlight) shines upon your breast, and it is said to you, come, come, from this house of yours to the living ones! May you look upon Re at the horizon of heaven and behold Amun shining. May you awake well in the sunlight and may every obstacle be removed for you throughout the land. May you travel forever with a joyful heart in praise of the god within you.” [87] The purpose of the light and of the hypocephalus which provides it could not be plainer—it is the now familiar theme of the joining of the spirit and body (Re and Osiris) after both have been preserved and prepared for the purpose. We can go back to the Pyramid Texts where the dead himself “protects the sun-god as Atum against his enemies by lighting a torch for him in the darkness of the underworld.” [88] In Book of the Dead 137B it is “kindling the flame for NN. . . . The bright eye of Horus comes . . . shining like Re on the horizon.” [89] That the cycle of resurrection and rebirth is represented is apparent from Cairo hypocephalus SR 10700, “O Great God, who rises from the primordial waters and illuminates the Two Lands on the day of his birth.” [90]

Jean Yoyotte describes the hypocephalus as “supplying warmth with a view to survival or to surround the head of the dead with a glorious awesome nimbus” —a flame to protect it.[91] But more fundamental still is the resurrection motif with the hypocephalus which conserves the bs or vital fire against the day when the body will again receive the ba or spirit.

Always the theme is to preserve the body against the day of resurrection, but more than that to keep it in touch with the spirit in heaven; if the tie between body and spirit is not broken, there is always hope for a reunion. On the monument of Sneferu at Dashur we read: “thou lamp of this Osiris . . . this Sśnw. Hail to thee, [thou Eye of] Horus which guides the gods in darkness. How restful is the beauty of this torch to Osiris . . . as thy father Geb and thy mother Nout give it to thee. . . . May the lamp of Sśnw be firm as the name of Atum is firm in Heliopolis.” [92] Thus even in the Old Kingdom we see the need for the flame of the hypocephalus or its equivalent.

Sweet Dreams: The Hypocephalus as a Pillow

Varga traces the origin of the hypocephalus to a pillow.[93] Long ago Birch found two related pillow amulets, one of which (Papyrus BM 9900) “was [possibly] disposed at the nape of the neck of the mummy.” The other is called “the chapter of the head . . . [and is] found on some mummies written in the outer cartonage on the fillet or bandlet which is represented binding the hair of the dead.” This leads him to conclude that “the amulet of the pillow helped to complete the sepulchral equipment along with the hypocephalus or mystical pillow which represented the pupil of the solar Eye, and restored the vital spark or warmth to the mummy.” [94] In the coffin of the estate manager, Waḥ, found in a Theban tomb of the late Eleventh Dynasty, “under the head were set a wooden headrest and a small disk of resin, or ‘hypocephalus biscuit.’ ” [95] The same loaves are found in the Eighteenth Dynasty tombs at Deir el-Medineh, and Varga calls them expressions of that religious thinking which later led to the hypocephali—all are related.[96] Here we have a close relative of the hypocephalus both in form and function. Indeed, the headrest or wrs under the head of every mummy would hardly invite pleasant rest without some sort of padding, since it was ordinarily made of wood, bone, terra-cotta, or iron. The wrs was designed on the pattern of the two pieces of the horizon and made contact with heaven . The owner of the famous Florentine hypocephalus had a “pillow, to rest the head on, very small, semi-circular, of hematite,” and to complete the picture, it was found on the breast of the owner.[97]

The bread is an anomaly. It was the custom in the Old Kingdom for the last person to leave the tomb to throw a loaf of bread behind him.[98] A clue to the oddity of the bread was suggested by de Rougé when he noted that in Book of the Dead 151, “the vital life principle was conceived of as a sort of leaven; it is constantly expressed by the idea of a fire, which stimulates life in the natural animal. It is said, for example, of the mother of the Sun, at the moment of the birth of this god, ‘his protection is bound upon his head as a flame.’ ” [99] This corresponds, de Rougé noted, to the idea of the hypocephalus producing “heat, inflammation or fermentation”; the word for causing a flame in both the Book of the Dead 161 and 162, bs, gives the Coptic , Latin intumescere, “which the Egyptian also used to describe the turgescence and inflammation of the abdomen and the uterus.” [100] At the same time the pitch or resin which goes along with the bread has a warm feeling of its own which gives the classic test for amber, while the fermentation of dough produces not only heat within but a spectacular rising. Thus whatever goes under the head of the deceased is designed both to heat it and to raise it up. Here one thinks of the well-known Osiris beds where the grain actually rises up spontaneously out of a mattress in the shape of Osiris—his body is the bread, but it is made possible by the visit of Re—that is, by the heat of the sun.[101] The alchemists, besides employing yeast for its heating and swelling qualities, packed the sacred chemical mix of the iksir (the original elixir) in organic matter such as horse manure or dove droppings to preserve it at an even temperature or to warm it in the winter.[102]

Origin?

W. M. Flinders Petrie in 1920–21 published a corpus of slate palettes with drawings of 164 such objects.[103] They obviously belong together and have a common meaning, for whatever animal each one represents, whether a ram, a hawk, or a fish, is always made as round as possible, and all have holes for suspending around the neck as pectorals. George F. Legge categorized seven different types of archaic palettes.[104] Grouped and classed together in the same cases in the Cairo Museum that display these slate palettes are perfectly blank slate or stone disks (e.g., No. 44172), along with equally blank blue faience disks (10693), placed beside a copper disk of the same size and shape and covered with a disconnected jumble of symbols (10690); next, a disk of stone with just the nub of a fish’s tail and a small hole for suspension, after that some slightly more fishlike circles with eyes and fins but still as round as flounders (31339). The next one is more elongated and seems to have a fish’s mouth but no tail, while on the next the lips have become the head of a hawk. This is followed by a smaller slate (33137) with the horn of an oryx at the top, while the one beside it has a hawk’s head and a hole for suspension. Finally there is a broad pectoral with the same incongruous little hawk’s heads added at one end, and in the same case a small “palette” crowned by a ram’s horn (35137). Slate disks lead to the blue faience disk by an imperceptible gradation (see color plate 1). Varga sees them as predecessors of the hypocephali.[105] On the faience disks are lotuses and fishes, which are symbolic of regeneration and have the same significance as the Hathor-Cow, the dominant figure on the hypocephali.

Resembling the fishes are the round turtle slates from Nagada I; they are sometimes divided into perfect quarters. A common figure in world religions is the cosmic turtle—the Hopis cannot dance without him as a rattle fastened to their legs. Petrie, Scharff, and Allen all held that the round Schminktafeln were variations of the cosmic turtle.[106] Egyptologists long failed to see the significance of the turtle for the Egyptians. Today it would seem that its role is cosmic. The turtle appears first in Egypt as an identified constellation, then at the head of the list of planets and later as first of the decans (see p. 245, fig. 24).[107] The turtle appears among the guardians of the coffin, bearing the unsavory title “Eater of Filth,” for he spends his time feeding head down in the depths of the river sludge—cold, dark, dull, and dirty—the antithesis to everything we would regard as pleasant and life-giving.[108] We are expressly told in the texts that his presence is to relieve the dead Osiris of having to do the unpleasant things he does.[109] Compare the office of the ushabti figures placed in tombs to do any labor the deceased was required to perform. In the Eighteenth Dynasty his place is “with the powers of darkness which are perpetually in conflict with the sun-god.” [110] He is overthrown along with Apophis; [111] in a festival at Heliopolis he is prevented from blocking the flow of the Nile.[112] In reliefs from the great temples Horus is shown spearing the turtle as a sacrifice before Re-Harakhty, who says to the king, “I have made a shield of bronze behind (or surrounding) thy majesty.” [113] Baudouin Van de Walle suggests that the idea of the shield came from the use of tortoise-shell shields,[114] but one of the salient characteristics of the hypocephalus is that in form as well as function it is a protective shield.

Another tie with the hypocephalus is Book of the Dead 161, closely related to Book of the Dead 162, the handbook to the hypocephalus. Book of the Dead 161 begins with a strange and enigmatic invocation: “May Re live and the turtle die!” which cry is repeated four times in each of the four directions and addressed to each of the four winds; a cosmic connection is clearly implied.

The Palettes

The earliest Egyptian invitation to the dance is the message of the predynastic palettes from Hierakonpolis. These “plaques of green schist . . . served as palettes for grinding malachite for green eye-paint.” [115] These palettes were “formed in the shape of animals that later appear as symbols of regeneration: fish, turtle, gazelle, and hippopotamus,” and the cosmetics mixed on them “were intended to return youthful liveliness to the dead.” [116]

The archaic palettes are covered with figures representing lively scenes, and some contain motifs similar to contemporary Mesopotamian artistic conventions. In both cultures, palettes and animal standards are divided into two parts, showing the animals in two situations; the most famous of palettes, that of Narmer (of green schist), presents on one side the animals at peace and at play, partying with music and the dance. On the other side we see the same animals preying on each other in a general free-for-all. On one side of the same palette we see a predynastic king slaying and enslaving his enemies, while on the other the world is at peace, with the monsters of destruction and chaos “leashed in like hounds” by order of the king. The same motifs appear in other famous standards of Ur and Uruk, [117] along with the first emergence of written text in both lands celebrating the great dramatic festivals of the New Year, the one side depicting the world in upheaval with battle or marching scenes, while the other shows the human race enjoying itself at banquets and festivals. Siegfried Schott found this contrast of war and peace to be “an allegorizing on the way of the world, . . . a carefully thought-out attempt to explain the world as an interplay (Wechselspiel) of joy and strife.” [118]

These are the games of the year-rite, whether feast or contest, always celebrated with a song and a dance; and for the dance always the drums and the painted figures. The round depression in the palette is to hold the ceremonial paint, for the palette really is a palette. Schott finds traces of the paint for the pupil of the Eye of Horus in use as early as Nagada II.[119]

It would seem that from the very beginning to the very end the hypocephalus is always just over the horizon; we are always faced with something very much like it, performing the same functions and resembling it in size and shape. It cannot be overlooked that the basic theme of the hypocephalus, the division into two opposing parts, as well as its role in the cycle of earth and creation which binds it firmly to the cosmos, is the perennial mood and motif of these remarkable artifacts. But more than that, we learn from a Demotic version of the Memphite theology that the palettes were meant to contain “what is worthy of being recorded of events of the years,” thus putting the whole thing in the familiar setting of the year-rites.[120]

Since the animal standards and palettes alike delight in scenes in which humans and animals exchange places, it is tempting to trace the later bestiaries from Alexandria and Aesop or the earlier Re and the Sun’s Eye (the Story of the Ape and the Cat) to the bestiaries of the Middle Ages and the famous one of Strasbourg which shows Abraham sacrificing Isaac preceded by the Phoenix sacrificing himself in Heliopolis in his resurrection rite.[121]

When Is Something a Hypocephalus?

The finding of objects very closely resembling the hypocephalus from prehistoric palettes to Christian halos makes the problem of origin and dating problematical. Hypocephali proper date from the late Ptolemaic to early Roman period. However, chapter 162 of the Book of the Dead that gives directions for making a hypocephalus first appears in the Twenty-first Dynasty (1070–940 b.c.). Yet silver platters from Praeneste and Cyprus dated to the eighth and seventh centuries b.c. have scenes similar to those on hypocephali, along with mock Egyptian hieroglyphic inscriptions, while also recalling scenes as early as the Palette of Narmer.[122] All four plates represent the same idea of the king as world conqueror endowed with power by the ankh symbol; whether represented as a plant, a tree, a fan, or a mirror, all have the same significance.[123]

In a Glass Darkly

The “hypocephalus biscuit,” which we found in the Middle Kingdom, was sometimes accompanied by a copper mirror before the face of the deceased.[124] Rather than a mere object of everyday use, the mirror was connected with “the ongoing life of the deceased . . . and . . . [was] placed in the mummy-wrappings.” [125] In a recent study Constance Husson notes that “in all epochs [of Egyptian history] there are disks without handles, . . . and these objects, substitutes for mirrors, thus replace, without a doubt, the hypocephalus which was designed to procure for the deceased the light which he was deprived of.” [126] The mirror like the eye supplies “a certain illumination, a divine reflection.” [127] It is Re sending his restorative rays into the depths. Likewise, it is the moon, for the Egyptians considered the sun and the moon to be the two eyes of the great god of the sky, Horus.[128] One mirror bears the inscription, “Opened is the face of [NN], justified, that he may see the Lord of the Horizon when he crosses the sky, that he may become a Spirit among the Spirits, the Indestructible Stars.” [129] The function of the mirror was to reflect the sunlight as the moon does; the Egyptian awareness of the parallel is seen in the image of the moon engraved on the backs of many mirrors.[130]

The Egyptian word for mirror is ankh, which is also the word for “life.” [131] “The bronze mirror portrays the image of the rejuvenated countenance [of the deceased] and with its name ankh [life] hints at renewed life.” [132] If the palette held the life-giving cosmetic ointment, the mirror helped in applying it.[133] An El-Bersheh inscription goes beyond earthly vanity: “divine life,” “divine,” “living,” and “belonging to the eternities,” etc.[134]

There are examples of mirrors on which are drawn a wedjat-eye, the complete or perfect eye (see p. 273, fig. 29), of which the hypocephalus is an example.[135] A copper hypocephalus in the Cairo Museum has a huge wedjat-eye held by the god Thoth.[136]

Hypocephalus and Drum

As from the Old Kingdom the mirror figures in the rites of the dance, so another indispensable item of celebration is the drum. Nothing is more essential to the dance than the drum or tambourine beating out the rhythm of the universe, struck to awaken all life on the earth.[137] Not surprisingly, “the round tambourine occurs in the mammisi, the birthplace of the child-god, . . . associated with the idea of birth,” and all the figures found on tambourines evoke “the idea of creating life and giving birth.” [138] When the tambourine is struck in the rites of the Stundenwache (Book of Hours), “Heaven and Earth unite! Rejoice, Heaven and Earth! is sung four times. . . . Heaven and Earth rejoice and jubilate”; the book concludes by announcing that “the purified House awaits thee; . . . the seat of Osiris, the first of the Westerners, has been purified by Re; . . . the followers of Re see their god in thee!” [139] Thus the drum, like the hypocephalus, provides the vital tie between the individual and his ultimate heavenly home, binding together heaven, earth, and resurrection, reflecting the creation as Re unites with Osiris.

The Egyptian ceremonial tambourines—small, round, green, and covered with cosmic designs—are close to the hypocephalus in function as well as in form. The Egyptian tbn[140] or sh.t[141] is a drum or tambourine whose size is “approximately that of a human head.” [142]

The “Bilura” Principle

Though presenting the same fundamental teachings through the same standardized symbols as thousands of other Egyptian documents, the hypocephalus excels in performing the office of maintaining contact at a distance, without which the resurrection would be in serious jeopardy. For its special purpose is to assure a permanent and unbroken tie between the body below and the spirit above. The familiar formula “Thy ba is in heaven, thy corpse in the lower world” presents us with the great gap—how can we be assured that the ba, an independent living entity, can be trusted to return to the body? The situation is a dangerous one, and it is precisely the hypocephalus that “provides . . . for Re a powerful protection when he goes below” (mk.t ʿ.t wr sp-2 ἰr.t.n h.t n s=s Rʿ m ḥtp=f).[143]

Plutarch explains that the Egyptians believed that after men died “their souls shine as stars in the heavens.” [144] It was a Mandaean teaching that “every star has a Bilura (a slender chain or cord) which connects the individual’s soul with the Sun and its cosmic movement.” [145] This suggests the Jewish idea that “every soul below has a Doppelseele (Dibbuk) up above, which has the power to speak for it, but is only a projection (copy, Abbild).” [146] An idea common to the ancient world is that the temple, a scale model of the universe, is a projection down to earth from the heavenly temple above. The principle applies likewise when bonds of communication are established between the great shrines on earth relayed from shrine to shrine as described in one of the hymns to Amun.[147] A scene from the shrines of Tutankhamun shows a number of celestial globes connected to each other with fine drawn-out streams of fire; certain figures “receive a flow of fire from a star placed above the head of the figure in front [fig. 20]. The four stars to the right are bound together by a flow of fire. . . . Before the face of each figure is a disk with three lines, representing fire, which enters the brows of the figures. Under each disk is a pair of walking legs, and a star.” [148] “The constant repetition and periodicity” of the entering of Re into the body of Osiris finds representation by “four Osiris figures with solar disk between them . . . which connect the individual sun disks over the heads of the Osiris-figures to each other” to establish a bond to the visible sun in heaven and its daily course and “its nightly stay with Osiris.” [149] An early Jewish work on the creation, the Berayta, speaks of the “chain of earths and heavens.” [150] This gravity-like mysterious tie applies to every aspect of the two worlds: “There is not a single herb,” says the Midrash, “which is not subject to the influence of a planet in heaven, which smites it and says to it, ‘Grow!’ ” [151] This is the well-known Arabic astrological principle of the Tali’, the star that rises at the moment of one’s birth and conditions his horoscope through life. When Abraham approached the altar on which Adam, Cain, Abel, and Enoch had all sacrificed, “The Majesty of God abode over the mountain . . . (and) he saw a pillar of fire which reached from earth up to heaven.” [152] The Prophet Joseph describes such phenomena in relationship to the first vision and the visits of Moroni (Joseph Smith—History 1:16, 43). More suggestive of the hypocephalus pillow are the stones Jacob used for pillows when he saw in a dream a ladder reaching up to heaven on which angels ascend and descend (Genesis 28:11–18).

Figure 20. These elegant gold-leafed bas-reliefs on the shrine of Tutankhamun show the mysterious transmission of starlight and sunlight to the foreheads of the Osiris figures. As with the hypocephalus, they bring heat and light to their heads. Shrine II, top of exterior right panel, ca. 1330 b.c. Copyright Institut français d’archéologie orientale.

Note: Lacking the original, we have no evidence that the Joseph Smith hypocephalus was actually placed under the head of its owner. Though the hypocephalus was curiously examined by hundreds of observers to the Nauvoo House, no one ever mentions what would have been its most striking feature, its platter-like composition. Apparently it was drawn on a papyrus just like the other sketches that go with it. There is significantly no mention on the Joseph Smith hypocephalus of providing heat under the head. It would seem to be first and last a didactic astronomical chart, which is how Joseph Smith treats it.

The User’s Manual

With the Re/Osiris formula firmly in mind, we proceed to move in on the hypocephalus, and to our great good fortune, we find awaiting us the key to the whole thing. For as the Lexikon der Ägyptologie tells us, the hypocephalus “often includes text from the 162nd chapter of the Book of the Dead,” [153] which in turn contains instructions for the making and use of the thing. This has been recognized since the days of Champollion, but alas, both the earliest and latest full texts of the Book of the Dead, those of Edouard Naville and Erik Hornung respectively, have banned this particular chapter from the book as being both too late in origin and alien in nature to be included in the original text.[154] Yet the text is found as early as the Twenty-first Dynasty (950 b.c.), in Papyrus Berlin 3031,[155] though, according to Hornung, it was only “connected to the hypocephalus in the Late Period.” [156] If that is so, how does it happen that Berlin 3031, a full thousand years older than the Joseph Smith copy, contains the same texts as the latter and provides the same instructions for making and using a hypocephalus as the much later texts do?

Yoyotte was surprised to rediscover the connection of chapter 162 with chapters 160 and 166, the neo-Egyptian chapters, in the temple of Amun at Tanis built in imitation of the Great Temple at Karnak. This chapter “was sometimes incorporated in the Second Book of Breathings.” [157] A Book of Breathings, as we have noted, was found along with a hypocephalus among the Joseph Smith Papyri. Moreover, the Book of Breathings, like a hypocephalus, was sometimes “placed directly under the head of the dead, . . . thus performing a function similar to the famous ‘hypocephali.’ ” [158]

The brethren at Kirtland tried to work out an Egyptian grammar by assuming that this papyrus was the original text of the Book of Abraham. They tried to match it up with the English text, but of course it did not work and they promptly dropped the project.[159] The hue and cry of the scholars after 1967 was that the Book of Breathings, Joseph Smith Papyrus VI, was not the Book of Abraham. But no such official claim was ever made for it. This was considered by the Mormons as the most valuable of the surviving Joseph Smith Papyri. It was worth far more than any of the critics realized; the Book of Breathings, the hypocephalus, chapter 162 of the Book of the Dead, Papyrus Berlin 3031, and Facsimiles 1, 2, and 3 are all part of the same story.[160]

The Ammonian formulas in these sections of the Book of the Dead show close ties between the “pontiff-kings of Tanis and the priests of Thebes,” and extracts from the Book of the Dead chapter 162 are found on statues from the time of Lehi’s contemporary, Psammeticus II (Twenty-sixth Dynasty).[161] All this places the content of the Joseph Smith Papyri, including the hypocephalus, which is an “Ammonian” document, much earlier than the Ptolemaic date assigned to those writings.

The Hypocephalus Handbook

Faced with a number of related documents, all connected with the hypocephalus, we attack first the universally recognized text which may well be called a guide, manual, or user’s handbook to the hypocephalus. Even more damaging to the authority of this all-important chapter than its omission from standard texts has been the practice by scholars of referring only to the last third of the text as relevant to the subject of hypocephali. Even the enthusiastic de Horrack dismisses all the rest of the chapter as having no bearing on the hypocephalus.[162] One might well say that the stone which the builders rejected was the keystone of the structure, for if we consider the whole chapter it at once becomes apparent that what we have here is the all-important text that explains the hypocephalus in terms of the Re/Osiris formula, the resurrection drama.

Chapter 162 opens with an invocation to Re whose aid is implored by one who is in dire straits.[163] The title heading calls this an utterance (r , the picture of a mouth) something to be recited by way of ordinance. Actually, the whole thing is a prayer “for producing a flame under the head of the Glorified One” (the owner).

Col. 1. Words to be recited: Greetings to you, mighty Par,[164] who wears the lofty double plumes and the White Crown [of Upper Egypt], and who holds the flail [of royal authority]. You have procreative power [165] and consistently rise and shine forth.

Col. 2. There is no end to your rising.[166] You are a master of forms who has a multitude of appearances (literally “skins” ), who hides himself from his children[167] in (the form of) the wedjat-eye.[168] You are the loud roarer [169] in the council of the gods.[170]

Comment: Figure 1 in the hypocephali is always identified with Amun, the Hidden One. The council of the gods is here referred to as the usual “Ennead,” but as Kurt Sethe noted, it has no reference whatever to the number nine, that being only something left over from the old Heliopolitan Ennead.” [171]

It is made clear throughout the document that the power, glory, and goodness, etc., of God are expressed in various figures. First of all the sun, which, as in the Solar Litanies, best expresses the most qualities. But with all the freedom of metaphor, one is never allowed to forget that behind it all is a god who is real. The solar imagery recalls to us the sun-god’s circuit to visit his various shrines as Apollo does in the Iliad 1:37–39.

Col. 3. a strong runner, swift of stride. You are a mighty god who comes to one that calls on him, and who protects the downtrodden from affliction. Come then to my call. I am Ihet.[172]

Comment: Our word succor comes from the Latin sub-curro, literally “to run under” with the extended meaning of “running to the aid of.” One recalls the words of the psalmist, “Our soul waiteth for the Lord: he is our help and our shield” (Psalm 33:20).

Col. 4. Your name [173] is in my mouth, and I will say it: Penhaqahagahar [174] is your name, Iuruiuyaqersainqrubaty[175] is your name,

Col. 5. Sapatmaisar [176] is your name. Kharsati [177] is your name. I have praised your name. I am Ihet. Hear my voice this day.

Comment: The proper names exchanged here belong to the symbols of the mysteries; “used as liturgical passwords,” [178] like the exchange of questions and responses, “they were probably spoken in an undertone both by the priests and the initiand, perhaps at the foot of the altar or at the door of the Holiest.” [179] These long non-Egyptian names include the leaf-lion-ram combination (see p. 303, fig. 33) found on many hypocephali, including the Joseph Smith. Though the Berlin 3031 text is hieratic, this combination is drawn out in hieroglyphic with great care; [180] the sheep in the Lepsius text is a magnificent ram. Thomas G. Allen takes these names in the Ryerson text as Hebrew and translates them as “Lion, He (Who) Rends, Embracer of the Great (Goddess) is thy name. Tail of the Lion is thy name; Graven Image is thy name.” [181] He identifies no less than nine Hebrew words among these names, rendering ḫrst as “work in wood or stone” (the root of the word is 	חךש ḥāraś to carve), and mἰw-srἰ as Chained Lion from Hebrew שךשךח (šāršĕrāh).[182] The suppliant, in grave danger, appeals to the god, identifying himself by secret names known only to the two of them, confirming his status as an initiated one with a claim to divine assistance. Following the exchange of secret names, the healing instructions are forthcoming.

Col. 6. You placed[183] a flame under Re’s head, and now he is in the divine afterlife in Heliopolis.[184] May you cause him to become like those upon the earth.[185] He is your soul. Do not forget him. Come to the Osiris, Efonakh, who is justified.

Comment: Here we see our Re/Osiris scenario in full swing. Note that the speaker identifies himself with both Osiris and Re who must come down into the netherworld so they can become one. Berlin 3031 specifies coming to the Gate in Heliopolis, which is both the place of passage between the worlds and meeting of men with the sun-god.

Col. 7. and cause a flame to be under his head. Truly he is the soul of the Great Body [186] which rests in Heliopolis. Akhukhaparsar is his name, Barkatatju is his name. Come indeed

Comment: Allen renders the first name ḫ-ḫpr-sr “The Essence of the Great One Who Shines” —that is, he has become an akhu.[187] The second name, Brktἰṯw, he reads as the Hebrew בךכןתצןח (bĕrākôt ṣiwwāh) “Blessings Hath He Commanded.” [188]

Col. 8. and cause him to become like one of your followers, for this one, he belongs to you. To be recited over a figure of Ihet which is made of fine gold and placed around the neck of the glorified being, and also put in writing

Comment: “This one, he belongs to you,” pwy ntf ntk could also be translated, “this one, he is you,” proclaiming their identity, for the dead is already considered an akhu.

Col. 9. upon new papyrus and placed under his head. [If this is done] there will be a multitude of flames all around him as it is with those upon the earth. This (i.e., the hypocephalus) is a very great protection that Ihet made for her son, Re, when he set.

Comment: The cow, the one indispensable dominant figure on the hypocephalus, here protects the spirit on its dangerous visit to the underworld. Re, in descending to the underworld, is running a great risk and needs the protection of Hathor, the ultimate guarantor of rebirth. Now the scene shifts to heaven.

Col. 10. His throne shall be encircled by a zealous (?)[189] army. He shall be made divine in the necropolis, and he shall not be turned back from any door of the hereafter successfully.

Comment: The dead person is surrounded by a bodyguard to protect him. He will be transformed into a divine being in the afterlife and will be able to pass through any doors or gates that might bar his way. The writing ends with the petition:

Col. 11. Then you shall say, after you have placed this goddess around the neck of the glorified being (akh), “O most hidden one who is in heaven, watch over the body of your son, and heal him in the necropolis.”

Comment: This prayer is found with slight variations on the circular borders of many hypocephali. It is in the necropolis where the healing must necessarily take place since that is where the corpse is. Our text ends with an admonition to great secrecy which may explain in part the rarity of the hypocephali, limited as they are to a few families and locations, with only a few more than a hundred preserved.

Col. 12. This is a great and secret book. Do not allow anyone’s eyes to see it, for that would be an abomination. He who knows it (i.e., the book) and keeps it secret, he will continue to exist. The name of this book is:

Col. 13. Mistress of the Secret Temple. The End.

Comment: “He will continue to exist,” ḫpr sw. The dead person is guaranteed continued existence or, as we would say, salvation.

With the dramatic situation clearly indicated we turn to what are perhaps the two most elaborate and well-executed of all hypocephali to match the situation presented in Book of the Dead 162. These are the Leiden and the Meux, which supply the best clue to the whole family of hypocephali. Our hypocephalus begins to take on some significance.

The Message of the Hypocephali: The Leiden Hypocephalus

The Leiden hypocephalus is perhaps the most complete and carefully studied of hypocephali to date (see appendix 5).[190] We start with the rim of the inscription, by far the most conspicuous and always considered to be the key to the rest. This writing regularly begins at the top of the circle, right over the head of the striding figure and reads from right to left or counterclockwise.

“O Debabty in the House of the Benben most high and most glorious (ḫ), procreating bull, god mighty and great in the house of the Great Old One in Heliopolis! Come to the Osiris, the priestess of Re, Tἰ-ἰr-.t, justified, begotten by the Divine Father (or the Father of the God, Re, i.e., Amun); born to the lady of the house ʾIs.t-wr.t Justified! Grant that she become as one of your followers. She is that god who is in the temple (house) of the Great Old One in Heliopolis.”

Comment: Debabty is the one “most high” on the Djeba, floating in space, negotiating the gap between the worlds. The benben-stone is where the phoenix sits to be resurrected; he is the bird who flies from Arabia to Heliopolis every five hundred years to expire in a sacrificial fire and arise renewed from the flames. The House of the Great Old One is rendered also as House of the Prince, or House of the Great Old Man. I prefer Ancient of Days. He too is always at Heliopolis.

The one thing you can be sure of in this text is that it is a prayer. Its significance is confirmed by the inscriptions on the two panels on the right and left of the central figure, the four-headed ram. The panel to the right reads: “O noble ba that rises in the sky, that illuminates the earth on the day of her birth! Cause the ba to live, the divine corpse to flourish, and the mummy to be rejuvenated, which belong to the Osiris Tiat Justified!” It is all straight resurrection.

And the panel on the left: “O Noble God who gives life by air, who walks on the waters, grant that Re come to hear his word. Come to the Osiris Tiat Justified; deliver her from everything.”

This is our Re/Osiris formula, where Re, coming to the aid of Osiris, thereby renews himself as well. But note the broadening of the scene! Shining in the heavens; earth, air, and water—the thing is becoming cosmic.

The Brussels Hypocephalus E 6319[191]

This hypocephalus allows even greater expanse of action (see appendix 6). On the rim we read: “I am a noble glorified being (ḫ) who has gone down into the underworld to provide the (necessary) things for his body. May you grant heaven to the soul (ba) and a secret place for the mummy. I am one who has come forth from the wedjat-eye. I am the Hidden One in the secret place. I am an excellent (ἰqr) glorified being (ḫ) among the followers of Re. I am one who enters and goes forth among the excellent ones. I am the great soul (ba) whose image shines. I am one who comes forth from the underworld whenever he pleases. I have come and gone forth in the wedjat-eye. I have come forth from the underworld with Re, from the House of the Magistrate” [192] in Heliopolis.

Here we have the same now-familiar Re/Osiris theme, but while it is Osiris who is praying for deliverance in the Leiden hypocephalus, in the Brussels hypocephalus the speaker is a messenger from Re coming to the rescue; the former is a prayer, the latter the answer to the prayer. The right-hand panel has some difficult parts: “O Ram/Soul [193] who begets forms, who hides his bodily form from his children, then gathers storm clouds (?) [194] that become the two wedjat-eyes, the soul and his bodily form. It is Mut who provides counsel. May he cause him to fear [195] his enemies. Come to Shainen, justified, in the underworld of the gods. Let no one be turned back forever and ever.”

On the left: “O Ram/Soul [196] of great majesty, inspiring fear, greatly dreaded, who causes the fire of the two excellent wedjat-eyes, which bring about the forms of existence. It is Mut who gives his body and conceals his body in life. It is his image that becomes the likeness of a great and terrible lion who provides for forms. Grant that the Osiris Shainen, justified, go out and come in forever.”

The flame of the wedjat-eyes reminds us that the hypocephalus itself which produces the vital fire is the wedjat-eye. One bronze hypocephalus has a giant wedjat-eye in its center. [197]

From these two hypocephali above the situation is clear. The Leiden rim is a prayer to and for a heavenly deliverer, and the rim of the Brussels hypocephalus is the answer to that prayer. Of the some one hundred copies of hypocephali examined by this writer, fifty are illegible and fifty-three have inscriptions that can be read in part or whole. By an interesting coincidence, forty-eight of the texts fall evenly between those containing the prayer and those with the answer—there are twenty-four of each.

The Joseph Smith Hypocephalus

The Joseph Smith hypocephalus covers the same ground. It belongs to that class which contains the answer to the prayer and reads around the rim: “I am the Provider [198] in the sun temple [199] in Heliopolis. [I am] most exalted and very glorious. [I am] a virile bull without equal. [I am] that Mighty God[200] in the Sun Temple in Heliopolis.” Then comes the damaged part of the rim which has been replaced with hieratic characters, which probably contained something like “ May the Osiris Shishaq live forever,” and then the conclusion, “with that Mighty God in Heliopolis.” [201]

The figures numbered 8 to 11 are numbered in reverse and read “O God of the Sleeping Ones [202] from the time of the creation.[203] O Mighty God, Lord of heaven and earth, of the hereafter, and of his great waters,[204] may the soul of the Osiris[205] Shishaq[206] be granted life.” Figures 17 and 16: “May this tomb never be desecrated,[207] and may this soul and its lord never be desecrated in the hereafter.” Figures 19–21: “You shall ever be as that god, the Busirian.” [208] In figure 2, “The name of this Mighty God.”

This is meager fare indeed, and it is fortunate that we have other texts to support it. But there is a much fuller exposition of the situation in documents which conspicuously involve Abraham.

Sp.t-mἰ-srἰw. sp.t, “lotus blossom”; mἰ, “lion”; and srἰw, “ram.” This name is found behind the Four Sons of Horus on the Joseph Smith hypocephalus and seems to refer to the three aspects of the sun: rising sun, midday sun, and setting sun—that is, Re, Khepri, and Atum. A similar phrase is found in one of the demotic magical papyri, r-wn n=y p t r-wn n=y t tw.t r-wn n=y p nwn, “Open the earth for me, open the netherworld for me, open the primeval waters for me.” Griffith and Thompson, Demotic Magical Papyrus of London and Leiden, London, line I 5.
