7

Reading the Hypocephalus: Part 1, Figures 1–4, 22–23

Facsimile 2, Figure 1: Culmen Omnium

When the engraving of Facsimile 2 was made, the Joseph Smith hypocephalus had already been damaged (see appendix 2). The top part of this figure was missing and the bottom pushed out of line. The absent heads had to be supplied, and it was done in a manner familiar to Egyptian artists, by borrowing parts of one figure from another—in this case permissible since they are both the same god. Indeed, there are hypocephali in which figure 1 has two instead of four heads.[1]

The figure in the center is Amun, the All-Embracing. Here he is, as Peter L. Renouf saw, “the great God, Lord of Heaven, the giver of light, lighting up the heavens and earth with his rays . . . to give life to the universe.” [2] The Litanies at Luxor list no less than 124 of his epithets or functions.[3] His four faces show him viewing and embracing the expanse of the universe, “fill[ing] the immensity of space” (D&C 88:12). Such glory calls up the figure of the Hebrew מלך העולם (melek hā-ʿōlām), the King of the Worlds, the King of the Universe, the eternal King, who rules in all time and space.[4] In the Pearl of Great Price his name is “Endless” (Moses 1:3), signifying infinite extent, both in time and space—literally the איןסוף (ʾēn sōp) of the rabbis. The famous chart of the Jewish scholar Raavad, giving the positioning of the constellations (fig. 21), is a series of circles around a large central circle which contains only the two boldly written names—Jehovah and below it Shemesh (Amun and Re in Egyptian).[5] Diodorus Siculus noted that the Greek god Zeus was called Ammon by the Egyptians.[6] Amun-Re tells us, “I have many names and many forms. My form is in every god . . . the Creator, King of All, who possesses many names, whom the gods of heaven do not know.” [7] In the Eleventh Dynasty, there was a syncretic merging of Amun and Min, the god of fertility, and Amun was often portrayed in ithyphallic form.[8]

Figure 21. This circular chart starts from the outside rim, which contains twelve permutations on the four letters of the divine name; the next circles contain sequentially the twelve houses, the twelve tribes of Israel, the twelve months of the year, and the twelve signs of the Zodiac. Published before 1562 (cf. p. 521, fig. 63).

The staff held by figure 1, Amun, is a combination of the djed-column, signifying abiding firmness and stability, the was-scepter of power and authority, and the ankh-staff of life—the three things on which all certainty depends. But before all else we are dealing with creation and birth. So it is enlightening to note that the Prophet Joseph begins his explanation of this figure as “the first creation, nearest to the celestial, or the residence of God. First in government, the last pertaining to measurement of time,” etc. It is not the celestial residence, but it is near to it, as the center of one great system, the largest system known to Abraham, and though he is aware of the existence of worlds without number, he sees only a particular segment. Indeed, Moses was sharply rebuked when he asked to see it all: “Worlds without number have I created . . . for mine own purpose; . . . here is wisdom and it remaineth in me” (Moses 1:33, 31). Moses is informed that he has all that he can handle in his own earthly mission and meekly apologizes, “Be merciful unto thy servant, O God, and tell me concerning this earth, . . . and then thy servant will be content” (Moses 1:36).

The most sublime aspect of Amun is the way he brings all things together in one, just as science today looks for the Grand Unifying Theory (GUT). That is what Amun gives us, and we should bear in mind that all the owners of hypocephali were priests and priestesses of Amun-Re, along with their associates.[9] Abraham, viewing the starry heavens, found that he “could not see the end thereof” (Abraham 3:12); while Moses, who is given “only an account of this earth,” is assured that worlds that now stand are “innumerable . . . unto man; but all things are numbered unto me, for they are mine and I know them” (Moses 1:35). As the doctrine of Min-Amun-Re, etc., proclaims, all the universe is full of life, sustained and rejuvenated in and by the One at the Center.[10]

A very important teaching of Abraham, which has recently been attributed to the Egyptians with great emphasis and now occupies the center place in the arguments on cosmology, is a form of “anthropism.” What is consciousness? What would exist if there were no awareness, no intelligence? The Egyptian was convinced, as Philippe Derchain has it, that “by the mind alone chaos is held at bay, implying that a cessation of thought would make ipso facto the end of the universe.” [11] As Abraham expresses it, intelligence is all. In something like the ontological argument of Anselm, Abraham is taught that if there is a hierarchy at all, one intelligence greater than another, there is necessarily one which excels them all, and since intelligence is an indivisible thing, it brings and enfolds them all into itself (Abraham 3:18–19, 21–23). “This is my work and my glory—to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man” (Moses 1:39). The word enfolding is being used a good deal today with reference to the implicit and explicit reality of things.

What does Joseph Smith tell us about figure 1? In the first place, these are not pictures of anything; to use Smith’s terms, the drawings only “represent,” “signify,” are “pertaining to,” “answer to,” are “called by the Egyptians,” are “said by the Egyptians,” “will be given in the own due time of the Lord,” “ought not to be revealed,” are “to be had in the Holy Temple of God,” and are “as revealed from God to Abraham.” To make it sporting, “if the world can find out these numbers,” they are welcome to try. What is being discussed here? If we single out the nouns and verbs in the official explanation, we can see what it is talking about, and, as with the sophisticated Amun theology of the Egyptians, there is nothing mystical or magical about it. The words that make up the brief explanation are

1. Cosmology: the words used are earth, planets, firmament, Sun, stars, moon, revolution.

2. Measurement and number: measurements of time, celestial time, day, cubit, years, one thousand, quarters, numbers, due time, revolution, equal with.

3. Transmission of power or energy: receiving light, borrows its light, governs planets or stars, receives its power, the governing power.

4. Hierarchy or dominion (intelligence and purpose): creation, residence, government, key, power, God, throne, authority, crown, light, holding the key, sitting clothed in glory, governing, Holy Ghost.

5. Ordinances and procedures (relating the above to humanity): key words, sacrifice, Holy Ghost, Abraham, dove, Temple of God, Egypt.

6. Special idiom or notation to convey the above: the figures represent, signify, pertaining to, answering to, called by the Egyptians, said by the Egyptians, will be given, not to be revealed, to be had in the Holy Temple of God, given to Abraham as he offered sacrifice, if the world can find out these numbers, etc.

The Condescension of God

One of the strangest things about the Supreme Ruler is that while his mighty crown shows him to be the highest of the highest, yet in this and every other hypocephalus in which he appears, the central figure is squatting on the bare earth. Where is the throne, the seat of glory? Hans P. L’Orange has shown at great length that the supernal throne is always mobile, moving through heaven on or as a chariot.[12] Our figure 1, on the contrary, must be the Unmoved Mover; he is the stable fundament of all. While his mighty crown proclaims him ruler of all the worlds above, he is not to be separated from any part of the universe—the brother of Busiris, the universal king, was Antaeus who could not be separated from the earth.[13] In Facsimile 2, figures 3 and 7 are seated on majestic thrones. In Facsimile 3 Abraham is seated on yet another throne. The scepter in the grasp of our great figure 1 shows that he is ruling in all three worlds. Is this because he will not separate himself from the worlds above, as his mighty crown proclaims, nor will he desert the worlds below, including this the poorest, as his threefold scepter makes clear, to say nothing of his Antaeus-like wedding to the earth?

Facsimile 2, figures 22–23

Inseparable from our figure 1 are the reverential apes on either side of him—figures 22 and 23 (see appendix 2). On other hypocephali they are sometimes two in number, sometimes four, six, or eight; sometimes standing and sometimes seated. They are identified as stars. As early as the Pyramid Texts, they are designated as “the Beloved Sons” of Sothis/Sirius, the brightest star in the sky.[14] It is assumed that the position of the apes shows them warming their hands as they greet the rays of the rising sun after the cold desert night and seeming to shield their eyes from the glory of the sunrise. So they are stars receiving light from a greater star, as Joseph Smith’s explanation declares. There was nothing to indicate to Joseph Smith that they are stars, yet along with the Pyramid Texts we have a vignette in the 17th chapter of the Book of the Dead in which each of these apes is preceded by a star (fig. 22).[15]

Figure 22. Ani raises his hands in the same gesture of adoration as the two apes behind the boat in which Khepri the sun-god sits with his scarab head. In front of each ape is a five-pointed star, the hieroglyphic sign of prayer and worship. The wedjat-eye ensures everyone’s continuing joy. P. Ani, BD 17, ca. 1250 b.c., BM 10470. The British Museum.

The eight apes in the New Kingdom stand for the Ogdoad, the eight primal gods, or the great council which met in the presence of Amun at the creation (fig. 23).[16] Amun as the president of the council in heaven would make up the Ennead, and there is, as one might expect, a cosmic significance to the quartet of apes in which Gustave Jéquier finds “a singular parallelism between their names and those given to the primal chaos in Genesis.” [17] The תהו (tōhû) and בהו (bōhû) of the Hebrew creation story match the Nun (Nwn) and Hehu (Ḥḥw) of the Egyptian, according to Jéquier, while Hebrew 	חושך (ḥôšek) is plainly related to the Egyptian Keku (Kkw).[18] In reading the Old Testament, Jéquier finds it “hard to believe . . . that the Elohist author did not draw from a Hermopolitan source.” [19]

Heh Hehet Kek Keket Nun Nunet Amun Amunet

Figure 23. The eight primal gods, alternating male and female, parade before us. In this version they are shown with frog and serpent heads. Red granite shrine of Amasis, Naos D. 29, ca 540 b.c. Louvre Museum.

Horopollo tells us that the special office of the apes was to function as timekeepers or time-reckoners.[20] The sitting cynocephalus, according to him, at each equinox urinates twelve times a day and twelve times a night; during the rest of the year he gives a cry twelve times a day on the hour, every hour.[21] The seated ape, then, is in charge of both the water clock and scales (fig. 24). In the Ptolemaic period the Egyptians wrote the determinative of the word for “hour,” wnw.t , by a heart-shaped plumb bob suspended from a carpenter’s square (see p. 253, fig. 26).[22] The purpose of this was to make sure that the post supporting the scale was perfectly upright, as is shown in Joseph Smith Papyrus IV. This new element suggests that the apes which face each other, worshipping the central figure both on the morning side and the evening side on all hypocephali (Fac. 2, figs. 22 and 23), establish among other things a sense of perfect balance in the universe—time, space, and matter are all measurably related. Finally, each of our apes is crowned with a disk and a crescent, the meeting of the sun and the moon at the New Moon marking the beginning of the month; the best-known office of the cynocephalus apes is in representing Thoth as the moon-god (see color plate 2).

Figure 24. This elaborate alabaster water clock was an ingenious solution to dividing the varying lengths of night into twelve equal portions, which allowed the priests to perform their duties in a punctual fashion. The circle of the year is divided into three seasons of four months each. The scale of dots below the appropriate month measured those nights as the water sank and passed through a seated ape and golden tube. The heavens are carved on the exterior; above the ape is the North Pole and its constellations (A). In a rare appearance, two turtles are used to represent an unknown constellation (B). Klepsydra of Amenhotep III, restored, ca. 1370 b.c. Cairo Museum.

		 It is significant that the apes are more than mere symbols. They are actual creatures doing what God’s creatures do as they also serve him. The same applies to the lowliest of all, the serpents, that seem to stand on their tails in front of each ape. In better-preserved hypocephali they are more conspicuous and are shown in various positions, usually one contained in a womb or amniotic sac of some sort. Sometimes, however, both snakes are in such bags, and sometimes both are free, the one crawling horizontally and the other vertically. Plainly this has to do with the bringing forth of life, and what the two opposing serpents most readily suggest are the coupling snakes of the caduceus (fig. 25), the staff of Hermes the medic, which is a sign both of copulation and of the life-saving serpent—a vivid reminder of the bronze serpent in the wilderness which overcomes the poison of the other serpents (see Numbers 21:9). To this we may add the Uraeus of the Pharaoh, which overcomes the serpents that oppose him. Added to the two flanking serpents, the one in the amniotic sac and the other on the loose, we have in most hypocephali serpents on the crowns of figures 1 and 2, either issuing above the horns on both the two-feathered Amun-crown and the atef-crown worn by figure number 1, usually crowned with solar disks, or in various other combinations now on one crown only, and now on the other, now wearing sun-disks, and now without them, etc.

Clarification for the above is set forth at considerable length in the study by A. L. Frothingham, who brings the Egyptian symbols into relationship with the Babylonian. In a Mesopotamian seal impression we see the supreme god–Nin-girsu, Ea, or Shamash–seated on a throne. In his right hand he holds a vase overflowing with two streams of water, on top of which is the triple flower emblem of fertility. He is presenting it to an another god who has two snakes projecting from his two shoulders. This god, who is identified in the accompanying inscription as Ningishzida, is holding the hand of Gudea, as he leads him into the presence of the supreme god.[23] Gudea calls Ningishzia “my god.” Strangely, “two snakes project, one from behind each shoulder of the god,” who is doing the introducing.[24] In another seal, the god is holding the snakes, one in either hand; on one side of him “is the orb of the sun inside the crescent moon, below them the seven circles of the Pleiades, and at the bottom the Egyptian ankh or symbol of life,” assuring us that this is not completely irrelevant. Our guide declares that he “cannot lay too much emphasis on the interest of this scene” since it shows “that the caduceus–god [the one between the two serpents] is the propagator throughout the universe in the various forms of life.” [25] Along with the Egyptian ankh-symbol in the same Babylonian scene are two cynocephalus apes seated before the god; like the ankh–symbol these beasts are native to Egypt and not to Mesopotamia.

Most puzzling are the strange epaulets that protrude from the shoulders of the god in the Joseph Smith exemplar, which, after the damage of the papyrus, were also added to figure 1. On some hypocephali they look like rays of light, on some like plants, on some like jackals’ heads; some have interpreted them as feathers. They are usually so tiny as to puzzle the viewer, but there are enough of them scattered among the hypocephali to determine what they are most of the time. One Babylonian god who had such objects protruding from his shoulders is accompanied by a large ankh-sign. There are in fact many such seals in which a god wearing horns (as in Fac. 2, figs. 1 and 2) has the serpents’ heads extending from his shoulders, the tiny heads being crowned and displaying forked tongues in a fussy and complex design often difficult to distinguish since they are so minute.[26] In exactly the same way in various examples, tiny Uraeus-serpents rear up on either side of the crowns worn by our Egyptian figure 2. Figure 2 in other hypocephali has snakes issuing sometimes from his shoulders and sometimes from his crown, in which case his Amun-crown of the two feathers was displaced by the elaborate atef-crown.

Figure 25. Two venerable bearded serpents form a perfect circle in this bronze herald’s staff. Early fifth century b.c., Dallas Museum of Art, gift of the Junior League of Dallas.

			 Coptic Christians of Egypt use a similar design for their pastoral staff, evoking the brazen serpent of Moses. Bronze. Coptic Museum of Cairo.

		 				 More easily recognized than explained are those scenes on which the god has sun-rays on his shoulders,[27] showing him to be the sun-god Shamash. In Babylonia as in Egypt the rays are denoted by three wavy lines shooting up from the neck and shoulders. There are many Babylonian seals in which the things are indeed plants; after careful examination these turned out to be in most cases very clearly drawn bulbs of the opium poppy.[28] Sometimes poppies and snakes issue from either shoulder together or in various combinations, both held in the hand and worn on the shoulders. Most clearly recognizable is a combination of a jackal’s head beneath shw-feathers. Is this to indicate passage through the lower world (Wepwawet = Anubis) and the heavenly spaces respectively? In every case we are dealing with healing, rejuvenation, and resurrection. These ideas have often been traced to the snake’s capacity to come out with a shining new skin every year. One of the best-known figures from the gnostic mysteries is Phanes encircled by a serpent.[29] It is based on the bearded, horned, crowned god “helplessly wound up in the coils of two serpents,” their heads protruding over his shoulders.[30] Though these themes proliferate in antiquity, they are, as we see, of primordial age.

Such themes occur in Egypt, where “the falcon is called ‘Horus of the underworld’ . . . [that] . . . comes forth from the belly of a snake and then enters into it again, thus he is regularly regenerated in the snake as sun-god,” [31] even as Re and Osiris unite in the renewal of the life cycle by passing through the body of the mother-goddess.[32] We see “the sun-god portrayed in human form with a ram’s head . . . [who] stands with the ws-scepter in his hand under the mehn-snake” (see pp. 298–99, fig. 32).[33]

If You Could Hie to Kolob

Kolob is the one name in the explanations not designated as Egyptian, and indeed it is basically Semitic, turning up with two principal meanings in the West Semitic languages. The late professor Louis C. Zucker suggested that Smith’s “Kolob . . . may be a variant of Kokob (star),” [34] he being possibly unaware of one of the most fruitful roots in Arabic where the guttural first consonant gives the vowel an o-sound. Let us look at Lane’s dictionary.[35]

qlb

verb: to alter, change, invert, turn upside down, turn over and over, go flip-flop.

noun: “Heart,” hence intellect or intelligence.

adjective: genuine or pure in respect of origin or lineage, “holding a middle place among his people.”

“A certain bright star (Alpha Scorpio = Antares) in the 18th mansion of the Moon”

“There are also 3 similar appellations of other stars, esp. Regulus, called qalb al-ʿasad, the heart of the Lion.”

The explanation “called Kolob because it is near to me” suggests Semitic qrb, meaning “to be near”; Kolob may accordingly be related to the rich Indo-European root klb, as in kleben, kolophon, cleave (unto), clyppian (OE, hug close), clap, clasp, clip (together), couple, clabber, etc.

Though the words qalb (heart) and kalb (dog) are different, the ancients could not keep them apart, as was inevitable to people always seeking puns and parallels. One ancient saying quoted in Lane shows a more than facetious connection between the two: “izza tala’ al-Qalb ja’a’ šita ka’l-Kalb—when the Heart-Star rises winter comes in like the Dog-star.” What is significant here is that these two actually are the brightest stars in the heavens, Sirius and Canopus, and are freely interchangeable. The Arabic kalb denotes what we call “the constellation of Canis Major (the Big Dog), and its principal star is Sirius,” which we call the Dog-star. Nearby is Procyon, the Little Dog-star. To complicate things, Sirius, the brightest star, has a rival for the title of Kalb in the red-giant star Antares.

The experts of old inevitably came to disagree as to which might be the Dog-star. Sirius, the brightest star in the sky, naturally qualifies for a number of roles in the Egyptian manner. Giorgio de Santillana and Hertha von Dechend comment on “the amazing significance of Sirius as leader of the planets . . . ruling over the ‘three worlds.’ ” [36] But that third candidate for leadership, the second brightest star of all, was Canopus, which for the Egyptians was the heart-star par excellence, but which the Arabs also called Kalb the Dog! Thus we see how heart and dog get switched, both being used to designate the most important star in the heavens. The Egyptians designated Canopus by drawing a picture of a human heart suspended as the bob on a plumb line, the cosmic plumb line from which all the universe was measured (fig. 26). As de Santillana and von Dechend put it, “the determinative sign for ‘heart’ often figures as the plumb bob at the end of a plumb line coming from the well-known astronomical device, the merkhet. Evidently ‘heart’ is something very specific, as it were the ‘center of gravity.’ ” [37] Here he refers us to the Wörterbuch, where the basic idea of heart is given not surprisingly as “the middle, the center.” [38]

Figure 26. (A) The ebony merkhet was a simple but effective astronomical device that could also be used as a sundial. (B) The stone plumb bob was carved in the form of the human heart, ib, as shown by the Ptolemaic hieroglyphs. Borchardt, Die altägyptische Zeitmessung, 52B. (C) The bay, carved from a palm frond, was held in front of the observer as a sighting device, ca. 600 b.c. (A, 4¾” and C, 13 1/4 ”). ägyptisches Museum und Papyrussammlung, Berlin.

				 So which is it to be, Sirius or Canopus? For the southern regions Canopus gets the nod. The Arabs also had another name for Canopus; besides calling it qalb at-taiman, “heart of the south,” it was also called suhail al-wezen, “the little adjuster of the scales,” or, as de Santillana and von Dechend put it, Canopus Ponderosus, suggesting to him that Canopus was the weight at the end of the plumb line, the star “by means of which the depth [of the universe] was measured.” [39] De Santillana and von Dechend further note that the measuring operation was carried on by Hephaestus, whom the Greeks always identified with Khnum/Amun in his capacity as creator, whose fall helped Zeus measure the universe. Later Hephaestus designed nothing less than the Shield of Achilles! [40] The great weight of Canopus Ponderosus explains why “of all the stars it alone was taken for static, exempted from the Precession [of the equinoxes] . . . the primordial star . . . called l’etoile immobile.” In Egypt it was also Sirius who enjoyed immunity from the Precession.[41]

In view of the immense conservatism of star nomenclature, it is not too much to assume that the Arabic name for the star Canopus, suhail al-wezen, goes back to the Egyptians who, to make sure that a balance was even, had a cynocephalus ape sit atop the center post supporting the scales, Anubis holding in his hand a plumb line with the weight in the shape of a heart; this was to keep things on dead center to obtain the correct weight (fig. 27). It is a regular detail of the psychostasy scene. To match this exercise in matter and mass, we have the same figure presiding over the measurement of time as he forms the spout of Egyptian water clocks to mark the hours with precision (see p. 245, fig. 24).[42] So we are back to cubits and days. The swinging pendulum used the mass of the earth to measure a field, and now the water clock employs the pendulum to get the exact time.[43]

Figure 27. The sun shines down on judgment day while Anubis, Lord of the Scales, steadies the heart-shaped plumb bob. The baboon of Thoth presides at the center. Two wedjat-eyes, vultures, and cobras ensure everything is done in order. P. Ta-Udja-Ra. Cairo Museum.

The Great Year

Another statement of time—“one day in Kolob is equal to a thousand years” (Fac. 2, fig. 1)—demonstrates different times in different systems. That is the great year of the ancients. They worked out all sorts of cycles. The whole Kolob concept suggests that “archaic order” which today is being retrieved through the serious study of the oldest myths, monuments, and idols of the race. “As we follow the clues—stars, numbers,” write de Santillana and von Dechend, “a huge framework of connections is revealed at many levels. One is inside an echoing manifold, where everything responds and everything has a place and a time assigned to it. This is a true edifice . . . a World-Image that fits the many levels, and all of it kept in order by strict measure.” [44] The concept of unity and identity so prominent in the Egyptian text is well expressed in the Pearl of Great Price: “And behold, all things have their likeness, and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual; things which are in the heavens above and things which are on earth, and things which are in the earth, and things which are under the earth, both above and beneath: all things bear record of me” (Moses 6:63). Such is the “echoing manifold,” with Kolob in control.

In this huge framework of connections, the unit of measurement is, according to de Santillana and von Dechend, “always some form of time.” [45] And it is the same in the Prophet’s explanation of the multileveled “firmament of the heavens” which “answers to the measurement of time” —that is, of the revolutions or orbits of the heavenly bodies.

Jah-oh-eh

The Lord used this earth as the basis in the explanation of his creations to both Abraham and Moses (Abraham 3:4–7, 9; Moses 1:35–36), “according to the measurement of the earth which is called by the Egyptians Jah-oh-eh” (Fac. 2, figs. 1, 4, explanation). This, of course, suggests Jaoel, the angel who visits Abraham in the Apocalypse of Abraham and who is easily identified by George H. Box as Jehovah.[46]

What is that mysterious name, Jehovah, and its real form? The name was deliberately withheld from the world; only one person was supposed to know it, and that was the high priest who whispered it only once when he entered the veil on the Day of Atonement. Its secret pronunciation was taught to the disciples of the doctors of the Jews only once every seven years.[47] The form we all know in common use, Jehovah or Yahweh, is held by Jewish scholars to be “only meant for the masses” and not the true or real Tetragrammaton at all.[48] Necessarily that must “consist of four vowels u-a-i-e,” according to Eliyahu Rosh-Pinnah.[49]

A doctrine common to both Jews and Egyptians is that “everything which is found in the heaven above has its counterpart on the earth below,” including “human speech . . . which is dead without the vowels.” [50] It is the vowels that live, and the original tetragrammaton uttered by the high priest once a year was really the four vowels u-a-i-e, which are “a manifestation of the Supreme Being, . . . Who with His Name seals the six ends of the universe and thereby prevents it from relapsing into chaos.” [51] In Jah-oh-eh, which Joseph Smith calls “the measurement of this earth,” the monosyllabic Jah is necessarily two vowels, as can be seen in the writing of Ya-h-weh, the letter j being foreign to Hebrew as it is to Latin and Greek. The Sefer Yetzirah, traditionally the oldest of books and the work of Abraham, tells us that “the whole creation and all languages” emanated from the name YHWH, the tetragrammaton, which the ancients regarded as consisting entirely of four vowels and was actually transcribed into other languages as i-e-u-o, but also as i-a-o-u-i; the arrangement varies because there is no agreement as to what the original Hebrew vowels were.[52] According to the same source, the prominent h in the name of Yahweh is neutral, “the equivalent of mathematical zero,” and can simply indicate the place where a vowel should stand. “The original letters of the Tetragrammaton,” Phineas Mordell concludes, “were 	יעוא instead of יהוה ” [53] which corresponds to Joseph Smith’s j-a-o-e (yod י ayin ע waw ו aleph א).

In the oldest of alphabets, the Phoenician and Hebrew, “the order of the letters in the West Semitic system of writing is almost as old as the invention of that system itself. It is only a short step from here to the hypothesis that the fixation of the order was part and parcel of that invention, that it was in fact a mnemonic device which helped the rapid spread of the West Semitic system of writing.” [54]

According to the Sefer Yetzirah, the letters “were made in the form of a ‘state and arranged like an army in battle array,’ ” as if coordinating human affairs with the order of the cosmos.[55] We are told that “when Abraham understood it, his wisdom increased greatly, and he taught the whole law.” [56]

A study of Egyptian heart-scarabs emphasizes the doctrine that for the Egyptians the heart was “the seat of all human existence.” “It represents,” wrote Adriaan de Buck, “the totality of life and the manifestations of life, the essence and personality of every man.” [57] It is the most intimate part of the individual, “the center of life, . . . the seat of feeling and intellect, of decision-making and conscience.” [58] The heart is often given the form of a scarab (ḫpr) because it is “the organ of change (ḫprw)” of eternal progression.[59] One cannot fail to notice in the scarab the cousin of the plumb bob; Kolob is still the center of everything. The creative function of the heart leads ever to new forms of life, as is expressed in a remarkable Coffin Text: “I came into being from the flesh of the self-created god. He created me by his wish, he made me by his power, . . . whose name the gods do not know, whom the sun folk serve; . . . he created me in his heart, he created me with his power when I was not yet born . . . I am he who is in the midst of his circle.” [60] To a Latter-day Saint this has a familiar ring and certainly fits with the hypocephalus. It is in Mesopotamia that Nibiru takes the role of Kolob, “Nibiru is his star which they caused to shine in the sky. He has taken position at the solstitial point (?), may they look upon him, saying: ‘He who crosses the middle of the sea without resting, his name shall be Nibiru, who occupies the middle thereof; may he maintain the course of the stars of heaven; may he shepherd all the gods like sheep.’ ” [61] He is the pole of the heavens by whom all cosmic motions are governed and measured.

Synesius, a learned bishop who lived in Libya in the fourth century, says that the Egyptians had a holy mountain on the top of which was a royal booth (σκήνη, skēnē), where the wisest men of Egypt would sit in order of wisdom in a circle or ring “as if encircling the king as first and foremost, as if he were the heart.” [62] It is also the concept of the Cebes tablet (see p. 496, fig. 59A).

It would seem that Joseph Smith has not done badly with Kolob. Even if the name with its many strikingly relevant connections should only be a happy accident, the rest of it is all in place—the crowned figure in the center of the cosmic circle representing first of all the creative power, also the governing power, by whom time and space are measured and structured as revealed in the stars. All this along with the graphic design is basic to the thinking of Egyptians and Hebrews alike. Speaking of the oldest records of the race, de Santillana and von Dechend write that in them the universe is “a true edifice of a mathematical matrix. . . . When we speak of measure, it is always some form of time that provides them, starting from the two basic ones, the solar year and the octave, and going down from there in many periods and intervals, to actual weights and sizes.” [63] That circular time and space are measured in the same units—minutes and seconds of arc—is far more evident than the subtle equation of the Egyptian cubit and the length of the day, which somehow did not escape Joseph Smith.

Why Rams?

The central figure of most hypocephali has four rams’ heads on one neck. Herodotus was intrigued by the representation of Amun with the head of a ram, as in our figures 1 and 2. First of all, he makes it clear that the Egyptians did not for a moment “think that is the way he really was.” [64] And he proceeds to explain that Zeus was determined that not even Hercules should see his true appearance, but he at least granted him the indulgence of “displaying himself wearing the fleece of a ram which he had skinned and beheaded.” And that is why the Egyptian images of Zeus have a ram’s head. They got the idea from the Ammonite Egyptian colonists, whose name for Zeus was Amun. The center of his cult in Egypt was in Mendes.[65] Because the Egyptian word for ram, ba, is the same as the word for soul, the Ram at Mendes also became associated with both Osiris and Re.[66] There also a four-headed ram-god was thought to combine the attributes of Re, Shu, Geb, and Osiris, and then was extended to include “the ba of every god.” [67]

Turning to the famous Mendes Stela, we view “the great god Mendes, the life of Re, the male potency of gods and mankind, who appears in the region of light (or as an akh), the divine issue of the Ram . . . eldest son of the Creator of All, who sits on the throne of the first of the gods . . . a prince in the womb, a ruler at the breast whom the potency of his father, the Ram of Mendes, made strong as a king, victorious, etc.” [68] This fulsome description is well summed up in Joseph Smith’s economical explanation as “the first creation, nearest to the celestial, or residence of God. First in government” (Fac. 2, fig. 1, explanation).

We all know that the ram is the bellwether who leads out the flock. Aries the Ram leads out the year in the Zodiac—the god Janus (after whom the month January is named) has the horns of Aries the Ram (see p. 397, fig. 48). As Min he always has goat’s horns and as Min-Amun he is the rejuvenated prowess of ram, goat, and lion . . . he with the four faces on one neck, the 777 ears, millions of eyes, and hundreds of thousands of horns.[69] As to the horns, Amun shares two types with Khnum as primal king and creator. There are the long, twisted horizontal horns of figure 2 and the new breed which appeared in the New Kingdom of the well-known curly horned beast associated with Amun. And yet the god continues to wear both types of horns together as he does on most of the hypocephali (fig. 28).[70]

Figure 28. The conservative nature of Egyptian religious art is shown in this bas-relief of the sacred ram of Amun, identified by the cropped beard. The distinctive long horns of the older breed are kept while adding the spiral horns of the newer animals. Both appear on images of Amun and Khnum. Ptolemaic. Leiden Museum.

In the hymns, “the eight stand in reverence” to Amun. In the rites of Mendes, “Pan is the god of the eight gods who the Mendesians say came before the twelve gods.” [71] With Amun at their head they form the original Ennead. In the upper left-hand section of many hypocephali, the eight rams are on display (see appendix 6A). They are drawn directly above figure 4, Horus ascending on hawk’s wings from his mummy wrappings into the sky. The rams are also spirits; they are bas. On the best executed hypocephali this is clearly depicted. The artist of the Viennese hypocephalus shows a reclining ram with eight short strokes beneath it, and the inscription is “You have your eight rams” (see appendix 4A).[72] The Joseph Smith hypocephalus most closely resembles this one in Vienna. The British Museum 35875 has the same ram with eight strokes, and the inscription reads: “You have the eight rams as your gods.” One of the hypocephali in the Ashmolean Museum is most specific: “You are as the eight rams,” with eight well-drawn rams.[73] The splendid Leiden hypocephalus has room for only seven rams, but it is so insistent that it crams an eighth one very awkwardly under the panel; it reads: “You have your ba as eight rams” (see appendix 5A).[74]

In all these cases it is not the ram but the ba that is being emphasized—the ram is simply a way of writing the sound, and the sound of evoking the spirit. This is seen in a number of hypocephali such as British Museum 36188, where we are confronted with five rams and three ba-birds, all pronounced exactly alike and having the same significance. They are drawn directly over the head of the rising Horus-hawk and need no inscription. The bronze hypocephalus from Abydos, G 50 C = BM 37330, draws five rams and abbreviates the word for ram three times, while a bronze Louvre piece [75] displays the same arrangement. The Henry Meux hypocephalus has the three ba-birds but instead of five rams, it has all eight (see appendix 6).[76] Herodotus was right. These things are purely symbolic and should never be viewed literally; they are as abstract as mathematical symbols.

One thinks of the fulsome repetition of the avenues of rams before the temple pylons and can be confident that such imagery runs no risk of displaying God in his true form—the one thing that he will not tolerate. In the Book of Moses this idea is repeatedly brought out: “No man can behold all my glory, and afterwards remain in the flesh on the earth” (Moses 1:5). “But now mine own eyes have beheld God; but not my natural, but my spiritual eyes, for my natural eyes could not have beheld; for I should have withered and died in his presence; but his glory was upon me; and I beheld his face, for I was transfigured before him” (Moses 1:11). “Moses lifted up his eyes, . . . being filled with the Holy Ghost, . . . and calling upon the name of God, he beheld his glory again, for it was upon him” (Moses 1:24–25).

Again we recall the admonition of science today that we may never hope to see things as they really are—our own image of them is as close as we can ever get, until we are allowed to enter into a wholly new state of things. Can we overlook the frequency of the name of Ammon in our Book of Mormon? Or the designation of God by that name in the early hymns, such as “The Time Is Far Spent” ? [77] It became a generic name for God, the ultimate and quintessential, as Adam is the same for man. They meet in Adam-ondi-Ahman—“Man in the Presence of (indi, ante, anti, and-swerian, OE for “answer,” etc.) God.”

Facsimile 2, Figure 2

This figure, as Joseph Smith explains it, “stands next to Kolob . . . the next grand governing creation near to . . . the place where God resides” (Fac. 2, fig. 2, explanation). Compare this to figure 1, which is “Kolob, . . . the first creation, nearest to . . . the residence of God” (Fac. 2, fig. 1, explanation). With this gradation of glory goes precedence in time, figure 1, “the first creation,” and beyond that, steps in degrees of authority, with figure 2, “the next grand governing creation.” Neither one is the center of everything. Figure 1, to be sure, is the center facing in all directions around whom all else revolves. Abraham is told that “Kolob is set . . . to govern all those planets which belong to the same order as that upon which thou standest” (Abraham 3:9). Moses was rebuked for wanting an immediate view of what the ancients and Paul called the πλήρωμα (plērōma)—absolutely everything (see Colossians 2:9); the Lord told him to hold on and be satisfied with this world, the only world which concerned him at present (Moses 1:30–36).

Note also that figure 2, the second in order, is “holding the key of power also pertaining to other planets.” On some hypocephali this figure is labeled both Re and Amun-Re, the same power at different levels. He stands at the zenith of the year and the noon of the day at his greatest moment of power—the sun, the ruler of the solar system, but everything about him reminds us that he is in motion. What about the rest of his journey, passing through the underworld from west to east? We are referred to the key, the Wepwawet, “Opener of the Ways,” which lets us out of the underworld.

In the small Nash hypocephalus (see appendix 7A), figures 1 and 2 are combined and their identity clearly established: In the top panel, wearing the two tall feathers of Amun, is the two-headed figure, but here he is seated on the throne, holding the scepters exactly like the four-headed Amun on other hypocephali.[78] In this case proximity is the main idea; we are near the center but moving out from it. Among the ancients and moderns, proximity to Divinity both in place and time was a direct measure of blessedness. This “Kolob” idea is set forth vividly in the Book of Abraham: “the name of the great one is Kolob, because it is near unto me” (Abraham 3:3). It is “nearest to the celestial, or residence of God” (Fac. 2, fig. 1, explanation). A most significant feature of figure 2 is the crown the god-figure is wearing, combining both ram’s horns and the two tall feathers. It is the Ta-Tjenen-crown, which combines the double feathers of Amun’s spiritual crown (see Fac. 3, fig. 1) with the ram’s horns that bring Re and Osiris together in a symbol of eternal beginning.[79]

Striding forth boldly on his eternal rounds, figure 2 is designated on a bronze hypocephalus as “Amun Lord of Heaven in his aspect of coming forth.” [80] You will notice that our figure 2 is not “residing” anywhere—he is on the move, right at the top of the circle. His feathers usually touch and often protrude above the confining edge to show him, as one scholar puts it, “here at the vertex of the universe, the Ram comes first of all in order, appearing at the head of the universe and the beginning of light.” [81] He is by all accounts a true two-faced Janus figure; classical writers, as we have seen, associated Amun’s ram’s horns with the constellation Aries the Ram, the Opener of the Year.[82] Let us recall further that some Egyptian inscriptions label our figure 2 as past and future, “I know,” and “I shall know,” explaining that “the one is Osiris who is Yesterday, and the other is Re who is Tomorrow.” [83]

The holy Egyptian feather which Shu wears signifies light traversing the space between heaven and earth and is represented by the god of that name; [84] “its filaments symbolized the rays of the sun.” [85] It is the famous atef-crown, designated by Joseph Smith as “emblematical of the grand presidency in heaven,” and indeed the two feathers are the prime emblems of celestial light and spirit. Significantly, the shw-feather is not a theological but an astronomical symbol, as Rudolf Anthes sees it; the god’s name is never written with the familiar divinity sign; rather it represents the actual sunshine or light and energy that traverses and fills the space between heaven and earth,[86] a light (to quote Joseph Smith) “pertaining to other planets.” The sign of the two feathers enjoyed a very wide range of interpretation among the Egyptian scribes.[87]

As to the staff or scepter, which the god carries as his staff of office and the emblem of his power, it is of the utmost importance. It signifies specifically the power to move or progress. It is the standard of the dog Wepwawet, whose image appears on it—the faithful dog who leads his majesty into the realm of the unknown. “The true character of Wepwawet is still far from being clearly defined, but the Pyramid Texts show him intimately related to the mystery of the rising sun and the resurrection of the king . . . that is why he is in the prow of the solar bark and always leads the parade.” [88] All these ideas come together in the figure on the staff of the character who bears it.

Staff and Key

Joseph Smith calls the staff a key, “the key of power.” The Hebrew word for key, מפתח (miptaḥ), means literally “opener,” while the Egyptian name of the god who bears this staff is Wp-w.wt = Opener of the Ways. The Egyptian obsession with “the Way” as the course of life here and hereafter, eloquently expressed in the First Psalm and in the preaching of the great high priest Petosiris—“I will show you the Way of Life”—has been discussed at length by scholars such as Oswald Spengler and Gertrud Thausing.[89] Peter is one who has the keys of the kingdom of heaven (Matthew 16:19). Janus also is the god “who holds a staff in his left and a key in his right,” [90] “the one who holds the key (clavigerus),” [91] and the one who is “the gatekeeper of the heavenly courts (coelestis ianitor aulae).” [92] The Egyptian is constantly concerned with being checked or blocked (ḫsf) in his career. Only real power, the power of the key, can overcome his determined opponents. It shall become apparent that the key plays a major role both in the hypocephalus and in the Prophet’s interpretation of it.

Facsimile 2, Figure 3

In the Church Historian’s Office there is an old pen-and-ink sketch of Facsimile 2 (see appendix 3). In this drawing figure 3 is entirely missing, showing that that part of the document had been destroyed by the time the facsimile was printed in the Times and Seasons. Along with it was missing that part of the rim that frames the upper right-hand segment of the circle where the hieroglyphic text has been replaced by hieratic characters taken from the Hor Book of Breathings. It is claimed that this document shows the condition of the hypocephalus when Reuben Hedlock engraved it and that figure 3, missing at the time, was added by the engraver. However, a comparison of the Hedlock version with other hypocephali readily shows that, when this copy was made, the ship, as we now see it, was still in place, or else whoever borrowed the boat and trimmings that filled panel 3 must have been inspired, since they are exactly what should be there. Note that many other hypocephali show the same ship-complex that we find in the Joseph Smith copy. The boat in the picture is identical with another piece of Joseph Smith Papyrus, namely JSP IV, which accompanies chapter 101 of the Book of the Dead.[93] It has been noted that the figure in the boat is facing the wrong way, to judge by other hypocephali; but since this is the most varied of all scenes on the hypocephali, and since in some other well-drawn hypocephali, such as British Museum 37330, the seated figure in the boat and the disk-crowned hawk are also facing to the right, one cannot be dogmatic. Moreover, by facing to the right the figure preserves the direction of motion of the whole hypocephalus. The most important figures at the top of the hypocephalus are moving to the right, as are those in the bottom panels when the figure is reversed, so that the disk itself seems to be revolving. The next world is not only an upside-down world, but it is also, naturally, a reverse world.[94]

Is this drawing simply lifted from the Book of the Dead, chapter 101, in Joseph Smith Papyrus IV? Either Hedlock’s picture was made when the original was less damaged or else our sly copyist has sneaked in a hieratic label behind the back of the seated figure, words not found in the Book of the Dead vignette from which he is supposed to have lifted it, but entirely fitting to the scene: “the Ship of the God,” written with the usual honorific transposition. Moreover, if figure 3 was copied from the vignette to Book of the Dead 101, it is hard to see why one of the eyes should have been drawn so badly as to be almost unrecognizable, while the two eyes from which the distorted figures in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus are supposed to have been copied are in the vignette, well drawn and perfectly preserved. Why were they not transferred in their proper form?

The giant wedjat-eye, which the figure of Horus-Re is facing, has been declared out of place, but we find just such an eye held by an ape before him in the Meux hypocephalus (see appendix 6). So we may accept the two wedjat-eyes that flank the god in the boat, often interpreted when shown together as the sun and moon. In the king’s progress by ship the wedjat-eye as the Eye of Horus overcomes the darkness both as sun and moon.[95] The “high-point of the mystery-drama,” according to Joachim Spiegel, is “fusion of the king’s nature with the god of heaven,” which takes place when his statue is crowned with the moon-eye of Upper Egypt and the sun-eye of Lower Egypt.[96] If anything, the moon is an even more obvious earnest of resurrection than the sun, renewing itself twelve times as fast and by perfectly visible stages.

Joseph Smith’s critics have pointed out that his explanation of figure 3 so far—the throne, the sun-crown—would be an easy guess. “Clothed with power,” however, is a palpable hit; for the big was-scepter that the king holds stands for “dominion,” according to Raymond O. Faulkner,[97] and for “dominion, lordship,” according to Alan H. Gardiner; [98] in either case it is the ultimate sign of “power and authority.”

In more elaborate hypocephali, the lunar symbols multiply in this panel. We sometimes find a cynocephalus ape in a shrine with another ape facing him and worshipping him—both representing Thoth as the moon.[99] Usually scarabs are the passengers either with or without the apes, though the two are by far the commonest figures in this panel, the one standing for the moon and the other for its changes. The Khepri-sign, indicating a change of form without the loss of identity, here denotes the changing or ongoing phases of the moon. Thus, in the imposing Meux hypocephalus (see appendix 6C), we see our seated figure (similar to figure 3) being presented with the wedjat-eye by the cynocephalus ape while between them is a large scarab labeled ḫprἰ, signifying that this is an exchange between the sun and the moon.[100] On the only bark in Louvre hypocephalus N. 3525 A1/3, one ape—crowned with a disk in which is drawn a large wedjat-eye—is presenting another wedjat-eye to another ape crowned with a simple solar disk and sitting within a shrine.[101] On the same hypocephalus is a Horus-headed sphinx crowned with the disk enclosing the wedjat-eye, while the four-headed Sobek-crocodile next to it has only a solar disk over his head. Here we remind ourselves that the hypocephalus itself is the iris of that eye.

Degrees of Glory

The Egyptian temple has been described as a powerhouse,[102] and a fundamental idea of the hypocephalus seems to be the transmission of power from a central source. If figure 1 is “nearest” to the source of power, somewhere beyond the stars, and figure 2 is “next” in order, and figure 5 is the sun, what is figure 3? It is a busy center of exchange, powerhouse and distribution center, fairly seething with activity—no two combinations alike in the hypocephali, but all depicting exactly the same idea. In relation to figures 1 and 2, it is one more step down conveying the same divine power at the sublunary level, for we are now on earth: “as revealed to Adam in the Garden of Eden, as also to Seth, Noah, Melchizedek, Abraham, and all to whom the Priesthood was revealed” (Fac. 2, fig. 3, explanation). This is another descending line of power; the hypocephalus, like the first chapter of the Book of Moses, not only gives us a glimpse of the heavens but puts the lowly human race in the picture and involves us all in the works of Abraham—a hologram to put man into his proper setting.

Wedjat means “the unharmed one.” The moon is “unharmed” or complete when it is full, and any observer can note the degrees by which it achieves fulness. By degrees means “steps,” and the Egyptians often represent the waxing moon by fourteen steps,[103] and the full moon by the conventional wedjat-eye, commonly drawn on the backs of mirrors—reflectors of light even as the moon. We find one copper hypocephalus almost completely filled by a huge wedjat-eye held by Thoth.[104] The strange seminal power symbolized by the wedjat-eye is strikingly brought forth in its significance in Egyptian mathematics. The basic fractions from 1/2 to 1/64 used in grain and land measurement are represented by various parts of the wedjat as “the eye of the Falcon-god Horus . . . (which) was torn to fragments by the wicked god Seth (fig. 29). Later, the ibis-god, Thoth, miraculously ‘filled’ or ‘completed’ (mḥ) the eye, joining together the parts, whereby the eye regained its title to be called wḏ.t, ‘the sound eye.’ ” [105] When these fractions are all added up, the total is 63/64; “the missing 1/64 was supplied magically by Thoth.” [106]

Figure 29. The long tradition of carving separate pieces of colored stone and glass for inlay work may also have inspired the scribes to use the parts of the wedjat-eye of Horus as symbols of fractions.

The number of boats shown in the hypocephali varies from one to five, with no apparent preference—one, two, three, four, or five boats occurring with about the same frequency (averaging six of each in our collection). On the upper right-hand panel above figure 3 there is often yet another boat, on which the hawk or ba-bird sits upon a cenotaph with a solar disk above his head, flanked by the figures of Isis and Nephthys performing the familiar gestures that accompany the awakening of the dead Osiris. It is the ba rising from the tomb like the sun, as the Two Ladies by their prayers and their gestures assist in the birth or resurrection. The two boats are also represented prow to prow (see appendixes 5B, 6B, 7A) and were long thought to display the meeting of the sun-bark and the moon-bark; now they also indicate the moment of passage or transition from the morning-bark to the evening-bark, namely the sun-boat in his nocturnal passage under the earth and his daily passage through the sky, both really one and the same ship.[107] The other three ships are also sun-barks in different operations such as showing the times of day [108] and the twelve aspects during the day.[109]

The passenger list of all the ships is a short one. There is first of all the seated god Re-Harakhty, Re of the Horizon, at the moment of passing from the lower to the upper world and vice versa. In most hypocephali he is accompanied by one or two apes and at least one large scarab labeled Khepri. The Two Ladies, Isis and Nephthys, are sometimes there to effect the safe delivery of the sun at the winter solstice.[110] On rare occasions Maat and Hathor are present to assure the integrity and legitimacy of the new rule. In the two large ships in the reverse half of the circle there are three to four gods along with three or four stars as crew, averaging seven; one of them is either the ram-headed Amun or Re standing under a baldachin. They are hailing the birth of the new sun-god, often represented as a babe seated at the bow of one of the ships and guiding it forward. This is another way of showing that portentous moment when the downward journey of the sun becomes the upward journey to the world of light. The scene has been summed up by Morenz: The figure on the throne is Re-Harachty, and an inscription informs us that he is both Re and Horus, Re of the Horizon; the horizon is the moment of passage denoted by the name of Khepri, the figure of the beetle, who represents the changing of forms or appearances.[111]

As for the ape, in the boat he is Thoth: “I am Thoth, the eldest son of Re, whom Atum has fashioned (msἰ ), created from Khepri. . . . I descended to earth with the secrets of ‘what belongs to the horizon.’ ” [112] For Selim Hassan, Thoth in the boat gives us “a glimpse of profound thinking and poetical expression,” for he represents Wisdom itself (Thoth) setting the course of the “ship of the primeval.” [113] Here too we find reconciliation of the Osiris mysteries with the solar cult. Back in the Old Kingdom the defunct Unas remains in the coffin chamber of his pyramid only long enough to undergo rapid transformation during which he briefly assumes the name of Osiris (“who is dead” ), only to relinquish it upon leaving the sarcophagus chamber to travel by boat under the new identification of Horus the son of Osiris.[114] The whole course of the fearful passage to the underworld and back is described in the Pyramid Texts.

Thus we see in figure 3 how all things represent a process, change, motion from one state or condition to another, constantly changing form but ever preserving identity, which the Egyptians designated as ḫprw. It must be clearly understood that this term has nothing to do with metempsychosis, or transmigration of souls. As in the seven ages of man, the changes of form and appearance do not mean that the old identity is lost; on the contrary, the ḫpr is a voluntary change of form—with the individual always in control—and thus a heightened assertion of individual identity.[115] It is not the loss but the intensifying of the ego; the initiate can change his form to match his environment or stage of the journey precisely because he is master of the situation. The main purpose of transformation is to escape dangerous situations, which makes it a form of adaptive behavior to assure the continued existence of the individual under all circumstances—it is a kind of natural selection, the very opposite of transmigration.

The ape from the ship wears the same old- and new-moon or sun- and moon-crowns as our figures 22 and 23 do in Facsimile 2. In either case it represents the moment of the new moon and shows the conjunction of sun and moon in their related cycles, consistent with the embodiment of Thoth the ape as the moon-cycle.[116] All of this is no more puzzling than the jumbling of our alphabet to make words and sentences. The ape, the beetle, and the sun-god appear in about every possible combination. The properties of the play are few—ship, crown, scepter, throne, shrine, cabin, canopy, cenotaph, in various combinations. Above all there is the wedjat-eye. Thus we find the sun-god and the ape flanking and both facing the scarab; sometimes the sun-god alone faces the beetle, who is above a cabin; sometimes a giant scarab is all alone in the boat with or without a cabin; sometimes it is the ape who occupies the shrine faced by another seated ape; sometimes a lone seated ape faces a scarab; sometimes the ape sits in solitary splendor in the shrine with no other figures in sight.

The main activity in this panel on most hypocephali is the handing around of the wedjat-eye. Thus we find the ape presenting the eye to the sun-god; [117] or the ape in a shrine wearing the solar disk receiving the wedjat from another ape who also wears upon his head yet another solar disk containing another wedjat-eye; [118] or an ape in the shrine receiving the wedjat-eye; [119] or a giant wedjat-eye held by an ibis-headed Thoth and worshipped by two apes; [120] or an ape presenting the eye to the sun-god—facing to the right as in our figure 3—between them a scarab labeled Khepri; [121] or a large ape in a shrine receives the eye from another ape—the infant rides up in the bow; [122] or the sun-god and the apes flank a Khepri-scarab to whom the ape is offering the eye.[123] Quite often the ape offers the eye to the crowned ape in the shrine; [124] the ape and the eye alone in the boat; [125] or the ape in a shrine faced by an ape with two wedjat-eyes.[126] Thus Khepri, the scarab, changes his position from hypocephalus to hypocephalus with the greatest freedom—he moves all over the place, as is proper for one who is the very embodiment of change.

We go on with these seemingly endless permutations to indicate what a hive of activity figure 3 represents, a lively exchange of position and powers with a full display of “power and authority, . . . the grand Key-words of the Holy Priesthood” being passed around, here represented as visual symbols, as we know it descended to “Seth, Noah, Melchizedek, Abraham, and all to whom the Priesthood was revealed.”

The cast of characters is very limited in the Joseph Smith version, but the idea is fully represented by the presence of the most potent symbol of all, the two wedjat-eyes flanking Horus-Re. They must be considered in the context of figure 7, but first we must look at figure 4.

Facsimile 2, Figure 4

Figure 4 is another form of the same god as in figure 3, another hawk in transition. Fortunately Professors G. A. Gaballa and Kenneth A. Kitchen have made a thorough study of this figure. He is Sokar, and the New Year is his time marked by various archaic New Year celebrations.[127] At Karnak “the whole complex would seem to be dedicated to the renewal of the divine king in terms of the Festival of Sokar . . . and the Sed-feast or jubilee. . . . The emphasis is on the King and his renewal.” [128] The idea is most potently symbolized in this figure. The bird on the boat is rising with outspread wings, shedding the mummy wrappings that still envelop his legs. This is “Sokar’s great festival,” as our authorities tell us, “fused with that of Osiris. [Everything] is at an end; but the next day (1st Peret 1),” the first day of the New Year, Horus mounts the throne “guaranteeing the rightful rule of Pharaoh.” [129] It is the old king resurrected in the new. It was at dawn of the day of the winter solstice after a night of mourning “that Sokar-Osiris made union with the solar-disk,” the Re/Osiris meeting, just as the sun struck the top of the benben-stone,[130] the most sacred and probably the oldest monument of Heliopolis (see pp. 342–43, fig. 41); its image occurs two and possibly three times on the rim of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus (see appendix 2B). Its significance will soon become apparent, for it is the stone on which the resurrected bird sits at sunrise.

Also significant is the resemblance of this ship to the one in figure 3. Both boats belong to the moon as well as to the sun, for the crouching Sokar-bird is also Khonsu, whose name means “he who traverses”—that the moon in the sky.[131] Like Janus, Khonsu has no myth, and there is no story about him; he is “the royal ‘twin’ elevated to the position of a god.” [132] All of which makes clear that Khonsu is first and foremost the moon.[133] The suppliant begs to join his following, “Grant that I may be his attendant in the great Nešmet-boat on the day of the Wg-festival; that my name may be called when he is praised; that my head be crowned with justification, . . . that I may be attached to the subjects of Sokar.” [134]

Our figure 4 goes back to the earliest Egyptian iconography, found on an ivory comb belonging to King Djet (serpent) from the First Dynasty (ca. 2840 b.c.); on it has been drawn “a boat beneath which two wings representing the sky are spread.” [135] It is “the sun-ship on the wings of the sky, . . . the two outstretched wings above the earth, the sheltering wings of the sky-god, from which was later derived the idea of the hawk as the sky-god.” [136] The inscription with the relief from Edfu adds: “the expanse [circumference] of the heavens is beneath his wings; . . . your body . . . is the sky which is adorned with its stars.” [137] The bird in the boat is sometimes exchanged for the sky-goddess Nut, whose outstretched wings are the symbol of protection,[138] their purpose being to enfold and embrace everything (fig. 30). She is often represented on the lids of coffins in the same crouching position with the same outspread wings as the bird; for Hans Bonnet the wings show that the woman is the bird “which is usually put in place of her.” [139] The best-known Egyptian symbol of the sky, she controls not only the cycle of the stars but also that of the sun.[140]

Figure 30. Carved on the interior roof of the second shrine, this delicate gold-covered bas-relief shows the goddess Nut under the heaven symbol (), spreading her winged arms protectively over the sarcophagus of Pharaoh Tutankhamun. Shrine IV, ca. 1330 b.c. Copyright Institut français d’archÉologie orientale.

			 In the more elaborate hypocephali, the space above Sokar is occupied by the eight rams, sometimes drawn out in full in the upper left-hand panel of the hypocephalus, sometimes indicated simply by a ram and eight strokes with the inscription “You are the eight rams” [141] or “Your ba is to you as the eight rams.” [142] With the completion of the eighth hour in the Amduat, the god assumes the form of a ram, a viable ba, on his way out of the underworld. Again figure 4 marks the turning point; the eight bas are always drawn directly over the emerging Sokar. Thus they represent the Ogdoad, the genealogy of the first gods. Likewise the eight apes and eight rams that appear on some of the more splendid hypocephali can fuse—the eight apes worship the central figure in some hypocephali as the eight rams face the standing figure in others.

So far so good for Smith; all that seems quite obvious, but what about the next statement: “Also a numerical figure in Egyptian signifying 1000” ? Professional Egyptologists have protested to the author that there is nothing known to them to justify attributing the number 1000 to figure 4. Yet here, if ever, the Joseph Smith explanation is right on target. The woman Nut, the sky-goddess of the outspread wings, has a peculiar epithet, and it is the same name as that given to the ship in figure 4, ḫ-b=s, which means literally “a Thousand Are Her Souls,” or “The One with a Thousand Souls.” [143] The Thousand Souls are stars, and she is so called because the stars are her children; a Pyramid Text says, “You (Nut) have taken to yourself every god who has his own ship (ḥb) and have instructed them in the starry sky (ḫ-b=s) so that they will not depart from you as stars. Do not let NN be moved far from you in your name of ‘Heaven’ (ḥr.t).” [144]

And this takes us to Abraham. Not only was he asked to count the stars as a metaphorical measure of his progeny, but he meets us in Genesis 15:5 as both an observer ( הבט ḥabbet) and counter ( לספוך, li-spôr) of the stars. We also see him, according to Facsimile 3, “reasoning on the principles of Astronomy, in the king’s court” (Fac. 3, fig. 1, explanation). Reasoning and counting are the same word in the famous stele of a great princess, a daughter of Psammeticus II, which reads, “Behold ye Khabasu of Heliopolis . . . the God is born . . . one who can take the helm. Osiris Anchnesneferibre (the princess) will reckon (calculate, reason, wḏ) with you concerning the secret which is in the Great Hall (ws.t, of the palace) of the gods and will take along Osiris in his Ship of a Thousand, even with the two heads, so that by it he can mount to heaven and to the counterheaven.” [145] This is our figure 4.

Even more impressive is the way the Joseph Smith explanation seems to parrot everything the Wörterbuch says about the Khabasu. According to the Wörterbuch, ḫ-b.ś means: “Literally, ‘a thousand fold is her [the goddess of heaven] souls,’ as a collective designation for the host of stars, the stars.” [146] It is made of the three words ḫ, “a thousand”; b, “spirit, soul”; and the pronominal suffix ś, “her” or “its.” Faulkner’s dictionary renders ḫbs as “the starry sky.” [147] Hannig’s dictionary renders ḫbs (ḫ-b=s) as “[collective] stars, the ocean of stars.” [148] This agrees with the first two elements of Joseph Smith’s explanation, first that it “answers to the Hebrew word Raukeeyang, signifying expanse, or the firmament of the heavens,” and that it is “also a numerical figure, in Egyptian signifying one thousand” (Fac. 2, fig. 4, explanation).

The third most significant thing about figure 4, according to Joseph Smith, is its office in “answering to the measuring of time,” namely by the cycles and revolutions of the heavens. Since all these figures mark both the completion and initiation of various life cycles, time is of the essence and the figure of the Sokar-ship is the most important agent of coordination. It was at the sed-festival or jubilee that the bird was borne forth in procession on his ship. It is specifically figure 4 that coordinates the funereal with the astral theme by virtue by its “calendrical” significance—that is, as the primordial measurement of time.[149]

The Boat Ride

The great feast of Sokar at Medinet Habu had as its main event the carrying of the Sokar-bark around the temple. It was called “Dragging Ptah-Sokar-Osiris (and) Going around the Walls by the King Himself.” [150] Sokar’s procession around the temple, according to William J. Murnane, matches “the sun’s daily path across the sky and through the underworld”; it marks the passing of time and “brings order out of chaos.” [151] Osiris rewards his son Horus with “millions and myriads of years forever.” [152] His festival “was a feast of renewal and reaffirmation, . . . the promise of life after death.” [153] An inscription from Denderah coordinates the whole thing: “The Great God, Lord of Heaven, with dappled feathers, coming forth from the horizon as the winged disk, president of the gods, head of the temple . . . Horus the great, King of Kings, the One and Only who hides himself (as Amun); Most High God (nb-r-ḏr), Creator, Father . . . the Horus of Horuses makes his way to the upper realm, and his falcon form rests on Maat (or Shu). He is the fair youth born in the morning; he is Re going up in old age, setting in the land of Manu. The morning breaks for his rebirth.” [154] Being both young and old is another aspect which invites comparison with Janus.

The hawk in figure 4 is indeed the rising sun emerging from the lower regions, faithfully preserving the resurrection-creation motif of the whole hypocephalus. In the Coffin Text, Osiris and Sokar are “virtually identical.” [155]

“Oliblish” is manifestly on the main circuit with Kolob, figure 2 being halfway between figures 3 and 4. We have passed from galaxy to solar system to the earth’s sublunary authority. Khonsu’s link to Re-Amun is the bond between the moon and the sun, while figure 1 represents the great cosmic center.

Murnane has also pointed out the prominence of the lotus blossom in the cult, as representing the coming forth of the light that is latent in Sokar and blossoms forth when the beams of light reach the depths of the underworld. The lotus signifies the new creation, “order out of chaos.” [156] This is of particular interest since the lotus which appears on the altar of the ship in figure 3 of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus is an anomaly, as is that in Facsimile 1. The unusual frequency of the lotus in all three facsimiles is noteworthy and plausible.[157] In Facsimile 1 it was claimed by Egyptologists that Joseph Smith had inserted it, but the discovery of the original proved that that was not so—it belongs where we see it. Among the many symbols of the lotus for the Egyptians, it is especially the agent of the daily renewal of the sun.[158] It is also the heraldic flower of Lower Egypt.[159]

As to the relationship of Kolob and Oliblish, it hooks figure 4 in on the main circuit with Oliblish, figure 2, as the go-between. Khonsu’s link to Re-Amun is the bond between the moon and the sun while figure 1, as noted above, represents the great cosmic center. The upper part of the hypocephalus brings together sun, moon, and stars in their various relationships.

Down Under

After figure 4 we go upside down. A century ago Philippe J. de Horrack displayed insight into the hypocephalus which has never been challenged: “All these different symbols simply represent on the one side the female, and on the other the male element, to express the idea of eternal generative power.” [160] The lower part of the circle represents “the lower or dark hemisphere” from which the sun emerged “to mark the beginning of time.” [161] The wedjat-eye “conveys the idea of the renewal of a period, like the full-moon, the solstice, the equinoxes, etc., and it designates here the accomplishment of the period of resurrection, always assimilated with the daily and annual revival of the sun.” [162] The circle is divided to represent “the two celestial hemispheres.” [163] All details refer to “the resurrection and the renewed birth after death, and this idea is more particularly symbolized by the course of the sun, the living image of divine generation.” [164]

In the Egyptian temple there is “a tendency to harmonize two opposites (which is fundamental to Egyptian thought) . . . reflected throughout the plan of the temple.” [165] There is also a vertical, or a right and left, division in the hypocephali, as well as a horizontal division with the same connotation—the day and night ships of heaven and counterheaven respectively are also the left and right eyes, the male (Shu) and female (Tefnut) elements, sun and moon, etc.[166] Throughout the Egyptian temple ceremonies “everything to the right of the worshipper has the idea of light, of life; everything on the left represents the idea of darkness, of death.” [167] This right and left division is as basic to the hypocephalus as the up and down one. Thus the two dominant male figures (Fac. 2, figs. 1 and 2) are both divided neatly down the middle, each facing both right and left.

In the Berlin Book of Wandering through Eternity, which is related to the Book of Breathings, we see the bird of light as “Messenger of the Lord of the Horizon” making his rounds between the two worlds.[168]

A well-known characteristic of Egyptian thinking is the duality of all phenomena. It is dramatized in the two kingdoms, two crowns, two thrones, two palaces, two tombs, etc., of the kings. “The calendric symbolism . . . of Gem-Aten, was to impress with the yearly cycle of the season of months, days in a duality of ritual at sunrise and sunset.” [169] It requires a reverse or counterheaven as well; “the most prominent belief was that we have a complete universe of sky and earth which has its counterpart reversed below it.” [170] This is a reminder that the function of the hypocephalus is to maintain ties between that which is in heaven and that which is in the tomb, a joining of the worlds above and below clearly designated in the opposing parts of the disk.

It is also a reminder of those accounts in the Books of Moses and Abraham in which peculiar emphasis is placed on the importance of division in the creative process. The sun is to rule over the day and the moon over the night “to divide the light from the darkness” (Moses 2:18). For life to appear on land it was also necessary to divide “the waters which were under the expanse from the waters which were above the expanse” (Abraham 4:7). In many hypocephali the two ships sailing in the solar upper part of the circle are matched by two ships (or sometimes one) sailing in the lower half. “The Gods organized the lights in the expanse of the heaven, and caused them to divide the day from the night; and organized them to be for signs and for seasons, and for days and for years” (Abraham 4:14). The same thing applies to human reproduction: “And from that time forth, the sons and daughters of Adam began to divide two and two in the land” (Moses 5:3), recalling the same order by which the animals entered the ark as well as the primal Ogdoad of Heliopolis in which each god has his female counterpart (see p. 244, fig. 23).
