8

Reading the Hypocephalus: Part 2, Figures 5–21

			 Facsimile 2 shows how God is everywhere. In figure 1 we are as near to “the residence of God” as we can get; everything points to the immovable stability around which all else revolves. Figure 3 is “God, sitting upon his throne,” visibly moving through the heavens, of which the sun is the similitude. Figure 7 is also God seated upon his throne. The long panel—figures 5, 6, and 7—is an example of an art form the Egyptians were fond of, depicting a procession of events by a series of drawings showing a progression both in time and space. Such are the vignettes in the Book of the Dead, the Amduat, and the Book of the Two Ways and in various historic battle scenes such as the Battle of Kadesh.

The need to keep moving is fundamental, as we learn in the Egyptian Wisdom literature: “Human life is subject to changes that are decreed by God.” [1] It is that Egyptian obsession with the Way, the idea of our entire existence as an endless journey, as Richard Lepsius noted long ago,[2] which is expressed on every page of the funerary literature, climaxing in the eternal pilgrimage of Papyrus Leiden T 32.[3]

The panel comprised of figures 5, 6, and 7 is one of the longest and fullest presentations in all the hypocephali. It is also the most often reproduced, the most unvarying, changeless part of all hypocephali, the most important, and the only series of scenes which the Egyptians themselves have fully explained for us.

Figure 5: Supercow

The single graphic panel in the reverse segment of almost any hypocephalus is dominated by the cow, thus fulfilling the instructions for the making of a hypocephalus by drawing a cow in pure gold, accompanied by inscriptions, on a fresh new papyrus.[4] It is part of a family group which includes the standing female figure with her back turned to figure 7—she is obviously ministering to the cow (see p. 322, fig. 37). This pair in fact is perhaps the most uniform and unchanging motif on all the hypocephali. It is not surprising that this is the longest and most involved of all of Joseph Smith’s explanations, for this is by all odds the most important figure on the disk, and we must pay her close attention, for when all elements of the hypocephalus are stripped away in order of their importance it is always the cow that remains, central and indispensable. One side of the two-sided Nash hypocephalus shows only a picture of a cow with the inscription, “Hathor the Mistress of Kingdom of the Dead” (see appendix 7B).[5] The only other single-image hypocephali are those showing a great solitary wedjat-eye, and we are most explicitly told that in this combination of symbols, “The Heavenly Cow [Mḥ(.t)-wr.t, Mehweret] is the wedjat-eye.” [6] Even more commanding of the student’s interest is the hypocephalus handbook (Book of the Dead chapter 162), in which the owner of the hypocephalus in his initiation must gain acceptance by identifying himself: “I am the Heavenly Cow [ʾIḥ.t, Ihet], your name is in my mouth, and I shall utter it. . . . I am the Heavenly Cow; hear my voice on this day.” [7] The instructions in the handbook further state: “To be recited over a figure of the Heavenly Cow, which is made of fine gold and placed around the neck of the glorified being, and also put in writing upon new papyrus and placed under his head. . . . This (i.e., the hypocephalus) is a very great protection which the Heavenly Cow made for her son, Re, when he set. . . . He (the deceased) shall be made divine in the afterlife and he shall not be turned back from any door of the hereafter successfully.” [8]

The Great Cow is Hathor or Isis, says Plutarch,[9] and Apuleius addresses her as “Queen of Heaven” [10] and “the fertile image of the universal divine parent.” [11] Though these are the characterizations of classical writers, the cow was no local deity later exalted to glory following the familiar pattern; hers is a “non-localized myth whose origin was in the sky”; [12] this cow of heaven is the prehistoric mate of the celestial bull (seen in the rim of Fac. 2, fig. 18; see appendix 2C) and may even account for the universal designation of the Milky Way. But though she stands out in bold simplicity, the Divine Cow “is a strange composite of contrary conceptions,” as Rudolf Anthes puts it.[13] Actually, the cow “represents an entire series of major divinities . . . all of which can be represented by the Celestial Cow, of which the Sun is the calf.” [14] She is Mḥ.t-wr.t, “the primal flood and the first living thing . . . called Methyer ‘the great flood-water’. . . the cosmic personification of the primal waters.” [15] Her story is told in the Book of Abraham (Abraham 1:23–24), where the cow is a metaphor for Egyptus, the great Queen Mother, the first to enter Egypt after the flood when it was still all wetlands—the classic representation shows the head and foreparts of a huge longhorn cow emerging from a veritable forest of papyrus (see p. 93, fig. 9).

The king sets the example of entering into his mother’s womb to be reborn.[16] In the Pyramid Texts the king rejoices, “It is my rebirth today, you gods . . . it is Nut who has borne me.” [17] Thus “the dead king has won a new life of eternal youth in the sky-goddess,” and from her he will never be weaned.[18] Even more graphic was the preparation of Osiris for his rebirth by being laid in a wooden cow-coffin.[19]

All of this is simply following the example of the Sun, whose “rejuvenating journey . . . can take place within the body of the Sky-goddess”; it is ritually repeated for all the dead who by entering the Waters of Nun are rejuvenated and “ ‘slough off’ their previous existence and ‘put on’ another.” [20] The Pyramid Texts teach us that the Heavenly Cow may help the dead to rise up and ascend to heaven even as she once carried the royal mummy of Re/Osiris to heaven on her back,[21] a scene vividly depicted on a number of hypocephali; as noted above, it occupies one whole side of the Nash hypocephalus (see appendix 7B).

But Why the Stars?

While Nut is the whole, Hathor is something very different, for her name is written with a picture of a house or temple with Horus inside it, . Thus she appears here as a cosmic place of some sort, and the return to the womb is really a return to the Heavenly Mother and home. Some interpret Hathor’s name as meaning “My house is in heaven,” but most scholars read it “House of Horus”; [22] Plutarch calls it “the Cosmic House of Horus.” [23] Interpreted either way, Hathor was a house, dwelling place, or womb, the place where Horus held forth before his earthly birth.

But Joseph Smith also tells us that figure 5 is the Sun. No problem. From being the mother of the Sun with the newborn disk rising between her horns—a design in evidence in prehistoric times—it was an easy step to becoming the Sun itself.[24] As early as the Old Kingdom, the cow appears “as the female equivalent of Re.” [25] At Opet in Luxor, where the Mother-Cow was worshipped as Hathor of Coptus, she was called the Sun of the Two Worlds—that is, both of Horus the son of Osiris and Amun-Re the Sun of Thebes.[26] Her horns, flanked by the same two feathers that our figure 2 wears as the Sun at the zenith, show that the cow resurrects the Sun as well as the human race.[27]

It is instructive to see how Brother Joseph explains the cow. First it should be noted that there is not the slightest indication in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus that this or any other figure might have anything to do with the starry heavens. Yet that is the Prophet’s main concern and the principal quality of the cow: “It is necessary to consider this cow as an emblem of heaven where millions of stars are gathered together, named after living beings.” [28] Joseph Smith’s main points are:

1. That the cow is the Sun.

2. That it borrows its light.

3. That the borrowing is done through intermediaries. The terms borrowing, medium, and governing applied throughout the system are here most explicit, bespeaking a hierarchical order of shared energy.

4. There is a key or control to the whole thing, the idea expressed by the label of Kae-e-vanrash.

5. It is said to be “the governing power, which governs fifteen other fixed planets or stars” (Fac. 2, fig. 5, explanation). We should note here that the word star has been used freely by ancients and moderns alike to designate any bright object in the heavens, from the sun to a meteorite.

6. Included in the same system is the local family or group of “the Moon, the Earth and the Sun in their annual revolutions” (Fac. 2, fig. 5, explanation). This is the only time the moon is specifically mentioned in the explanation.

7. All these bodies are interconnected in their motions and exchanges of energies, and all are ultimately dependent on Kolob.

The Odd Number

In his discussion of the Book of the Cow in the royal tombs, Charles Maystre pays special attention to the Tutankhamun version, the most carefully executed of the Heavenly Cow pictures (see p. 324, fig. 38): “Along the belly of the cow are stars.” [29] These are set in a line; at the front end is the familiar solar-bark bearing the symbol of Shemsu, the following or entourage, and at the rear end of the line is another ship bearing the same emblem. Both boats are sailing in the same direction through the heavens. The number of stars varies among the cows; in the instructions contained in the tomb of Seti I, it is specified that there should be nine, though the three groups of three strokes each can, and often do, signify an innumerable host.[30]

The number here plainly belongs to the cow, but what about the fifteen stars? “The number fifteen cannot be derived from any holy number of the Egyptians,” writes Hermann Kees, and yet it presents “a surprising analogy” with the fifteen false doors in the great wall of the Djoser complex at Saqqara, which was designed by the great Imhotep himself, with the Festival of the Heavens of Heliopolis in mind, following the older pattern of the White Wall of the Thinite palace of Memphis.[31] Strangely enough this number fifteen keeps turning up all along, and nobody knows why, though it always represents passing from one gate or door to another. Long after Djoser, Amenophis III built a wall for his royal circumambulation at the sed-festival, marking the inauguration of a new age of the world; it also had fifteen gates.[32]

Figure 31. Light in the form of suns and stars radiates from the head of the upside-down Horus emerging from the sun-boat. P. Amonemsaf. Cairo Museum.

				 In the funeral papyrus of Amonemsaf, in a scene in which the hawk comes from the starry heavens to minister to the mummy, “the illustration . . . is separated into two halves by the sign of the sky ” —the heaven above and the tomb below (fig. 31). Between the mummy and the depths and the hawk in the heaven, there are twelve red dots and fifteen stars.[33] Again, twelve is the best-known astral number, but why fifteen? One Egyptologist wrote years ago that “The author’s impression is that these [the fifteen stars] are purely theological features without astronomical significance.” [34] “The ‘great Ennead which resides in Karnak’ swelled to fifteen members.” [35] It was structured in three phases: one became two, two became four, four became eight, which is fifteen altogether. Étienne Drioton associates this with the dividing of eternity in the drama of Edfu into years, years into months, and months into fifteen units, these units into hours, and they into minutes.[36]

Furthermore the idea of fifteen mediums or conveyors may be represented on the fifteen limestone tablets of the Book of the Underworld found by Theodore Davies and Howard Carter in the tomb of Hatshepsut.[37] Let us recall that the basic idea, as Joseph Smith explains it, is that “fifteen other fixed planets or stars” act as a medium for conveying “the governing power.” Coming down to a later time of the Egyptian gnostics, we find the fifteen helpers (παραστάται, parastatai) of the seven virgins of light in the Coptic Pistis Sophia, who “expanded themselves in the regions of the twelve saviors and the rest of the angels of the midst; each according to its glory will rule with me in an inheritance of light.” [38] In an equally interesting Coptic text, 2 Jeu, there are also fifteen parastatai who serve with “the seven virgins of the light” who are with the “father of all fatherhoods, . . . in the Treasury of the Light.” [39] They are the light virgins who are “in the middle or the midst (μέσος, mesos),” meaning that they are go-betweens.[40] Parastatai are those who conduct one through a series of ordinances, just as the fifteen stars receive and convey light.

In all this we never get away from cosmology and astronomy. In the Old Slavonic Secrets of Enoch, “four great stars, each having one thousand stars under it,” go with “fifteen myriads of angels,” [41] all moving, to quote Joseph Smith, together with “the Moon, the Earth, and the Sun in their annual revolutions.” In the Book of Gates, one of those mystery texts reserved for the most secret rites of the greatest kings, we see, as Gustave Jéquier describes it, “a long horizontal bar with a bull’s head at either end, supported by eight mummiform personages, and carrying seven other genies” (fig. 32).[42] These fifteen figures are designated as carriers or bearers (f.w), and the bar is the body of the bull extended to give them all room. A rope enters the bull’s mouth at one end and exits at the other, and on the end of this rope there is a sun-bark being towed by a total of eight personages designated as stars.[43] Just as the ship travels through the fifteen conveyor stars on the underside of the cow, the two heads of the bull make him interchangeable with the two-headed lion Aker, or Ruty, who guards the gate. “Aker,” writes Jéquier, “is a personification of the gates of the earth by which the sun must pass in the evening and in the morning.” [44] He is the same as the two-headed Janus, the gate-god, whom we have already met.

Figure 32. The ram-headed sun-god, surrounded by the serpent Mehen, is towed by eight personified stars through a mysterious pipe with a bull and bull’s head at each end, carried by eight standing figures alternating with seven seated figures. Book of Gates, Tomb of Ramses I, ca. 1290 b.c., Valley of the Kings.

Figure 6: Canopic Figures

It is notable with what care all the draftsmen of the various hypocephali place the fertilizing object in the same position, exactly over the cow’s posterior. The four figures facing the cow carry on the theme. If you are going to have either a birth or rebirth, it will require the bringing together of the elements of this earth. These elements are represented by the four canopic figures or “sons of Horus,” [45] designated as figure 6. They “represent,” says Joseph Smith in his explanation to Facsimile 2, “the earth in its four quarters” (Fac. 2, fig. 6). In Facsimile 1, as the four jars under the bier, they have a historical and geographical significance, being the gods of four regions of the earth embraced in the worship of Pharaoh (Fac. 1, figs. 5–8). The history we have treated elsewhere.[46] In Facsimile 2 their function is primarily cosmic. The canopic figures are of the earth, earthy; they hold the most perishable internal organs of the individual. The mummified body is an empty husk until the spirit returns to it, when the vital parts must also be included to restore everything to its proper and perfect form. They were removed and placed in jars for safekeeping. Inscriptions on the four jars sometimes contain prayers to the divinities to whom the protection of the internal organ is especially entrusted, namely the four sons of Horus and the four great goddesses: Isis, Nephthys, Neith, and Selket.[47] The four represent various things but always with the idea of resurrection; though they contain only certain organs they represent the restoration of the entire body.[48] The four carry on the same work of completion and perfection as the wedjat-eye; indeed Théodule Devéria saw in figure 1 of Facsimile 2 with its four faces “the spirit of the four elements, . . . the four winds or the four cardinal points; the soul of the material world.” [49] Following Devéria, Philippe J. de Horrack also saw “the spirit of the four winds or four cardinal points.” [50] At Opet the “genies of the four winds” in that capacity go back to the Pyramid Texts.[51] It is interesting that while W. M. Flinders Petrie declared quaintly: “It may be safely said that there is not one single word that is true in these explanations,” [52] E. A. Wallis Budge wrote of the same canopic figures in the Meux hypocephalus, “these jars . . . represented the south, north, east, and west respectively.” [53]

It has often been noted that these figures are the four creatures of Ezekiel and Revelation as well as the symbols of the four evangelists.[54] Jéquier notes that “there is a singular parallelism between the names of the four cynocephalic apes, personifications of the primeval abyss, and the four words employed in the account of the creation at the beginning of Genesis.” [55] The names of the four sons of Horus are also star gods that follow behind the Big Dipper.[56]

Cyprian, in his work on Zion, maintains that Adam had his name from the four cardinal points.[57] The Jewish tradition held that Adam was composed of earth taken from the four corners of the world representing the four basic elements (earth, water, air, and fire), the four humors of man in medical alchemy, and for the Egyptians the four basic colors and the four races of man.[58] The name Adam in Hebrew (אדם ʾādām) is related to red earth (אדמה, ădāmāh), the pure undefiled earth of the Egyptian red rock country, called by them dšr.t, Desheret, “the red land, the desert.” [59]

The canopic figures, then, represent the basic elements of which all earthly things are composed and which must be brought together in correct shape and proportion to achieve the birth or rebirth of an earthly creature. This takes us back to Genesis. The Egyptian names of the four cynocephalic apes surrounding the throne of the central figure of the hypocephalus (only two are shown in Facsimile 2), nw, ḥḥw, kkw, and tnmw suggest so strongly the בהו, תהו, and 	דוח אלהים (tōhû, bōhû, ḥōšek, and rûaḥ ĕlōhîm) of the Hebrew creation story in Genesis that Jéquier can declare that “it is difficult to believe that . . . the Elohist author [of the Genesis creation account] did not borrow from a Heliopolitan source.” [60] They represent, according to Jéquier, “chaos, . . . darkness, and space dominating matter unorganized (l’espace dominant la matière inerte).” [61] We should recall at this point that the hypocephalus is meant to go back to “the first creation” (Fac. 2, fig. 1, explanation).

Perpetuating the Order

Once a biosystem is established on earth, the next problem is how to keep it self-operating, as is pointed out in Abraham 4:18, 21, 25, where the gods, having established various systems, “watched those things which they had ordered until they obeyed,” after which point they could be trusted to carry on by themselves.

Figure 33. The great cycle of life is symbolized by these three living creatures as shown in (A) Ashmolean 1982-1095 and (B) Fac. 2, behind fig. 6. Courtesy of the Church History Library, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints; hereafter Church History Library.

			 Behind the four canopic figures are three enigmatic objects often omitted from this panel in other hypocephali when the space is too crowded for them. They are the lotus leaf, the lion, and the ram. Fortunately, when they have to be left out, their place is regularly taken by a less bulky substitute, the scarab. This is particularly welcome because it shows us exactly what the function of the missing figures really is. For the Khepri-beetle, proclaiming the possibility of changing form while preserving identity, shows that the life process is to be carried on from one form of being to another. The Egyptians were fond of representing the idea by polliwogs and beetles. Marie-Louise Ryhiner notes that the lotus blossom, the lion, and the ram symbolize the gods of the rising, midday, and setting sun—that is, Re, Khepri, and Atum.[62] It also suggests the food chain in which the ram eats the plant, the lion eats the ram, and the lion, as Samson reminds us (see Judges 14:14), in turn becomes the food base for new vegetation (fig. 33).

That this is a valid interpretation is strongly indicated in the Sumerian record where the three figures occur frequently combined in the oldest glyptic art of the Near East. Anton Moortgat has explained how everywhere the carnivore, ruminant, and green plant are found in combinations on the oldest seals, reliefs, and vessels of Mesopotamia, whence the motifs spread to other parts of the world.[63]

Figure 7: The Man on the Throne

Figure 7 “represents God sitting upon his throne.” But figure 3 is also “made to represent God, sitting upon his throne,” here in another capacity. At the same time figure 7 is “also the sign of the Holy Ghost unto Abraham.” Yet another progression is the three degrees of glory with prelude and postlude shown on Facsimile 2: from the eternal background (fig. 1) to the glory of the sun at the zenith (fig. 2), to the glory of the moon (fig. 3), to the glory of the stars, the Khabasu (fig. 4)—and so down to earth with figures 5, 6, and 7.

The man on the throne is Min, the oldest incarnation of the Father, Creator, King, Most High God. He was brought to Egypt by the first immigrants, Naqadah I and II.[64] Notice that the image does not depict God, but is a representation; the artist, like a mathematician, can use anything he pleases to represent anything else as long as he explains it. Likewise, what is being handed over is not the Holy Ghost but “the sign of the Holy Ghost” —a sign does not describe, it only points to something.

The epithet of Min is “He of the upraised hand,” and his identification is the flail and the erect phallus with which he appears in the oldest known Egyptian statue—he is always in human form.[65] They are also the signs of procreation.

Min was intimately related to Amun, and Amun was probably derived from him.[66] As the Supreme God “Amun was Zeus, . . . the meteorite and omphalos were representatives, the one of the other in ancient art and religion,” the meteoric form was displayed as the classical thunderbolt.[67] The sacred stone from the skies after it landed was venerated as the omphalos, the navel of the earth at various ceremonial gathering places of the race; they combine the two concepts of heavenly visitations to earth and the great assembly of the human race.[68] In particular, the sacred symbol of Amun was made of smaragdus—a green stone, which in the ancient world was connected with meteorites, and was intended to act as a substitute for an original meteorite, which served as an omphalos—the center of the earth—at Abydos.[69]

As the name shows, “Amun is the other self of Min; . . . his high priest was called ‘The Opener of the Gates of Heaven,’ while the high priest of Min at Letopolis was the ‘Opener of the Mouth upon the Earth,’ i.e., the mortals here on earth upon whom the heavenly power was conferred.” [70] Finally, “Min belonged to the Bull-gods . . . while Amun belonged to the Ram-gods.” [71] The Bull dominates the Nun of outer space on our hypocephalus (Fac. 2, fig. 18), while Amun monopolizes the rest. In short, without further exploiting the efforts of Professor Gerald A. Wainwright, it should be apparent to all that Min is transferring life from worlds above to the earth below.

Running through the list of Min’s attributes, we learn that he is the great consort, Lord of Reproduction, human and animal.[72] As a vegetation god, Min had a garden in Achmim, and he was celebrated in the great harvest festival that is known from monuments from the New Kingdom.[73] The Greeks and Romans associated him with Pan and Priapus.[74] Min is the “Creator god “who made the heaven and brought forth the gods, who made the earth and created men . . . and who keeps all things alive.” [75]

Figure 34. The earliest example of the name of the serpent-god Nḥb-kw appears in the (A) Pyramid Texts (§489) of Unas with a unique determinative , ca. 2340 b.c.; (B) Tshemmin, one of the owners of the Joseph Smith Papyri, stands in front of Sata, another form of legged serpent, ca. 3rd century b.c., JSP V; (C) hawk-headed Nehebkau offers the wedjat-eye in the hypocephalus BM 37908, as in the (D) Leiden hypocephalus AMS 62. (E) The Church Historian’s copy shows what survived of the damaged hypocephalus, while (F) Hedlock’s version attempted to complete it. The curious symbol at the bottom may echo the earliest determinative (A). Church History Library (B, E, F).

		 To emphasize the god’s function as Min-Amun, the great procreator, he holds high the flail which indicates conception; it is the februa of the oldest Roman New Year’s rite of the Lupercal, when the women would stand at the doors of their houses, holding out their hands to be slapped by a running priest with the februa—that was to make them fertile.[76] It was the role played by the sportive Anthony at the beginning of Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar.[77]

The Serpent

But to the serpent! The strange-looking creature we have here is the Egyptian Nḥb-kw, Nehebkau (fig. 34). The name means something like “the provider of kas.” [78] He stood guard at the entrance of the hereafter and examined the dead to see if they were qualified to enter into the blessed realm. If they were he then provided them with the ka, the power that made eternal life possible.[79] In the 149th chapter of the Book of the Dead the deceased moves through fourteen locations (ἰ.wt, literally “mounds” ) in his quest for eternal life. We find Nehebkau active in the very first location as the deceased enters and asks Nehebkau to ensure that his body is whole and properly functioning, that he be given the double crown of Atum, and that he might rule with the gods and Min the creator.[80] The name of the god Atum is related to the verb tm “to be complete,” [81] and the nouns tm “everything, the universe,” [82] and tmw “the totality of men, everyone.” [83] This should be noted in view of Moses 1:34: “And the first man of all men have I called Adam, which is many.” All the premortal spirits must pass through him as all “the totality of men” must descend from him—“many” indeed! Right at the bottom of the crypts in the temple of Denderah, where the visitor must turn and retrace his steps back up to life, three such serpents, each in a bag, are drawn, representing the first stirring of organic life (fig 35).[84]

Figure 35. The temple of Hathor at Denderah, like all Egyptian temples, was a model of their universe, from the great chart of the heavens on the ceiling of the roof chapel of Osiris (A) to the deepest crypts built within the massive foundation and walls. In one of the deepest and most inaccessible crypts, serpents in baglike shapes are being lifted up (B). Sometimes called serpent-stones by scholars, this representation goes back to the earliest times of the shrines of Upper and Lower Egypt.

The round Zodiac of Denderah (also A opposite) is held by four goddesses and eight kneeling Horus gods among the waters of heaven above. The thirty-six decans of ten-day intervals encircle the rim of the Zodiac with the northern constellations at the center.

Passing It On

To review, beginning on the extreme left we see something being transmitted between the odd-looking birdlike and serpentlike creature and the man on the throne. Next comes a standing woman, who has for a face the same object as that which is being transmitted between the bird and the bird-man. The odd lady in turn is holding something over the cow, who addresses the four canopic figures, protected or supported by a rearguard of three enigmatic creatures drawn up at the end.

What is it that is being passed down? What goes between the bird creature and the figure on the throne is the wedjat-eye, which stands for the complete and perfect integration of the body. “The Horus eye can be understood,” writes Winfried Barta, “as the symbol of begotten and self-renewing life” in the many combinations in which it occurs, with special emphasis on its solar significance.[85]

As Rolf Gundlach notes, “with respect to the fertility aspects, one can presume a fundamental affinity between the ‘primal god’ Amun and Min.” [86] There can be no doubt as to what figure 7 is about. Conception is the theme, a transmission of life from the spiritual to the carnal level.

In Egypt, Amun-Min sends out holy doves as his messenger birds.[87] This was to announce his enthronement, at which time a stall was erected where Min resided as the Divine Bull and consort to the Cow-Lady.[88] As referred to above, the bull on the rim of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus boasts of his copulative powers as “without equal,” and the recipient of his favors was, of course, the all-important Hathor-Cow, figure 5.

Notice also that Min himself on the throne is wearing wings and displays, as on most hypocephali, the broad tail of the Horus-hawk as the divine ruler who succeeds himself. Any god may be endowed with wings, for example, as the king ascends to heaven on the wings of Thoth.[89] On Stele C-30 in the Louvre, we read, “Praise of Min, exaltation of Horus with the upraised arm. Greetings to you, Min, as you come forth, god of the tall feathers, son of Osiris, child of Isis, Great God in the sn-wt-shrine, great one in Akhmim and in Coptos, Horus of the smiting arm . . . sovereign of all the gods,” etc.[90] There is an interesting survival right at the beginning of the Pistis Sophia, the basic Egyptian Gnostic work. There, we are told, the first mystery “which is before all mysteries—[is] the Father in the form of a dove.” [91]

Cultural Note

Cooing in the eaves of the temple, the dove was the bird of the love-goddess. All around the archaic world of the Mediterranean, the dove was especially a symbol of Aphrodite (Venus), Semiramis, and Ishtar (Astarte).[92] According to Herodotus, the famous oracle of Zeus at Dodona was established when a dove flew from Thebes in Egypt, landed in an oak tree in Dodona, and with human speech declared that an oracle for Zeus should be established there. Another dove flew to Libya and told the Libyans to establish an oracle to Ammon there.[93]

When Noah repeated Adam’s role of replenishing the empty earth, first sending forth his messenger doves to discover the extent of his domain, he planted a garden, made wine, and celebrated with a picnic which was not without a touch of scandal (see Genesis 9:20–21). It was, as might be expected, the great agrarian festival like that of Min-Atum to assure that “seedtime and harvest . . . summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease” (Genesis 8:22). It was the dove who informed Noah that the earth was available for his stewardship (see Genesis 8:8–12). According to the Book of Abraham, Pharaoh and his early successors claimed his right to run things through none other than Noah (see Abraham 1:27). The Abraham text goes on to explain that the founder of the line was the woman Egyptus. It is generally agreed now that the name of Egypt was derived from Hw.t-k.Ptḥ, the House of the Spirit (ka) of Ptah, the father of the race and hence the creator of man. As we have seen, the name by which the mother-goddess is known in Egypt is Hathor, written like that of Egyptus with a house containing that other spiritual being, son and heir to all the world, Horus. Hat-Her, or Ḥw.t-Ḥr, the house of Horus, was the Cow-Lady, crowned with horns embracing the sun’s disk.[94] Both Egyptus and Hathor are graphically designated by the same symbols—a house containing a god. It is a case of parallel names fitting parallel situations.

But the connections go much farther. We have to reckon with another father of the race and founder of the dynasty—Abraham. It was highly characteristic of Egyptian to have one symbol represent many things; thus figure 7, according to the explanation, represents God on his throne, revealing to Abraham “the grand Key-words of the Priesthood.” It is “also, the sign of the Holy Ghost unto Abraham in the form of a dove” (Fac. 2, fig. 5, explanation), since it was his heavenly guide who explained the mysteries to him.

Here the dove is holding the wedjat-eye. It is the eye that sees, knows, comprehends, teaches, and in fact creates. In the Apocalypse of Abraham, the angel who is Abraham’s guide places him on the right wing of a pigeon and sits himself on the left wing of a turtledove and so ascends to the throne of God.[95] The “Grand Key-words” and “the sign of the Holy Ghost” go together, for the wedjat-eye has an almost inexhaustible number of meanings. It holds “the key” to everything, and that calls for a fuller explanation.

A Note on the Wedjat-Eye

The wedjat-eye appears as a property in many mythological situations. Others such as the ankh-sign are more common but have a limited range of meaning, while the wedjat-eye seems to have numerous identities. In particular it stands for the one thing that concerns the Egyptian most—the restoration of that which has passed away. The Wörterbuch defines it as, “strictly speaking, the undamaged Eye of Horus, i.e., the Full Moon; also from the earliest times the Eye of Re and the Divine Eye in general.” [96] It stands for the miracle of restoration.

The stolen and swallowed Eye of Horus was naturally interpreted in the days of the nature religion hypothesis as both the dark phase of the moon and a solar eclipse.[97] The same also applies to the sun at midnight and the winter solstice. The Egyptians employed full poetical license in dealing with the wedjat-eye: “You are the apple of the eye of the Sun in the wedjat-eye, the daughter of the Moon, born in the middle of the month by night, . . . your right eye is the evening bark and your left eye is the morning bark, your two eyes, O Horus, which come forth from Atum, are Shu and Tefnut.” [98] A text in the cenotaph of Seti I takes us back to a primal drama full of mystifying references to the wedjat-eye: “Horus provides himself with his two Eyes on the second day of the month” [i.e., the day on which the moon is visible again for the first time].[99] The approach of Seth, the adversary, takes away the two Eyes of Horus.[100] Isis then takes Horus and puts him in her mother Nut so she can protect him.[101] At the time of the sailing of the morning-bark, Horus goes to receive his wedjat-eye, which is restored to him at dawn, and with it his strength and joy return to him.[102] Then Osiris commanded Horus to take his two eyes “that he might open his face and see,” and Horus is “reborn as a child.” [103]

Such fantastic ideas as opening the face with the wedjat are plainly before us in the standing lady in figure 6. The antiquity of the play is confirmed in the Pyramid Texts where the theologians of Heliopolis, according to J. Gwynn Griffiths, already gave an astral emphasis to the object and introduced the other eye into the plot.[104] It can be the Eye of Osiris, of Horus, or of any other god in the state of restored perfection. Budge saw that the hypocephalus itself “represents the pupil of the Eye of Horus” and suggests that “it superseded the gold figure of the Cow,” which is perfectly feasible since Hathor/Ihet herself is the wedjat-eye.[105]

The Mystery of the Eye

Bewildered by such strange connections, one asks, “Just what is the wedjat-eye?” Hans Bonnet notes that its association with healing and life was of universal application.[106] Eberhard Otto maintains that it is a symbol of “physical health” and “the beautiful connection between God and man through light.” [107] Nothing better represents the good old Re/Osiris factor than the wedjat-eye. In the Pyramid Texts it is returned by Horus to his father Osiris as the first step in his revival.[108] The main function of the hypocephalus is that of the eye.[109] “O Divine Eye, that art in heaven, Great One . . . grant the gracious eye in brightness, illumine the tomb of Osiris . . . and overthrow his enemy in the Necropolis in the night, in the day, and at any time.” [110] One could recognize here a common prayer in the border inscriptions of sundry hypocephali, as in figure 18 of the Joseph Smith exemplar. The vignette accompanying the supplication here shows an inverted Horus head in the sky from which beams of light stream down to the sleeping figure; it is a fairly common motif (fig. 36).[111]

Figure 36. The hawk-head of Horus emerging from the bottom of the sun sends a beam of light to the deceased. He is surrounded by the light of suns and stars. Ca. 1140 b.c., Tomb of Ramses VI, sarcophagus chamber, Valley of the Kings.

Drioton sees in the eye, as he does in the hypocephalus itself, a sort of hologram. Within the pupil is the complete personage: “the ensemble shows the embryo of the sun in the process of taking form in the interior of an egg, . . . the egg of Hermopolis,” which is “a representation of the embryo of the sun” surrounded by “the divinities of the Ogdoad, each male with its female counterpart” (see p. 244, fig. 23).[112] The presentation of the wedjat-eye depicted so graphically both in figures 3 and 7 of many hypocephali is, according to Erich Ebeling, the ultimate endowment and is given along with “the procreating seed which . . . is received along with the solemn Opening of the Eye; . . . the Divine Child can now see. The final act of the new creation is consummated.” [113] One remembers the All-Seeing Eye—which illuminates the endowment—on the east and west towers and in the Garden Room of the Salt Lake Temple. It was used on archways and adorned church buildings throughout Deseret.[114]

The eye with its feminine ending can represent Maat, “the daughter of Re, . . . related to the ‘divine pupil’—that is, with Horus, and without a doubt, in a larger sense to the king himself.” She is “Ma’at the Great, who rules . . . at the throat of the Divine Pupil of the Eye” [115]—that is, as a royal wedjat-pectoral, the equivalent of our hypocephalus. The connectedness between seeing things as they are and the truth of things is obvious. Thus as the goddess of truth, Maat can be likened to the All-Seeing Eye of Justice, in the same way as the King’s Eye of Persia or the Vizier of the Great Khan with his scrying-crystals and mirrors, which go back to the shamans of the steppes, the mirror being the pupil that reflects all things. The book reporting the dialogue between Isis and Horus at the beginning of the world is called “The Sacred Book of the Pupil of the Cosmos.” [116]

In the Hermetic writings, the Eye of the Cosmos is the κόρη κόσμου (korē kosmou)[117]—that is, the little girl whom we still call the pupil of the eye, from the Latin puppila, doll; it is the image which the primal mother sees when she looks into the eyes of her child—a tiny image much like herself. In Egypt it is “the child in the eye of every god.” [118]

In the Greek mysteries the yearning (πόθος, pothos) for what is distant, like Eros (ἔρος), was “associated with the eye, which was popularly regarded as the channel through which the image or phantom (ἴδωλον) of the desired object entered to inspire love in the soul.” [119] “The earth lies in the middle of the cosmos like a man on his back looking up to heaven as to her (the earth’s) Father in which he sees his own transformation.” [120] All of this is justified in view of our hypocephalus itself as a giant eye.

The most formidable aspect of the eye is its female role: Hathor is the Eye of Re,[121] and it destroys the enemies of Re.[122] This is the fire of the hypocephalus protecting the king. “It is the image of the Eye of Re which rises and sets every day. As the Meh-weret, it is the wedjat-eye.” [123] To this is immediately added, “I am also one of the four canopics of Horus called ‘Beloved of His Lord.’ ” [124] The wedjat-symbol found on representations of the moon shows that the Egyptians knew that the moon borrowed its light from the sun.[125]

But most especially the eye belongs to the king and to kingship. Osiris gets the eye back after Horus has rescued it for him; he needs it to rule the kingdom below as Horus and Re need it to rule on earth and in heaven.[126] The eye was, according to Griffiths, the Eye of Horus.[127]

The eye fills the king completely; [128] it purifies him; [129] it gives him special knowledge, visionary power.[130] It exalts the king and places him at the head of the Greater and Lesser Councils.[131] The king himself is the Eye of Re on the Horn of Hathor.[132] The eye gives the king as Osiris a soul and strength “at the head of the spirits,” restoring his supreme authority.[133] In the Pyramid Texts the fusion of the king’s nature with the God of heaven takes place when his statue is crowned with the moon-eye of Upper Egypt and the sun-eye of Lower Egypt, and then is anointed, passing through the middle chamber of stars into a room in which heaven was scenically depicted.” [134] It is the ultimate supreme power over men and gods.[135] Its power is especially protective, encircling the king,[136] a refuge of strength and security.[137]

The Pyramid Texts go on: the eye imposes order and reverence on the world as the power of the crown, the wrr.t ḥkw,[138] specifically the Red Crown denoting the wrr.t or headship of the Gods.[139] It is the design depicted on the coronation robe.[140]

With all its power, the wedjat is an important element in the ordinances. The functions of the wedjat-eye are combined in the anointing oil, both as the oil of healing that revives the smitten hero [141] and as the very precious oil used in the ordinances of anointing the brow or breast, specifically to bestow authority and power.[142] It is the anointing which transforms the nature of the individual.[143] All this is in the wedjat-eye itself, which by anointing imparts soul and body, restoration, joy, and thankfulness with its obligation of obedience.[144] Finally, it is the instrument of exaltation.[145]

In other ordinances it is the food of the sacrament; the wedjat-eye is the power of the bread which fills, revives, and strengthens the king.[146] It is the strength given by sacramental food.[147] It is eaten at the time of public sacrifice,[148] representing the sacred paat-cake, the food of the immortals which Osiris eats.[149] It is the wine that opens (restores) the mouth.[150]

For all its sanctity the wedjat-eye is a singularly available object. In the Pyramid Texts it is bestowed and distributed widespread,[151] suffused like incense.[152] Its power is available to Gods and mortals alike; it guarantees the individual that he too can be born like the Sun.[153]

No matter who possesses it, the eye can function under the proper authority. The king is carefully admonished in the Pyramid Texts to keep the thing out of Seth’s ambitious and unauthorized hands: “Never let it get away from you!” [154] Its physical and spiritual powers accrue only to those properly anointed and may be claimed only by those who are obedient.[155] Every day in the temple of Osiris in Abydos there was recited or enacted the drama of the combat of Horus and Seth, necessarily in reduced form.[156] In the famous duel Horus lost an eye while Seth lost his power of reproduction.[157] Horus’s loss is restored as a sign of fall and redemption, but Seth never regains his power—Satan can never beget or create.

By now it should be clear to any Latter-day Saint reader that the elusive wedjat-eye, intimately familiar yet strangely elusive, is a symbol of that equally common all-but-indefinable power called the priesthood.

Professor Erman felt that in recognition of his toilsome endeavors, the Egyptians owed him at least simple, straightforward, rational grammatical texts. Like Otto Neugebauer he magisterially rebukes them for vitiating their writings with priestly humbug. At the same time the professors considered the Egyptians worth their effort, but never asked whether those people might have had something in mind to which their own gesunder Menschenverstand (sound human understanding) rendered them impervious to all Romantik and Fantasie, which they despised. To them the least one could ask of the Egyptians was strictly parsed sentences and realistic situations. But we must excuse the Egyptians for lacking the absolute assurance of nineteenth-century positivism and twentieth-century reductionism; they realized that the scholar and priest, no matter how great his wisdom and knowledge, is never playing with a full deck.

Figure 37. These variations demonstrate the consistent depiction of the wedjat-eye lady holding her offering over the womb of the Hathor-Cow. (A) Fac. 2, fig. 5, Church History Library. (B) Berlin 7792. (C) Cairo SR 10691. (D) BM 37908.

The Lady in the Middle

Back to the woman in the middle whose head is a wedjat-eye. She has obviously accepted the serpent’s gift of the eye wholeheartedly. She stands between the man on the throne and the cow, holding what is in almost all hypocephali a stylized lotus with three petals over the womb of Hathor (fig. 37). In some exemplars it appears to be a maat-feather that she is holding.[158] Or it could be a shu-feather. Sometimes it is a simple rod or scepter.[159] In the damaged British Museum 36188, it appears to be a bundle of something.[160] In a number of cases it is a small tree or plant.[161] In the Louvre hypocephalus it is more like a plant than a lotus.[162] Most instructive is a hypocephalus from which both figure 7 and the standing lady are entirely missing, the whole process being performed by an enormous lotus that bends over the womb of the cow.[163] In another, the bird-man on the throne is missing and the standing serpent alone holds the wedjat up directly over the head of the wedjat-lady, who in turn holds the large lotus resembling the giant one just mentioned over the cow.[164]

All of this shows that the company of figures is busy passing along one and the same object; in this chain reaction the transmitting party is expendable, but not the eye! In the Florence hypocephalus it is the standing lady who is missing, and the god on the throne holds the flail where the lotus should be—it is quite obvious what is going on, expressed in the modesty of symbolism.[165] Book of the Dead chapter 17 makes this clear: “I have seen Re who was born yesterday from the buttocks of the Celestial Cow (Mḥ.t-wr.t). I am complete (whole) if he is complete (whole), and vice versa.” [166] An ancient gloss adds, “It is the image of the Eye of Re in the morning, when he is reborn every day. As for the Celestial Cow, she is the wedjat-eye of Re” (fig. 38).[167] To this is immediately added, “I am also one of those gods who are in the following of Horus . . . Imsety, Hapi, Duamutef and Qebesenuef,” [168] which takes us to the next figures in the procession: the four sons of Horus.

Figure 38. In this earliest known depiction of the Heavenly Cow, from the tomb of Tutankhamun, she is described in the text as having solar boats traversing her starry belly held up by Shu as eight Heh-gods of eternity support her legs. Rear wall, Shrine II, ca. 1330 b.c. Cairo Museum.

The Book of the Cow

We have proceeded from heaven to earth and from spiritual to earthly in figure 7. The Heavenly Cow has duly brought forth life on earth, and that has been perpetuated but is not the end of the story. There are yet further steps of descent to the tomb and the materia prima of the alchemist, where all ends up as dust and ashes, and the spirit ends up in the underworld. Now the question is, “Is there no happier sequel?” The answer is found on those full-blown hypocephali where there is still more space at the end of the line—it is occupied by the design of a cenotaph with the head of the Horus-hawk rising from it in triumphant resurrection. The joy of the event is clearly conveyed from the Papyrus of Ani. How is this achieved after the final quietus? Again the cow comes to the rescue.

The famous epics of the cow, the Book of the Cow and Re and the Sun’s Eye, tell us how it is done. As to the first of these—no minor opus—the text is written out in the tombs of five of the greatest pharaohs of the New Kingdom, and in all but one of them the large imposing figure of the cow holds center stage with the texts distributed on either side. The instructions for drawing the cow take us directly to the familiar rules for making a hypocephalus as given in the Book of the Dead chapter 162, and the cow is the ultimate image of the starry heavens.[169] The emphasis is on the nursing motif. Along with the cow’s picture goes the epic of the cow, the destruction of mankind in which she figures as Mehet-weret (Greek Μθύρ, Methyr), the Great Flood, as also she is the Sun’s Eye, including the restored solar-eye of Horus, the wedjat.[170] The whole thing is cosmic; we are specifically told just how many stars bespangle the belly of the cow, though the number differs in various representations.[171] Over the hinder parts of the cow is written “It is his coming forth,” [172] referring to the eternal rebirth of the sun, which as the drawings clearly show was supposed to pass through the body of the cow during the night to be delivered anew every morning.

Facsimile 2, Figures 8–21

Figures 8–21—that is, all the hieroglyphic passages—were left untranslated by Joseph Smith and everyone else, though similarly placed passages on other hypocephali contain the same or related statements which we have only to compare with these for a fuller reading. In spite of that, only the vignettes were ever explained from Joseph Smith’s day right down to the 1960s. These passages are translated only “as far as we have a right to give at the present time.” That was some one hundred and fifty years ago with the proviso, “If the world can find out these numbers, so let it be, Amen.” The many critics of the Book of Abraham as late as 1967 made no attempt whatever to translate the writing on Facsimile 2 and, indeed, some insisted that it could not be read at all.[173]

A drawing of the hypocephalus in the Church Historian’s archives shows that figures 12–15, except for a few characters on the left, were missing (see appendix 3). About a third of figure 18, the rim inscription, is also missing. Apparently Reuben Hedlock, the engraver of the facsimiles, inserted hieratic characters from the Hor Book of Breathings papyrus into the missing sections when he made the engraving of Facsimile 2. It is interesting that we are told in the explanation that figures 12–15 as well as 18, “will be given in the own due time of the Lord.” Since they were badly damaged or completely missing, they would have to be restored to be read. Today that can be done by comparison with other hypocephali unknown in Smith’s day; in particular the rim, figure 18, the large missing part of which can be restored from the rims of other hypocephali. But the Joseph Smith hypocephalus was the first one for which an explanation was ever attempted, and so had to wait on further discovery, until the damaged and missing parts could be replaced.

Figures 8–11

Joseph Smith explained that the three lines of text, figures 8–11, contain “writings that cannot be revealed unto the world; but is to be had in the Holy Temple of God” and “ought not to be revealed at the present time.” These lines contain a prayer to Osiris, the god of the dead, to grant life to the owner of this hypocephalus. A common theme of all Egyptian funerary literature is the resurrection of the dead and their glorification and deification in the afterlife, which is certainly a central element of our own temple ceremony.

There follows a transcription, transliteration, and translation of figures 8–11.

(11) (10)

(9) (8)

(11) ἰ nṯr sḏr.w m sp (10) tpy, nṯr ʿ nb p.t, t, (9) dw.t, mw=f ʿ, (8) d ʿnḫ b Wsἰr Ššq.

(11) O God of the Sleeping Ones [174] from the time of (10) the creation.[175] O Mighty God, Lord of heaven and earth, (9) of the hereafter, and of his great waters,[176] (8) may the soul of the Osiris[177] Shishaq[178] be granted life.

As stated above, this is a prayer or plea by Shishaq, the owner of the hypocephalus, to Osiris, the god of the dead, who is the Lord of all things, to grant him eternal life.

Figures 16 and 17

As we have seen, the upside-down part of Facsimile 2 represents very plainly the dark underworld, the womb to which all return to be reborn. The final disposition of the dead was not to wander bemused in Hades, the gloomy regions of the lower world forever but, after a brief taste of such unpleasantness, to pass to a much happier existence.[179] This is made very clear at the end of the long unfolding vignette of Book of the Dead chapter 17 (fig. 39). In the Ani Papyrus we see the black chamber beneath the tumulus in which the four canopic figures cooperate to produce the scene that follows. Right above their heads we see Ani bursting through the green mound of Abydos, which is covered with vegetation—it is the revival of spring, the equinox. Almost unique in Egyptian art is the merry smile he has on his face as he holds high above his head in each hand a big ankh-cross; a more vivid or unimpeachable proclamation of resurrection could not be imagined.[180]

Figure 39. Ani breaks forth from the tomb in triumph. The artist has gone to some effort to paint the tomb green, leaving in reverse outline the images of the living sons of Horus, who also appear standing as mummies on either side. P. Ani, BD 17, ca. 1250 b.c., BM 10470. The British Museum.

			 Figures 16 and 17 in Facsimile 2 are brief repetitive sentences, yet they tell as much as any other panels in the circle. Michael Rhodes finds these passages garbled.[181] But strangely enough the very same garbling appears in a number of the most carefully done hypocephali, including the Leiden and the Ashmolean.[182] Other hypocephali treat this space at the bottom of the circle merely as something to be filled in with the usual commonplaces, a sort of dead space or lumber room; it would seem that any fit conclusion the scribe or owner could think of would do for a conclusion. Thus the Florence hypocephalus simply repeats the familiar formula, “A flame under the head of Ta-Ker-Heb.” [183] One hypocephalus has the bottom space filled with the words “Osiris, Lord of Busiris” written on their sides to make them vertical.[184] The Meux hypocephalus repeats the rim formula: “O you who hides himself, who conceals his forms, who illuminates the earth with his forms in the netherworld. May he grant that the ba live forever.” [185] The damaged hypocephalus, Vienna AS 253a/2, though most closely resembling the Joseph Smith in other things, has only “The Osiris Iset-weret, born of [Khonsu-Ir-dis Justified].” [186]

There are other such sentiments, but none of them at all like that in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. These two passages take the form of half a dozen formulae in the various hypocephali consistent with their position at the very bottom of the world: (1) The theme of light coming into the darkness, the best known of all; (2) a warning to one in danger; (3) the assurance “you are secure” (written with the djed-column); (4) the stock flame-under-the-head statement; (5) snatches from conventional statements found in hypocephali in general such as “you go where you want,” etc. (6) But the Joseph Smith readings are the most interesting, backed up as they are by the carefully composed Leiden and Ashmolean hypocephali.

The clue to these two lines is a recent study by Hans Goedicke. Most of line 17 is taken up by the significant expression b pn ḥnʿ nb=f. This particular formula, as Goedicke points out, citing examples, emphasizes “the continuous company between man and his ba . . . [and] reflects a continuous link.” [187] For example: “When I hurry to the caves, may my ba be still with me” [188] and “While I sleep my ba watches over my body.” [189] Just so Re watches over the sleeping Osiris. The idea of course is that the ba continues after death “to remain in contact with its former lord.” To achieve that unbroken contact between spirit and body against the moment of resurrection is of course the main purpose of the hypocephalus; or as Goedicke puts it, “the implicit motive for the desired contact between ba and corpse is the hope for a reunification of the disintegrated person in a physical rebirth.” [190]

The subject of Goedicke’s special study is the famous “Lebensmüde,” or as Goedicke renders it, “The Report about the Dispute of the Man with his Ba” —perhaps the most impressive of all Egyptian theological compositions. Though the idea here treated “occurs repeatedly in all periods of Egyptian culture,” the “Lebensmüde” is the earliest and most moving exposition.[191]

But the Joseph Smith version carries the theme farther. The whole inscription reads:

(17) (16)

(17) ḥ.t th.t, n nth.tw, (16) nn tḥ.tw b pn ḥnʿ nbf m dw.t ḏ.t

(17) May this tomb never be desecrated,[192] (16) and may this soul and its owner never be desecrated in the hereafter.

The word ḥ.t refers to the tomb [193] or, more precisely, the tomb shaft.[194] Most hypocephali bear the name of their owner at least once, and it is his tomb that this passage is supposed to protect. The next word is thἰ, to overstep, transgress; desecrate.[195] The concern here is that the deceased body which is in the tomb as well as his spirit (ba) which is in the netherworld, not be disturbed or desecrated, which would prevent their ultimate reuniting in a resurrection. As Coffin Texts explain, all the parts of the physical body will be brought together with the soul, and the person will be “reestablished” in his original form.[196] The eternity motif means that the person is not stuck in the netherworld forever, but rather the body and spirit are reunited, and the person can “live forever.” [197]

Figure 18

The most conspicuous text on all the hypocephali, and hence the most often translated, is the rim inscription. It is also the most uniform and the most instructive—the universal approach to the hypocephalus. We have the outer border inscriptions on almost all hypocephali dealing with the same general subjects and every bit as important as we have been led to believe.

The Iris

From the first it was recognized that the hypocephalus, in the words of Samuel Birch, “represents the eye of Shu or Horus, which was supposed to preserve the natural warmth of the body till its resurrection. The middle represented the retina of the eye, and the narrow concentric ring between the centre and the edge the iris.” [198] “The hypocephalus represented the ‘Eye of Horus,’ ” [199] wrote Budge, “In the course of time . . . it superseded the gold figure of the cow . . . and became, strictly speaking, an amulet.” [200] For Heinrich K. Brugsch it was the Eye of Osiris and Horus.[201] More recently Griffiths has shown that in the Pyramid Texts the wedjat-eye is always the “Eye of Horus,” to which was added in the Heliopolitan theology a second eye, “the antithetic sound eye,” which became the solar equivalent of Nun, the heavens as the cosmic element came to predominate.[202] Considering the primacy of the resurrection, the eye could hardly ever have been anything but wedjat—perfect, flawless, complete.

The broad rim is often referred to as the Waters of Nun that surround the cosmos, from which all earthly things are born. A famous passage reads, “My Eye meditated upon the divine Tear which I shed on myself, and men and women came into being from it.” [203] There is a word play here between rmἰ, to weep, and rmṯ, man, mankind. We are told how in a time of the cosmic calamity the gods shed various bodily excrescences, including blood, sweat, and tears, especially tears, which, mingling with the earth brought forth precious substances.[204] This suggests also that the race came forth from the Waters of Nun.

Budge, in his uninhibited eloquence, translates from a Hymn to Amun-Re “Amun who art hidden in the pupil of thine Eye, Soul who dost shine forth from thine own Eye, wondrous god of transformations, Sublime One who cannot be known.” [205] At the same time another form of Amun can declare: “I am Shu, the image of Re, seated in the interior of the Eye of his Father.” The one reciting this has it drawn on his hand, bearing “in its interior a figuration of Onouris.” [206] “I have come forth from the wedjat-eye. I am Amun (or the Hidden One) who is in the secret place. . . . I enter and I leave amidst the true and faithful ones; I am the great soul whose form is shining. . . . I have come, I have come from the eye; I have arrived coming forth from the wedjat-eye; I have come forth, along with Re from the temple of the Ancient One in Heliopolis.” [207]

One Eternal Round

Another tradition suggested by the rim is the significance of the solar circle as the year-circle or shenen, the course of the sun in his rounds. Alan H. Gardiner explains, “Strictly speaking, the loop would be round. . . . The Egyptians called the cartouche šnw from a verb-stem šnἰ, ‘encircle,’ and it seems not unlikely that the idea was to represent the king as ‘ruler of all that which is encircled by the sun,’ a frequently expressed notion.” [208]

The commonest Egyptian sign for eternity is read nḥḥ , a solar disk between two twisted hanks of hemp. ḥḥ is the common root for “many,” “very many,” “a million”; with the sun-disk (the time element) added it means eternal and eternally in the end formula of countless inscriptions, nḥḥ ḥnʿ ḏ.t, “for time and eternity.” [209] The point of all this is that the combination of šn and ḥḥ with vowels supplied can be read as Shinehah, which according to Joseph Smith designates “Shinehah, which is the sun,” with reference to its motion relative to that of the other heavenly bodies (Abraham 3:13) and signifies “one eternal round.”

The Amun inscriptions encircling the hypocephali deal with the transmission of heat, light, and power. In the Meux hypocephalus rim inscription Amun states, “I have come forth from the wedjat-eye. . . . I have come forth from the netherworld with Re (the sun),” [210] the hypocephalus itself being the means by which light and heat are transmitted from their celestial source to the individual whose head rests upon it. Amun is commonly implored in the rim inscriptions to “turn his face toward the body of so-and-so,” [211] to warm and preserve it through the virtue of Re. The hypocephalus as a transmitter of both heat and light was to make available to one in the netherworld the life-giving force of the sun.

The following is the text of figure 18:

(18) ἰnk ḏbb.ty m ḥw.t bnbn.t m ʾIwnw, q sp-2, ḫ sp-2. k nk ἰwty snwf. nṯr pwy ʿm ḥw.t bnbn.t m ʾIwnw <dἰʿnḫ b Wsἰr Ššq r nḥḥ ḏ.t ḥ>nʿ nṯr pf m ʾIwnw.

(18) I am Djebabty in the temple of the benben in Heliopolis. [I am] most exalted and very glorious. [I am] a copulating bull without equal. [I am] that Mighty God[212] in the temple of the benben in Heliopolis. <May the Osiris Shishaq live forever> [213] with that Mighty God in Heliopolis.[214]

Between Heaven and Earth

The border inscription of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus begins with the answer to a prayer spoken by one who says: “I am Djebabty.” This identifies him as one who belongs to the ḏb.t. What then is the ḏb.t? Few words in Egyptian have a wider range of interpretation; in Raymond O. Faulkner’s modest dictionary alone no less than twenty definitions are given for various forms of ḏb (fig. 40; see appendix 2A).[215] The nisbe form occurring on our hypocephalus denotes, according to Conrad Leemans, “both a divine person and a personified object.” [216] The word is very old and is confined to archaic religious texts denoting what is holy and top secret.[217] The ideogram represents inflated skin floats which were attached by a line to a harpoon used in the ritual hunting of hippopotamuses. The commonest translation of the term ḏbǃ.ty by early interpreters of the hypocephalus treated it as a chest or box. The Wörterbuch defines the related word ḏbǃ.t as “shrine, coffin” and notes that it is connected with the Hebrew word תבה (tēbāh),[218] the ark of the covenant, which is generally recognized as a borrowing from Egyptian.[219] It may seem a far cry from an inflated skin to a shrine or coffin, but it only takes a step or two to make a perfectly obvious derivation take a totally new direction. The most complete study by far of the ḏb is that of Kees, who begins by noting that all applications of the word are “derived from the basic meaning ḏb.t, something which is equipped or adorned,” from which in turn are derived the sarcophagus as the coffin. The idea of plugging up the last gap is certainly consistent with that of putting the finishing touches—” adornment” —on something. This is not an ancient usage, however, but what is behind it is the earliest idea of all—namely, that of the ḏb.t as the king’s dwelling place, his palace, “specifically perhaps the ornamental gate of the same.” [220] An inscription at Esne shows that ḏb.t refers to a most holy and secret place.[221] At Edfu it signifies the same; the dual (ḏb.ty) designated by the doubling of the leg-hieroglyph, as in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus , applies to Re in his diurnal and nocturnal aspects according to the surmise of Louis Speleers.[222] He also suggests that ḏb.t refers specifically “to the circular plan of a rounded temple built by Ptolemy III at Heliopolis.” [223] An inscription from Esne tells us that the ḏb.t “contains the great secret which is found in the Duat and the disk of Re illumines it,” [224] and in another, “This god is depicted extending his hand from ḏb.t, the secret (place) which the gods themselves do not know, . . . the mysterious figure which is among them in the midst of them, the great thing which is hidden in it.” [225] For Constant De Wit, ḏb.ty is a place for settling scores, the showdown between Horus and Seth.[226]

Figure 40. Not surprisingly, the shape of the ḏb.t hieroglyph varied over the more than two thousand years of its use. Scholars believe it represented a float of some kind, perhaps of green rushes or inflated animal skins. Attached to the harpoon rope, it floated on the surface to show where the hippopotamus had dived. 8 1/4 ” wide, restored. Tomb of Mereruka, ca 2300 b.c., Saqqara.

Examples of the ḏb.t hieroglyph: (A) Pyramid of Unas, ca. 2340 b.c. (B) Niuserre, ca. 2430 b.c. (C) Thutmose I, ca. 1500 b.c. (D) Nectanebos, ca. 370 b.c. (E) Church Historian’s copy, Church History Library. (F) Fac. 2, Hedlock, Church History Library.

			 Kees concludes that all ḏb-words are related and gives some interesting connections. Thus the idea of a stopper, brick, plug, or replacement suggests the paying of a debt or the righting of a defect, hence the settling of scores and hence the combat between Horus and Seth. Likewise, the idea of improving appearances suggests clothing, adorning in sacred garments, hence a change of garments, hence the bestowing of a quality as by censing or anointing, including the receiving of the wedjat-eye, etc.[227] Kurt Sethe explains its basic meaning of “substitute or repayment” as the settling of scores by Horus the Beḥdetite, the avenger of his father.[228] Most specifically, ḏb.ty is for De Wit the place where the serpent was smitten.[229] Inscriptions tell us that the ḏb.t is a place or an island that contains a great secret which is found in the Duat and is illuminated by Re.[230]

The ḏb.t is the place, the ḏb.ty the person in it, as Leemans noted from Book of the Dead chapter 162, column 10, where he appears as “the president (ḫnty, presiding at the head of) of the ẖr.t-nṯr, the Other World, who is Re at the temple of the exalted benben.” [231] As we have seen, the rim inscriptions begin either “I am ḏb.ty” or “I am an Akh,” in either case an exalted spirit. Speleers counted four rim texts invoking Osiris, two calling Amun of the Amuns, and five more calling him ḏb.ty, which he takes as the surname of Osiris. In some cases all have been replaced by the simple ba of Osiris.[232] ḏb.t was also the title of a high priest of Memphis, referring to his duty of dressing the god,[233] and an epithet of Osiris, Horus, or Khonsu.[234]

Finally, as payment of provision the ḏb.w (pl.) are food, provisions, offerings.[235] From this comes Jan Bergman’s pronouncement that ḏb.t is the greening fields where Isis is.[236] Hence also Michael Rhodes’s translation of ḏbb.ty as “provider.” [237] The Egyptians may have had many ideas in mind in various combinations which still escape us; Sethe notes that in historic times the Egyptians themselves had no clear idea what it meant.

We have dwelt on Djebaty at such length not only because it shows us what we are up against, but also because it shows the richness of possibilities that confront us and the ease with which we might take a wrong direction. On the other hand, the Egyptian genius for fusing things together in their multiple approaches prevents amateurs like the author from becoming discouraged.

The Ceremonial Complex

In its place at the head of the list of holy shrines, the ḏb.t which launches the discourse is part of a large complex of sacred structures in the great ceremonial center at Heliopolis (see map on p. 542, fig. 66). This great center is designated three times in our rim inscription (see appendix 2B), whereas no mention is made of the ordinary function of the hypocephalus—the flame around the head. It is one of the things that sets our hypocephalus apart from others.

The Joseph Smith text reads, “I am [1] ḏbb.ty in the [2] Temple of the [3] Benben in [4] Heliopolis,” identifying four different structures. ḏbb.ty is next identified with both the akh and the bull. It is true that akh is here an adjective, “glorious,” but on other hypocephali it is a person, a glorified spirit. All of these places and figures have a celestial and a generative force; we are dealing with cosmic matters. What every use of ḏb suggests is the establishment of contact and agreement between lower and higher beings, looking to a place for such contact that is out of this world yet not identified with any other. The rim inscriptions commonly call upon Amun in his aspect of the Hidden One, the Unknown One, the inaccessible one seated on the throne of glory at the center of the circle.

There is that connection between the Egyptian ḏb.t and the Hebrew תבה (tēbāh), and the meaning of the Hebrew word broadens out in much the manner of its Egyptian equivalent. The תבה was depicted as everything from a moderately sized chest to a house-sized shrine to accommodate the ark of the covenant, and finally the temple itself, which expands to embrace the Holy City and even the universe itself.[238] In such a manner the Egyptian ḏb.t designates the shrines and temples of Esne, Denderah, and Edfu, as well as some cities in which they are found. The most puzzling one is Thebes—can it too be tēbāh? Can that be the origin of its name? The Greek name still remains a complete mystery, first appearing in the Iliad.[239] This same claim is made for the necropolis of Memphis near Saqqara.[240] As noted above, the chain of shrines running the length of the Nile is held together by the common name based on the root ἰwn. Thus ἰwnw (Coptic ϣⲛ and Hebrew 	און, ōn) for Heliopolis, ἰwn.t for Denderah, ἰwnt.ty for Esne, ἰwny for Erment.[241] The determinative for all these shrines is a standing stone marker, ἰwn.

Heliopolis was the primal seat of kingship. It is the king “for whom the world of Heliopolis assemble in order that he might be fashioned and act as king of eternity, as the enduring Horus of everlasting.” [242] The resurrection theme is still with us. “The high-priest of the Heliopolitan sun-god in pre-dynastic times was, of course, the King of Heliopolis,” as Aylward M. Blackman notes, for kingship and priesthood are not to be separated in high office.[243]

The Benben

The rim inscription of Facsimile 2 states, “I am Djebabty (ḏbb.ty) in the Temple of the Benben in Heliopolis.” The Temple of the Benben is the name of the sun sanctuary in Heliopolis (fig. 41).[244] Breasted recalled a time when “the sun-god under his earliest name, Atum, . . . sat high on the sacred ben, the pyramidal symbol at Heliopolis, and created the other gods.” [245] Jéquier notes that the benben-stone is “the earliest manifestation of solar divinity.” [246] The two pillars must be related to the ḥn.ty “period,” “end,” [247] the two extreme ends of the world or the course of time, recalling the Greek τέρματα (termata), Latin metae. They mark the extreme ends of the racecourse as of life.[248]

Bonnet finds the original benben to have been a stone fetish placed atop obelisks and pyramids at the high point where Atum-Kheper first emerged from the primal waters.[249] Jéquier posits that all obelisks and pyramids of Egypt were derived from the benben-stone of Heliopolis, the original form of Re himself, which was perhaps a meteorite.[250]

		Figure 41. Heliopolis, City of the Sun, was one of the most ancient cities in Egypt. It claimed to be built on the original High Sand (A) or mound that emerged from the waters of creation and was crowned with the benben-stone. Some believe the original monument was a conical iron meteorite set on a square pillar, the House of the Benben, conjectural reconstruction (B). Of the hundreds of obelisks erected there, that of Senwsret I (C; ca. 1950 b.c.) is the only one still standing, as seen in this 1841 view by C. Ravioli (bottom left). This sun pillar inspired the pyramids and obelisks of the next three thousand years. We are still making them; see, for example, the monument at Joseph Smith’s birthplace.

		 				 Henri Frankfort considered the benben-stone a sexual or procreative symbol.[251] The term “Temple of the Benben” also denotes the place where the soul of Osiris dwelt, and the name was applied to the Holy of Holies in the temple of Atum at el-Amarna,[252] for it was the place where the sun made his appearance, the name ben perhaps coming from the root wbn “to appear in glory.” [253] Even in this underworld aspect, with the sun-god in the lowest depths as Sokar or Khonsu at Karnak and at Edfu, he “conceals his secret form in the benben.” [254]

Heliopolis

As we have seen, the rim inscription of Facsimile 2 indicates that the Temple of the Benben is in Heliopolis. Heliopolis was perhaps the most famous shrine in the ancient world and probably the most ancient. Karnak was consciously represented as “a new Heliopolis in Upper Egypt.” [255] Gardiner assures us that Sethe’s theory that “all the cardinal doctrines of the Egyptian religion evolved in Heliopolis cannot be refuted”; [256] while Blackman maintains that “the daily service performed on behalf of all Egyptian divinities in historic times was based upon that performed in the Heliopolitan sun-temple.” [257] The temple of Amun at Karnak far to the south was an exact duplicate of the shrine of Heliopolis, and its high priest had the same awesome title of wr mw, “Greatest of Seers.” [258]

Figures 19–21

The text found in figures 19–21 are as follows:

(21) (20) (19)

(21) ἰw wnn=k (20) m nṯr pf (19) ḏd.wy.

(21) You shall ever be (20) as that God, (19) the Busirian.[259]

This continues the overall theme of the hypocephalus, and indeed Egyptian funerary literature in general. The deceased is promised that he will be like Osiris—he will be resurrected and live eternally as a god.

^245. James H. Breasted, Development of Religion and Thought in Ancient Egypt (Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1972), 76; also PT 600 (§1652).

^246. Jéquier, Considérations sur les religions égyptiennes, 85.

^247. Gardiner, Egyptian Grammar, §77; Wb 3:105–6.

^248. Sophocles, Oedipus the King, line 1530, last line of the play.

^249. Bonnet, Reallexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte, 100.

^250. Jéquier, Considérations sur les religions égyptiennes, 86–87.

^251. Henri Frankfort, Kingship and the Gods: A Study of Ancient Near Eastern Religion as the Integration of Society and Nature (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1948), 153, 380 n. 26. John Baines, “Bnbn: Mythological and Linguistic Notes,” Orientalia 39 (1970): 390, notes that several other writers agree with this opinion.

^252. Bonnet, Reallexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte, 101.

^253. Ibid., 100.

^254. Ibid., 101.

^255. Hermann Kees, “Ein Sonnenheiligtum im Amonstempel von Karnak,” Orientalia 18 (1949): 430.

^256. Alan H. Gardiner, Egypt of the Pharaohs (London: Oxford University Press, 1978), 427.

^257. Blackman, “Sacramental Ideas and Usages in Ancient Egypt,” 48.

^258. Kees, “Ein Sonnenheiligtum im Amonstempel von Karnak,” 430–31.

^259. ḏd.wy is a nisbe adjective formation of Ḏd.w, Busiris, a cult center of Osiris in the Delta, and thus used as an epithet of Osiris. Cf. Wb 5:630, 7.

9

The Ascension Dramas

			 An ascension is an ancient apocryphal text that describes the ascension into heaven or cosmic tour of a patriarch, prophet, apostle, or other religious figure with his subsequent return to earth to reveal what he has seen and experienced. Sometimes the terms assumption and testament are used to describe the same thing. The Book of Abraham is a classic example of just such a text.

Abraham Redivivus

In 1972 a conference of scholars in Los Angeles was dedicated to the examination of a singularly important apocryphal writing—the Testament of Abraham.[1] In Klaus Koch’s significant book on apocalyptic writings, we learn that the two most important ancient apocryphal figures by far to receive attention in our day are Enoch and Abraham.[2] These happen to be the two apocryphal writings which Joseph Smith has placed before us—another score for Joseph Smith, who includes his book of Enoch in a reediting by Moses. Let us consider Abraham first.

Abraham’s great story is found in three main ancient sources: the Bible and two apocryphal writings belonging to a cycle other than Genesis 11–25. They are the Testament of Abraham and the Apocalypse of Abraham.[3] The Testament of Abraham was a Greek text described by M. R. James, who first edited the text in 1892.[4] It was made clear to all at the Los Angeles convention that the Testament of Abraham, the main object of study, was composed in Egypt in the first or second century a.d. by a Jewish or Christian convert with a thorough knowledge and understanding of Egyptian religion. Among other things the scholars recognized from explicit descriptions of the otherworld obvious borrowings from the vignettes and texts in the Book of the Dead, especially chapter 125.[5] Most notable are “the judgment scene of two late [Egyptian] documents, the Book of the Dead of Pamonthes and the Tale of Satni-Khamois, both of which date from the second half of the first century a.d.,” which Francis Schmidt claimed to be the inspiration of the Abraham story.[6]

The Testament of Abraham
