The text of the Testament of Abraham survives in two recensions, A and B, and seems to have been originally composed in Greek in Egypt around a.d. 100.[7] The story takes place at the end of Abraham’s life when God sends Michael to carry his soul aloft.[8] But instead of carrying him off, Michael, out of pity, returns to heaven to inform God that he cannot tell the righteous Abraham of his impending death.[9] God tells Michael to return to Abraham’s house and that he will send a vision to Isaac. “Then Isaac will relate the vision, you will interpret it, and he himself will come to know his end.” [10] After experiencing the vision, Isaac describes it to his father and Michael. “I saw, this night, the sun and the moon above my head, and its . . . rays were encircling and shedding light on me; . . . and I saw a light-bearing man coming down out of heaven, flashing (beams of light) more than seven suns. And that sunlike man came up and took the sun from my head and he went up into the heavens, whence also he had come.” [11] The heavenly messenger comes a second time and also takes the moon from Isaac’s head. Isaac pleads with him to at least leave him the moon. But the messenger tells him that they have to be taken to “the king above, because he wants them,” but “he left the rays with me.” [12] This is reminiscent of the purpose of the hypocephalus, which was to preserve the body against the time when the spirit should return to it, retaining meanwhile a lingering flame, spark, or aura behind the head.[13] Michael then explains that the sun is Abraham and the moon is Sarah and tells Abraham “at this time you are about to leave the earthly life and journey to God.” [14]

But Abraham flatly refuses to leave the earth, and Michael again returns to heaven to consult further with δέσποτα παντοκράτωρ (despota pantokratōr) the Lord and Ruler of All, a close approximation of the Egyptian nb-r-ḏr[15]. God instructs Michael to return and tell Abraham that all men must die and that he had sent word through Michael so he could make preparations for his departure from this life.[16] Michael returns and delivers the message to Abraham, and when he hears it he falls at Michael’s feet and begs as a last request to be permitted to see “all the inhabited world” while he is still in the flesh; then he would be willing to die.[17]

So up they go, Abraham and the angel, in a chariot of light to make a tour, beholding all the living things on earth, the whole human race at work and at play.[18] At the first gate of heaven, Abraham sees two roads—of righteousness and wickedness.[19] At the parting of the roads stood a golden throne on which sat a man, who alternately wept and smiled at the sight of evil and good souls respectively entering their appropriate gates.[20] Michael explains that this is Adam, “the first-formed” (ὁπρωόοπλαστος, ho prōtoplastos).[21] Then he sees a fiery crystal throne, also between two gates, and a man “bright as the sun, like unto a son of God” before whom two angels were writing, while another stood by to weigh the souls in the balance,[22] a scene right out of the Egyptian judgment portrayed in the 125th chapter of the Book of the Dead. Michael explains that the judge seated upon the throne is Abel.[23] There is also a “fiery and merciless angel” who carries the wicked “to the place of sinners, a most bitter place of punishment.” [24] This is reminiscent of the monster Ammit (ʿmmy.t < ʿm-mw.t, swallower of the dead) in chapter 125 of the Book of the Dead that gobbles up the hearts of the wicked who fail the weighing of their hearts.

When, after his cosmic tour, Abraham still refuses to accompany Michael, God sends Death disguised as a shining spirit of glory.[25] At Abraham’s insistence the imposter shows his true form—a series of monsters right out of the Egyptian underworld.[26] At the sight of Death unveiled, seven thousand of Abraham’s servants die.[27] Death then tells Abraham that he too would have died “had not the right hand of God been with you.” [28] Finally, Michael appears and carries Abraham’s spirit off to heaven with the furnishings of an Egyptian funeral.[29]

In the B Recension of our story, the angel’s initial visit takes place just as the sun sets, at the time of passage between the worlds.[30] When Michael takes Abraham “from darkness into light,” the rays of light remain shining in Isaac’s crown for twelve hours, holding the light like a hypocephalus.[31] Abraham will be taken to heaven, but his body will remain in the earth following the familiar Egyptian formula.[32] Abraham then asks to “see the things of creation . . . in heaven and on earth” before he dies.[33] “This is not my task,” says Michael, like the ahku in the Coffin Text drama (CT 312), “but I shall go and report to the Lord about this.” [34] He gets the permission, and the first thing Abraham sees on his trip through space is the ocean river (the Waters of Nun) encircling the cosmos.[35] Then he sees two gates and Adam seated on the throne of glory.[36] He sees the Angel of Death taking spirits to the law court [37] where sits a giant judge wearing three crowns,[38] while two scribes, Abel and Enoch, record the deeds of the righteous and the wicked,[39] as Thoth does in the Egyptian judgment scene.

After Abraham returns from viewing the creation, God orders Michael to disguise Death with youthful beauty, having two heads. Abraham asks to see his true form, but when he is seen in his true form, all of Abraham’s servants drop dead at the sight, only to be promptly resurrected at Abraham’s request—for resurrection is the theme of the story.[40] Then God “drew out the soul of Abraham as in a dream,” and Michael takes him to heaven.[41]

The Apocalypse of Abraham

A companion piece to the Testament of Abraham, the Apocalypse of Abraham, is preserved only in an Old Slavonic translation made in the eleventh or twelfth century.[42] It was probably originally composed in Hebrew sometime after a.d. 70 and before the middle of the second century.[43] The first English translation of this work appeared in none other than the first volume of the Improvement Era as a translation by Edward H. Anderson and R. T. Haag of the German rendering of Professor G. Nathanael Bonwetsch.[44] It is gratifying to know that Latter-day Saints were not slow in recognizing “many things of a character both as to incidents and doctrines that ran parallel with that which is recorded in the Book of Abraham, given to the world by Joseph Smith in 1836.” [45] The first part of the Apocalypse of Abraham (chapters 1–8) deals with Abraham’s youth and his father’s idolatry. The second part (chapters 9–32) opens with God commanding Abraham to sacrifice upon a mountain, in return for which he will soon be shown all of God’s past creations and all that is to come.[46] The explanation to Facsimile 2, figure 2, says that these same things were shown to Abraham “as he offered sacrifice upon an altar.” At the sound of the voice, “my soul fled from me. And I became like a stone, and fell face down upon the earth.” [47] God immediately sends the angel Iaoel (whose name means “Jehovah is God” ) to raise Abraham up and consecrate him and strengthen him.[48] The angel is compared to sapphire, chrysolite, and the rainbow,[49] like the menat-swallow or even the Horus-hawk in the Book of the Dead.[50] They reach the mountaintop, where, among the sacrificial victims, the turtledove and pigeon are spared so that the angel and Abraham may ascend on their wings “to show you (what) is in the heavens, on the earth and in the sea, in the abyss, and in the lower depths,[51] in the garden of Eden and in its rivers, in the fullness of the universe. And you will see its circles in all.” [52] “Its circles in all” calls to mind the hypocephalus, which is a portrayal of the creations of God in a circular form with him seated in the center.

What happens next? A false angel, “an unclean bird,” appears and warns Abraham to flee from Iaoel.[53] But Iaoel denounces and exposes the bird as an imposter, as Azazel, the evil spirit that tempts mankind,[54] and he tells Abraham to command him to depart.[55] Then at sundown, amidst the smoke of a furnace, the angel puts Abraham on the right wing of the pigeon and seats himself on the left wing, and they ascend in wind and flame.[56]

As they approach the heavenly throne, where God himself remains invisible (in the manner of Amun, whose name means “the Hidden One” ),[57] Abraham swoons, but he is sustained by the angel[58] and worships the “Eternal One” [59] who makes “the light shine before the morning light upon your creation.” [60] He sees a throne of fire under which are four fiery creatures, each with four faces, those of a lion, man, ox, and eagle [61]—these are the canopic figures, figure 6 on Facsimile 2. The creatures on either side of the throne oppose and threaten each other, but they are reconciled by the song of peace taught them by the Eternal One.[62] Abraham is now instructed to consider the expanse of the universe and the hierarchical powers and orders of the seven firmaments and sees the “hosts of stars, and the orders they were commanded to carry out, and the elements of earth obeying them.” [63] God compares the stars in power and number to the progeny that he promises Abraham.[64] Powers? Obey? Governed? We begin to catch echoes of the Joseph Smith explanation to figures 1–3, 5. Next he is shown the firmaments and the ends of the earth, including the Garden of Eden, in a picture.[65] He sees the division of the earth’s inhabitants into opposing hosts, “half on the right side of the portrayal, and half on the left side of the portrayal.” [66] Again we are reminded that all normal hypocephali have two opposing parts.

Abraham asks, “Eternal, Mighty One! What is this picture of the creation?” [67] And he is told that the picture represents the plan or outline of creation before it was created [68] and that the people he saw were those who “existed previously.” [69] (Premortal existence is another Book of Abraham motif found in Abraham 3:18–28). With the angel pointing out and explaining what is depicted in the drawing (for it is not the real thing but a picture), Abraham is shown the progress of the human race starting from the Garden of Eden, where as in a moving picture he beholds the drama of Adam and Eve and their progeny, down to the increasing perversion of his own descendants, and, having seen enough, cries out in dismay, “How long?” [70] Returning to earth he finds himself “no longer in the glory in which I was above,” [71] but he is reassured that the elect will be gathered in the end, and the wicked go to hell (of an Egyptian type).[72]

The Horror of Great Darkness: Genesis 15

The Testament of Abraham and the Apocalypse of Abraham are closely linked; they betray their common source by citing frequent passages from the fifteenth chapter of Genesis, “a favorite theme for apocalyptic speculation.” [73] The Bible version begins with the patriarch in a dangerous situation and in dire need of reassurance: “Fear not,” he is told, and is promised as a reward for his righteousness a shield both to protect him and to give him confidence (Genesis 15:1). This, along with the guarantee, “Thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace; thou shalt be buried in a good old age” (Genesis 15:15), gives clearly to understand that the hero is loath to leave the earth prematurely. What concerns him most, as he is about to enter the fearful passage, is that he is leaving no posterity behind. So God brought him forth to study the stars, which means, according to the oldest commentators, that Abraham was actually taken above to view the stars and learn in them the nature, both in number and quality, of his progeny.[74]

When he asks for an earnest of such infinite inheritance, he is told to make a sacrifice, splitting all the animal victims in half, with the exception of the turtledove and pigeon. Next, birds of prey like the unclean bird in the Apocalypse of Abraham come to spoil the sacrifice but are driven off by him (Genesis 15:11). Then, “when the sun was going down . . . an horror of great darkness fell upon him” (Genesis 15:12) and he passed out. The “deep sleep” תדדמה (tardēmah) or blotting out of the senses is translated in the Septuagint as ἔκστασις (ekstasis from which the English ecstasy comes), which is a “trance.” [75]

The situation is quite frightening; in fact, we are still with our basic theme—how to face the fearful passage. Abraham, far from being cheered, is told in grim foreboding of his progeny’s four hundred years in Egypt (Genesis 15:13–14), but again is comforted with the promise of a long life for himself (Genesis 15:15)—this was but a preliminary death. Then, “when the sun went down and it was dark,” there occurred a strange phenomenon—a תדונ ןע (tanûr ʿāšān) “a smoking furnace” and a לפידאש (lappîd ʾēš) “a flaming torch.” The fireworks passed down between the halves of the divided victims (Genesis 15:16–17).

The nearest thing to this odd display and close call for Abraham is the account of Nimrod’s attempt to sacrifice the youthful Abraham in the fire (אוך, ûr). Scholars in the past have never tired of pointing out the correspondence of the two words אוך (ûr, fire) and Ur (city) of the Chaldees as meaning that Abraham did not necessarily suffer in that city but simply passed through the fire. When a burst of flame surrounded the altar, consuming all bystanders, Abraham remained unscathed, standing with his delivering angel surrounded by a brilliant display of flames.[76] Satan as an evil bird had tried to frustrate that Chaldean sacrifice too, and it was on that occasion also that God promised to Abraham his posterity.[77] In the biblical account, the Lord promises virtually the entire known world to Abraham’s seed, pointing out to him the regions of the inheritance with its rivers and boundaries, obviously indicated by a map (Genesis 15:18–21).

These ancient and established, but long-neglected, episodes from Abraham’s life show up in the Book of Abraham and also in the accompanying explanations to Facsimile 2, the hypocephalus. The relevant apocalyptic sources were unknown to the world until the 1860s and went unheeded, as Koch has shown, until the 1960s. According to the Book of Abraham, the patriarch studied the real cosmos with an instrument using transparent stones—the Urim and Thummim (Abraham 3:1)—not by mere eyesight but working according to faith. He saw the stars “nigh unto the throne of God” (Abraham 3:9), and the Lord explained to him the whole cosmic layout as he watched (Abraham 3:11–12). The whole is laid out with reference to Abraham’s own position in the cosmos—“the earth upon which thou standest” (Abraham 3:4–7); all is viewed within a strict framework of relativity. Approaching the throne, Abraham talks with God “face to face” (Abraham 3:11); the infinity of stars has direct relationship to the endlessness of Abraham’s own progeny (Abraham 3:14).

All this would be profoundly secret were not Abraham expressly ordered by the Lord to “declare all these words” in Egypt (Abraham 3:15). Abraham is reminded of the eternity of these things and how his own existence takes its place in the infinite plan (Abraham 3:17–23) and how the heavens intervene in his own earthly life (Abraham 3:20). He is reminded of God’s power and wisdom and how he came down in the midst of “the intelligences that were organized before the world was” (Abraham 3:21–22).

The Testament of Isaac

We have already noted that when Abraham departed this earth it was Isaac who was left with the glowing crown which bound him to his departed father for a certain period of time. Though it is far less well known than the Testament of Abraham, newly discovered texts of the Testament of Isaac deserve at least a glance.[78]

It begins with Isaac learning that he must leave the world and his body.[79] The Merciful One sends Michael to him as he had to his father Abraham.[80] Here the writer openly recognizes that what is happening is a repetition of what happened before, leaving no doubt as to the source. When the angel appears on schedule he announces that he has been sent “to take you to heaven to be with your father Abraham and to all the saints.” [81] “There has been prepared for you the throne beside your father Abraham; likewise for your beloved son Jacob.” [82] The angel takes Isaac up to heaven to the curtain before God’s throne, where he worships God with his father, Abraham, and all the saints.[83] In Isaac’s presence, God, sitting on his throne, tells Abraham that whoever writes a testament or covenant, his posterity shall be eternally blessed.[84] In parting with a frantically mourning Jacob, Isaac instructs him to properly bury him and God will do the same for Jacob that he has done for Isaac.[85]

Enoch

But before Abraham there was Enoch. The only apocryphal writings which today rival those of Abraham in importance are those attributed to Enoch, the earliest examples of which are found among the Dead Sea Scrolls.[86] In it, Enoch “as a creature of flesh and blood, and not a redivivus on leave from his sojourn from Paradise, . . . draws up in writing his scientific and religious teachings, his moral exhortations, and his revelations concerning the future.” [87] The prize ascension text among many Enoch manuscripts remains the long Hebrew account which Hugo Odeberg called 3 Enoch.[88]

The one thing the Bible tells us about Enoch is that he “walked with God: and he was not; for God took him” (Genesis 5:24; Hebrews 11:5–6), and he is often mentioned along with Elijah as one of those who died but did not die. Third Enoch opens with Enoch praying: “I looked up and said: ‘Lord of the Universe, grant . . . that Prince Qaspiʾel . . . may not prevail over me and cast me from heaven.’ At once the Holy One . . . summoned . . . the angel Metatron. . . . He flew out to meet me with great alacrity, to save me from their power.” [89] “He grasped me with his hand” and said, “Come in peace into the presence of the high and exalted King to behold the likeness of the chariot.” [90] In this Enoch sees the opposing hosts and “all the mysteries of the world and all the orders of nature.” [91] “I looked,” he says, “there is nothing in heaven above or deep within the earth concealed from me.” [92] There is placed on his head a crown like a disk of the sun with rays projecting in the four directions,[93] and on it the angel wrote with a pen of flames the letters by which were created the heaven, the earth, the sea, etc., and all the orders of creation in which every letter shows like the rising of the sun, moon, and stars.[94] Enoch himself was surrounded by fire [95] and sat on a great throne and judged while “the princes of kingdoms stood beside me, to my right and to my left,” [96] as the angel ʿAnapiʾel explains “the records concerning the inhabitants of the earth,” [97] while another angel “weighs men’s merits in scales in the presence of the Holy One.” [98] The Gizeh Enoch, discovered in 1887, has the guide saying: “Look at the earth and consider the works that have been done in it from the beginning to the end; . . . all the works of God are shown to you.” [99] After this psychostasy, the Metatron “took me by his hand, bore me up on his wings, and showed me those letters, engraved with a pen of flame upon the throne of glory,” [100] and everything else as well, including, as with Abraham, a glimpse into the premortal existence, “the souls of the righteous who have not yet been created,” [101] and the history of the world to its end.[102] All this was “printed on the curtain of the Omnipresent One [Holy One],” [103] while his guide points out all the stars to him “with his fingers.” [104]

Like Abraham in the Apocalypse of Abraham, Enoch falls down in terror before the throne and the angel puts him on his feet again; [105] and though he passes through the gates, his presence is challenged in heaven as earthly and alien.[106] Yet he was lifted up to a higher heaven with the Shechina,[107] where he is declared one of the elect from the beginning. Most significantly he sees how God “made for me a throne like the throne of glory.” [108] Here we should recall that one of the oddities of the Joseph Smith story which has impressed non–Latter-day Saint scholars is Enoch’s crying out to the Lord, “thou hast made me, and given me a right to thy throne, and not of myself, but through thine own grace” (Moses 7:59). The reader would have to be very dense indeed not to see the hypocephalus connections here.

Both the text and the maps in which Enoch draws up “his scientific and religious teachings” lead us “with complete certainty to the Mesopotamian centers of scholarship.” [109] Józef T. Milik has supplied ancient Babylonian maps to illustrate his commentaries on Enoch. A notable feature of such maps of Mesopotamian background is that they mingle heaven and earth in the same plan, as might be expected, since what is below is a faithful copy of what is above.[110] One Babylonian map shows the earth with Babylon near the center in its cosmic setting as part of the whole universe (fig. 42); such a map Milik has designated as “the map of the world of Enoch” (fig. 43).[111]

			 Figure 42. This small broken clay tablet contains the only surviving Babylonian map of the cosmos. The seven mountains enclose the compass-drawn encircling waters. Though made in the seventh to sixth centuries b.c., this plan represents a much older conception of the time of Sargon the Great (ca. 2300 b.c.). 3 1/4 " wide, BM 92687.

This 1930 reconstruction of the Babylonian cosmos shows our earth as just a pale reflection of the eternal world above. We still use much of the Babylonian Zodiac today. Adapted from a drawing by Karl Maass as directed by Eckhard Unger.

Figure 43. In the conception of the universe in the book of Enoch, the sun emerges from six eastern gates, moves in the six months between the winter and summer solstices, and sets in the western gates. The seven great mountains are based on the ancient Babylonian conception of the universe. Adapted from Milik’s reconstruction.

Ascension of Moses

In the first chapter of the Joseph Smith Book of Moses we have a good example of an ascension text that could accurately be called the Ascension of Moses. It is an account of how Moses obtained the record that follows. It takes the beginning clear back to the premortal existence, or rather to the ongoing eternal order of things, which is where all our stories so far take their beginning. In the first eight verses we see Moses clothed with glory in the presence of God in an utterly timeless ambience (Moses 1:2), in a realm without end (Moses 1:3), where the creative process never ceases (Moses 1:4); it lies totally beyond anything that mortal man can adapt to or comprehend (Moses 1:5). Though Moses, as a son of God, is allowed to see a vast expanse of it, yet that is only the small segment that concerns the mission to which he is about to be called (Moses 1:6).

The first eight verses are a flashback to put us into the picture; with the ninth verse the earthly phase of the story begins. There we find Moses completely cut off from the presence of the Lord, entirely on his own. The culture shock is complete; as a man “left unto himself,” he “[falls] to the earth,” where we find him out cold like a dead man (Moses 1:9–10). When he comes to, he declares, “Now . . . I know that man is nothing” (Moses 1:10). Taking advantage of Moses’ weakness, Satan attacks with imperious commands and arguments bent on reducing him to complete submission (Moses 1:13). He is in the position of Abraham on the altar, or of Osiris lying helpless in the dark at the mercy of his cruel adversary, Seth. He is overcome “and as he began to fear, he saw the bitterness of hell,” but with his last ounce of energy, “calling upon God, he received strength” and exposed and dismissed his adversary (Moses 1:20). Now the Holy Ghost comes and invests Moses as he “lifted up his eyes to heaven,” and presently—for the Holy Ghost is able to “[bear] record of the Father and the Son” —he finds himself in God’s presence where he was before as “he beheld his glory again” (Moses 1:24–25).

Moses does not go to heaven “while yet in the flesh,” as the others do, yet he does not die: he sees God, and it is the Holy Ghost who takes him into the glory. In the explanation to Facsimile 2, figure 7, we are told that the Holy Ghost “in the form of a dove” revealed “through the heavens the grand key words of the Priesthood” to Abraham. Does this mean that the heavens provided the instruction, or that it was revealed by passing through the heavens? In either case the Holy Ghost was both the conveyor and the instructor.

In assigning him his mission, God tells Moses he shall “be made stronger than many waters; for they shall obey thy command as if thou wert God” (Moses 1:25), just as Enoch was promised the right to God’s throne, both in the Joseph Smith book of Enoch and in the others. Moses also takes the grand tour: first he is shown the physical earth, “every particle” of it, with all its land masses, and then he sees the inhabitants to the last man (Moses 1:27–29). God instructs him personally, telling him “worlds without number have I created; and I also created them for mine own purpose” (Moses 1:33)—that is, to forestall endless and futile discussions about such things (Moses 1:30–33). “The heavens, they are many, and they cannot be numbered unto man” (Moses 1:37). Finally, as he is about to be left to himself again, Moses is commanded by the Lord: “Thou shalt write the things which I shall speak,” for the benefit of his posterity, “and in a day when the children of men shall esteem my words as naught and take many of them from the book which thou shalt write . . . they shall be had again among the children of men—among as many as shall believe” (Moses 1:40–41). The whole thing is to be kept most secret from the world. In our time it has enjoyed a very small circulation indeed, both within and outside the Church.

Mesopotamian Versions

As we noted above, Milik declared that section 77 of the Dead Sea Scrolls book of Enoch describing a map of the universe which Enoch copied “leads us, with complete certainty, to the Mesopotamian centers of scholarship.” [112] Certainly no one disputes Abraham’s Mesopotamian background. The earliest Sumerian ascension takes us back to an archaic “chthonian” age in which “the Ancient City” (la Cité de Jadis) flourished in the “Embryonic Universe.” [113] That was at a time prior to the “Great Day,” that day around which Sumerian cosmology centers, the day of the “cosmic hieros gamos” on which the new world is founded and all life is brought forth by the conjunction of An, the Bull of Heaven, and Ki, the Good Cow, mother earth.[114] On the fateful day we see Enki lying helpless on his bed in a secret underworld chamber.[115] All the gods are weeping and only Nammu, the Great Mother, dares approach the hero. “My son,” she says, “you are sleeping . . . the captive of sleep . . . arise from your couch and perform your divine offices.” [116] “At these words . . . Enki leapt from his bed” and proceeded to go his rounds inspecting creative bodies and forms and arranging for the creation of man and the ordering of the cosmos as we know it.[117] In this fragmentary creation story, one notes the grim opening scenes of our ascension drama as well as the leading figures on Facsimile 2, the copulating Bull on the rim and the Mother-Cow who dominates the circle.

A notable Babylonian parallel to our other ascension stories is the tale of Adapa, another space traveler, one to whom was given “understanding . . . to disclose the designs of the land . . . and wisdom to him was given, eternal life was not given him.” [118] What makes this especially relevant is that the text was found in Egypt in the fourteenth-century b.c. library of the renowned pharaoh Akhenaten at El-Amarna.

When Adapa cries out from the depths, Anu sends Ea to rescue him and bring him up to heaven. But Ea will not bring him to heaven until he goes through the proper funeral procedure—he must die before he dies, he is made to wear funeral weeds with his hair unkempt as a prelude to being taken on the cosmic tour: “The road to Heaven wilt thou take . . . at the Gate of Anu. Tammuz will be standing by and ask, ‘Whom do you seek and why the mourning garb?’ ” The answer is “because the gods have disappeared from our land.” When the gods find out that the hero knows their names they allow him to pass on to the presence of Anu, and a messenger conducts him before the throne: “He made him take the way to heaven and to heaven he went up.” Following instructions, the hero refuses all food and drink offered him in the underworld as well as the anointing and garments awaiting him, for though celebrating his funeral, he is not to die at this time. Anu as we might expect, is sitting as a judge: “Adapa has broken the South Wind’s wing,” he says, “bring him here!” One thinks here of Abraham on the right wing of the dove, and, like Abraham, Enoch, and Moses, Adapa’s presence as a mere mortal is offensive to the heavenly host: “Why did Ea disclose the plan of heaven and earth to a worthless human, making him distinguished and giving him a great name?” Anu asks him why he doesn’t eat and drink—thus passing by the chance for eternal life. In reply Adapa says that Ea told him not to drink. “Take him away and return him to earth!” says Anu, and there the tablet ends.[119]

In another version, “as Adapa from the horizon of heaven to the zenith of heaven cast a glance he saw its awesomeness.” As a result of his visit, according to this version, Adapa’s city was relieved of the plague, recalling the motivating prayer of the hypocephalus, the prayer for healing.[120]

To summarize, certain motifs dominate in the Mesopotamian records, especially astral ones. Of interest to us is the recurrent theme of the prayer and rescue from the depths, the ascension and cosmic tour, and especially the diligent drawing of maps and models and the careful recording of all that is seen and heard.

Ezekiel

Ezekiel’s account begins with the prophet in Babylon “among the captives by the river of Chebar” (Ezekiel 1:1). The heavens open, and the hand of Yahweh is upon him; there is a cloud of fire in the midst of which there was a brightness “the colour of amber” (Ezekiel 1:4), followed by four beasts—the canopic figures of the hypocephalus (Fac. 2, fig. 6)—lion, ox, eagle, and man (Ezekiel 1:10)—each with four faces like the central figure of many hypocephali (fig. 44). There is a “wheel in the middle of a wheel” (Ezekiel 1:16) and a throne with the Lord sitting on it, surrounded by fire (Ezekiel 1:26–27). All these possible makings of a hypocephalus are found in the first chapter of Ezekiel’s book.

In chapter 3 the hero is carried aloft, and in the following chapter he is instructed to draw a map of the heavenly Jerusalem, following its well-known cosmic pattern (Jerusalem as the center of the universe) on a tile. Now he takes up a balance for use in a court of law which is being held in the midst of the city. Chapter 8 is another ascension story: The prophet takes his leave of the world while sitting with the elders in his house, mounting up through fire to a place “between the earth and the heaven” (Ezekiel 8:3) where he sees both a righteous and a wicked city, the usual throne and a counterthrone supported by some idolatrous beasts as in the Egyptian fashion. Egypt in fact is on his mind, and chapters 16 to 40 are obsessed with what sounds like paranoid jealousy of Egypt. Finally, chapter 40 reverts to the cosmic charts of the temple, with sacred measurements and numbers of the place presented in the sight of the people. Ezekiel’s testament to posterity ends with his presentation of a layout of the heavenly Jerusalem, the earthly counterpart of the heavens.

Figure 44. A 1569 artist tried to represent Ezekiel’s vision of the chariot and throne of God, with the wheels within wheels and cherubim and their heads, all in fours. Bear Bible, Bern, Switzerland.

			 				 Especially striking is one image which Ezekiel uses that implies a crassly literal resurrection: “And the bones came together, bone to his bone. And when I beheld, lo, the sinews and the flesh came up upon them, and the skin covered them above” (Ezekiel 37:7–8). The only people that talk in such a crassly realistic way about the resurrection of the flesh are the Egyptians, with whom the joining together of bones (ṯs qs.w) is a familiar routine. The great historian Eduard Meyer contrasts Ezekiel’s ravings with the spiritual struggle and conventional insights of Jeremiah, who behaves like a proper prophet.[121] Yet Jeremiah’s own two companions would seem to share the wild extravagances of Ezekiel.

Baruch

There are two pseudepigraphical apocalypses associated with Baruch, son of Neriah, Jeremiah’s scribe (Jeremiah 36:4). Second Baruch, entitled “The Book of the Apocalypse of Baruch the Son of Neriah,” [122] survives only in a Syriac translation from the Greek, although it seems to have originally been composed in Hebrew in the early part of the second century a.d.[123] Third Baruch, which has the title “Apocalypse of Baruch,” [124] survives in both Greek and a Slavonic translation of the original Greek and is dated between the first and third centuries a.d.[125] In 2 Baruch we find Baruch grieving for Zion; “suddenly a strong spirit lifted me and carried me above the wall of Jerusalem.” [126] He sees four angels standing at the four corners of the city [127] and is told of the coming judgment and gathering of the righteous.[128] He is further told how God instructs the creation with his understanding and gives “wisdom to the spheres so that they minister according to their positions.” [129] The affairs of men take place in two contrasting realms—the black and the white, with the black or evil ages prevailing through the generations of history, with alternate periods of light and darkness.[130] As for Baruch, “You will surely depart from this world, nevertheless not to death but to be kept unto (the end) of times” [131]—another hero who dies but does not die.

The instructions continue: “Therefore, go up to the top of this mountain, and all countries of this earth will pass before you, as well as the likeness of the inhabited world, . . . the depths of the seas, and the number of rivers, so that you may see that which you leave and whither you go.” [132] Baruch must write all of this down for a witness or testimony to the people.[133] He folds it and seals it and sends it off by an eagle (the messenger bird).[134]

Third Baruch has much to say about mounting on wings and fiery rays of light [135] and how the crown from the head of the sun has to be renewed “because it and its rays are defiled upon earth.” [136] Third Baruch is clearly drawing on the Egyptian otherworld literature, when it has the archangel taking the hero to the third heaven where he sees a plain with a large lake in which there were “many birds of every species” and particularly a large crane.[137] When Baruch asks about the plain, the angel explains, “This plain which surrounds the lake . . . is the place where the souls of the righteous come.” [138] He further explains that the lake provides the clouds of earth with the water that causes fruit to grow.[139] In Egyptian religious literature, the Field of Reeds and the Field of Rest (or offerings—ḥtp) are constantly mentioned together, with the common writing of the inundation of the Nile and its bounty as a heron on its perch . This is a clear example of an apocryphal writing using Egyptian drawings to explain his story.

Ezra

Another interesting ascension text is 4 Ezra,[140] dating to the late first century a.d., which survives in Latin, Syriac, Ethiopic, Armenian, and Arabic versions as well as fragments in Coptic and Georgian, which all derive from a Greek text, which in turn may have been translated from a Hebrew original. [141] It repeats the ascension theme a number of times for different dispensations. The story opens with Ezra lying on his bed in Babylon in great distress.[142] He prays, and the angel Uriel appears, announcing, “I have been sent to show you.” [143] Uriel then shows Ezra the usual panorama of the cosmos and hears the gloomy prophecy for the future of Israel. In this book, everything is weighed, measured, and timed,[144] and the upper world of light is matched by the dark pregnant world of the womb.[145] A blazing furnace passes in front of Ezra before he sees these things.[146] The angel describes a sea set in a vast expanse; in this expanse is a “city . . . full of all good things,” with a narrow path leading to it with fire on one side and water on the other.[147] Ezra is shown the state of the cosmos in the millennium and the fate of the righteous and the wicked at the judgment.[148] Then he sees a woman, who identifies herself with the city,[149] and then a sinister eagle who rules the earth until overcome by a lion.[150]

John the Revelator

Moving to the New Testament we find the best of all known ascensions, that of John the Revelator. We find John on Patmos “in the Spirit” (Revelation 1:10), and he sees “one like unto the Son of man” whose “eyes were as a flame of fire” standing in the midst of seven lampstands (Revelation 1:13–14). John falls down like a dead man, and Christ puts his hand on John telling him not to be afraid, that he has “the keys of hell and of death” (Revelation 1:17–18). He commands John to “write the things which thou hast seen, and the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter” (Revelation 1:19). He further says, “He that overcometh shall not be hurt of the second death” (Revelation 2:11), recalling that ascension chapters from the Book of the Dead have the title, “Rites for keeping a man from dying a second time in the after-life.” [151] Christ promises, “To him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me in my throne, even as I also overcame, and am set down with my Father in his throne” (Revelation 3:21).

John then sees a throne in the midst of heaven with four figures—lion, calf, man, and eagle, the well-known canopic figures—in the attitude of praise (Revelation 4:1–7) (fig. 45). He sees a polarized world of two conflicting societies and twice beholds Christ sitting in judgment on the throne (Revelation 19, 20). Satan wages war and loses, but there is no place for those who flee the “great white throne” (Revelation 20:11). As in Ezekiel, a New City of Jerusalem descends from heaven (Revelation 21:2) while everything is renewed (Revelation 21:1; see color plate 8). John is again reminded to write these things down (Revelation 21:5). He is then taken up to a high mountain as the New Jerusalem descends from heaven, and he describes the city in detail, including proportions and measurements (Revelation 21:10–22:5). Like Ezekiel, John is the despair of the critics and the delight of crackpots when confronted with such passages as that describing an angel with an iris (rainbow) upon his head and a face like the sun, putting his right foot in the sea and his left on the land and roaring like a lion. Also, like Ezekiel, John is given a rod to measure the temple (Revelation 11:1).

Figure 45. God the Father sits enthroned in the center with the Lamb of God. The four quarters of the universe are represented by the four beasts: lion, man, calf, and eagle. Artist unknown.

			 This puts us in mind of a much later Timothy, an archbishop of Alexandria, who claimed to have found in Jerusalem “a parchment book, which Proclus, the disciple of John the Evangelist, had written.” [152] In this book John describes his vision of hell, where he sees a wheel of fire on which the wicked are bound and submerged into a fiery lake as it rotates.[153] He then sees Michael the archangel, who lowered his right wing into the lake and brought out “a multitude of souls . . . out of their tortures” . . . and carried them to the heavenly Jerusalem “before the Father of goodness.” [154] Then Michael “took them into the rest which is everlasting.” [155] Then he is told, “If a man . . . shall light a lamp in the church in the name of the Archangel Michael . . . whether he be the greatest of all sinners . . . he shall be . . . comfortably warm until God shall visit him and shall shew mercy unto him, and bring him out of the places of punishment, and take him to a place of rest through the supplication of Michael.” [156] The lamp thus serves a purpose similar to that of the hypocephalus.

Paul the Apostle

The Apocalypse of Paul is one of the New Testament apocryphal writings that have come down to us. Although it was originally written in Greek, only translations survive; the best being the Latin, although there are some important variants and additions in the Coptic version.[157] The text was probably composed near the end of the fourth century or early fifth century a.d., at least in the recension that we now have.[158] The Apocalypse of Paul describes how he is caught up in the third heaven,[159] where he sees the heavenly throne,[160] and the ocean surrounding the heavenly city.[161] Within the city he sees an altar by which a figure stands whose visage shone like the sun.[162] He also sees the judgment of both the good and the wicked.[163] An angel then returns Paul to the Mount of Olives, where he finds the apostles assembled. The apostles then command Paul, Mark, and Timothy to write down what they have seen.[164]

Gnostic Materials

Such material naturally offered tempting bait to the hungry gnostic imagination, even though the basic premise of Gnosticism was the denial of that literal heaven and resurrection which make the ascension stories interesting. The problem of reconciling the literal ascent to heaven and return to the body with an immaterial heaven and a purely spiritual resurrection was easily solved by abstraction and allegory.

Thus, in the gnostic Gospel of Truth[165] God himself is the schema of the cosmos who takes on the form of all.[166] He absorbs the physical universe “like a flame.” [167] He rests in the midst of the pleroma.[168] The crucified Christ is planted as the διαθήκη (diathēkē, testament, will, covenant) of the Father while he descends clothed with eternal life [169] into “the empty spaces of terrors.” [170] In the manner of Pharaoh in the underworld of the Amduat, he teaches those in the darkness,[171] and those who accept him are written in the Book of the Living and mount up with him to the presence of the Father.[172] For all its spiritualizing, nowhere can one find a writing more insistent on the importance of having a tangible document brought from the other world. The Father, it tells us, wrote the letters to carry knowledge between the worlds, without which nothing can be plainly set forth or believed.[173] It is the written testament which proves the Lord’s claim to his estate.[174]

The dematerializing gnostics favored female leads for their scenario. Thus in the Hypostasis of the Archons,[175] it is the Lady Norea who calls out to God from the black depths of despair,[176] and a dazzling angel of light comes down to rescue her “from the rulers of unrighteousness.” [177] He is one of the four ϕωστῆρς (phōstēres, light bearers) that stand before the ἀόρατον πνῦμα (aoraton pneuma, the Invisible Spirit)—that is, the canopic figures and the throne of glory have become pure abstractions. The angel then tells Norea how “Sophia (Wisdom) who is called Pistis (Faith)” created out of the physical matter that was the shadow of the veil of heaven a living being, which took the form of a beast shaped like a lion.[178] Sophia and Zoe (Life) then take Sabaoth to the seventh heaven and stand at the right side of the throne, as the chariot of four-faced cherubim surrounded by angels, where he has charge over the veil that hangs between the upper and the lower worlds (see p. 369, fig. 44).[179]

Among the Coptic garblings of the story, it is the untitled Coptic treatise on the origin of the world in which the ladies really shine.[180] It begins with an appeal for a rational and scientific view of the universe and then resorts to pure abstraction.[181] Envious Sophia tries to imitate in a counterheaven the graphic pattern of the real one ( ),[182] the dark side, thus dividing all things into opposites—wet and dry, male and female, and especially the right and left.[183] Seeing her own reflection in the water, reversed, she realizes that she is but a shadow of something greater, and so she returns to the Light above,[184] the sevenfold heaven,[185] where she sees the four familiar figures—lion, ox, man, and eagle—before the great throne.[186] Needless to say, these images become more and more diluted and confused as the Neoplatonists and other intellectuals, especially the little-understood gnostics, go to work on them. It is time to get back to something more specific.

Narrative of Zosimus

Echoes of such things resound in the Apocrypha, both Jewish and Christian, though usually in tatters and snatches—the whole story almost never occurs. One of these rare exceptions is the Narrative of Zosimus,[187] in which Zosimus, after living forty years in isolation,[188] was taken away from the earth by an angel to visit the land of the blessed.[189] It is plain that we are dealing again with the hero who dies but does not die, when his hosts tell him, “We are holy but not immortal, for we are also of your race.” [190] They also tell him he can remain with them for only seven days,[191] while all that he observes is written down on tablets of stone.[192] He is shown “entering into the firmaments.” [193] Then he is handed the written documents, and upon arriving back in his cave “placed the tablets upon the steps of the altar,” [194] while Satan declared that he would destroy the evidence of the book.[195]

Isaiah

We have saved Isaiah for special treatment because of the enormous importance of the Ascension of Isaiah [196] for understanding both the hypocephalus, the history of the Reformation, and the immense antiquity of the leading motifs. The Ascension of Isaiah seems to have been composed in Greek, other than chapters 1:1–3:12 and 5:1–16, which were probably composed first in Hebrew and translated into Greek.[197] The Greek text (of which only a fragment survives) was translated into a number of different languages including Ethiopic, Latin, Slavonic, and Coptic. Of these, the Ethiopic is the most important, since it is the only complete text to survive.[198] The portion of the text dealing with the martyrdom of Isaiah was probably composed near the end of the first century a.d.,[199] and the rest sometime during the second century a.d.[200] In the Ascension of Isaiah the actual description of the vision begins in chapter 6, when Isaiah enters into the palace and sits down on the king’s couch.[201] Besides Hezekiah, all the princes of Israel, the eunuchs, the king’s counselors, and forty prophets and sons of prophets have gathered together to hear the words of Isaiah.[202] As Isaiah begins to speak to them, the Holy Ghost falls upon him, and his spirit leaves his body and is caught up in a vision.[203] After the vision ends and his spirit reenters his body, Isaiah then relates what he has seen to his son Josab, to Hezekiah, and to the prophets.[204] He tells them that a glorious angel came to him and said he had been sent to take him up to the seventh heaven and show him the mysteries of God.[205]

First they went to the στρέωμα (stereōma, the firmament), the lowest vault of the sky, divided (as we might now expect) into two warring armies as Satan instigates uproar and rebellion.[206] Then he is taken to the first heaven where he sees a throne in the middle of it with angels to the left and the right uniting in a hymn of praise to one in the seventh heaven.[207] They pass through four more heavens with the usual thrones, but when they come to the sixth heaven, Isaiah remarks that the brilliance of this sixth heaven makes the others seem like darkness.[208] The angel then tells him if he rejoices over this light, how much more he will rejoice when he sees the light in the seventh heaven where the Lord and his Beloved are.[209] There is also the usual divine rebuke to the heavenly inhabitants who object to the presence of a mere mortal among them.[210] Isaiah sees the patriarchs of old from Adam to Enoch.[211] Then Isaiah asks the angel to “show me how everything which is done in that world is known here”; [212] whereupon another angel shows him some books. Isaiah reads the books, which contain “the deeds of the children of Israel.” [213] Isaiah then remarks that “truly, nothing which is done in this world is hidden in the seventh heaven.” [214] Isaiah then sees God commission Christ to descend through the seven heavens to earth to carry out his redemptive mission for mankind.[215] Like Enoch (Moses 7:47, 55–56), Isaiah sees the birth, mortal ministry, crucifixion, and resurrection of Christ.[216] Finally he sees Christ return through the seven heavens to sit down in glory at the right hand of the Father.[217] The angel then tells Isaiah that he has “observed what no one born of flesh has observed” and promises him that when his mortal days are through, he will come there.[218]

Legitimate Rummaging

At this point we feel obliged to explain to the bemused reader what is going on. From the mid-nineteenth to the mid-twentieth century a vast traffic in comparative studies stimulated the activity and fed the vanity of the schoolmen. Beginning with comparative philology in India, it spread to folktales, rituals, and doctrines and from Max Müller to the “patternism” of the Cambridge School to the popularizing of Joseph Campbell; they speculated and argued endlessly on who influenced whom and what sources were the sources of other sources or were not. The diligent searches often led to surprising conclusions which were largely ignored because they tended to contradict evolution, as Giorgio de Santillana, Hertha von Dechend, and Lord Raglan have pointed out. Are the comparisons and parallels we are presenting here just more of the same?

It is the Ascension of Isaiah which breaks out of the stuffy old study and forces us to recognize that we are dealing not with primal images but with a single account which spread from the most unlikely places to the most unlikely places. The Ascension of Isaiah turns up in every century of the Christian era in almost every civilized country. It played an important part in the Albigensian movement when Bishop Bélibaste took it to his cathedral at Valencia in Catalonia.[219] He had received it from the Catharii of Languedoc in southern France who took it in turn from the poor Lombard preachers who shared with the poor Catholic brothers; [220] they had it from the hands of the Catharian bishop Nazaire who brought it to his see in Concorezzo in Lombardy, after he had got it from the Bogomils of Thrace, who valued it highly, as did the Patarins of Dalmatia.[221] For centuries it was smuggled by itinerant preachers, always on the move to keep one step ahead of the Inquisition, to those little communities where it was read, studied, and debated in secret. The earliest Christian mention is Origen’s charge that the book was tampered with (ῥρᾳδιούργηται) by the Jews.[222] Jerome reports, “They have as evidence (for their preaching) the Ascension of Isaiah and the Apocalypse of Elijah, and by it and other means take advantage of and deceive the simple women of Spain and especially of Portugal.” [223]

This all-important text kept constant pressure on the official Church, and it is from depositions made to the Inquisition that one sees it working among the people. The two most important of these are the testimonies of a pair of renowned preachers, written down shortly before their martyrdom at the stake and published in full by Mathias Delcor.[224] The first record is that of Arnaud Sicre. He tells how a certain brother wondered whether he was holding the true belief. One day while he was praying about it, an angel appeared to him and said he had come to show him the glory of the Father and the true way. The angel carried him up to the first heaven but forbade him to worship the one presiding there as he was not the Father. The same thing happened at the second heaven, and so on until the sixth, each more glorious than the last. In the seventh heaven he worshipped the Father who asked him whence he came. His answer: from the tribulation of earth. In heaven he saw hosts of angels, glorious flower gardens filled with singing birds, etc., a place of joy without sorrow. He told the Father he wished to stay but was told that he would have to go back to the earth again so that he could preach there the things that he had learned; but the Father granted his request to remain above a little longer. When the angel told him his time was up, he thought he had been there only two hours, but the angel told him it was actually thirty-two years, as he would find when he returned to earth. So the man mounted on the neck (collum) of the angel for the descent through all the heavens. Returning to earth, his report of his journey confirmed the faith of the people and strengthened the Church (secta).[225] This particular ascension closely parallels many after-life or near-death experiences being published abroad in our own day, in particular works by Raymond Moody and Elizabeth Kuebler-Ross.

The second deposition is twice as long, and the witness, Pierre Mauri, attributes the whole situation to the reading of the Ascension of Isaiah. On Christmas Eve he preached a sermon to the people “to strengthen their testimonies” (volens ostendere quomodo ipsi tenebant bonam fidem et credenciam). He told of a certain brother who was perplexed by reading the Ascension of Isaiah and by the various disagreements about it among the brethren. He became distrustful of what was in the ancient books and read without stopping for three days and two nights to settle his mind. From other cases it would appear possible that he may have dozed at this point; but anyway, as he was reading, an angel appeared and asked him why he doubted. He answered that it was because of some things he was reading and all the disagreements among the members. “Doubt not,” said the angel, “but get upon my back!” So they went up to the first heaven where there was a great and joyful congregation being addressed by their preacher. When the man wanted to worship him as the Father, the angel told him that those people were only spirits who had not yet been judged and were awaiting the mercy of the Father.[226]

Hence to the second heaven and an even finer congregation and preacher, but still not one to be worshipped. And so on to the seventh heaven, a realm of surpassing brightness where all wore crowns and sang the song of Zion. There he was told that there was no difference between the spirits of men and women—a distinction emphasized only among carnal mankind under the influence of Satan, the prince of this world. To be carnal-minded is to be subject to Satan, but to be spiritual-minded is to worship the Holy Father (see 2 Nephi 9:39). The man, according to Delcor, was free to worship here because this one was “the Holy Father of the people of Israel,” which the speaker identifies with his own persecuted church on earth. Of course he asks to remain but the Father told him that it would be necessary to return to earth “and comfort his little ones” with his message from heaven. “But Holy Father, how can I return to the lower world?” The answer, “Do not call me Father—I am not your father because you have doubted; but you must go back to earth again to lay aside your garment of mortality, and after that your spirit, having put aside the body, shall return here.” The man was not seen on earth for a long time; then the angel came to take him back to earth “so that you can teach what you have seen here” and so greatly strengthen the foundation of the church and give comfort to the members. He did not tell them everything for he did not know it all, as he explained; no one man knows all the secrets of God, but they are to be had distributed among the whole human race.[227]

Far and Wide

Delcor has summarized the situation for the Middle Ages and Early Reformation for which Eugène Tisserant also has supplied the texts; Armin Schmitt has done the same for Mesopotamia and Egypt. Everywhere it is the same basic scenario; in Schmitt’s terms the main ideas are indisputable—“so blieb er doch in seinem Kern unangetastet” (nevertheless it remains in essence undisputed).[228] The basic themes are

1. The cosmology is real; the sun, moon, and stars in the second heaven are real as is the treasury of the snow, rain, and hail in the sixth, frequently mentioned in the gnostic and especially Enoch literature, and between the two are the heavenly Jerusalem and the temple in the fourth heaven.[229]

2. In the cosmos the hierarchy of powers is duly noted by Irenaeus in the second century.[230]

3. Just above the earth’s atmosphere is the firmament, which is that of the hypocephalus pattern, since it is a place of conflict and not of heavenly peace, where Satan and his hosts dwell in ceaseless hostility.[231] “This is the condition of the world,” Isaiah is told, “war in earth and in the firmament, . . . envy and conflict; this has been the way from the beginning even until this very moment!” [232] It is also the state of things depicted in the prehistoric Egyptian palettes, ancestors of the hypocephalus.

4. We are ever reminded that the one on the throne is the Unknown, the Hidden One: “The One who is not named, . . . and no heaven can learn his name.” [233] We are not ready either to hear the name or to behold the person of the one.[234] Need we recall that the one at the center of many hypocephali is Amun, whose name means “the Hidden One,” who “conceals his true nature from his children” ?

5. In the seventh heaven he meets a hostile challenge: “How far is he who dwells among aliens [or in the flesh] to go up?” He is terrified until another voice speaks: “The holy Isaiah is permitted to come up here, for his robe is here.” [235] God tells the visitor that he has only a limited stay and must then return to earth. [236]

6. Just before his return he is shown some books containing “writing . . . but not like the books of this world” in which “nothing which is done in this world is hidden.” [237]

The Ascension Texts

Through the years comparative studies of the apocryphal texts have grown steadily broader, and Ioan P. Culianu has given the broadest survey of all, viewing the whole of ascension texts; he calls them psychanodia, which is a new name for the “Himmelsreise der Seele” or “heavenly ascension of the soul.” [238] The literary genre of ascension texts was first publicized by Martin Haug in 1878,[239] and scholars were soon in full cry recognizing the type everywhere and speculating and arguing as to just where it began and how it spread. Because Haug was working with Persian sources, there was from the first an overwhelming consensus attributing the origin of the ascension motif to the Iranian zone, which was immediately challenged by champions of an Egyptian origin, and then in 1893 Moses Gaster claimed Hebrew beginnings, tracing the theme to an original revelation of Moses.[240] By an odd coincidence, as we have seen, the first chapter of the Book of Moses, companion piece to the Book of Abraham in the Pearl of Great Price, presents all the details of a classic ascension in powerful dramatic form. Culianu himself finds such ascension accounts among “Greek medicine-men such as Abaris, Epimenides, and Pythagoras.” [241] He notes that “the Jewish and Jewish-Christian tradition of the heavenly journey forms the main source of the mi’raj or heavenly ascension of the Prophet Mohammed.” [242] These, however, are one-way ascensions and not the peculiar genre with which we are dealing.

Culianu finds “three main types of apocalypses” : (1) the elective or call-apocalypse, in which “the hero is elected from above by reason of his special merits”; (2) the “apocalypses by accident” where “the hero is the victim of an accident or serious illness”; and (3) the quest-apocalypse in which “the hero strives to obtain revelation.” [243]

We, however, have considered another type in which the hero at the point of death begs for a rescuer from above to take him aloft and is not only carried to heaven but is allowed to return again and be in his physical body. During the journey through space, his guide points out and explains to him the nature and structure of the universe, with the understanding that he is to put it all down in a chart or book when he gets home and pass that treasure on as a secret heritage to his posterity. That is a large order, and it is surprising that so many ascension texts of this very peculiar nature have come down to us, every one of them appearing as a strange and alien intruder in the literature in which it is found.

From Other Worlds

Major disturbances caused by the Ascension of Isaiah centered in the Ariège (meaning the Ridge). On “the spectacular rocky slopes of the Ariège valley, not far from Andorra,” is a system of caves including “La Cave des Eglises,” rightly named because “in the thirteenth century, a sect called the Cathars took refuge from religious persecution in the upper galleries of the cave” (fig. 46).[244] The same cave had been used for religious purposes for quite some time: “This cave of Les Eglises had been the successive shelter not only of Paleolithic hunters, but also of Bronze Age peoples whose fragments of pottery have been excavated from the entrance.” [245]

Figure 46. This particular area of the Pyrenees in France has been a human refuge for millennia. The decorated cave at Les Eglises in the Ariège valley shows the great effort that these Paleolithic artists expended on getting to a nearly inaccessible site for their work. Map by Tyler Moulton.

At various locations in southern France are found caves with a variety of Paleolithic artifacts and paintings. The most famous is Lascaux cave, where ancient artists left behind on the walls of this cave the greatest and finest collection of prehistoric art in the world, dating “between the end of the Aurignacian-Perigordian, some 20,000 years ago, and the Magdelenian of about 15,000 b.c.” [246] That is going back a long time, but there would seem to be a connection: “The contents of the caves vary, the masks vary, the batons and their stories vary,” but nevertheless it is clear that we are dealing with the “art, symbol, rite, and notation [which] operated within a culture that for some 25,000 years had a certain basic homogeneity.” [247] The same activities going on for 25,000 years? In the cave of Lascaux, “the decorated walls have a patina; worn and smoothed by time and friction, they bear witness to the constant use and frequentation to this part of the cave. At one far end of the Chamber of Engravings, a stone, highly polished, worn, and much blackened by the constant passage of countless human bodies, forms a kind of lip over a yawning pit.” [248] “At the bottom of the shaft, which is about 16 ft. deep, and must once have had to be negotiated by means of a rope, there is a small irregular chamber . . . [where] all attention is focused on the painted panel about 6 ft. 6 in. in length. . . . The central figure is a bird-headed man” (fig. 47).[249] Though there has been endless debate about each of the hundreds of drawings in the Lascaux cave, on this one alone there has been general agreement. It depicts a man who “appears to be falling backwards; . . . in front of him the schematized figure of a bird . . . with a head . . . similar to his own, is perched on a pole.” [250] He appears to be dead or wounded, or in a trance. Professor Alexander Marshack explains, “clearly the bird had a value and story of its own. . . . Its comings and goings could only be storied at this stage, and the bird was, therefore, probably a sign and symbol . . . of an equation that recognized flight, disappearance, and return.” [251] “Since every shamanistic performance or trance is, in a sense, also a story . . . the bird as a subsidiary or specialized character, able to fly and return and associated with the shaman, would have a certain storied validity. . . . Did the bird carry or leave the spirit of the shaman on a journey? . . . Was this the image of a journey to a far land—say to the land of the spirits or the land of death—which required a journey or return in bird-shape or with bird-help?” [252] This seems to be describing a holy man’s ascension some twenty thousand years ago.

Figure 47. This famous, dramatic composition in the well of the Lascaux cave has been a source of endless debate, ca. 15,000 b.c.

			 The important question is Where do these people (since we cannot deny their existence and can only speculate on their activities) fit into the gospel plan? We can thank the Pearl of Great Price for a clear-cut solution. Abraham and Moses hold the door open to all who desire to enter the company of the Lord, regardless of when and where they lived. The barriers of space and time are removed in both books: “millions of earths like this . . . would not be a beginning to the number of thy creations” (Moses 7:30). “There are many worlds that have passed away by the word of my power. And there are many that now stand, and innumerable are they unto man” (Moses 1:35). There are other creatures and other people of whom we know nothing, for the world was designed for multiple use. We are told that the people who made the paintings of Lascaux displayed “high intelligence, cognition, rationality, knowledge and technical skill.” [253] And yet apparently they lived in another world from ours—it is safe to say that an Ice Age made this the equivalent of another planet in those days.

When Moses wanted to see what was going on everywhere, God told him: “For mine own purpose have I made these things. Here is wisdom and it remaineth in me” (Moses 1:31). “But only an account of this earth, and the inhabitants thereof, give I unto you” (Moses 1:35). That was none of Moses’ business—pity the species in which the human race shows too much interest! Those other worlds need not be all other planets; there have been on this earth “holy men ye know not of” (D&C 49:8). The Lord himself has “other sheep,” unknown to any of our race (3 Nephi 15:17; John 10:16). There have been occupants here long before our time.

The new perspective which the Books of Abraham and Moses give us is actually the old one, rejected by the doctors of the schools and well-nigh obliterated by the fourth century. And then there is the matter of dispensations. The Pearl of Great Price alone of all our scriptures gives us original testaments from seven great dispensations, all personal testimonies written in the first person: Adam (Moses 2:26–3:25; 4:6–6:11; 6:51–68), Enoch (Moses 6:25–8:2), Noah (Moses 8:9–30, esp. 24), Abraham (Abraham 1–5), Moses (Moses 1), Jesus Christ (Joseph Smith—Matthew 1:1–55), and Joseph Smith (Joseph Smith—History 1:1–75).

Vixere fortes erant ante Agamemnona[254]

Are all seven dispensations necessary? Is it impossible for there to be more? After all, they all lay down the same principles and tell the same story, and the countless worlds, “worlds heretofore formed,” we are told, are composed of the same substances and on the same pattern and subject to the same physical laws as our own. Moreover, all the worlds share each other’s treasures so that at the crucifixion of Christ, “the heavens were veiled, and all the creations of God mourned” (Moses 7:56), and at the destruction of the wicked in the flood “the whole heavens shall weep over them . . . wherefore should not the heavens weep seeing these shall suffer?” (Moses 7:37). With all this sharing there is no monotony since each world is a unique combination of items contributed from all quarters of the universe. So, why should those clever people living ages before our Adamic takeover not be permitted to live without our approval? One beauty of the hypocephalus is the broadening of our mean provincial existence. We ignore the fall of the sparrow, but strangely, God does not; we “suffer the hungry, and the needy, and the naked, and the sick and the afflicted to pass by [us], and notice them not” (Mormon 8:39). We are not even interested in our own world except where it concerns our immediate success and comfort; we refuse even to consider the doctrines the Prophet Joseph has given us about the lives of other creatures in their respective sphere and element. It is the singular value of the Pearl of Great Price that it recognizes the reality of races, peoples, civilizations, and great empires, which everyone knows have existed through the ages but to which modern Christianity grants no access to salvation—to the Christian world it is as if they had never existed, though they represent at least ninety percent of the world’s population.

The Shield of Achilles

Those same scholars who were quick to recognize Egyptian elements in Abraham’s view of “the fullness of the universe,” adding “and you will see its circles in all,” [255] were equally prompt to accept Francis Schmidt’s announcement that the same imagery meets us graphically set forth on the famous Shield of Achilles in the 18th book of the Iliad.[256] In an anonymous fourth-century Christian text, Hortatory Address to the Greeks, wrongly attributed to Justin Martyr, the writer pointed out that Moses’ description of God’s creation is just like Homer’s discourse on the shield and explained the coincidence by suggesting that Homer, “having learned this in Egypt, and having been much taken with what Moses had written in the Genesis of the world, . . . fabled that Vulcan had made in the shield of Achilles a kind of representation of the creation of the world.” [257] It will be recalled that Hephaestus was both the creator of the world and the god of artisans, and in both capacities was always equated with the Egyptian Khnum = Ptah.

The experts who saw Abraham’s cosmic views reflected in the Book of the Dead and the Shield of Achilles could not be expected to make the connection with the hypocephalus, since the all-important chapter 162 of the Book of the Dead is missing in almost all editions available to them. They could not know that the shield showed remarkable parallels with the hypocephalus, and they were even less likely to be aware of the exciting ascension story that tells how the thing was brought to earth.

But first the description of the shield itself: it was circular, with a broad outer rim representing the cosmic stream of Oceanus,[258] the Egyptian Waters of Nun. The big circle was covered with many things cunningly wrought (δαἱδαλα, daidala),[259] including the sun, moon, and constellations—significantly not the Zodiac but the favorite constellations of the Egyptians: the Pleiades, Hyades (Taurus), Orion, and Ursa Major (one of the circumpolar stars, which never set, called by the Egyptians the “Imperishable” or “Unwearying Ones”; see p. 42, fig. 4).[260] There were two cities depicted, one depicting a world at peace, the other at war. Central to the peaceful scene was a law court in full session, along with the happier aspects of civilized town and country life.[261] On the dark side all the horrors of war and destruction were depicted in detail.[262] Furthermore, it is the shield, not the armor as a whole, which is the subject of the chapter—the shield gets 130 lines while all the rest of the panoply gets only five.[263] The hypocephalus design was apparently no invention of late antiquity, and neither is the story behind it.

Hans P. L’Orange has written a valuable study on the many ancient traditions of the sacred shield, depicting the universal ruler, the cosmocrator, in the center of the disk, surrounded by all the signs of the Zodiac (fig. 48). In many legends its circle not only depicts the high heavens but the very means by which the king ascends to them. All over the ancient world, from Yazilikaya to the comic book character Asterix, the earthly king representing the Supreme Lord of Heaven is depicted being raised up on his shield as his ascension to heaven to receive his throne and government of the universe (fig. 49).[264]

Figure 48. Jupiter Optimus Maximus is enthroned at the center of the turning Zodiac with Aries the Ram leading out at the top. The shieldlike heavens are held up by Atlas. Marble (Atlas figure restored), second century a.d. Vatican Museum.

This is how Achilles got the shield. The scene opens with a great mourning, ostensibly for Patroclus, but in reality for Achilles himself, who lies flat on the ground throwing ashes over himself while the maidens sing of his coming demise.[265] His mother Thetis comes to him and bids him farewell, clasping his head and wailing.[266] Achilles is ready: “Let me die at once. . . . I shall never again return home!” [267] But whoever is being lamented, the whole funeral operation, including the promised sacrifice of Hector with the twelve Trojan victims to be slaughtered at the funeral pyre of Patroclus, must be suspended until Thetis, the heavenly go-between who, we later learn, on this occasion takes the form of a hawk, can go up to heaven and fetch the shield. Why must he have a shield if he is not going to do battle? Why must his mother go to fetch it for him?

Figure 49. (A) An Assyrian image of a horned god holding an ax and thunderbolt is carried by four soldiers. Yazilikaya relief, ca. 730 b.c. (B) The prophet Nathan anoints David king as his men lift the shield toward heaven. MS illustration to Psalm 26, eleventh century, Vatican, Biblioteca, Codex gr. 752, fol. 82r. (C) Zadok anoints Solomon in a similar fashion. Vatican, Biblioteca, Regin. Greco 1 fol. 285. (D) The pompous chieftain of Asterix’s village in Gaul likes to be carried around on a shield by two of his soldiers.

			 While she is gone, another rescuer—an ἄγγλος (angelos), Homer calls her, a messenger, “Swift Iris,” with all the colors of the rainbow (like a true akhu or menet-bird), and, by a quaint coincidence, also the iris of the eye—puts in an appearance and takes over. She is also a false messenger, since Hera sent her without the knowledge or permission of Zeus or the other gods.[268] She tells Achilles to make an effort to bestir himself (as the Two Ladies regularly do in the case of the supine Osiris).[269] He complies, and as he arises, “from the head of Achilles flame mounted to heaven,” [270] kindled by Athena, and the opposing hosts “when they saw the unextinguishable fire blazing above the head . . . were paralyzed with fear.” [271] As if to spare us nothing, next comes the grim sunset as “all night long the Achaeans made moan and lamentation” for Patroclus [272]—a night of unrelieved gloom, a “horror of great darkness” indeed!

While all are mourning for Patroclus,[273] Achilles’ mother in “the imperishable starry abode” [274]—the stock Egyptian expression, ἰḫm-sk—consults with Hephaestus and gets the shield, returning to earth with it “like a hawk sweeping down from cloudy Olympus.” [275] Her arrival makes it possible for Achilles to complete his earthly mission and then have a proper funeral.

As Dietrich Mülder states, “The most interesting question in all the practical criticism of Homer is how these poetic images got transferred to a material shield.” [276] The hypocephalus connection clears up the matter. The real shield brought from above is solid proof that the bearer has been there; it is the round star-and-planet chart that every visitor to heaven has been required to bring back with him to instruct the less enlightened. It is the answer to the terrible question: “Yes—I have been there!”

The Story of Er

The best ascension stories are in a class by themselves, exotic and mysterious, and all very secret. To find a good example, Plato, who wants to believe that there is more life hereafter, has to go beyond the bounds of the Greek world to the haunting tale of Er the son of Armenios, the Pamphylian. Left for dead on the battlefield for ten days, Er came to life again as he lay on his funeral pyre, about to be burned.[277] He then described what had happened after his death on the battlefield. When his spirit left his body along with those of the others slain in battle, they arrived at some supernatural place (τόπον τινὰ δαιμόνιον, topon tina daimonion) to behold the usual polarized universe—two pairs of chasms, two in the earth leading down, and two in the sky leading upwards. Between these chasms sat two judges holding court with the righteous moving to the right and upwards, and those found guilty to the left and downwards.[278] As Er approached the judges, they told him that he was to become a messenger (ἄγγλος, angelos) to mankind of the things there, and they commanded him to carefully listen to and observe everything in that place.[279]

After seven days in a sort of marshaling area, he was shown the structure of the universe in what de Santillana and von Dechend call a “complete planetarium” (fig. 50).[280] The center was “a straight shaft of light” like a pillar, which passed through both heaven and earth.[281] From this center stretched cables (δσμοἱ, desmoi) in every direction, holding the revolving heavens in place like the supporting cables of a ship.[282] Connected to the ends of the cables was a spindle (ἄτρακτος, atraktos) that indicated the force or “necessity” that holds things together—as near as anyone has come to defining gravity.[283] The concept is no more nor less realistic than that of gravitation. On the shaft of the spindle were eight circular whorls of diminishing size, nested one within the other to form a single large whorl, but each whorl rotated independently.[284] These whorls represented the orbits of stars, the sun, the moon, and the other planets. Seated on three thrones equally spaced around the whorl were the Moirai, the Fates who declare past, present, and future times.

Figure 50. An accurate diagram of Plato’s spindle of necessity is difficult to create because of his ambiguous descriptions. (A) The spindle whorl resting on the knee of the goddess Necessity was made of eight crystalline hemispheres nested within each other, each moving at a different speed and creating a beautiful sound: the Harmony of the Spheres, presided over by the three Fates. Each individual chooses the kind of life he or she desires and receives a corresponding destiny. The souls then pass under the throne to the fields where they will be reborn. (B) Six hundred years later a similar model of the cosmos was made in bronze and used for divination. It also has the three Fates surrounding a pillar, which supports an engraved disk filled with mystic letters, understandable only to the initiated. Second century a.d. Pergamum Museum, Berlin.

What makes the myth significant is that Plato takes it literally: “The story (μῦθος, mythos) has been preserved and not lost, Glaucon, and it can save us too if we believe it (πιθώμθα, peithōmetha), and we will safely cross the River Lethe (forgetfulness) and not defile our soul.” [285] He is speaking in all seriousness with actual survival in the hereafter as his goal; he will settle for no mere abstractions.

The Shamans
