In his study on the psychanodia, Culianu repeatedly refers to a particular type of hero as possibly the ultimate source of the ascension story in this or that part of the world. Thus the whole “Greek apocalyptic genre,” which, though Culianu considers it autochthonous, took its rise in the “medicine-men” from the prehistoric Abaris to the time of Empedocles and “the account of their journeys through the realm of visions.” [286] It is common to refer to such men as shamans, and they are found throughout the ancient world. Among students of the ascension motif “from the early thirties to the early fifties,” Culianu notes, “the shamanistic theory was very fashionable.” [287] The theory was that all the ascension stories which proliferate in the tremendous mixing and processing of apocalyptic literature—Jewish, Christian, gnostic, and pagan—have a single origin, and that origin is shamanism. For a century there has been heated argument as to just where shamanism began, with widespread candidates for the honor—Iran, Thrace, Central Asia, Greece, the Balkan Peninsula, etc.

Stripped of “the astonishing mythological and religious wealth” that has accrued to it, the essential elements of shamanism are the shaman’s journey to the other world above and below and the peculiar gadgets with which he equipped himself for the heavenly and earthly missions. His great adventure is “the death and resurrection of the candidate by a feigned descent into the underworld and ascension to heaven.” [288] The distinguishing aspect of the scenario is that the shaman actually survives his temporary death. The journey begins with the shaman lying in extreme distress since he must go into “long fainting spells and lethargic sleep,” [289] in which dangerous condition he nearly dies and indeed sometimes actually does die.[290] He feels himself being dismembered, but then he is restored and has the experience of ascending to heaven, carried up on the wing of a bird amidst fire and smoke [291] and passing in a bark or canoe through the ocean that surrounds the cosmos.[292] As he rises above the earth, he beholds everything below as in a map.[293] Accordingly, at the center of the cosmos, he beholds the universal king and the cosmic tree from which he is instructed to make himself a drum.[294] "The shaman, through his drumming, is magically projected into the vicinity of the Tree; he is projected to the 'Center of the World'" (fig. 51).[295]

Figure 51. (A) The shaman usually makes and decorates his own drum, based on his experience of what he has seen in the other worlds. (B) The design of his costume also reflects this understanding, such as this Tatar Tungus shaman of the Bird type. (C) The mirrors are of Chinese origin, following 2,000-year-old patterns, and were acquired by trade. (D) A round wooden-handled object was used to help restore the sun at the winter solstice. Nineteenth-century sources used by Holmberg.

“The drum depicts a microcosm with its three zones—sky, earth, and underworld—at the same time that it indicates the means by which the Shaman accomplishes his break-through from plane to plane and establishes communication with the world above and the world below.” [296] On the drum’s round surface are painted representations of everything, in particular the sun, moon, stars, sacred animals, snakes, boats, gods and goddesses, etc., all surrounded by the great ocean stream.[297] The drum was also fitted with the a handle.[298] The universe is also reflected in the round mirror which the shaman must wear to help him see the universe. Worn on his breast, back, or cap, the mirror is of prime importance, and “a Shaman can perform without costume and drum so long as he has the mirror.” [299] But the cap is also very important, and “shamanizing without a cap, they were deprived of all real power.” [300]

While the shaman is in heaven, a blacksmith, like Hephaestus or Khnum, forges and shapes the visitor’s head on an anvil and teaches him to read the writing which appears in fire on his head.[301] The shamans of the far north had, along with the cosmic drum, a round wooden object with a handle and a hole in the center of the disk, which “they held up in their hands against the sun, so that it might shine through it” at the winter solstice.[302] Also, “a ring of copper was placed in the roof-hole of the tent so that the moon could shine through” to protect reindeer cows during pregnancy.[303] There was yet another hand-held ring “to prevent the moon from withdrawing its light” —that is to say, the disks brought down the light of heaven to earth and kept it there much in the manner of a hypocephalus, the object of the devices being to maintain an uninterrupted flow of light from heaven.[304]

The connection appears more obvious in the shaman’s round copper mirrors which are thought to be of Sino-Manchurian origin.[305] The pierced disks that direct light from heaven to the shaman on earth are certainly related to those Chinese jade disks five to seven inches in diameter with a round hole in the center through which a priest would take sightings of the North Pole at critical periods (fig. 52).[306] Shamans also possessed jade disks, “Symbols of the Deity of Heaven” adorned with cosmic designs.[307]

Figure 52. The jade disk Pi and square tube Ts’ung were used as a sighting device by the Son of Heaven to align himself with the still center at the North Pole. Ca. 1000 b.c. Conjectural reconstruction.

A special kind of Pi with three sets of notches, dividing it into twelve hours, was used to track the Big Dipper in its revolution around the North Pole in a time when Polaris was not yet our North Star.

			 Being scattered all over the world, the shamans have no standard costume, though feathers must never be lacking to indicate that the subject was always a bird—an eagle or a hawk.[308] Iron or copper pectoral disks, pierced disks representing the earth, lunar crescents, miniature canoes, metal mirrors with twelve animals on them, more copper mirrors “to help the Shaman ‘see the world’ ” [309]—every item of the odds and ends of junk that dangle, clink, and flutter around the person of the shaman recalls some like object in use elsewhere in the world. These symbols raise questions as to how an ignorant shaman could know, for example, the twelve animals in the signs of the Zodiac,[310] or how primitive hunters could know of a “writing god” who writes in “the Book of Fate” the length and circumstances of the life of each person being born.” [311] The main question that has been puzzling the experts for a long time is whether the shamans living on the periphery of ancient civilizations picked up their assortment of bric-a-brac as it filtered down to them through the years, or whether the rest of the world derived the practices and souvenirs from the primitive practitioners. As Mircea Eliade puts it, “the problem of the origin and dissemination of the shamanic drum in North Asia is extremely complex and far from being solved.” [312] Shaman cosmology described the heavens as consisting of stories (usually seven) similar to Mesopotamian and Hindu concepts; some even find Mandaean ideas present,[313] or elements of Greek mythology such as crossing the river of death in a boat.[314] Some shaman concepts of afterlife are even said to be borrowed from the Russian Orthodox Church,[315] while their gods and goddesses on the drum suggest Scandinavian mythology.[316]

So we choose between shamanism as primal and primitive, teaching everybody else, and shamanism as a vacuum cleaner bag picking up litter in the suqs and sarais of the East and the camps of the nomads. The tendency of recent years has been to regard it as a derivative of more ancient civilizations, moving from the civilized southwestern into northeastern Asia instead of the reverse, with especially strong influence from Buddhist and Lamist elements from India.[317] And yet we still ask, “Who is borrowing from whom?” The resemblances are undeniable, but who has priority when we find the drum with its rich and profound cosmic symbols thumped to the rhythm of the spheres by female shamans of the Mapuche Indians of Tierra del Fuego (see color plate 4)? [318] All we can be sure of, Eliade concludes, is that shamanism is “a survival, profoundly modified and sometimes degenerated, of this archaic religious ideology centered in faith in a celestial supreme being and belief in concrete communications between heaven and earth.” [319] What more save residuals has religion to offer?

The Far East

It would be hard to say at present what high civilization has closest ties to shamanism; the principal candidates are Buddhist India and Tibet. When the Buddhist saint, Milaräpa, wished to occupy Mount Kailâsa, the axis of the world, the priest of the archaic Bön religion, already in possession, raced him to the top but fell from his shaman’s drum on which he was riding. Milaräpa magnaminously allowed him to remain in the place, and the art of the mandala was shared between them.[320] No people are more obsessed with making drawings of the universe than the lamas of Tibet. The mandalas were spiritual sketches of the cosmos and followed a uniform pattern, the most conspicuous figure being the glorious one enthroned in the center of the flaming circle with four guardian kings surrounding him, denoting the four cardinal points like the Egyptian canopic figures; they guarded the four gates.[321] Such a quadrate with Osiris in the center of a structure having four openings to the sky, “wind-eyes,” is described in the chapter of the Book of the Dead immediately preceding Book of the Dead chapter 162 and, according to Jean Yoyotte is closely related to it.[322]

The great lotus-throne in the center, supported by the disks of the sun and the moon, symbolizes “the Twin-unity of Contrasts.” [323] There is a square palace plan, but the central figure is “encircled by spheres of light.” [324] Seated on the throne “he holds in one hand a mirror . . . in the other hand he bears a small Tantric double-drum.” [325] In this picture of the universe “the outermost line of the Mandala appears as a circle of flames,” and one passes “spiritually through the flames” to move from world to world.[326] What we have is “the ground plan of the visionary heavenly abodes.” [327] The familiar motifs are the “four Guardian-kings of the cardinal points” and “the outer lotus circle of twelve Yaksha-Princes.” [328] Inevitably some scholars have noted parallels with Egypt here and have turned to the Tibetan Book of the Dead for parallels; the Great Mandala of the Peaceful Deities is the official illustration of that book (fig. 53).

Figure 53. This modern version of the Great Mandala of the Peaceful Deities preserves the ancient tradition of the Yab-Yum, Father-Mother, sitting at the sacred center. Tibetan thanka, Gyantse Monastery, before 1960. W. Y. Evans-Wentz, The Tibetan Book of the Dead. Used by permission of Oxford University Press Inc.

			 One does not hear much about the resemblances between this and the Egyptian Book of the Dead. For one thing because it is of late date, and for another because Egyptologists have been exceedingly loathe to enter into a discussion where they do not control all the sources. In the Great Mandala in question, “Occupying the four corners of the great circle are the four chief Door-Keepers . . . on a fire-enhaloed lotus throne . . . in the [top] center . . . Buddha Amitabha (red), on enhaloed lotus and lunar throne . . . with lotuses and the moon (white) on the left and lotuses and sun (gold) on the right.” [329] The Tibetan, “like The Egyptian Book of the Dead, [was] meant to be a guide for the dead man during . . . [the] intermediate state of forty-nine days [seventy in Egypt] between death and rebirth.” [330] Plainly the purpose of both is to see one through the fearful passage and the “wide gap” between this world and the next. In fact, both writings are “nothing more than guide-books for the traveller in the realm beyond death.” [331] The Egyptian writing tablet held by Thoth in the court scene “corresponds,” according to Walter Y. Evans-Wentz, “to the Mirror of Karma held by Dharma-Raja or . . . by one of the divine jury” as the deceased is brought to trial.[332]

However vaguely either of the ancient teachings may be grasped at present, there is at least one enormous difference between them. Briefly, “Buddhism of all schools maintains that personal immortality is impossible; . . . in other words, Buddhism holds that individualized mind or consciousness cannot realize Reality.” [333] In contrast the Egyptian does everything possible to insist on eternalizing his private and personal identity.

Joseph and Asenath

One of the most dramatic of the ascension texts is a story of Joseph, the great-grandson of Abraham, and Asenath, the daughter of the high priest of Heliopolis.[334] The story itself is found to contain elements of apocrypha, scriptures, classic mythology, hagiography, and the Greek novella, in which some see strong influence of certain cultic initiatory rites or minor mysteries,[335] although this is disputed by others.[336] For the romantic part of the tale, there is some sharing of motifs with both the Nineteenth Dynasty Egyptian romance of the Foredoomed Prince and Greek traditions concerning Helen of Troy.[337]

Most scholars agree that Joseph and Asenath was composed in Greek [338] and was then translated into several other languages, including Syriac, Armenian, Latin, Slavonic, Romanian, and Ethiopian.[339] The majority of scholars concur that the author was a Jew living in Egypt and date its composition to somewhere between 100 b.c. and the Second Jewish War of a.d. 132–35.[340] The Jewish author was not only of Egyptian birth and culture but possessed “a profound knowledge of Late Egyptian theology, especially the Heliopolitan cosmology and mythology, as only an Egyptian could know it.” [341] The rim inscription of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, if you will recall, is uniquely Heliopolitan; and it was Joseph Smith himself who first declared the document to be cosmological, at the same time placing it in the patriarchal setting. Joseph and Asenath is, in fact, the last of the patriarchal romances running from Adam and Eve to Abraham and Sarah, Isaac, Jacob, and Judah to culminate in the most romantic figures of all—Abraham’s great-grandson, Joseph, and the Jewel of the Nile, our own ancestress, Asenath. Like our other “hypocephalus” stories, this one is strangely aloof from the mainstream. “Neither the rabbinical nor the patristic literature has preserved any trace of the story,” according to Kaufmann Kohler.[342]

The theme of the story is one of supreme importance for the Book of Abraham—namely, to explain the deep involvement of Abraham in Egyptian life and thought. The immediate problem of the writer is to explain how Joseph the Just came to marry the devoted daughter of the high-ranking pagan, Potiphar, the high priest of Heliopolis (Genesis 41:50). The rabbis found the proposition quite unacceptable and proceeded with practiced skill to produce pious stories demonstrating that Asenath was not Egyptian at all, but in reality was the daughter of Dinah, the niece of Levi and Simeon. How, then, did she ever end up as a child in the court of Heliopolis? Among a number of convenient explanations the favorite was that it was a bird who took her to Egypt, and by far the most important version of that, according to Marc Philonenko, was a Syriac account telling how on a day when Potiphar went into the temple of On to perform his priestly duties he beheld lying on the altar the babe Asenath wrapped in swaddling clothes and “an eagle with wings extended above the little girl.” [343] The bird had brought the child from Canaan to its nest in Heliopolis—it was the phoenix, that same benu-bird, the bird of light (akh) which perches upon the benben “in the House of the Great One of the Benben at Heliopolis.”

Just as the famous psychostasy or courtroom scene from the 125th chapter of the Book of the Dead is thought to have supplied an important feature in Abraham’s picture of the otherworld, so the author of Asenath’s translation from Palestine to Egypt was helped by hints from other vignettes of well-wrapped figures, sometimes mummified children, lying upon the lion couch with hawks hovering above them—variations of our Facsimile 1.

The Egyptian author of Joseph and Asenath rejects all Jewish tales of the lady’s Israelite origin and provides instead an ascension solution: the Egyptian Asenath undergoes a sacrificial death; she dies but she does not die, for at the last moment she is revived and reborn as an Israelite, transformed but still herself.

A Cosmic Romance

The story begins with Joseph driving his golden chariot into the city of Heliopolis, the center of sun-worship in Egypt, exactly at noon on the eighteenth day of the fourth month, perhaps the summer solstice.[344] He wears on his head a dazzling crown with twelve jewels, from which twelve golden rays shoot forth,[345] leaving no doubt of his identity: “And now behold the Sun of Heaven is come to us!” cries Asenath.[346] The whole story is “an astronomical allegory in which Joseph represents the sun and Asenath the moon in a ‘hieros gamos.’ ” [347] The youthful hero has just completed his rounds of the city and the land and is ready “to go away and drive around the whole land.” [348] Joseph is a faithful image of the solar Apollo, and the wedding is to be a mystic union of Selene with Helios, for before the new life cycle can begin, new life must be assured by the sacred marriage.[349]

Before Joseph arrives, Pentephres,[350] the priest of Heliopolis, together with his wife, proposes that their daughter, Asenath, marry Joseph, but she becomes angry and scornfully rejects the proposal, saying she will marry the pharaoh’s firstborn son and retires to an upper room of the house.[351] When Joseph arrives, Asenath sees him and falls immediately in love with him, willing now even to be his slave.[352] Joseph enters the house and sits down on the throne of Pentephres, who washes Joseph’s feet and prepares a meal for him, just as Abraham did for the angel and Hezekiah for Isaiah.[353] Joseph sees Asenath in the upper floor window and asks who she is. Pentephres tells him it is his daughter and brings her to meet Joseph.[354] When Asenath arrives, Pentephres tells her to go and kiss Joseph, but Joseph refuses, saying it is not fitting for a man who worships God.[355] Asenath is crushed and begins to cry, whereupon Joseph puts his hand upon her head and prays to God to bring her “from darkness to the light, from error to truth, from death to life . . . renew her through your Spirit . . . let her eat of your bread of life, and drink your cup of blessings, and be numbered among your people that you have chosen before all things came into being.” [356] Joseph then departs to continue his inspection of the land but promises he will return a week later.[357]

In the meantime, Asenath throws all her valuables away, including her idols, and repents in sackcloth and ashes, and for seven days she neither eats nor drinks, confessing her sins and praying for forgiveness.[358] On the eighth day, she rises up on her knees, spreads her hands to the east, raises her eyes to heaven, and begins to pray.[359] The first half of the Asenath story “is centered entirely around the prayer of Asenath,” writes Philonenko.[360] She prays, “O Lord, stretch forth thy hands upon me . . . and snatch me from the hand of the enemy! For behold, the wild old lion persecutes me, because he is the father of the gods of Egyptians; . . . from his mouth deliver me, lest he carry me off . . . and throw me into the flame of fire; . . . rescue me, Lord, the desolate and solitary.” [361]

It is the cry in extremis from the lion couch. When she finishes her prayer, the morning star rises in the east, and Asenath says, “The Lord God listened to my prayer, because this star rose as a messenger and herald of the light of the great day.” [362] The morning star announces a greater light, and forthwith “a man came to her from heaven . . . his face was like lightning, his eyes like sunshine, and the hairs of his head like a flame of fire.” It was Michael, “chief of the house of the Lord and commander of the whole host of the Most High,” and yet he was in the express image of Joseph.[363] He tells her to take off her sackcloth, wash herself with “living water” and dress in a new linen robe with the twin girdle of her virginity, and then return to him.[364] When she returns, he tells her that her name is written in “the book of the living in heaven” and that it will never be removed. “From today,” he tells her, “you will be renewed and formed anew and made alive again, and you will eat the blessed bread of life, and drink a blessed cup of immortality.” [365] It is the old Re/Osiris formula, the sun entering into the underworld to revive the nearly dead. He further explains, “I have given you today to Joseph for a bride, and he himself will be your bridegroom forever.” [366] He then gives her a new name, “City of Refuge (πόλις καταφυγῆς, polis kataphygēs), because in you many nations will take refuge with the Lord God.” [367] It is repentance (μτάνοια, metanoia), who is personified as a daughter of God, that has made this possible.[368]

The messenger then instructs Asenath to dress in her wedding robe and put on all her wedding ornaments, because he will next go to Joseph and tell him all that has happened, and he will come and wed her this day.[369] Asenath falls at his feet and exclaims, “Blessed be the Lord your God the Most High, who sent you to rescue me from the darkness and bring me up into the light.” [370] The messenger then miraculously provides a honeycomb, gives Asenath a portion of it to eat, and says, “You have eaten bread of life, and drunk a cup of immortality, and been anointed with the ointment of incorruptibility.” [371] He then puts his finger on the remaining honeycomb, and a huge swarm of bees come forth from it and surround Asenath from head to foot.

What we have in the sharing of the honeycomb and the swarming of the bees is an “initiation comparable in many ways to that of the mystery cults . . . valuable evidence for the ‘Mystical Judaism’ whose existence has been the subject of much controversy.” [372] And what we also have is new light on the hypocephalus. Today it is recognized that Asenath is a good Egyptian name: “She belongs to, or is the servant of, (the goddess) Neith.” [373] There is much to show that “the portrait of Asenath corresponds in every detail to that of Neith.” The bees confirm that, for Neith, the creator goddess of Sais, is regularly portrayed wearing the red bee crown (bἰ.t) of Lower Egypt.[374] In Sais was located the “House of the Bee.” [375] Sais was the twin city to Athens on its bee rock, where Athena is none other than Neith, as noted by the classical writers.[376]

The actual marriage ceremony for Joseph and Asenath was performed by Pharaoh himself: he “put golden crowns on their heads which had been in his house from the beginning and of old,” and he placed his hands on their heads and said, “May the Lord God the Most High bless you and multiply you and magnify and glorify you forever.” [377] It is only after the Egyptian wedding at Heliopolis that the couple goes to Jacob for his blessing.[378]

Levi Is Another One

As if as an afterthought, right at the end, the writer of Joseph and Asenath tells how it was necessary to protect Asenath against the jealous son of Pharaoh, who was a rival wooer. Her uncles, Simeon and Levi, walked with her, one at her left hand and one at her right, “and Asenath grasped Levi’s hand. And Asenath loved Levi . . . because he was . . . a prophet of the Most High.” [379] But most especially because he had seen “letters written in heaven by the finger of God and he knew the unspeakable (mysteries) of the Most High God and revealed them to Asenath in secret.” [380]

And what was Levi’s experience that he should instruct Asenath? He tells his story. “I kept grieving over the race of the sons of men, and I prayed to the Lord that I might be delivered. Then sleep fell upon me, and I beheld a high mountain, and I was on it. And behold, the heavens were opened, and an angel of the Lord spoke to me: ‘Levi, Levi, enter!’ And I entered the first heaven, and saw there much water suspended. And again I saw a second heaven much brighter and more lustrous, for there was a measureless height in it. And I said to the angel, ‘Why are these things thus?’ And the angel said to me, ‘Do not be amazed concerning this, for you shall see another heaven more lustrous and beyond compare. And when you have mounted there, you shall stand near the Lord. You shall be his priest and you shall tell forth his mysteries to men. . . . Through you and Judah the Lord will be seen by men.’ ” [381]

Levi is told that the lowest heaven is dark because “it sees all the injustices of humankind.” [382] In the second heaven are the armies arrayed for the day of judgment.[383] “In the uppermost heaven of all dwells the Great Glory in the Holy of Holies, superior to all holiness. . . . There with him are thrones and authorities. . . . But the sons of men, being insensitive to these matters, keep sinning and provoking the anger of the Most High.” [384] There is an apocalyptic description of the last judgment,[385] and then “the angel opened for me the gates of heaven and I saw the Holy Most High sitting on the throne. And he said to me, ‘Levi, to you I have given the blessings of the priesthood until I shall come and dwell in the midst of Israel.’ ” [386] The angel then leads him back to earth, gives him a sword and shield and tells him, “Perform vengeance on Shechem for the sake of Dinah.” [387] He then awakens.[388] Thus we have a full-blown ascension of Levi, in which hypocephalus motifs abound.

Summary

As we have seen, the Book of Breathings and the hypocephalus both have affinities with the ascension text genre. The Book of Abraham (chapter 3) and the Book of Moses (chapter 1 for Moses and chapters 6 and 7 for Enoch) in the Pearl of Great Price are also classic examples of ascension texts. Joseph Smith’s claim that these were all related and were, moreover, translations of ancient texts is certainly compelling evidence that he knew what he was talking about.
