10

Jewel of Discernment

			 The panel to the left of the central figure in most hypocephali contains data relevant to the individual owner of the thing. In the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, figures 8 to 11 (numbered backwards) unfold an astonishing tale. It reads, as we have seen, “O God of the Sleeping Ones from the time of the creation. O Mighty God, Lord of heaven and earth, of the hereafter, and of his great waters, may the soul of the Osiris, Shishaq, be granted life.” It takes us on a journey that would be quite fantastic were it not that the trail is clearly and explicitly marked.

Abraham’s Seer Stone

Abraham, as the spiritual successor to Noah (Abraham 1:19), merited the titles of צדיק (ṣaddiq) and תמים (tāmmîm), “righteous” and “perfect, . . . but a seer is what he was from the very first moment when God ‘let Himself be seen.’ ” [1] The Talmud says that Abraham possessed an astrological instrument for which he was much sought after by the potentates of East and West. . . . Abraham had a precious stone hung round his neck which brought immediate healing to any sick person who looked on it, and when Abraham our father departed from this world, the Holy One, blessed be He, suspended it from the orb of the sun.” [2] It had returned to its proper place after having acted as a connecting link between the worlds. Here one thinks of the great solar pectorals worn by the pharaohs (see color plate 2).

To match this, beneath God’s feet as he sits on his throne is displayed, as on a ceiling of light, the entire universe with pictures of all creatures depicted on it; to the right of the throne was a garden, and before it was the temple, in the center of which in turn was an altar with a jewel upon it bearing the name of the Messiah, while a voice called upon all men everywhere to repent.[3] This, as we shall see, gave rise to the tradition of the famous Tabula Smaragdina which fascinated, baffled, and eluded the wise men for centuries.

The Tabula Smaragdina

What is the mysterious Tabula Smaragdina? A tablet of smaragd, of course. The Latin lexicon tells us that smaragdus is “a transparent precious stone of bright green color; including not only our emerald, but also beryl, jasper, malachite, etc.” [4] The late Latin form was smaraladus which became esmeralde in old French, and ultimately English emerald. Much is made of it, as the Greek letter omega (Ω) represented the all-embracing circle of the cosmos and the waters of Oceanus which surround the world.[5] Omega completes the eternal round, for as soon as you reach that letter, you are back to alpha again.

Tabula Smaragdina is an alchemist’s term, and modern scholars insist that the object is an invention of later times, that the Tabula Smaragdina first emerged in the early Middle Ages, and that its purported great age was just a fiction invented to give it its awesome authority. Like the philosopher’s stone, the Tabula Smaragdina was for the alchemists “the basic document . . . on which they said all their knowledge rested”; it was necessary for them to think of it as having passed from the Egyptians through Alexandria and the Roman Empire to the Arabs, the great men of the Renaissance, Christian mystics, and the founders of modern science.[6]

There are many stories of the finding of the Tabula Smaragdina, which we shall consider presently, but the earliest says that it was found under the head of a mummy, making it in the most literal sense a hypocephalus. In that account an Arab savant describing the opening of a small pyramid in a.d. 903 tells of finding there a coffin with a leaden lid; in the coffin was a venerable old man under whose head was a tablet of white onyx.[7] This suggests the Florentine hypocephalus accompanied by a stone “feather, striated, perhaps made of Ethiopian emerald,” and a “pillow, to rest the head on, very small, semi-circular, of haematite.” [8] The Egyptian find of a.d. 903 excited speculation as to the identity of the person in the sarcophagus. Some said it was Moses, some Jesus, and some Mohammed; in any event all considered the stone beneath the head a most portentous object.[9]

This raises the issue of color. Clear back in the Coffin Texts we learn that the green stone is an aid to salvation: “Verily I have the green stone which delivers one.” In particular, it delivers one’s family: “I am the green stone, clay was given to me to seal the decree” —namely, “to seal the decree of a man’s family, to return a man’s family to him in the next world.” [10] There are two chemically distinct materials that are accepted as jade: nephrite and jadeite, and the color varies from lavender to black, green, and white.[11] As mentioned above, “Chinese emperors spoke to God through ritual pi, disks of jade with holes in the middle, themselves the symbols of heaven [see pp. 408–9, fig. 52]. . . . Jade was the link in China between earth and heaven, the bridge from life to immortality.” [12] There is a resemblance of the pi disks to the Emerald Tablet of the medieval alchemists, and certainly the resemblance to the hypocephalus both in form and function cannot be denied.

Other documents besides hypocephali have been found under the heads of mummies; most notable is the Book of Breathings, which was received by the prophet along with the hypocephalus. A peculiarity of the Tabula Smaragdina is its frequent association with a red stone which the Arabs called yāqūt. According to the dictionary it can be a hyacinth, sapphire, ruby, corundum, or carbuncle and can be specified as either red or green.[13] The striking contrast is found in nature as “large hexagonal ruby crystals” are found “growing inside a rind of green zoisite.” [14] The striking conjunction of red and green is found in the earliest texts saluting the Red Crown of Pharaoh. “It is conceived of as an eye, being in fact the bodily eye of the king himself . . . ‘its red’ is the blood which it sheds, the ‘green’ stands for the flourishing of the king.” [15] In the archaic Ramesseum Drama, carnelian and faience ornaments are brought to the king as symbols of dominion, as of death and resurrection.[16] He is crowned by the Keeper of the Two Great Feathers,[17] and a ladder is set up for his ascension.[18]

Changing color is one of the sapphire’s marvelous qualities. The sapphire turns green with aging like the Indian fever-stones, but far more wonderful is the sea change of the red yāqūt into green; the alchemists insist that yāqūt in the course of time always turns green. To quote a famous Hermetic work, On the Cause of Things, “I declare that the smaragd is a yakut [the red kind], because in the beginning of its formation it condenses as a yakut throughout. Its color is naturally red, but because of the force of condensation black parts begin to appear in it so it becomes sky-colored. The sky-colored parts in turn gravitate to the center, leaving the surface changed from red to yellow; then through the heat of condensation the blue and yellow are mixed and end up as the final and official green of the smaragd, though its origin was as the yakut; . . . it is indeed possible that the yakut can become a smaragd and smaragd become a yakut, just as silver can become gold and copper can become silver.” [19] Equally accommodating is the sapphire’s changing, depending on the mineral mix, into blue, pink, green, yellow, orange: “They’re all sapphires.” [20] It is by cooking at various temperatures and for various lengths of time that one gets the effect, and this is just what fired the enthusiasm and the endless experiments of the alchemists.[21]

The Tabula Smaragdina is among the supreme mysteries of the alchemist’s art. It is constantly referred to by mystics and the cultists right down to the twentieth century. Those who have published the story and the content of the Tabula Smaragdina in its later versions have routinely traced it back through Plato and Pythagoras to Hermes and “The Emerald Tablet of the Hermetics.” Some give fuller genealogies, for example, passing down the line in chronological order beginning with Chymes, to the Egyptian Horus, to Agathodaimon (the Egyptian Wen-Nefer or Osiris, benefactor of the race), to Hermes (Thoth), and so down to Pythagoras, Plato, Aristotle, Democritus, Meria, the daughter of King Saba. The descent continues to Theosebeia, to Archelaos, Theophilos, the Emperor Justinian, Heraclius, and so on to the Middle Ages.[22] In the Late Period its principal possessor and transmitter was the famous Apollonius of Tyana. Professor Julius Ruska of Heidelberg has obligingly collected and published a wealth of original text to which I shall refer hereafter. Solomon gave the great sapphire to the Queen of Sheba in one tradition, and Christ used it at the last supper.[23] This is notable because the so-called Solomon’s cup, brought from the East to Genoa where Napoleon robbed it from the cathedral, is normally a light greenish-yellow, but lit from within it becomes deep red! (fig. 54; see color plates 5 and 6).[24]

Figure 54. Though called the Lycurgus cup after its largest figure, the entire scene shows the triumph of Dionysius, the recently arrived god of wine, over the unbelieving King Lycurgus (A). The king tried to chop down a grapevine, which the god then caused to grow up and strangle him. This scene was carved in high relief by spinning small copper wheels into a special green glass cup made with fine gold particles dispersed throughout (B). This formula enabled the cup to show a miraculous transformation: when light shines from within, the cup glows deep wine red. The artist even went to the effort to carve hollows in the figures from behind so the light would pass through more evenly. This elaborate cup with its ability to change from green to red was probably used in the Dionysian mysteries. 6½” high, fourth century a.d., Roman, MLA 1958.12–2.1. The British Museum.

More than 1600 years earlier, a similar magical effect was created by Egyptian craftsmen when they carved an elaborate calcite lamp to glorify Tutankhamun (C). The two openwork sides show Heh, the god of eternity, holding an ankh and a notched palm rib around the pharaoh’s name, thus giving him life for one hundred thousand years. When the floating wick was lit, the light revealed a ghostly image in full color of the pharaoh relaxing on his throne, while Queen Ankhesenamun offers him two palm ribs and heb-sed amulets. This was painted on the outer surface of the close-fitting inner cup. Found in the southeast corner of the burial chamber, it may have been lit to bring light to the pharaoh in the darkness of the tomb. 20 1/4 ” high, ca. 1330 b.c. JE 62111. Cairo Museum.

			 Thai dealers have taken advantage of Sri Lankan stone merchants by not telling them that “simple heat-treatment turns the bland sapphires brilliant blue or yellow.” [25] More sophisticated treatment includes packing sapphires into titanium and iron oxides and treating them to 2050o C for six hundred hours.[26] Such a process produced the marvelous Jalakanta stone of India and the shining stones of the Jaredites, which, like the Jalakanta, lit the travelers through the midst of the sea.[27]

The Passion for Green

No Egyptian artifact is more familiar than the scarab beetle; it represents the heart and is a symbol of rejuvenation, health, and resurrection. If possible it should be made of green jasper.[28] The observant visitor to any Egyptian collection, especially the Cairo Museum, cannot but notice the total domination of blue-green and gold—especially green or lapis lazuli, among the exhibits of widely varying artifacts. One of the Pyramid Texts tells us how the Lady Hathor “strides forth strewing green stone, malachite, turquoise of the stars; if you are green then will the king be green as a living rush is green.” [29] In the coronation drama of the Ramesseum Papyrus when ornaments of lapis lazuli and carnelian are brought to the king, “two tall feathers” are placed on his head.[30] The well-known maat-feather was worn as a large amulet of green stone by the Egyptian judge on the bench.[31] The Lady’s green stone goes back to prehistoric times as the tranquilizing amulet of Sachmis, the Lady of the Turquoise Amulet and the green plant, who holds the papyrus stalk in her hand as ruler of the natural world.[32] Hers is the shesmet-girdle in the town of Shesmet, meaning the green mineral malachite, which is associated with mfk.t “turquoise” in the Pyramid Texts.[33] Likewise Osiris is addressed in one of the Pyramid Texts: “Thou art like the green turquoise of the stars,” for his face is often painted green, “green like the living plant of light is green.” [34] Green is the color of living, thriving plant life.

It should hardly be necessary to go into details about the wedjat-eye at this point, since the word wḏ means whole, complete, healthy, undamaged, thriving, flourishing.[35] The prescription for making the hypocephalus in Book of the Dead chapter 162 requires that the cow be drawn in ink upon new or fresh papyrus (ḏmʿ n mwy(.t)).[36] “The children of Nut are they who descend upon thee,” says another of the Pyramid Texts, “their crowns on their heads and their collars of green foliage around their necks; they make green the crowns of the North.” [37] Let us recall that Nut is the Lady of the Turquoise Amulet and also observe that the Pueblo Indians usually dance with a collar of green juniper around their necks. When they are not dancing it can be replaced by a less clumsy but more spectacular pectoral or necklace of turquoise. Alfred Lucas found that the Egyptians in their rites do not use cedar or pine pitch but only that of the juniper.[38]

The special powers of turquoise are prized by American Indians, Egyptians, and Asiatics alike. Egypt seems to share a common prehistoric tradition with Mesopotamia. Bel sits in his cella on a dais of lapis lazuli in the middle heavens of saggilmud-stone. The lower heavens, where the stars are, are of jasper, and Bel “drew the constellation of the gods on it.” The upper heavens are of luludānītu-stone.[39]

Amun’s sacred object at Thebes was a piece of an iron meteorite with a breastplate adorned with “knobs or jewels in seven rows of nine each, i.e. making use of not one, but both of the well known mystical numbers—the most potent talisman of magic virtue.” [40] The prehistoric palettes, already noted, were “plaques of green schist, they served as palettes for grinding malachite for eye paint” —green on green.[41] Egyptian malachite is always green, while turquoise, if not already green, slowly fades from blue to green.[42]

In one of the Coffin Texts the person on the altar is “the green stone who delivers one. I am the green stone which comes forth from the Lady of the People.” [43] By the purifying effects of the green stone, “Not one of my members is void of truth [mʿ.t, maʿat] . . . what did they give thee? A firebrand and a block [or amulet] of green fayence”; when the fire is put out and the amulet is thrown into the water, the deceased is allowed to pass through the gate.[44] Osiris among the dead has his face painted green, and it is said to him, “Your hair is genuine turquoise (mfk.t) when you come from the Fields of Turquoise. Your hair is lapis-lazuli (ḫsbd) . . . your teeth are turquoise.” [45] “O Lady of the Horns, . . . Hathor, Lady of Turquoise . . . who makes the Two Lands green (wḏ), leader of the gods in her name of Wadjit (Wḏy.t) . . . Wedjat has power over fire in her name of Lady of the Iam-trees. . . . This book shall be recited 16 times by those who keep the tempo.” [46]

One only need survey the realms of folklore to know that green brings one uncomfortably close to the other world. Besides the Green Knight,[47] who has so much in common with the hero of the Egyptian Setne romance, we have the green revenants; [48] green as a symbol of martyrdom, of the devil, dwarves, and fairies; and “little people” usually clad in green like the green-clad huntsman, Robin Hood, or the “grass green silk” of the Queen of Elfenland and her attendants, who clad True Thomas of Erceldoune in green garments for his return to earth after seven years in the underworld. More particularly, there is the quest for the green stone in strange and dangerous lands where it is guarded as the plaything of lions, elephants, or serpents.[49]

Some of the mirrors that conveyed the light of the sun into the earth inevitably turned green, being copper. Less subject to deterioration were the crystal or beryl gems which conveyed the real image of the sun into the temple with such intensity as to light the year-fire. The renewal of light and life at Easter was effected in Egypt, in Byzantium, and in Old England by a crystal or burning-glass of beryl, which would literally bring a tiny image of the sun into the shrine to kindle the Easter-fire.[50] “For example, on a recently discovered fresco from a monastery in Macedonia in the thirteenth century, you can see Abraham the Righteous. He holds two round mirrors, which show his offspring on the one mirror, the sons of Sarah, and on the other the sons of Hagar, meaning the two peoples who share the world between themselves and will be found again at the feet of the apostles on the day of Pentecost.” [51]

Green dominates at the creation. Thaʿlabi, collecting legends from a wide area, tells how “when God desired to create the heavens and the Earth, He created a green jewel many times the (size of the) layers of the heavens and the Earth.” [52] Then he sent down a green gem from the highest level of paradise to provide a foothold for a wondrous bull.[53] To make his place still more secure, “God created a green rock, the thickness of which was equivalent to that of seven heavens and seven earths.” [54] But there was no resting place for the rock, so God created a great fish called Lutiyah (Leviathan) and Bahamut (Behemoth) and put the rock on its back.[55]

But Ali ibn Abi Talib says that “God created a huge mountain from green chrysolith from which the greenness of the heavens came. This (mountain) was called Jabal Qaf.” [56] So here we are back at the Tabula Smaragdina, which, as we noted, was called the Qaf. “Wahb said that Dhu l’Qarnain (Alexander the Great) came to the Jabal Qaf and saw the small mountains around it, so he said to it: ‘Who are you?’ And it said: ‘I am Qaf.’ ” [57] Alexander then said: “Qaf, tell me something of the greatness of God.” [58] I shall “lay thy foundations with sapphires,” says the Lord (Isaiah 54:11; see color plate 8). Giorgio de Santillana and Hertha von Dechend make much of an ancient and widespread legend well known to the shamans that in the middle of the whole earth there is an island, “ ‘the center of celestial power,’ upon which there is a tree, or a stone, . . . or the ‘mountain of Zion’ itself; or there is the ‘White Altar-Stone,’ which is a ‘fiery’ one . . . ; under this stone there is ‘a green fire, the king of all fires.’ ” As long as this unquenchable fire remains under the stone, the earth is safe.[59] “Those (accounts) of the stone,” de Santillana and von Dechend observe, “resist successfully every decoding for the time being.” [60]

The Green Flash

From the fear that haunts all men, the best escape the Egyptians found in contemplating that moment of passage that would settle the question is the assurance of the passage, however dangerous, upon which the Egyptian fixed his attention. One can see the sun changing its aspect from zone to zone in the heavens, but it is the moment of passage on the horizon that is the moment of truth. Then, if ever, we can hope for a look at what Erik Hornung calls a peek through a chink in the wall; and there we come nearest to getting it in a spectacle to convince any beholder that something most unusual is going on at the critical interface. For when the big moment comes, everything turns green in a dazzling flash! The writer’s own experience, while walking around the ridge of Crater Lake, Oregon, at dawn in 1926, is what put him onto this.

The great event takes place at the moment the sun makes the transition: “The Sun appears tinged with red at its lower limb (the familiar yakut or carneol combination with emerald-green), and as the last lune of the disk approaches the horizon, it becomes entirely green, and there is a sudden, brief, upward flash of green light at the moment of setting. A similar effect can occur at sunrise” (see color plate 7).[61] The phenomenon seems to be especially frequent in Egypt, and “Egyptologists have often seen the flash in the dry land of the Nile and state that the ancient Egyptians were familiar with it. They believed it to come from the underworld, and therefore, it is said, mummies were fittingly colored green. According to other superstitions the green ray actually shines out of paradise and therefore reveals the true color of hope.” [62]

The Endless Game

The favorite sport of modern scholarship has been the arguing about which document is dependent on which; the game never ends. Ruska suggests that the ancient connection for the Tabula Smaragdina is with Apollonius.[63] The best he can do is, he says, “to throw light on large areas of religious history.” [64] With this he leaves us hanging.

Peter Bonus of Ferrara in his work The Pearl of Great Price or Account of the Treasure and the Most Precious Philosopher’s Stone (Margarita Pretiosa, novella de thesauro, ac Pretiosissimo Philosophorum Lapide), cultivates what seems to be the deliberate vagueness of the alchemists. Is the aqua vita the true philosopher’s stone? Are the stones in question produced from vegetable life? Are the pearl, the treasure, and the philosopher’s stone one and the same? [65] The threefold title of Hermes Trismegistos suggests that animals, plants, and stones have a way of turning into each other. Always a vital piece of the puzzle or ingredient in the formula has been deliberately withheld. “Nothing is better known among the chemists than the Smaragd Tablet of Hermes,” Athanasius Kircher announces as he proceeds to debunk the whole thing as a complete fraud.[66] It is just a fiction of the philosophers, he concludes; Hermes was not Egyptian, and the text is full of Hebraisms.[67] Thus the most learned man of his time, though one of the most gullible, brushed the Tabula Smaragdina aside because he just couldn’t see any real connections and could not lay any claim to it.

In the Sanskrit mania of the nineteenth century, Gottlieb Latz argued in 1869 that the Tabula Smaragdina had nothing to do with alchemy but was Vedic, the Egyptians having got it from India as a chemical interpretation of Moses’ creation story. Thales took it over from the Jews, Latz insisted, and all the major philosophers inherited it from him.[68] Typical of the times, he notes distinct echoes of the Gospel of John and the Sibylline Books.[69] But it was all trash after all, and the nineteenth-century scholars condescended to spend their days studying and quarreling about it simply because that was the thing scholars did. At any rate Ruska concludes that it has nothing to do with us: “The world of the elements is now unveiled, everywhere rigid mathematical-physical law holds sway. There are no more surprises, no more wonders.” [70] And that is his final word: Science has spoken; it is 1929, and all is known.

In the end Egypt wins hands down; “the famous Emerald Tablet is so linked with Egyptian thought that it would be easier to ascribe it to an Egyptian priestly college than to alchemists.” [71] It would now appear that Egyptian priests and alchemists are not far separated, since Professor Philippe Derchain has discovered in the gold-work shop (fig. 55) in the temple of Denderah a poetic text which “clarifies in a singular manner the beginnings of the two currents of alchemy and hermitism which will frequently intersect . . . and demonstrates with extreme clarity their roots in the Egyptian religious tradition.[72]

Figure 55. The master of the workshop approves the necklaces and inlaid boxes presented for his inspection by two workmen. The head of Maat, the goddess of truth, oversees the weighing of gold rings, thus ensuring their integrity. The black objects being worked on are the wooden cores which are then covered by gold foil. They represent Osiris and Isis and are set into a panel remarkably similar to one found on Tutankhamun’s shrine. Ca. 1360 b.c. Tomb of Nebamun and Ipuky (Thebes 181).

The Finders

The three basic questions concerning the possible relationship of the hypocephali with the Tabula Smaragdina were (1) the circumstances of their discovery, (2) the content of the documents, and (3) their form and function.

Ruska goes to Egypt for the story of the finding where all sorts of traditions about writings hidden in crypts and temples flourished from the earliest times.[73] Ruska notes that by a.d. 200 when Clement of Alexandria was writing, there were chemical-technical texts which were designated as Books of Hermes.[74] Even the Persian classic on the subject, The Crown, reports that when the philosophers recorded the inscriptions of Hermes, they taught their King Ostanes the true science, whereupon he built a crypt in Egypt having seven chambers with seven doors of seven metals to contain seven tablets.[75] Most important, Ruska’s main source, the Book of the Syrian Christian Sergius was found in a serdab or mastaba in Egypt.[76] The Arabic author states that Sergius had handed on Apollonius’s own account of how he got the tablet from Hermes: “As I entered the chamber where the talisman was, I came upon an old man sitting on a golden throne; in his hands was a tablet of smaragd and behold, it was written in Syrian, the primal language.” [77]

There are other stories about the finding of the tablet, usually attributed to Apollonius. The most important of these works are the Book on the Nature of Things and the Treasure of Alexander. Almost all such writings have a common scenario to account for their discovery. One short account tells us that “After the death of Hermes Trismegistos, Nechepso the king of Egypt discovered the tablets of Hermes, approached them and falling on his face besought God to reveal to him the things engraved on them.” [78] The Nechepso is a real person from the third century b.c. and is mentioned in connection with Petosiris.[79] It is a strange coincidence that the opening lines of Sergius’s book read exactly like a quotation from the tomb of the famous Petosiris. Sergius reads, “I teach and describe by way of this book of mine, wisdom . . . so that you might hear my wisdom and that it might penetrate your mind and permeate your being . . . [at the end] I am Balaniyus (Apollonius), the Lord of Talismans and Miracles. I am the one who went into the dark tomb and brought the knowledge of the secrets of creation from the book of Hermes the Wise.” [80]

The more expansive story attributed to Krates begins with the familiar hypocephalus motif, with Krates praying for deliverance from the dragon; in the ascension, his guide and deliverer shows him the Ancient One sitting on a throne holding a shining tablet. “And I asked about the Old Man and it was said to me, This is Hermes, the thrice blessed and the document which he holds in his hand is the document of that which thou hast seen of the hidden meanings which is concealed from ordinary men (‘am al ‘ibad).” Finally, his guide orders Krates to write out or draw everything he has seen on his heavenly journey and pass it down from father to son.[81] So we have here another full-blown “hypocephalus” scenario, a standard ascension scenario.

An important guide through this wilderness is Zosimus, who traces the mystery back to the Pharaoh Memphres, who gave the ordinances of the temple to Solomon, who then wrote them down. He invites us to “plunge into the krater,” the cup of Poimander, to complete our initiation.[82] Zosimus places the greatest significance on the letter omega, and “the embracing one, the Zone of Kronos, the Oceanus that surrounds us, its mystic meaning completely unknown.” [83] Repeatedly we are told that in this later phase of the legend no one has the vaguest idea of what is going on; but the circle and the universal four-faced figure strongly suggest figure 1 of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. But now comes the surprise.

Book of the Dead Chapter 64

As we have seen, in the two oldest accounts of the finding story, the tablet was under the head of reclining figures. Moreover, alchemy itself came to the Arabs and Byzantines not through Greek but through Coptic—that is, Egyptian sources. But the most decisive evidence is chapter 64 of the Book of the Dead. This chapter is particularly important because it claims to be an epitome of the entire Book of the Dead: “Spell for coming forth by day in a single spell.” [84] Let us here note that the formula of the coming forth of Re/Osiris from the tomb is nothing other than the coming forth of the dead by day after the manner of the sun. The opening utterance of chapter 64 gives the words which the god—depicted in figure 2 of Facsimile 2—declares as he steps forth in the new day: “I am yesterday and I know tomorrow!” [85] He then refers to himself as the Two-faced One—our figure 2.

The text of chapter 64 of the Book of the Dead first appears on the sarcophagus of Queen Mentuhotep of the Eighteenth Dynasty.[86] Hornung writes “We are still far from a thorough understanding of the text,” of which there are two versions.[87] The vignette for this chapter “shows the sun with radiating beams over a tree, in front of which the deceased is worshipping” (fig. 56). [88] The text is almost as full of hypocephalus motifs as chapter 162 itself. “I belong to the western sky and also to the rudder of the East.” [89] “The Two-faced One whose rays are visible fayence (ṯḥn > tḥn)” [90] that has been cast (molten) by Ptah, which is on his metal (bἰ).[91] Metal reminds us that the Hebrew ךקיע (raqiʿa), always rendered “firmament” in the King James Bible, originally meant beaten copper. Is this already a hint of the alchemy of metallurgy? Mystical numbers follow in the Egyptian text, and then comes a finding story. It is the concluding statement of the text which catches our attention: “This chapter was found in Hermopolis (the city of Thoth) on a slab of metal inlaid with lapis lazuli, at the foot of the god in the time of King Mykerinos the Blessed. It was Prince Djedefhor who found it.” [92] A like account is found in the Ebers Papyrus, which Ruska dismisses as nothing but an obvious doublet of the story of Joseph which the Jews must have introduced into Egypt at the time of Ptolemy.[93] Today we know that the Ebers Papyrus refers as an eyewitness to a far more ancient event, a real famine.

Figure 56. In a remarkably clear depiction of the resurrection, we see the ba-bird carrying food and drink down to a well-furnished tomb. The artist has placed the vignette to BD 64 above the mummy within the tomb to show the living man dressed in his best white linen advancing toward an unusual depiction of the sun, source of light and heat, entering the tomb chamber below. P. Nebqed, ca. 1370 b.c. Louvre Museum.

		 But with all these hints of the Tabula Smaragdina, it is the content of chapter 64 that is impressive. For it begins with the usual prayer for deliverance: “Save me! Protect me from him who closes his eyes in sleep.” [94] The dead is acquitted by Osiris in the law court and is duly identified with Re in both the upper and lower worlds; he prays to the sun-god in his three forms; [95] he is like Facsimile 2, figure 2 “of yesterday and tomorrow,” the lord of two faces,[96] conducted from the lower to the upper world,[97] passing over heaven and earth in their vastness to the presence of God.[98] He is shown a glass disk made by Ptah, having a surface of lapis lazuli, and to crown it all he is commanded to write and publish everything.[99]

The Content of the Tabula Smaragdina

But the splendor of the Tabula Smaragdina as a jewel does not identify the genuine original, for which there have been many claimants; neither does it proclaim its real value, which is contained in the priceless knowledge of the thirteen articles written on it. The content of these precepts, as of Hermetic writings in general, falls into two classes: the religious-philosophical and the pseudo-scientific. Walter Scott, who edited the Hermetic writings, considers only the safely philosophical and abstract parts worthy of attention and advises “ignoring the masses of rubbish which fall under the other head.” [100] He had it just backwards, for it was the physical-science part that comprised the original text. The philosophical part was added as the doctors of Alexandria banned the literalism of earlier Christianity and Judaism. The Tabula was “the father of talismans” obtained by “one who had ascended to heaven in order to comprehend the light of the firmament.” [101]

For all its special appearance the Tabula Smaragdina is to be identified by its contents alone; its thirteen articles identify it wherever it turns up. These are preserved in only slightly varying form in a number of Arabic and Latin manuscripts to which Professor Ruska has obligingly supplied the original texts. The oldest, an Arabic translation of the sixth-century report of the above-mentioned priest Sergius of Nablus, reads as follows:

1. This is the true explanation regarding which there can be no doubt. [This declares it to be binding].

2. Namely, that which is above is from below and that which is below is from above, the two united in a marvelous whole. [The two halves of a hypocephalus].

3. All things originated (talabatat) from this substance (jawhar) by a single process. How wonderful is his work, for he is the ruler (ras, the head, peak) and the preserver (qayyam, sustainer) of the world (or universe, dunya). [Cf. Fac. 2, fig. 1].

4. Its father is the sun and its mother the moon, and the wind sustains it in its belly, and the earth nourishes it. [Cf. Fac. 2, figs. 1–2 vis-à-vis the female functions of figs. 5–6].

5. The father of all talismans and the container of miracles.

6. Perfect in power, that which fixes (muḥaqqa, assigns, places) the lights in the firmament.

7. The fire became our earth, and earth was removed from the fire, and the finer matter dominated over the coarser, according to counsel and wisdom. [This suggests the laboratory proceedings of the alchemist. In the older cosmic connection it recalls a hypocephalus itself as a recipient of fire from heaven. As we have also seen, the Egyptians ground malachite on the palettes.]

8. He ascends from earth to heaven to comprehend (yaqtabas, understand, receive enlightenment from) the lights on high, and descends to earth bearing the power of that which is above and below alike, that the light of lights might be with him and for that reason darkness flees from him. [The hypocephalus records the enlightenment or cosmic knowledge acquired by the hero in his ascension from earth to heaven and brought by him down to earth].

9. The Power of Powers, which masters all which is latīf (of a refined nature) and penetrates into that which is coarse. [“Power of Powers” suggests the hierarchy of powers in the Joseph Smith explanations].

10. The nature of the macrocosm (al-ʿālam al-ākbar) is that of microcosm (al-ʿālam al-āṣghar).

11. On this principle the learned operate (salakat).

12. And thus Hermes is manifested threefold in wisdom. [For the alchemists the three divisions of nature are mineral, plant, and animal. The universal division of the three worlds is also expressed in the three main zones of the standard hypocephalus.]

13. And this is the last book which he hid in the chamber. [Referring to Apollonius the Wise, Apollonius of Tyana.] [102]

This strange text is “the root of formal alchemy for at least 20 centuries.” [103] Summing up in broad and general terms, Cottie A. Burland finds “all throughout the book . . . a feeling of a double path. There is the class of opposites, of aboveness and belowness, of air with earth, of fire with water . . . the double path was really one. It was the road to perfection.” [104] The Tabula Smaragdina, like the hypocephalus, is divided to show a constant “to and from, from and to, the constant moving from one condition to another.” [105] Compare this with the Egyptian ḫpr.w “transformations.” It is the content of the Tabula Smaragdina that associates it with a number of basic Hebrew writings.

The Jews and the Tabula Smaragdina

Nothing is more significant to us than the constant intrusion of the Jews into the story of the Tabula Smaragdina. Indeed one might say the Tabula Smaragdina is their discovery. The fourteenth-century reviver of the oldest commentary on the Tabula Smaragda (a.d. 1040) recounts that none other than Hermes came to the Valley of Hebron and there found seven stone tablets on which the “Antediluvian Wiseman” (Enoch) has transcribed the knowledge of the seven arts and so preserved them from the flood. He goes on to explain that a letter to the Egyptian King Menophre explains that this wisdom had been given to the children of Israel for the adornment of the ark of the covenant. But others said that the work had been discovered not by revelation but by the mediation and intense concentration of Pithomea, Rebecca, Solomon, Philip of Macedon, and especially Hermes who found it in the Valley of Hebron at the place where Adam was buried.[106] Inevitably, nineteenth-century scholars looked for its origin among the Jews of Alexandria, where the Greeks and Egyptians turned the cosmological teachings into alchemy, for originally it was a guide to the starry heavens.[107]

God shares his gems with others, and with the stone goes his own authority. It is common to identify the Urim and Thummim with the twelve stones on the breastplate of the high priest as “a symbol of God’s presence,” which is indicated by the “comprehensive name of Urim and Thummim.” [108] This clearly suggests the stones of the brother of Jared, which were actually touched by the finger of God (Ether 3:6–8)—what better “symbol of God’s presence” on earth. A Dead Sea Scrolls commentary on Isaiah 54:11–12, “I will . . . lay thy foundations with sapphires,” informs us that “the priests (כוהנים) are the sapphires (ספרים) which—on the basis of the biblical text—are the foundations of the community!” [109] Yigael Yadin cites in this connection Revelation 21:14 and Ephesians 2:19–22 (and could well have cited the Pastor of Hermas, one of the earliest Christian writings): “the community of His elect is the ‘building’ itself (the stones). The next phrase . . . sums up the pesher by emphasizing that the priests are like ‘sapphires’ among the ‘stones’ which are the other members of the [society][110] (עצה).” In lines 4 and 5 of the commentary, the twelve stones Yadin believes to be the priests “are those who by using the Urim and Thummim light (enlighten) the law” (compare Deuteronomy 33:8–11).[111] Line 6 of the interpretation (pesher) is “based on Zeph. 3:5” and “refers to the precious stones of the Urim and Thummim whose number is full, and thus their light is not inferior to the sun in its full light.” The “pleasant stones” of Isaiah 54:12 are seen as both “the priests and the lay(-leaders).” [112]

The drawing and studying of the cosmic cycles is essential to Kabbala.[113] “As he [the Kabbalist] passes through the fixed prayers, the utterance of each and every word or phrase is expected to cause a particular zone in the Sephirotic map to flash into his contemplative consciousness.” This is the system of Kavvanoth (revelation), in which “the mind of the Kabbalists at prayer confronts not the god of traditional religion but the whole Sephirotic Universe.” [114] Each sephirah radiates a reflected light from one to the next.[115] “A vital role was played by the midrashic legend concerning the worlds which were created and destroyed before the creation of the present worlds.” [116] It also describes “how God entered into the form of Adam Kadmon or Primeval Man.” [117] From the head of the Adam Kadmon “tremendous lights shone forth and aligned themselves in rich and complex patterns.” [118]

What is going on here? This is only a beginning, for it seems that the stones, being of identical nature, are free both to divide and scatter their powers abroad or to fuse and to combine into one. Anton Jirku pointed out that “Urim and Thummim were not two things but a single object,” [119] like a pair of spectacles or a pair of pants. Louis Ginzberg posits that the message of the Urim and Thummim was read by interpretation of letters upon it: “The high priest . . . looked to see which of the letters engraved on the stones shone out most brightly, and then constructed the answer with these letters.” [120] The article on Kabbala in the Encyclopedia Judaica concludes that the sephirot were nothing less than sapphires.[121]

God’s precious stones are also foundation stones, like the one beneath his feet. The walls of John’s heavenly city were of jasper, set with sapphire, chalcedony, and emerald (see Revelation 21:18–19; see color plate 8). “And her light was like unto a stone most precious even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal” (Revelation 21:10–11). As the heavenly Jerusalem’s foundations were laid with sapphire (Isaiah 54:11–12), so its Egyptian counterpart, Heliopolis, where the patriarchs studied and learned, was discovered by Alexander the Great “through a mist of fog and it was all of smaragd and gold.” [122]

Passing It Down

Abraham’s green stone was, according to tradition, actually a book that was originally given to Adam. “This book was written on a sapphire stone.” [123] The angel who delivered it to Adam showed him the order of the planets and took him on a tour of the entire cosmos showing him every particle (see Moses 1:27) and explaining the creation.[124] He also saw the whole human race before its judges.[125] Adam taught Seth about the engravings, and Seth hid them from a corrupt world (fig. 57).[126] Enoch was shown in a dream where to find them, and the stone taught him all about the seasons, the planets, and the lights, and everything about the earth.[127] He also hid it until it was transferred to Noah by the angel Raphael.[128] By it Noah was able to discern all the workings of celestial mechanics and behold also the two sides of everything—life and death, good and evil—and the cycles of birth and death.[129] At the hour of his death, he gave it to his son Shem, and Shem gave it to Abraham.[130] By means of it Abraham “was able to see the glory of God.” [131]

Figure 57. Adam taught Seth geometry and astronomy as divinely revealed to him. To preserve the teachings through the prophesied flood and fire, they were inscribed on two pillars of stone and brick. This tradition, merged with that of the two bronze pillars of Solomon’s temple, is preserved today by the Freemasons. 12th–13th c. MS illustration, Chronica S. Pantaleonis. Herzog August Bibliothek Wolfenbüttel.

Possessors of the Stone

In the many accounts of these wondrous stones, the objects are closely identified with individuals, as personal seals establishing identity. Indeed, some would derive the name of the diamond, ἀδάμας (adamas), from that of Adam the indestructible.[132] We are told that when Adam found himself in dark night after being cast out of the garden, he despaired of ever seeing daylight again, until God gave him two stones which could produce both fire and light simply by being struck together.[133] This is a homely and practical explanation of two miraculous stones for starting fires, thus sharing salient aspects of the Urim and Thummim.

The stone, even like the seal, gives possession. Ancient kings would present a memorial stone with a grant of land, and boundary stones were sacred as emblems of divinity assuring the inviolable nature of the land.[134] It is God’s seal on the stone which gives Israel possession of the Holy Land.[135] The capstone of the temple itself is called in the Septuagint the stone of possession (see Zechariah 4:6–7). As there was a stone of possession so there was also a stone of separation.[136]

Many studies have dealt with the problem of how the various stones—the Urim and Thummim, the Tablets of the Law, the stones of the high priest’s garment, the breastplate, and the Babylonian tablets of destiny—are related. All had in common the office of agents of revelation but not magic—they have no power in themselves. “Only a high priest who was permeated with the Holy Spirit . . . might obtain an answer, for in other cases the stones [of the Urim and Thummim] withheld their power.” [137] Geo Widengren notes that “the Tablets of the Law, as well as the Urim and Thummim, play the same role as the tablets of destiny, in being the instrument by which the will of the deity is communicated to the leader of the people, be it Moses or the king.” [138] J. Massingberd Ford also suggests that “the [Babylonian] tablets of destiny may have been engraved on precious stones like the Urim and Thummim.” [139] The Urim and Thummim are often explained “as the symbolic designation of the breastplate on the ephod of the high priest, as the comprehensive name of the twelve jewels which are the symbol of God’s presence; they are not identical with the breast covering” and go back to “times before the institution of the high priestly ritual clothing” of Moses’ time.[140] Yet according to a midrash on Isaiah 54:11–12, “The 12 shine after the manner of the Urim and Thummim.” [141]

Philo has described the garment of the high priest: “On the collar, stones were fitted in, two being costly emeralds of exceeding value, on which the names of the patriarchs of the tribes were engraved, six on each, making twelve in all.” [142] And on the breast are twelve other precious stones of different colors resembling seals, four rows of three each.[143] Philo tells us that the garment of the high priest is a representation of the heavens.[144] The two great emeralds represent the hemispheres, the one above the earth and the other below it.[145] The twelve precious stones in 4 x 3 rows are representations of the Zodiac.[146] The whole arrangement is called logeion; all the heavenly bodies are arranged according to reason and analogy, and there is nothing illogical about it.[147] One must grant the Prophet Joseph true insight into this mode of thinking in reading Facsimile 2.

As Erwin R. Goodenough explains it, “the breastplate is double . . . for both in the universe and in man the Logos is double; . . . there is one Logos which has to do with the immaterial and prototypal forms . . . and also the Logos which has to do with visible things which are imitation and likeness of those forms.” [148] This full array of symbols qualifies the high priest to enter the Holy of Holies and he “ought to try unceasingly to be worthy of the cosmos . . . to become one who bears in mind the original pattern, so that he . . . becomes, if one may say so . . . himself a little cosmos.” [149] “The dress of the high-priest is then a copy of the universe (μἱμημα τοῦ παντός, mimēma tou pantos).” [150]

The stones vary in their numbers and functions but all perform in the same theater. In the Book of Breathings the initiate is told, “May you enter into the Hall of the Two Truths,[151] having been purified from every sin and misdeed. Stone of Truth (maat) is your name.” [152] In John 1:42 JST Christ tells Peter, “Thou shalt be called Cephas, which is, by interpretation, a seer, or a stone.”

Not only discernment but knowledge is the gift of the stone on the right side. Consider the tablet bound in mother of pearl, with its two covers of red sapphire, suspended between heaven and earth which showed everything occurring here below. Ibn Abbas reports that God consults the tablet 360 times a day and by it creates and sustains life; the first thing God created was a pen which reaches (like a laser beam) from heaven to earth.[153]

City and Temple

How do we make sense of the huge accumulation of green-stone lore found throughout the world? It is not as great a problem as it might seem, for as we go through the many sources it soon becomes apparent that we are always dealing with a few basic ideas and that they are closely interrelated.

To begin with, the green stone always takes us back to the beginnings, to an eternal ongoing premortal order of things of which our own world is a projection. The “great city . . . descending out of heaven from God, Having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal” (Revelation 21:10–11). It emerges from the bosom of eternity. Surprisingly the omphalos at Delphi, another projection of the universe down to this earth, was “adorned with emeralds and jewels.” [154] It comes from the apex of the universe, the throne of God. It contains the plan of the universe. Gudea, the Sumerian ruler of Larsa (2144–24 b.c.), described a dream he had in which the god Nindub (whose name means “lord of the tablet” ) holds a tablet of blue stone on which is engraved the outline of a temple—the cosmic design.[155] The Caliph al-Mansur built a palace in Baghdad called Medinat as-Salam, “the City of Peace,” in which there was a great dome called Qubbat al-Khadra, “meaning ambiguously ‘the Green Dome’ or ‘the Heavenly Dome,’ ” from which the worshippers would leap “as if to fly.” [156]

With the creation and the plan goes the concept of the foundation below, which is of green stone just as the heavens above are of green stone. It is a green barrier that sets off the worlds from each other. Thus for the tenth imam, ʿAlī al-Naqī, green “represents Malakūt, the world of the soul, immediately above our own, which is dominated exclusively by black. And Malakūt is defined as a ‘green belt of emerald, with which God makes the sky green; which is the veil beyond which God possesseth seventy thousand worlds . . . It is the key to the celestial vault.” [157] The Sefer Yetzirah says that “the whole universe emanated” with the number one: “With ‘one’ the living God brought forth graved and hewed out voice, air, and speech, and this is the Holy Spirit. With ‘two’ God graved and hewed out void and chaos. Void is a green line that surrounds the whole universe; . . . with ‘three’ God graved and hewed out mud and clay. He arranged them like a garden bed,” the green Garden of Eden.[158] In the Egyptian mysteries “Geb (the Earth) is hidden in his form, . . . the Great Circle, the Great Green is stable (firm, mn.tἰ) in his circuit.” [159]

The Personal Stone

We have seen that the hypocephalus is a strictly personal and private possession. Identification with a special stone has ever been a means of establishing possession. The boundary stone that marks possession of the land goes back to the creation where the plan, the foundation, and the boundaries are all set out. Creation, plan, foundation, and boundaries, all that brings a new world into existence, is done with green stone. Each of the chief men of Israel was himself a precious stone (Isaiah 28:16). Each of the tribes of Israel was identified with a stone, and according to the Pseudo-Philo, an angel engraved the names of the twelve tribes on twelve stones which were then put into the ark of the covenant.[160] In the Odes of Solomon, each of the righteous receives “an everlasting crown [which] is Truth. Blessed are they who set it on their head. (It is) a precious stone.” [161] The effectiveness of the alchemists’ stone depended on the intensely personal and individual relationship between it and its owner, so that “the changes in the state of ore and metal are seen as a picture of the development of the human person as well as the universe.” [162] At different times different aspects of the thing were emphasized by the dogged and befuddled searchers, who never seemed to know exactly what they were looking for, but their search was for “the secret paths of salvation.” [163]

Brigham Young reports that the Prophet Joseph declared, “every man who lived upon the earth was entitled to a seer stone, and should have one, but they are kept from them in consequence of their wickedness, and most of those who find one make an evil use of it.” [164]

A seal is a mark of identity, a test of authenticity. That makes it a stone of discernment, a stone of truth, a stone of testing, if you will, a stone of divination. Recalling that the Tabula Smaragdina became an alchemical treasure, it is significant that to this day a touchstone has the power to discern precious metal from base.[165] The Moslem tradition is that at the building of a temple Gabriel gave to Adam “a large diamond of exquisite brightness, which was afterward sullied by sins of men, and at last became entirely black” and became “the most sacred treasure of the blessed Kaaba.” [166] It has often been suggested that the Urim and Thummim originated as divination stones. “Thus the ‘precious stone’ of Isaiah 28:16 and the stone of offense in Isaiah 8:14ff are seen as the ‘jewel of discernment’ or the priest-king symbolised by a jewel which, like the Urim and Thummim and the rest of the jewels of Aaron’s breastplate, judge between good and evil.” [167]

One’s own seal, if a stone of truth, testified to one’s own bona fides; it was a certificate of initiation or endowment, showing that one had passed the most rigorous tests. Remember that the “white stone is given to him that overcometh” (Revelation 2:17). “Put on the crown in the true covenant of the Lord, and all those who have conquered shall be written in his book.” [168] In the Shepherd of Hermas the stones are the members of the church, the tested stones; others were found to be sons of iniquity and cast away, but the true stones of the tower are all qualified.[169] The Pyramid Texts describe the two stones of purification by which Horus with his little finger opened the mouth of Osiris,[170] which also suggest the two stones with which Gabriel suckled the infant Abraham in Egypt. So the man of truth may have his share of the Urim and Thummim coming down from Adam through the high priest: “A man of understanding will trust in the law; for him the law is as dependable as an inquiry by means of Urim.” [171] As the Urim and Thummim enlightens, so the individual’s stone is his guide through the mysteries. “If it please thee,” says the Tested One in a Mandaean prayer, “we will set up a pure Crystal . . . and arcana that are within it shall be established (and) . . . one by one will come into being. There will be a Pearl that will enlighten darkened hearts.” [172]

In the ancient discipline, whoever overcomes receives the crown: “Put on the crown in the true covenant of the Lord, and all who have conquered shall be written in his Book.” [173] At Solomon’s coronation eight angels came and presented to him a jewel with the inscription “If thou hast need of us, raise this stone toward heaven, and we shall appear to serve thee” [174] When the high priest wears “on his breast . . . the ‘oracle’ to which are attached ‘twelve stones’ of different kinds, set in gold . . . upon his head he has what is called the ‘tiara’ . . . their appearance makes one awe-struck and dumfounded: A man would think he had come out of this world into another one.” [175] Joseph wore a crown of gold encrusted with twelve stones, and on top of each of the stones were twelve gold rays. Asenath's crown was also of gold with a large sapphire in front surrounded by six smaller precious stones [176]

The Urim and Thummim was a reading device only for those qualified to use it. “Only a High Priest who was permeated with the Holy Spirit, and over whom rested the Shekinah, might obtain an answer, for in other cases the stones [of the Urim and Thummim] withheld their power; . . . on these stones were engraved all the letters of the alphabet, so that all conceivable words could be constructed from them.” [177] They were thus to be used along with the Tablets of the Law: “The Tablets of the Law, as well as the Urim and Thummim, . . . were instruments by which the will of Deity was communicated to the leader of the people.” [178] Indeed, Widengren shows us that the two belong together; the one must be used with the other.[179] Thus the ancient traditions are confirmed by the Prophet Joseph when he tells us how the Urim and Thummim was found with the plates of Nephi.

In the gnostic Discourse on the Eighth and Ninth—that is, the concentric spheres around the earth—the ninth sphere “seems to be intermediary between the Eighth and God in heaven,” in the ascent of the mind to the highest heaven.[180] It shows “the influence of Gnosticism upon the Hermetic mystery communities in Egypt.” [181] The all-important instructions for passing on the knowledge (final instructions of Amun to Thoth) are “that this teaching is to be carved on stone, and that you place it in the sanctuary . . . and put a square milk-stone at the base of the turquoise tablets (καλάϊνος πλάκς, kalainos plakes) and write the name on the azure (σαπϕἱρινος, sappheirinos) stone tablet in hieroglyphic characters.” [182] He is to “write an oath in the book, lest those who read the book bring abuse (κακουργέω, kakourgeō), and not (use it) to oppose the acts of fate (ἱμαρμένη, heimarmenē). Rather, they should submit (στοιχῖν, stoichein) to the law (νόμος, nomos) of God.” [183] It ends with the wrath of God against anyone who fails in his oath to guard these things.[184] As Lewis S. Keizer sums up the situation, “apparently, then, our Coptic manuscript claims to be deposited in a scriptorium in Diospolis and written in some form of hieroglyphics on a sapphire tablet.” [185] “O my son, write the language of the Book on the steles of turquoise (καλάϊνος, kalainos). O my son, it is proper that this Book be written on the steles of turquoise in hieroglyphic characters. For Nous (intelligence) alone has become the guardian of these things.” [186] The adjective καλάϊνος describes the color of turquoise or jaspter that is “shifting between blue and green.” [187]

Summary

In this chapter we have examined the Hermetic Tabula Smaragdina and noted the similarities it shares with various objects described in Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Chinese, Jewish, Early Christian, gnostic, and Arabic sources. These include shining stones, jade disks, the tablets of destiny, the Urim and Thummim, and especially the hypocephalus.
