11

Joseph Smith, Hermetic Tradition, and the Hypocephalus

			 Critics say that Joseph Smith’s preoccupation with ancient and recondite things, especially with the Egyptians, puts him in the category of a Hermetic practitioner.[1] What is meant by Hermetic? A simple definition of the Hermetic is “everything which in the course of the centuries has been attributed to the Egyptian God or wise man Hermes (Trismegistos)—namely, in the areas of astrology, magic, alchemy, and medicine.” [2] The body of literature has its origin in Egypt.[3] The idea of such a corpus of ancient Egyptian Wisdom literature passed from father to son “lies at the origin of the Hermetic logos.” [4] That, of course, would make the hypocephalus, which was passed from father to son in a priestly family (as both Ms. Edith Varga and the Book of Abraham would affirm)—and apparently concealing great wisdom from the vulgar—a typical Hermetic document.

Hermes Trismegistos

The Greeks associated their god Hermes with the Egyptian god Thoth, who was the conveyor of the word as “Re’s representative.” [5] Trismegistus is the Latinized Greek Τρισμέγιστος (trismegistos) meaning the “thrice greatest.” In Egyptian inscriptions Thoth is sometimes called the twice great, meaning very great. The three is supposed to stand for the three kingdoms of knowledge: vegetable, animal, and mineral. At the creation he is the tongue of the Creator, who conceives his creations in his mind and then activates the thought by the Word, or as the Memphite theology puts it, from the heart to the tongue.[6] As his lieutenant, who communicates the mind of Re, Thoth is his counterimage, even as the moon reflects the sun; in fact the moon is the type of Thoth as “the Lord of Time, the Reckoner of Years,” he who “divides the times and the months and the years,” the recorder of coronations and jubilees, keeper of the annals. He sets the day of every man’s birth and death [7] and is “Thoth who foretells the morrow and foresees the future.” [8] He is the secretary and messenger of the gods, and the personal representative of Re.[9] This is the very type of teaching that Joseph Smith attributes to the hypocephalus.

One could call Hermes Trismegistos the “Word” in the mystic sense. But his image is blurred (verschwimmt), a favorite word with commentators on the Hermetic. Nobody can pin it down; things slop over and run together.[10] Do not ask for tidy definitions. Hermetic and alchemy face us with “an unavoidable muddling of ideas (unverschuldete Verschwommenheit der Begriffe).” [11]

But what does this have to do with the hypocephalus? The Hermetic books offer layouts of the cosmos and the earth and the regions of Egypt in proper maps.[12] Another Hermetic text claims to be deposited in a scriptorium at Diospolis and written in some form of hieroglyphic on a turquoise tablet.[13] From the same source we learn for the first time that there were actually Hermetic colleges, secret think tanks in Egypt. Julius Ruska has observed that Hermetic charms must be on smaragd, because emerald was the special stone of Hermes.[14] The oldest technical manual on alchemy, Κλοπάτρης Χρυσοποιἱα (Kleopatres Chrysopoiia) or Cleopatra’s Book of Gold-making, presents us with a drawing of a disk divided into three circles; the inner one containing the moon and a planet (Venus?) and a comet, while the rim and the two outer circles bear Greek inscriptions, declaring this to be a hologram of everything: “ ‘One is All, and through it is All, and by it is All,’ and ‘if you have not All, All is Nothing’ ” (fig. 58).[15]

Figure 58. (A) Surrounding heavenly bodies, the middle circle starts with the sign of Christ and reads, “One is the serpent, which has poison according to two compositions.” (B) The serpentlike form at the upper right connects the circles with the sign of Christ and a cross. Various alchemical apparatus are drawn below. (C) The serpent eating his tail is the famous Ouroboros, symbol of endless creation and destruction. 3rd century, Coptic papyrus. Leiden, St. Mark’s MS 299 f. 188v.

				 The name of Hermes Trismegistos itself was “introduced into the West by Albertus Magnus . . . made especially famous by the Tabula Smaragdina.” [16] We have seen how such maps showing the heavenly terrain as it is projected on the earthly, the regions of Egypt matching heavenly segments, have turned up on ceilings and coffin lids of the Late Kingdom. And it is not irrelevant to note that Trismegistos was viewed by the Hermetic scholars of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries as the grandson of Abraham! [17]

In both Egypt and Greece, Thoth and Hermes respectively were the psychopomp or psychagogus who conducted the spirits from the upper to the lower worlds and vice versa, binding the upper and lower worlds as indicated on the hypocephali. In this capacity, moreover, Thoth is the author of the Book of Breathings, which is connected with the hypocephalus.[18] His command of all wisdom is seen in the vastly proliferating library of which he is in charge, the books of wisdom handed down from the beginning, the Hermetic books. Clement of Alexandria has given a famous description of a solemn procession of the Egyptian priests issuing from the temple. First in the parade comes the cantor, bearing a symbol of music. This cantor is required to learn two of the books of Hermes. One book contains hymns to the gods, the other regulations for the king's life. Divine authority in heaven and earth is the theme.[19] The wings on his sandals show him to be the messenger, specifically the messenger of the king whom all must obey, for the wand of office which he bears is the arrow of Zeus, subduing all within its range—it is his thunderbolt by which he rules the universe.[20] His business, as we read in the ancient Greek tragedy Prometheus Bound, is to sustain the king’s authority under all conditions.

According to Clement of Alexandria, next in the procession comes an astrologer bearing a clock, and a palm branch—the symbols of astrology; the Hermetic library of astrology contains four books that the astrologer must know by heart. One of them deals with the aspects of the stars, another with the orbits and phases of the sun and moon, as well as the rest of the stars. Then comes the hierogrammateus, the sacred scribe with wings on his head and a book and ruler in his hands. The sacred scribe must know about cosmography and geography and contain maps of Egypt and the Nile. After that comes the stole–keeper with the cubit of justice and the cup for libations; and then the ten books that deal with Egyptian religious activities. Last of all comes a prophet who is the governor of the temple. Thirty-six books contain the whole philosophy of Egypt, and six more are medical.[21] The two most famous surviving Hermetic books are Poimandres, the Shepherd of Men and To Asclepius; the basic theme is “Who is in charge around here?”

The books were passed down from father to son in great secrecy from the beginning, as we read in the Book of the Dead: “You shall not recite this book of the Wen-Nefer in the presence of anyone but only to yourself; . . . let no man witness the reading except the king and the lector priest; . . . at the wag-festival (the New Year), during the mysteries of the Duat (the Re/Osiris drama), . . . this book shall make one know how he came into being in the beginning. Do not speak about it to any man; do not repeat it. Let no eye see it; let no ear hear it . . . except yourself and the one who taught you.” [22]

But what makes a teaching definitely Hermetic or not Hermetic? The answer is always the same. Nobody knows! “There is no distinctive (Hermetic) doctrinal system, so that many Hermetica might just as well be assigned to the gnosis.” [23] In alchemy, astrology, and medicine, no distinction is made between Hermetic and non-Hermetic.[24] Therefore, comparative scholarship cannot give certain results because “strictly speaking, one cannot speak of a uniform Hermetic doctrine.” [25] Karl-Wolfgang Tröger also agrees that “the Hermetic writings contain no uniform doctrine,” [26] and Richard Reitzenstein notes that “the collections of Hermetic writings that have come down to us consist of eighteen pieces, all independent of each other, which belong to different theological systems . . . and very different time periods.” [27] It is even impossible to discover whether there is a Hermetic magic distinctive from other magic.[28] In short, there is a general agreement that it is quite impossible to sort out the innumerable intertwined threads that come together in Hermeticism, which for all the self-important careerists it has produced, remains for Professor Wilhelm Kroll a pale, washed-out religion with little or no cultic practices.[29] Mircea Eliade has traced back the alchemical and Hermetic until it disappears in the mists of prehistoric time. He finds that the elements of Hermeticism are present to various degrees and in various combinations over vast expanses of time and space—and it is never without that air of mystery.[30] Just how literal and how “spiritual” are we to take this?

Until recently everyone held that “Trismegistic piety held itself aloof from cultic, sacramental, and liturgical practices.” [31] “Hermeticism constituted not a cult but a movement of thought, probably carried on as school tradition or even just as literary reading-mysteries.” [32] There is very little indication of organization, and they are supposed to have eschewed all rituals and sacraments.[33] Lewis S. Keizer detects “the radical distaste Hermeticism as a religio mentis had for all physical sacramentalism.” [34] But now, since the discovery of an important Hermetic document from Nag Hammadi, the much-discussed Hermetic Sixth Tractate, he admits that that conviction “has certainly lost ground.” [35] It is now reluctantly admitted that Hermetic brotherhoods definitely existed, with initiation ceremonies, fraternal meals, and the kiss of peace.[36] Until now it was thought that the whole thing was only a literary movement.

So what can we conclude of these strange people if we don’t know anything about their origins, organization, or their teaching, except that they are scattered far and wide and are hopelessly mixed together? Is there any field of study or area of the ancient world which cannot lay claim to Hermetic teachings?

Theologia Prisca

If the real beginnings of Hermeticism are completely out of sight, who gets credit for the historic beginning? Clement of Alexandria notes that Plato derived the concept of the immortality of the soul from Pythagoras, who in turn got it from the Egyptians.[37]

In the Clementine Recognitions we read that wonderful story about Abraham, who, “wishing to know the causes of things, was thinking intensely on these things.” He had been studying the heavens; this we might note is a typical Hermetic situation, setting the scene for a heavenly dialogue. Presently the true prophet appeared to Abraham and “taught him the knowledge of Godhead; showed him the origin of the world and its end; revealed the immortality of the soul and the manner of life which was pleasing to God; declared also to him the doctrine of the resurrection of the dead, of the future judgments, and the rewards and punishments to come. And then he departed again to the invisible abodes.” [38] That certainly sounds like Abraham 2:12, “Now after the Lord had withdrawn from speaking to me, and withdrawn his face from me, I said in my heart: Thy servant has sought thee earnestly; now I have found thee.” That is, he taught him the gospel—all ideas that might have been taken from the Book of Abraham. It also agrees exactly with the closing section of the Kabbalistic Sefer Yetzirah, where Abraham carves the revelation out on a tablet for his posterity! [39]

Lactantius, one of the early Latin fathers, says that Hermes Trismegistos bears witness to the propriety of certain rites of sacrifice, “along with the prophets.” [40] The great thinkers of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries paid respects both to the Bible and the Hermetic literature.[41] Newton and Boyle both believed firmly in the transmutation of matter. Long ago Jacques-Paul Migne, the editor of both First Clement and the Clementine writings, pointed out “striking parallels” between the Asclepius, a basic Hermetic work, and Philo, the most eminent Jewish theologian, as well as the Jewish Kabbalists.[42] Abraham Epstein found the Sefer Yetzirah to “resemble astonishingly the Clementine books.” [43]

No less a scholar than Philippe Derchain finds the cosmology of the Secrets of Enoch at home in “the cosmogony of Heliopolis,” the oldest cosmogony in Egypt, and suggests that the two came together as part of a “vast syncretistic movement at the end of antiquity, with Alexandria as one of the most active centers.” [44] The Hermetic insistence on going back to the beginning of everything is Kabbalistic: Kabbala means reception, tradition, transmission from the beginning, from father to son. In the same way Hermetic literature begins with Adam. Al-Biruni says that Ja’far, the father of Arabic alchemy, got his knowledge from the Cave of Treasures, where Adam had deposited it.[45]

Did the Hermetics borrow from the Kabbalists or vice versa? “It is difficult to say how this Jewish influence on the Hermetic writings came about.” [46] But Adam is considered their beginning. This is a very important principle of Hermetic wisdom—it does not evolve. The idea that the best are always the best, that “there were brave men before Agamemnon,” has been revived by such scientists and scholars as Giorgio de Santillana and Lord Raglan. Moreover, Hermetic writers felt it necessary to give a line of descent for their knowledge to make it convincing. There are many transmitters in the line; the Arabs, coming late, were particularly exercised to establish their legitimacy. Their most famous sequence is Hermes to Apollonius of Tyana, to Aristotle, to Alexander, to Antiochus, his successor, to al-Mu’tasim.[47] Ruska notes that a large part of Hermetic goes back to ancient Egypt,[48] but many challenge that. Edgar Blochet showed that the Persian Hermetic was very close to the Egyptian.[49] Walter Scott and Theodor Zielinski take the famous Tractate XIII of the Corpus Hermeticum to be a product of Platonic idealism; Heinrich Dörrie sees in it a conversion of Platonic philosophy into a religion of revelation; Richard Reitzenstein found its origin in Iran; Gilles Quispel in Alexandria; and André Marie Jean Festugière also sees connections with Platonism.[50] In syncretistic rites combining Mesopotamian, Iranian, Jewish, Egyptian, and Hellenistic elements, “the dominant influence can no longer be discovered.” [51] You can take your pick. Kroll finds the first cultic activity appearing among the Sabaeans or in Haran, Abraham’s old home town—but what started them?[52] Scott makes the sweeping claim that the authors “were almost all Egyptians by race, though Greek by education.” [53]

Joseph Smith does not avoid the Egyptian element. After all, the facsimiles are part of his scripture, and of course the Book of Mormon started out at least in “reformed Egyptian.” Yet he never plays it up. Others attracted to Hermeticism—from the Isis worshippers to Athanasius Kircher [54] and beyond, including the Freemasons [55]—put heavy emphasis on the allure of Egypt and the theatrical trappings of pseudotemples and rites.

Christian Hermeticism

Scott has supplied a list of thirty-seven fragments from Christian writers that can easily be taken for Hermetic,[56] yet he insists that the “Hermeticists” loathed Christianity as “either a thing too hateful to be spoken of, or too contemptible to be worth mentioning.” [57] On the other hand, Arthur D. Nock shows that most Christians looked favorably on the Hermetic.[58] Others claim that though Hermetic might look Christian, “one cannot speak of a direct Christian influence (on Hermeticism)” — the resemblances come from their common background.[59] Thus “even though there is no indication of a dependence between Christian and Hermetic literature, still it is significant that there is a certain resemblance in the substance and manner of thinking.” [60]

Charles Dodd has suggested that Hermeticism arose from Christianity and asks whether James 1:27 cannot be Hermetic.[61] It is the accepted routine of scholars, when they find resemblances, to argue endlessly about their significance. This is particularly futile in the case of Hermetic writers. Wilson reminds us that “the resemblance in form or content, or the use of a common terminology do not by any means prove necessarily that there is any connection, since the words and ideas can be used in a very different manner and completely different intent. It is not enough simply to show parallels; one must consider what those parallels signify; . . . should not one rather think of a common Gedankenwelt (intellectual environment) from which the writers independently . . . drew for their purposes?” [62] Pointing to some parallels between the Poimandres, the leading Hermetic text, and John 17:13, 20, 30, Dodd concludes that “It seems clear that as a whole [the Hermetic writings] represent a type of religious thought akin to one side of Johannine thought.” [63]

This is the same airy, intuitive method of John L. Brooke when he tells us that in “the climax of the hermetic Radical Reformation, the religious sects of revolutionary England . . . framed continuities that would stretch down to the eighteen-century revival of the hermetic occult, the ground upon which Joseph Smith built the Mormon cosmology.” [64] But where we get into a real hassle is when we bring in that old favorite of the omniscient scholar, Gnosticism. But “Hermetic is no more to be exactly and all inclusively defined than is Gnosis.” [65] Tröger notes that “the relationship between gnosis and mystery religions, often mentioned together in the same breath, has to the present day never been satisfactorily cleared up.” The trouble, he says, is that “there is no generally accepted image of the gnosis, while the fundamentals of the mysteries are disputed.” [66]

So where does that leave us? In the gnostic fog. Scott tells us that Hermes “was the first and greatest teacher of the Gnosis,” [67] and Kurt Rudolph held that the Hermetic writings had their origin “in the first two centuries of the Christian era” and teach us much about the soil on which the Gnostic plants grew.” [68] Nock dates it to the second century b.c. or earlier.[69] “At present there is wide agreement that Hermeticism is evidence for the directions and tendencies that had already begun in the early Christian period and then developed into Gnosticism . . . but, although the relationship is clear enough, the steps of the development are still in the dark.” [70] The final verdict to date is that the relationship of the two “is still far from a conclusive and generally recognized solution.” [71]

But how can it be possible that after all these years of deep study things are so unclear?—mostly from refusing to call a spade a spade. Hermetic knowledge is the same as gnosis—knowledge of everything concerning man’s condition in the universe, knowledge handed down from the beginning in carefully kept secrecy. The question is not who claims it—everybody does—but who has it. Most significant is that everyone seems to overlook the fundamental all-important distinction made in the scriptures and by the earliest writers between the gnosis and the gnosis so-called (1 Timothy 6:20).

Gnosis can be defined as that knowledge imparted secretly by the Lord to the apostles after the resurrection. This was the knowledge the gnostics claimed.[72] Specifically, Eusebius tells us, “to James the Just, and to John, and to Peter after the resurrection the Lord transmitted the gnosis; then he passed it on to the other apostles, and they in turn to the Seventy, of whom Barnabas was one.” [73] This was the true gnosis often mentioned in the New Testament: “How can I help you,” Paul asks the Corinthians, “except I shall speak to you either by revelation, or by knowledge (γνῶσις, gnōsis), or by prophesying, or by doctrine (διδαχή, didachē)?” (1 Corinthians 14:6).

The early writers are even more emphatic in denouncing the “gnosis so-called.” This is Paul’s “science falsely so-called,” as he warns Timothy to avoid arguing with those who claimed to have the gnosis but do not (1 Timothy 6:20). “They took utterly false ways and announced themselves as bearers of what they falsely called the gnosis.” [74] This is how Eusebius begins his history—with trouble in the Church. It is the second century—not the century of faith but “the century of heresy,” of collapse and confusion in the Church.[75]

Hegesippus, the oldest Church historian, quoted by Eusebius, explains quite clearly what happened. “When the holy chorus of the apostles ended their lives in various ways, and that generation passed away of those who had heard the divine wisdom with their own ears, at that very time the conspiracy of godless error took its beginning through deception of false teachers who, when the last remaining apostle had gone, first came out into the open and opposed the teaching of the truth with what was falsely styled the gnosis.” [76] This is exactly what the apostles had predicted would happen (see Acts 20:29; 2 Thessalonians 2:3; 2 Timothy 1:15). The world suddenly exploded with Christian sects, each one claiming to have the true gnosis that Christ left with the apostles. It seems that any quack who could put forth a claim to have that knowledge could gain a following. In that decadent time people clamored for the wonderful things that Christ taught the apostles in secret for forty days after the resurrection; it was only then that “their eyes were opened.” [77] But of those great teachings nothing has come down to us except what was already found in the Gospels. The reticence of the apostles is a part of the story that conventional Christianity has been loath to recognize.[78] So we have two packages, the one which Christ gave to the apostles, and that was lost as had been predicted, and the other package was the new one, borrowed from “Neoplatonic philosophy . . . and the imagery of the Mysteries,” as Eliade puts it, adding that the people “came in the end to borrow from the liturgies and the vocabulary of the Hellenistic Mysteries.” [79] He goes on to report how “certain patterns of initiation, more or less Christianized, continued to survive for many centuries, . . . especially in the form of folk customs, of games, and of literary motifs.” [80] These were Eliade’s specialty: “puberty ceremonies . . . men’s secret societies . . . symbols . . . secret traditions . . . entrance rites . . . peculiar dances . . . (and) artisan’s guilds. . . . Traces of ancient initiatory ceremonies are still discernable in the rites peculiar to masons and blacksmiths.” [81] Everywhere in the Christian world he finds these things, “the masquerades and dramatic ceremonies that accompany Christian winter festivals.” [82] The least that can be said is Adolf von Harnack’s verdict that “in Catholocism gnosticism won half a victory.” [83]

Epiphanius of Salamis in his Panarion lists eighty different heresies (κατὰ αἱρσέων ὀγδοήκοντα, kata haireseōn ogdoēkonta, “against 80 heresies” ). He maintains that they are all false except his own strict Nicaean party line and accuses Origen of being the spiritual “father of Arius and the root of the other heresies,”[84] “blinded by his Greek education.”[85] For him, there is only one perfect dove, one true bride, his church. All the others are merely concubines.[86]

Pot and Kettle

The charge of fakery began early, forcibly expressed by Jude: “There are certain men crept in unawares, . . . ungodly men, turning the grace of God into lasciviousness” (Jude 1:4). They are counterfeiters. Jude reminds the Saints how the Church is slipping: “I will therefore put you in remembrance, though ye once knew this” (Jude 1:5). He uses a striking image: they are “clouds without water,” promising great things and delivering nothing; they are “trees . . . without fruit” (Jude 1:12). This is picked up in the Shepherd of Hermas, one of the earliest post–New Testament writings, deploring the oncoming “Wintertime of the Just,” when it would become impossible to distinguish the live and bearing fruit trees from the deadwood until the Lord comes again and brings the Summertime of the Just.[87]

But charges of fraud do not begin so late; they are native to the Hermetic heritage, going back to the time of Hermes himself. Plato pokes gentle fun at the claims of the Hermeticists when he tells how Thoth, having invented letters, goes in high excitement to his father Ammon to report his wonderful invention that will “make the Egyptians wiser and give them better memories.” Instead of the compliments he expects, he receives a blunt rebuke from King Thamos, who tells him that what he has introduced is really a great mischief “because they will not practice their memory, but trust in the written word; . . . what you have discovered is not the elixir (Φάρμακον, pharmakon) of memory but a mnemonic device,” which will make them appear to know far more than they do.[88] What people ask of a teacher is not his knowledge but his credentials.

When Alexander the Great matched his wit with that of the Brahmins of India (Eliade rates them as alchemists), they complained that it had been sufficiently shown by abundant evidence that the Greeks had stolen all manner of writing.[89] All the ancient mystery religions claimed that their teachings were for all mankind, and each insisted that theirs was the original religion which was taught to the first men by God.[90]

And yet, there is recognition that something is missing. Both Faust and Prospero were magicians in the grand manner, accepted by the public as the lords and masters of recondite learning. Both of them end up laying all their trappings aside after admitting their real weakness—Faust feels so guilty that he is ready to commit suicide, and Prospero declares that it is all “rough magic,” [91] an “insubstantial pageant,” [92] “the baseless fabric of this vision.” [93] As one specialist on ancient alchemy puts it, the Hermetic is “both wise and foolish, confused and clear.” [94] This strange suspended state of mind is the very essence of Hermeticism. Hermes introduces the subject to Thoth by telling him that “we really hear or see nothing in this world”; we have no idea what is going on. It is impossible to gain a clear picture of anything. “We have eyes that do not see and ears that do not hear. . . . There is nothing truly real on this earth, O Thoth, neither can it ever exist here.” [95]

But how could they cover up so effectively and show off to the public at the same time?—by covering their poverty of ideas with a veil of mystic secrecy. This is regular procedure with these people. They hand us a sumptuous menu, but the dinner never shows up. It was the excitement of alchemy that kept things going. Eliade assures us that “through alchemy, certain initiatory rites of archaic structure survived in Europe even down to the dawn of modern time.” [96] In fact it was specifically the ancient Hermetic doctrines that alchemy preserved and transmitted.[97] The lack of originality was perfectly natural, since it was a strict rule for every alchemist to keep his secrets to himself. As a result the various schools supply all the same commonplaces, “a pale, anemic religion.” [98] Frederic J. E. Raby has shown that it was actually possible for all the great and admired thinkers for centuries on end to produce absolutely nothing original.[99]

Those who have been most diligent in surveying the whole field of Hermeticism all agree on its suffused and elusive nature. Thus Eliade says, “The ‘conquest of matter’ began very early, perhaps in the palaeolithic age.” Then he notes how agriculture and pottery followed “during the neolithic age.” Next he comes to the point: “Now these techniques were at the same time mysteries, for, on the one hand, they implied the sacredness of the cosmos and, on the other, were transmitted by initiation.” Next, mining and metallurgy “put primitive man into a universe steeped in sacredness.” [100] He goes on to note that we today live in another world; we might say that the Hermetic is beyond our comprehension.

But it is important to trace it back to the archaic. It gives us a whole different picture of religion and vindicates the Books of Abraham and Moses. The argument being pushed against Joseph Smith today is that those books show derivation from sources no earlier than the sixteenth and seventeenth century a.d. In the later period even in criticizing each other, Hermetic disciples failed to show any sharpness or bring forth specific knowledge. It is precisely because they were so much alike that the rivalry was so bitter, and yet in their sterile debates all they can do, instead of bringing forth real wisdom, is to “impugn each others’ motives” and throw “massive and general accusations” back and forth.[101] Irenaeus in his work Against Heresies promises to expose all.[102] Yet as has often been noted, he ends up differing with them “only in the matter of terminology. Propator is his God by another name; their Pleroma is his Cosmos; what they call the Logos of God, he says, is Jesus Christ, no more nor less—so he falls in with nearly all their arguments, beliefs, and concepts, and the only real argument is about words.” [103]

So why do we bother about Hermeticism at all? Because it is there, taking up a large part of the stage or screen. The best that can be said of it is that it puts more depth and perspective, a touch of mystery, into otherwise even more drab and empty lives. Above all it makes people feel more important, rising above the trivia of daily life. But how? What do they teach to raise their hearers above the terror of death on the one hand and the tranquilizing trivia on the other? What interests us are the teachings all the so-called Hermetics have in common. Such lists are not difficult to find. A most useful catalogue is that prepared by Professor Kroll. It can be matched point for point by Reitzenstein’s description of Hermetic teachings from a much earlier period, taking the mysteries in their worldwide setting as far back as they can be traced. Let us run down his list of doctrines in order.

Naturally the question of God comes first. The Corpus Hermeticum begins with a declaration of Hermes: “I want to learn about things that are (τὰ ὄντα, ta onta), and to understand the real nature of things (τὴν Φύσιν, tēn physin) and to know God.” [104] Poimandres can instruct him because he is endowed “with the authority of intelligence (ὁ τῆς αὐθντἱας νοῦς, ho tēs authentias nous).” [105] This, of course, recalls the Book of Abraham, where existence depends on intelligence, and God is “more intelligent than they all” (Abraham 3:18–19).

The opening statement of the “Sacred Book of Hermes Trismegistos to Asclepius, or the Initiatory Teaching,” includes the first article of faith of the Hermetics: “God himself has led you to me, Asclepius . . . and if you see and understand, your whole mind will be filled to the brim with all good things, . . . and you will recognize that all things are in one and the one embraces all.” [106] This is similar to the inscription on Cleopatra’s disk (see p. 466, fig. 58) and is the very essence of alchemy—to bring the opposites together in one all-embracing whole, as all the other alchemists teach.[107] And this, of course, takes us back to the old Egyptian solution bringing Re and Osiris, light and dark, upper and lower worlds together for an eternal and perfect existence. In another prime Hermetic text, the very first sentence in Stobaeus is the old familiar chestnut of the Schoolmen: “It is hard to know God, and to describe him is impossible.” [108]

These teachings are common to most religions, but there are five important points of early Hermetic teaching which are rejected by conventional Christianity and Judaism but are found in the teachings of the early Church. The doctors of the fourth and fifth centuries, after furious assaults, succeeded in condemning the doctrines of: (1) literalism, (2) cosmism, (3) plurality of worlds, (4) premortal existence of man, and (5) the creation as organization of matter. All of these, as we have shown elsewhere, were accepted teachings in the earliest church and among the Jews.[109] But let us return to our list of Hermetic beliefs.

Next, as Kroll points out, in the Dialogue between Hermes and Thoth, it is taught that there is no destruction but only change.[110] So we start out with the most familiar of all themes, παλιγγνσἱα (palingenesia, rebirth), the hope of redemption, the yearning of all mankind, assuming the foundation of Platonic dualism[111]—this world and the other world, the light and the darkness. More significantly, Hermeticism, rather than teaching creatio ex nihilo, maintained that “matter came not from nothing but from God himself. . . . Divine spirit was thus the original primal matter, the alchemists’ materia prima, from which sprang the entire universe,” [112] a doctrine taught in both the Book of Abraham and Moses 1:38.

The teachings about God and the universe, however, are merely a foundation for the doctrine of redemption[113] —it is still our desperate predicament that comes first. Fundamental to this is man’s dual nature as body and spirit, temporal and eternal. He not only progresses in this life but will return to his Heavenly Father of the premortal existence. To qualify for this he must keep his passions within the boundaries.[114] Such are the elect as compared with the “hylic” (ὑ λικοἱ, hylicoi) or merely carnal people.[115] As Nephi says, “To be carnally-minded is death, and to be spiritually-minded is life eternal” (2 Nephi 9:39). The elect are the perfect ones, the initiates (τέλιοι, teleioi), and they are few and unloved in this world.[116] For the earth at this time is the domain of demons.[117] The elect came to earth along with them, having been chosen spirits in the premortal existence. They occupy various mansions in the hereafter. And the object of all this trouble is salvation, to be saved (σώζιν, sōzein), which depends on the amount of knowledge of the things of God one gains in this earth.

The doctrine of dispensations or cycles, of great destructions and restoration, is shared with religions throughout the world. It is “the whole conception of the Aeon . . . the birthday of the world, natalis mundi . . . Sol Invictus . . . the Alpha and Omega.” [118] This is the best-known aspect of “patternism,” and “all of these [New Year] rites are in some way related, though today the nature of the connection can only be surmised.” [119]

Universal among the Hermeticists themselves is the belief that everything goes back to Adam, that the best comes first, that Egypt is the real source of all, and that the whole body of knowledge was passed from father to son secretly. Also that it all begins with νοῦς (nous)—mind, intelligence. On the other hand there seems to be an awful lot of stage properties and contrivances. These, we might say, are what modern educators would call the “learning tools” of the Hermetics. They include vestments (made as impressive and sometimes as gaudy as possible), passwords, handclasps, crystals, secret names, auric colors, elixirs, drugs, tokens, and of course the Hermetic emerald.[120] None of these except the last are actually very distinctive, because you find them everywhere along with the organizations and devotees listed above. This is the usual meltdown of the Hermetic into the indefinable.

The Great Objective

In all Hermetic doctrine, according to Kroll, there is “a certain unity . . . [which is given] by a hope of redemption and a doctrine of redemption, and compared with this everything else is so insignificant that contradictions are of little consequence.” [121] It overshadows everything. In the Sefer Yetzirah we are told that the seven double letters represent the desires of man’s heart, which are those set forth in the Book of Abraham 1:2: wisdom, wealth, fruitfulness, life, dominion, peace, and beauty.[122] All these amount to the same thing. “The Hermeticist, like Philo and Plotinus, sought a unity with God through ecstasy with the object of achieving certainty of a better lot in the next world.” [123]

The Chinese boldly attempted a frontal attack on death, constantly seeking “cyclical transformation,” not to be reborn but to postpone old age indefinitely by chemical restoration of tissues. “They dared to believe that by their efforts the normal course of events could be arrested and set moving backwards.” [124] They are continually searching for the medicine that will turn white hair black and restore teeth, thinking they have a better chance of saving the bodies than we have of getting them resurrected.[125] It was a forlorn program; the Egyptians were more sensible. They followed the course of nature in having things perish but return with the New Year; the process was not one of annihilation and restoration but change of form—the Khepri principle, of which the sun-god Re was the supreme and highly visible example—he changes his form hourly, but at the same time remains unchanging, constant, unceasing, complete, and so shares his complete existence with the entire universe. So it may be with Pharaoh and with all men.

The Alchemists

Ja’far’s story begins with another Tabula Smaragdina report. You should know that alchemy is called the science of the Emerald Tablet. This account is given by one Dubais ibn-Malik, a Druse living in Antioch in the eleventh century. He tells how a friend of his, a jeweler, was sitting in his shop in Antioch when a stranger entered and showed him an armband of four gems ornamenting a taʿwidh (amulet) of gold, which bore an inscription of green smaragd with the name el-Haqim, who was Ja’bar el-Haqim, the chief disciple of Ja’far. The jeweler paid him 3,000 dinars for it.[126]

The taʿwidh must have been a sizeable one for it contained a writing in the king’s own hand, the king being the emperor at Constantinople. This writing recounted how Ja’far al-Sadiq passed on the secret of the alchemist’s red elixir, the most potent of all chemicals, “by the method of Moses and the Prophets.” [127]

The heading of Ja’far’s letter to his son states that it is about “the Study of the Science [of Alchemy] and the Most Precious [Noble] Stone.” [128] He begins by announcing that this high art: (1) “deals with no one simple thing but with what is combined of masculine and feminine, warm and cold, coarse and fine, wet and dry, etc.” [129] (2) From this combining of opposites the masters conceived of the three foundation pillars and the doctrine of man as spirit, soul, and body. (3) The problem that faced them was how to make a fusion of these three in everlasting unity, forming a single body that would be indestructible and imperishable; a single “perfectly hard substance which neither fire nor water could dissolve.” [130]

The nearest thing known to that was, of course, gold. But that was not the end product. It was something higher, our Re/Osiris factor: “For the learned,” writes Ja’far, “the most difficult and exhausting thing in the world is the uniting of spirit and body and their fusion, so that the spirit would remain eternally in the body and could never suffer the effects of fire.” [131] In al-Haqim’s introduction to the Tablet, he tells us how “the three foundation pillars must be brought together . . . in the manner in which a man is united as spirit, soul, and body. . . . There must be for us something mediating between spirit and body so that they fuse together and never again separate from each other—and that is the revival of the dead at the resurrection.” [132]

So the making of gold was merely a demonstration to prove that the harder problem was solvable. We are often reminded of that by the alchemists—it is the “higher goal, the spiritual goal” which they are after. Such being the problem, all the learned men in the world came together by prior arrangement in a grand convention to discuss the question from every angle. They came to the conclusion that “there must be something” to effect the joining, something by which body and spirit could communicate, blend, and fuse together, which is the raising (reviving) of the dead.[133]

The whole thing seems to be a demonstration to settle once and for all the problem of an afterlife. They also concluded that the solution would have to come by revelation. Incidentally, the Sefer Yetzirah tells us the same things. The alchemist (מגיר, maggîd) can change an object to gold, but the height of his achievement is to create a golem, the beginnings of a human being.[134] For that, body and soul must be joined as an integral whole.[135] The go-between is the Holy Ghost.[136]

And what did Ja’far’s wise men find to be the joining element? They concluded that the thing in the middle must be the soul—“and the spirit and the body are the soul of man” (D&C 88:15). This joining of the three meets us throughout the Hermetic writings. The first prerequisite to effecting the unity was the absolute purity of the elements in question. Impurities in dealing with metals can spoil everything. The alchemist himself must be free of any spot or uncleanness in body or mind, and if he ever makes gold it is for the poor, even as his medical art is meant to heal the sick.

Masculine and feminine would be joined as they are in nature, as well as hot and cold, moist and dry, each union becoming an amalgam, neither male nor female, dry nor wet. And this would be the most precious thing in the world, the purple garment of the king, ruler of the world. It is the joining element that works on all things while nothing affects it; it unites the powers of all things, and fire has no power over it. It is the philosopher’s stone, the elixir of life, the pyrophilus, the king’s crown—one expert in 1652 listed 170 names for it. In 1787 a Hermetic dictionary in Paris listed 600 different names for it. They are after a real substance, all right; the three pillars are real things made of the four elements with which the alchemist works. He begins by calcifying metal, by heating an amalgam with a distillate of vitriol, alum, and salammoniac. The mixing is always done in a vitrious glass vessel, suggesting more smaragd. This “kills” the alloy, which becomes “dead as earth,” irreducible slag or ash, the materia prima of the alchemist that can be no more mixed or melted. Here you have matter and nothing else. To get that you must have taken something out of it. The spirit has been freed by a process frankly described as torture. It must be contained and controlled too, and this is done with mercury, salammoniac, ferrous salts sublimated with the impure distillate of calcium sulphide. If you think you have caught the spirit, then you put it through the same process as the body: reddened (oxidized), tortured, purified, and refined.[137] The third pillar, the one that joins them, is an amalgam, the proportions of which the wise men have kept secret. It is subjected to a bleaching and purifying process until it flows over a heated silver plate without either turning black on the one hand or vaporizing on the other. Then the three pillars are united with red water to produce the smaller elixir. This is fixated and projected by various prescriptions to prepare the greater red water, dissolve vitriol in vinegar, reddened by adding iron oxide, arsenic sulphide, etc. Nine parts of this are mixed with three parts of the bleached, purified soul in the greater gate or experiment for which the water of life (alcohol), alum water, and oil of egg are required. These are the proportions of Ostane and his pupil Democritus, also accepted by the Egyptians.[138]

Of course, there is not just one Hermetic formula. Since it is secret and private one may expect all manner of variations, but according to Ja’far the various schools—Egyptian, Greek, Arabic, Persian, and Indian—all work with the same substances and employ the same procedures, but each has its own secret measurements. Since everything depends on how much of each ingredient is used, and on exactly how long it is to be cooked, cooled, slaked, pounded, etc., the alchemist, not having measuring instruments of great refinement, had to repeat the same experiment over and over again until he might accidentally hit on just the right proportions—something that could go on forever. But it was a good excuse for any alchemist to keep up regardless of a lifetime of repeated failure. They all did it, and Ja’far tells us that to his day no one has succeeded. He admits that his explanations are quite incomprehensible without the divine vision of the Imam.

The vast number of alchemists were fakes, but the real alchemist was “healthy, humble, patient, chaste, . . . intelligent and scholarly; he must work, meditate, pray, etc.” [139] Like Nicolas Flamel of Paris, the greatest alchemist of the fourteenth century, he should be charitable, strictly upright and honest in all dealings, and engage in plain living, using the wealth (purportedly from the gold he made) in accomplishing good works.[140]

There were not many alchemists like Flamel. Most of them were frauds. The mystery, excitement, and vanity of being an alchemist was too much for countless quacks to resist. They made good livings at it, put on a great show and commanded vast respect.

Then there was secrecy that kept the thing going. As long as there was fanatical secrecy, there was hope that somebody, somewhere might really have the answer. A person who boasts a secret has no intention of giving it away, but it heightens his importance. And this sort of thing went on everywhere. Ja’far’s almanac goes back to Adam and matches comparable works attributed to Daniel, Solomon, and all the Indian, Persian, and Greek wise men.[141] Many faked tablets were attributed to Adam. Divination was an important part of the art, of course: books by Pythagoras, Alexander, Christian writers—after all, the apostles drew lots to choose the next apostle (Acts 1:26). Lehi’s use of the Liahona to direct his party was similar to arrow-divination used by the ancient Arab chiefs.[142]

But this all seems to be so indefinite and confused. That is its nature. The great Ibn Khaldun says the whole thing takes place “beween doubt and belief” and that the changing of physical substances is a spiritual, not a practical, operation.[143]

Tablet of Cebes

If one wanted to accuse Joseph Smith of stealing the graphic material in the Book of Abraham from scholars of the Renaissance, as he has recently been charged with stealing the Book of Mormon, etc., from them, one could find no more irrefutable proof than in the strange Tablet of Cebes. This was a manuscript by Cebes, purportedly the only surviving one of the three dialogues attributed to him. He was supposed to be the Cebes of the Platonic dialogues. After studying in Urbino, Joannis Camers, who wrote the standard commentary for the sixteenth century on the Tablet of Cebes, became a professor of theology and philosophy in Vienna in 1499.[144] Aestricampanius, the poet laureate of Germany, also studied in Italy with Filippo Beroaldo, who edited the first Latin version of the Tablet of Cebes.[145] In 1507 this Aestricampanius published what was to remain the standard text of the Cebes for three hundred years.[146] Along with Xenophon and Epictetus, Cebes became the required Greek text for schoolboys all over Europe. By 1550 there had been over 60 editions; one hundred years later there were 120, and by 1750, 200 printings.[147] Then the sophistication of the eighteenth century, especially the criticism of the snobbish Count Caylus, exposed its mediocre art and pedestrian philosophy.[148]

The ancient text does not survive. There is an eleventh-century manuscript in Paris, but no ancient documents. It pretended to be a writing from the time of Plato, but sixteenth-century scholars, true to form, discovered it to be an eclectic work from the first century a.d. It must be at least that old because the cynical Syrian, Lucian, writing in the second century a.d., mentions it. He is describing the dismal state of hangers-on in rich houses: “I would, like the celebrated Cebes, like to paint you a picture of that life, so that you can look at it and see whether it is the kind you want for yourself.” He goes on to say he would prefer it to be done by a competent artist, “but I will have to give you the picture in plain ordinary language.” [149]

But what has this to do with the hypocephalus? The Tablet of Cebes is a special type of document, an ekphrastic text, that is, a description or explanation of a picture or diagram. In this case the picture is not provided but only described, and the reader is supposed to construct it accordingly. There is a rich ekphrastic literature beginning with Homer’s description of the Shield of Achilles in the 18th Book of the Iliad. The Tablet of Cebes was placed on the wall of the temple of Kronos as a votive offering to him by an aged Pythagorean wanderer. Note the significance of the temple. Kronos was the Father of Zeus and all the Olympian Gods. He was the leader of rebellious Titans against his father Ouranos (heaven). He swallowed his own children to prevent their usurping his power. Naturally that and his name has caused him to be frequently identified, whether correctly or not, with Chronos, time.[150]

Cebes tells the story of how he and some others were looking at the tablet on the wall of the temple of Kronos and speculating about it, when an aged Pythagorean wanderer showed up and offered to explain things, telling them how, many years before, another aged Pythagorean wanderer put it there (fig. 59). With his staff he points to the various figures on the tablet, which is a painting, and explains what they signify. The challenge to the ancient student was to provide the correct explanation of the picture, while that of the modern student is to provide the correct picture itself, according to the description.

It should be emphasized that since an ekphrastic drawing or sketch is never simply the picture of something, it is something drawn to specifications and always means more than meets the eye. We cannot emphasize this too much in the case of the facsimiles, which people have tried to interpret as pictures.

Figure 59. A helpful Pythagorean pilgrim instructs the curious Cebes and his friends about a large wooden panel placed on a temple wall. It was painted with three concentric rings containing large masses of struggling people and mysterious captions expressing Pythagorean beliefs. The true purpose of life is to avoid the vice of this world, pass through the level of false learning, and enter into the central and highest goal, true learning.

The only surviving example of an ancient version of the Tablet of Cebes shows the spirits pressing forward to enter the gate labeled life (bios). Printed version of a lost Renaissance drawing of a Roman bas-relief fragment, also lost.

				 The Pythagorean factor suggests mysteries with a touch of alchemism: the Tablet is said to be more valuable than gold because it banishes fear of time and its ravages, as does the alchemical discipline.[151] Moreover, the student is warned “before all else” that it is very dangerous to engage in this study; one runs the awful risk of the initiate in falling short of the test, for whoever undertakes to interpret the image is committed as an initiate and, if he gives the wrong answers, he will be hopelessly lost for the rest of his life.[152] In the drawing dimensions are ignored; this is not a cosmic but a moral discourse.

The problem is to draw the picture as described, and an army of artists and engravers, including even the illustrious Hans Holbein the Younger, eagerly addressed the task. It is not the editions of the text that multiplied by the hundreds—after all, once that is established it requires little alteration, though pedants love the game; no, it is the pictures that captured the imagination of Europe for two hundred years. Reinhart Schleier has provided us with well over one hundred exemplars. He compares the subtle double meanings with Poussin’s famous painting Et in Arcadia ego.[153] One drawing from Venice around 1600 shows man being formed out of Chaos et Materia prima. The artists frequently put labels on their figures. It bears the caption Peregrinationem totius humani generis, that is, The Passage or Pilgrimage through Life of the Entire Human Race.[154] That is, it is a hologram, a theatrum of all the world. The Renaissance artists, especially the engravers, had a passion for those themes seething with figures and presenting vast and complex panoramas and landscapes—they wanted it all.

The Tablet of Cebes itself specified that its most fundamental aspect is a circular enclosure “made up of three concentric circles.” [155] This is like Cleopatra’s alchemical disk but vastly more elaborate (see p. 466, fig. 58), for this one attempts to represent for us the up and down, and the right and left as well, with the whole human race separated into the good and the bad and the cosmos divided into underworld, middle world, and heaven, even as it is in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. Sometimes we see the good people on one side and sometimes on the other, as in the Shield of Achilles.

The universal quadrate and the up and down is the ascension motif. The ultimate reward is the embrace and the crown one receives at the very top from the One on the throne.[156] The most significant division is that into the types of humanity. This is reinforced in the Tablet of Cebes by the “Wye of Pythagoras,” more properly the Greek capital letter upsilon, ϒ (fig. 60). It denotes, indeed depicts, the fork in the road, where everyone must choose the right or the left to determine his fate. This is awkwardly imposed in many of the drawings, for it seems redundant; after all, the ways of ascension and decline, the contrast of the righteous and the wicked, are plain enough. But this is the famous and ubiquitous doctrine of the Two Ways. That doctrine in the Tablet of Cebes is the main subject of the drawing and is repeatedly drawn to our attention. The circular areas rise in three steps, higher and higher, until at the top is the throne awaiting the victor. As in the ascension texts, there is at each level a gate through which the worthy and the unworthy are separated by monitors or directors as they pass on to the next station or degree. This is similar to the judges in the ascension texts—Adam, Abel, or Enoch—who sit at the gates to each of the ascending levels of glory and decide who shall pass to blessedness and who goes elsewhere. And there are the usual questions and answers at the gate. Cebes says the teaching here is derived from the Egyptian Sphinx, the dangerous riddler, with the head of a woman and the body of a lion; it is the Egyptian goddess Sekhmet who eliminates imposters at the gate.

Figure 60. Though heavily damaged, this funerary stele is the earliest surviving depiction of the Pythagorean ή, symbolic of the constant choice between good and evil. The scenes on either side would have shown examples of these choices. Pythagoras himself may be standing at the fork. 2½’ high, first century a.d. funeral stele, white marble. Restoration based on 1910 archaeological photograph and report. Philadelphia, western Turkey.

			 Now what have we learned from all this that might possibly have reference to the hypocephalus? It certainly suggests various ways of approaching the hypocephalus that have been overlooked. We can say without hesitation that the hypocephalus is an ekphrastic document, which tells us nothing without a proper interpreter, and that is exactly how Joseph Smith treats it. Some Egyptologists have declared that the hypocephalus was nothing but an indiscriminate jumble of magical charms. So that is the first major step to the solution—the thing has a consistent message.

The second step reveals the kind of message, “holistic,” that is embracing the whole of life and the whole of the human race. There is a great difference between the two circles. That is the coincidence or coming together of opposites (coincidentia oppositorum), which is the essence of ancient wisdom, including alchemy; the one disk is cosmic, the other is moral or “spiritual.” The Tablet of Cebes avoids the cosmic to concentrate on the moral or spiritual condition of man—that accounts for its immense popularity over two centuries, but its neglect of the cosmic brought about its demise with the scientific advances of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, when it suddenly came to be treated as an obvious and pedestrian, didactic and moralizing school piece.[157] It was finished by 1769.

Of course neither view is complete without the other. That is the whole wisdom of the mysteries, the Hermetic, the Kabbala, and science, which today is seeking to bring all things together in one as never before. But we must not downplay the Tablet of Cebes. It is presented in just as firm, decisive, and realistic a manner as any cosmic design.[158]

The Shield

There is still another approach to the problem—the importance of the shield. A common object of ancient temples was the dedicated shield, left as a votive offering by heroic donors, who were grateful for surviving the dangers and pitfalls of life’s pilgrimage (see 1 Kings 10:16–17; 14:26–27). As dedicated objects, they were sacred. The shield was a clipeus caelestus and represented the plan of the heavens (fig. 61). Hans P. L’Orange has made an extensive study of the subject.[159] From the Achaemenid rulers of Asia to Gaulic chieftains like Asterix, the chief and only the chief was raised up on his shield at the New Year, representing the rising of the sun and his own dwelling among the stars (see pp. 398–99, fig. 49).[160] To quote L’Orange, “The Ancient World conceived the ἀσπἱς [aspis], the clipeus [Latin]—that is, the circular shield with a border around the middle disk—as an image of the revolving All, the cosmos. . . . This shield is compared with the rotating wheel, the ever apt symbol of the Universe; one speaks of the shield of heaven. . . . Ovidius speaks of the imago mundi of the shield of Achilleus.” [161] So here we are again, back to the Shield of Achilles with all its hypocephalic implications. The Roman emperors regularly dedicated their shields in the temples, and “the imagines clipeatae are current all over the empire—always as an expression of apotheosis. . . . The dead in his clypeus caelestis is elevated as a new star high above the elemental gods or demons.” [162] “When the imago of the living emperor is placed on the clypeus, the idea of the imperial cosmocrator, the Sun-emperor, comes to the fore.” [163] He often sits on a throne mounted on wheels to denote his royal progress among the stars.[164] The shield is always round, and the border displays the stars of the Zodiac. But it is as a shield that it is closest to the hypocephalus, because the purpose of the hypocephalus is to provide a flame to protect the owner on his dangerous journey. Its symbols guarantee his safety. So do the other important symbols on the shields. The best-known symbols on shields are the monogram of Christ that Constantine put on the shields of his soldiers after his close call with Licinius, and the Star of David was put on the shields of the Maccabees. We have already noted that the hypocephalus serves the same office as the protective fire, candle, or torch that protects the body during its long repose through the perils of the dark tomb.

Figure 61. Two winged victories fasten a large garland on the shield representing the world. The rim shows the encircling waters of the cosmic ocean like the Shield of Achilles. Two cherubs hold up a panel with a faded inscription proclaiming a victory. The eagle of Jupiter carries his thunderbolts and supports the victory palm from which two crowns and a fillet hang. Ca. 100 b.c. Heads and arms restored. Musei Capitolini, Rome.

	 The Book of Breathings was also quickly identified as a sort of guidebook, map, or passport to see one safely through a journey. The handbook to the hypocephalus, Book of the Dead 162, says that it is Hathor’s “protection for her son Re at his setting” [165]—that is, when he goes down into the dark underworld. So is the hypocephalus a shield. It is made just like the ancient shields, notably the Shield of Achilles, and is carried with the dead into the underworld, pressed against his head to provide the protection of a helmet or crown. Specifically it envelops his head in a protective flame, not unlike the fiery dragons on the earliest shields. Alexander’s shield, like the Golden Shield of Hasdrubal, bore his own image and so rendered him invulnerable.[166]
