12

The Kabbala

The Kabbala Speaks

We have been hinting all along that the hypocephalus may belong to a special type or class of documents: palettes, mirrors, drums, crowns, emerald and jade disks displaying heavenly bodies, revelatory plaques, which in both form and function show some resemblances to the hypocephalus. Is there not something even more specific? There is indeed—a group of documents to which the hypocephalus must surely belong. The oldest and greatest of them all is the Sefer Yetzirah, the Book of Creation or Formation, proclaimed in a recent study to be “without question the oldest and most mysterious of all Kabbalistic texts.” [1] Although it is a short book of only about 1,600 words, even in its larger version, “for more than 1,000 years the book was expounded in a great many different ways.” [2]

According to Kaufman Kohler and Louis Ginzberg, “this work had a greater influence on the development of the Jewish mind than almost any other book after the completion of the Talmud.” [3] “With the exception of the Bible, scarcely any other book has been the subject of so much annotation.” [4] Indeed, more than eighty commentaries have been published on this one book.[5] Written in “an obscure and at the same time laconic and enigmatic style,” [6] it is, not surprisingly, “exceedingly difficult to understand on account of its obscure half-mystical style.” [7] After centuries of research, its origin remains a mystery: “there is a wide divergence of opinion regarding the age, origin, contents, and value of the book, since it is variously regarded as pre-Christian, Essene, Mishnaic, Talmudic, or geonic.” [8] “The date and origin of the book cannot be definitely determined so long as there is no critical text of it.” [9] And there is no critical text because there are hundreds of manuscripts to be examined; indeed, “the Sefer Yetzirah never developed beyond the state of being a ‘hidden scroll’ ” —that is, a manuscript reconstructed from the notes of students through the centuries.[10] In short, “despite all efforts of a large number of scholars of repute for more than a thousand years,” according to Phineas Mordell, “the Sepher Yetṣirah remains a sealed book; . . . there is no book in Jewish literature so difficult to understand.” [11] The obscurity is intentional: “The Kabbalists restricted their teachings to very small secret societies.” [12] As a small book with a limited and familiar vocabulary, the Sefer Yetzirah offers a field day to scholars wishing to exercise almost unlimited personal judgment—the number of translations is astonishing. While Lewis N. Dembitz declares this “Book of Formation” to be “the oldest text-book of the Cabala,” he dismisses it out of hand: “No important secrets can be obtained from the little volume.” [13]

This is a strange situation. Nobody understands it; nobody will let go of it. How does one explain its immense appeal? How has it deserved such passionate research through the centuries? The answer is Abraham. The most venerated of the great Hebrew scholars, “the oldest gaonim . . . and such philosophers as Saadia, Donolo, and Judah ha-Levi, . . . never doubted that Abraham was the author of the book.” [14] “Very few Biblical passages are quoted, and with the exception of Abraham, no name or authority is mentioned.” [15] “It was Abraham our father . . . who wrote this book against the wise men of his time; . . . at least that is what R. Saadia . . . claims in the first chapter of his book ‘The Philosopher’s Stone.’ ” [16] According to others “The Essenes trace much of their teaching to Abraham and attribute to him the composition of metaphysical writings, including the Sefer Yetzirah.” [17] Some would attribute it to Rabbi Akiba, but Isaac de Latte asks in his introduction to the Zohar, “Who permitted Rabbi Akiba to write the book which has been orally transmitted since Abraham’s time?” [18]

But is it orthodox? Is it the gospel? The answer to that can be found in the person of Ben Zakkai, who got from the Emperor Vespasian—even while the temple was burning—permission to depart from the city with his disciples and establish a school at Jamnia. It was the first rabbinical school—a complete break with the temple. One of the remarkable things about the Sefer Yetzirah is the almost total absence of concern for the temple. The “elders of Pumpadita were versed in Maaseh Bereshit [mysteries of creation—a reference to the Sefer Yetzirah].” [19] One of Ben Zakkai’s main disciples was Rabbi Yehoshua, “the greatest expert of his time in the occult.” [20]

The great importance of all this is that it frees us from any doctrinal commitment to the teaching of these Hebrew mystics. Like the writings of Egyptians and like other apocrypha, they have preserved for us “many things . . . that are true” and also “many things contained therein that are not true, which are interpolations by the hands of men. . . . Therefore, whoso readeth it, let him understand, for the Spirit manifesteth truth; and whoso is enlightened by the Spirit shall obtain benefit therefrom” (D&C 91:1–2, 4–5). The thing to note is the abrupt and enthusiastic turning of the doctors from the temple to their own mysteries. They joyfully filled the vacuum left by the destruction of the temple.[21]

They justified their position by putting it all onto Abraham. One account is that “God then handed it [the Sefer Yetzirah] over to Abraham, who sat by himself studying it without being able to understand anything, until a heavenly voice came forth and said: ‘Dost thou seek to compare thyself with Me? I am “One,” I created the Sepher Yetṣirah, and studied it.’ ” For God used the Sefer Yetzirah as a blueprint to help him in his work of creation. But the voice continued to Abraham: “ ‘But thou canst not understand it alone. Get thee an associate, and look into it together, you will then understand.’ At once Abraham went to his teacher Shem, and stayed with him three years. They looked into it and they knew how to form the world. From that time to this, there is no man who can understand it alone, there must be two wise men, and they cannot understand it before three years.” [22]

How anyone at all can understand it remains a question. According to a kabbalistic work, “Jeremiah began to study Sepher Yetṣirah, when a heavenly voice came forth and said: ‘Get thee an associate.’ He accordingly went to his son Sira, and they studied the Sepher Yetṣirah together.” The two of them together were able to perform marvelous things by means of it.[23] The strongest witness of all to Abraham’s authorship is the Sefer Yetzirah itself, which concludes with the ringing words:

And when Abraham our father, may he rest in peace, looked, saw, understood, probed, engraved and carved, he was successful in creation, as it is written, “And the souls that they made in Haran” (Genesis 12:5). Immediately there was revealed to him the Master of all, may His name be blessed forever, He placed him in His bosom, and kissed him on his head, and He called him, “Abraham my beloved” (Isaiah 41:8). He made a covenant with him and with his children after him forever, as it is written, “And he believed in God, and He considered it to him for righteousness” (Genesis 15:6). He made with him a covenant between the ten fingers of his hands—this is the covenant of the tongue, and between the ten toes of his feet—this is the covenant of circumcision.[24]

Getting a Handle

The materials we are dealing with are so voluminous and the parallels are so numerous and impressive that all our efforts might appear a monstrous overkill. We must be more brief, and to do that let us summarize the main points in common between the Sefer Yetzirah and Facsimile 2 as explained by Joseph Smith.

Provenance

Both books were (1) revealed to Abraham (2) while he was studying the heavens on his own. (3) Jehovah came down and gave him personal instruction. (4) Abraham was sacrificing on the occasion (on a mountain?), and the Lord made a covenant with him. (5) He then explained the heavens to him. This story is told in the last section of almost all major texts of the Sefer Yetzirah.

There was a Talmudic teaching that Abraham handed it on for the benefit of his descendants born to his concubines.[25] Such were worthy to receive a secondary teaching, the “earnest imitation” (Abraham 1:26) of the Egyptians as we have received it in Facsimile 2, which we are explicitly told by Abraham is not his own or the Hebrew version, but that of the Egyptians (Abraham 1:9–14; Fac. 2, figs. 1, 3, 4, 5, explanation). Not surprisingly, the Sefer Yetzirah has been dated to the time of Abraham, specifically of Abraham in Egypt: “The attribution of the mysteries of Sefer Yetzirah to Abraham would place its origin in the 18th century [b.c.]. . . . Since Abraham was the greatest mystic and astrologer of his age, it is natural to assume that he was familiar with all the mysteries of ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia. Abraham was born in Mesopotamia, and he also lived in Egypt.” [26]

Other Documents

The Sefer Yetzirah is the oldest of a line of secret documents resembling it, another being the Sefer Raziel, or Book of Secrets, which was used in the Middle Ages; it trained students for studying the Sefer Yetzirah.[27] Raziel, the great angel of secrets, gave it to Adam in the beginning. When Noah entered the ark, Raziel gave him the book, “written on a sapphire, by which he scanned the heavens at all levels, determining the orbits of the planets and the course of the sun, moon, and stars, as well as the division of all things into two opposing regions—life and death, good and bad, day and night.” [28] It would be a dense reader indeed who could not recognize at least a cousin of the hypocephalus in this. Noah then gave the stone to Shem at his death, and Shem in turn gave it to Abraham.[29]

The Sefer Raziel is so close to a work called the Berayta di Reshit that the two are published in parallel columns.[30] Both books offer diagrams, mostly circular, of the universe.[31] The Berayta tells of the great circle of jasper or smaragd that divides the foundation of earth from heaven. Above the firmament is “a sapphire stone placed above the totality of the luminous appearance of heaven; . . . above that is the throne of glory, standing on a sapphire stone.” [32] Berayah means creation, and the seven chambers of the universe are founded on “Brickwork of Sapphires,” that stone being the “Essence of Heaven.” [33]

The publishing of such books from the beginning was accompanied by a profusion of cosmic charts usually displaying concentric circles with appropriate signs and labels. When we consider the strange stone documents and their purpose and then read: “And I, Abraham, had the Urim and Thummim, which the Lord my God had given unto me, . . . And I saw the stars, that they were very great” (Abraham 3:1–2), recalling that Joseph Smith described the Urim and Thummim as transparent stones, our curiosity, if not our faith, should be somewhat stirred. One wonders whether our Facsimile 2, as interpreted by the prophet, is not indeed a legitimate member of a class of documents which the rabbis have for ages striven to interpret, such as the Zohar, the Sefer Raziel, Berayta di Reshit, the Bahar, and most notably the Clementine Recognitions. Aryeh Kaplan presents a long list of other books containing parts of the Sefer Yetzirah.[34] Abraham Epstein notes “an astounding resemblance of the Clementine writings to the Sefer Yetzirah,”[35] we have already observed it as reporting Clement’s intended journey to Egypt as his only hope of assurance in a hereafter.

We might consider the illusive Facsimile 2 as part of a whole library of sacred records, all having the same purpose. Inevitably, more distant relatives of such documents appear, and we have the varied but related forms, from the prehistoric palettes to the late silver plates, maps, and zodiacal charts. The Kabbala and the Hermetic literature that supposedly began with Thoth or Hermes in prehistoric Egypt share the age-old tradition of the book handed down from the beginning, which is such an essential teaching of our Latter-day Saint scriptures.

The important relevance of the Hebrew records to our Facsimile 2 is the Egyptian background of both. Kohler and Ginzberg saw the clear Egyptian background of the Sefer Yetzirah.[36] Pseudo-Eupolemus reports that “Abraham lived with the Egyptian priests in Heliopolis, teaching them many things. And he introduced astrology and other sciences to them, saying that the Babylonians and he himself had discovered them, but he traced the discovery to Enoch.” [37] Artapanus reports that “Abraham went together with his whole family to Pharethon, the King of the Egyptians, and taught him astrology.” [38]

Purpose of the Sefer Yetzirah and Facsimile 2

The Sefer Yetzirah is a textbook in fundamental science: The Sefer Yetzirah “set itself to the task of teaching the science of cosmogony.” [39] Like Clement, Abraham turned from the prevailing schools of mysticism and astrology for his answers: “Philo and Josephus depict Abraham as a convert from this philosophy (astrology). Philo describes him as the first man to have discarded astral determinism and, from a consideration of the harmony of the universe, to have reached belief in the existence of a transcendent Organiser, a God-Creator. Discovering this, he ceased to be an astrologer.” [40] Kaplan considers the Jewish doctors privileged to indulge in astrology, but the reader is advised to accept the well-known transition from astronomy to astrology. The Sefer Yetzirah itself, in the last of the 32 paths of wisdom, declares that it is “prepared so as to destroy all who engage in the worship of the seven planets.” [41] In short, the Sefer Yetzirah “knows nothing of the fantastic inventions of Gnosticism and Mysticism.” [42] It even “denies the popular belief that the world was evolved from nothing.” [43]

The Sefer Yetzirah is “a compact discourse on cosmology and cosmogony.” [44] Again the Sefer Yetzirah “is referring to God’s creation,” and “the grammatical form more closely resembles the imperative” giving strict instructions to the reader.[45] It starts out by telling us that God used such a manual to create the earth. The famous Rabbi Rava (a.d. 299–353) declared that “if the righteous desired, they could create a world.” [46] Following the Sefer Yetzirah, the student learns how everything was created by Jehovah and is enabled thereby to become a creator himself, even “a creator of worlds!”

The usual objective, however, was the creation of a golem which was “considered to be the most advanced—and dangerous—of all meditative techniques.” [47] A golem (lit. embryo) was an android which could be given physical form after its mental imaging: “The creation of a mental Golem is therefore a culmination of the arts of Sefer Yetzirah, as well as a test to determine if one has mastered them.” [48]

More to the point than such demonstrations is that the Sefer Yetzirah purports to solve the terrible question by the power of intellect alone. Though given by revelation, both the Sefer Yetzirah and Facsimile 2 call upon the reader to exercise his own mental powers. He is to follow the 32 paths of wisdom, requiring an “amount of mental discipline . . . virtually beyond description.” Understandably the “creation of a physical Golem . . . was only done on very special occasions.” [49] “If the world can find out these numbers, so let it be. Amen” (Fac. 2, fig. 11, explanation).

One of the most striking parallels between the Sefer Yetzirah and the Book of Abraham is the concept of intelligence as the ultimate and unsearchable beginning of all. The first of the 32 paths is the Sekhel Mufla, or imponderable intelligence, “mystical consciousness.” Mufla “is the Light that was originally conceived, and it is the First Glory. No creature can attain its excellence.” [50] For the Latter-day Saints too, “the glory of God is intelligence” (D&C 93:36). “Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created, . . . neither indeed can be” (D&C 93:29). Or “And as one shall be more intelligent than another, . . . I am the Lord thy God, I am more intelligent than they all.” This is from the Book of Abraham 3:18–19 where the subject is creation; the power to create is the measure of intelligence.

The ancient tradition of the Sefer Yetzirah has left with the Jews that celebrated passionate concern for things of the mind, to which the Latter-day Saints remain indifferent if not hostile. But this is one of the things that firmly links the Book of Abraham to the Abraham of the Sefer Yetzirah. To the mere intellectual both books are all total nonsense or total mystery. Only the righteous (צךיקים, ṣaddîqîm) who are truly wise (	חכמים, ḥakamîm) need apply; mere smartness (בינה, bînāh) is not enough. The difference between the two is constantly stressed in the Sefer Yetzirah. The incomprehensible manner in which intelligence operates is בלימה (bĕlîmāh), the equivalent of the bila-kaif of the Arab wise men: there are things about which it is useless to ask how it is done.

The Rule of Three

The real issue behind the mysteries is never far out of sight; it is the thirst for immortality. As with the Egyptians, the problem is always the same, the crossing of the two great gaps—the one that separates the body from the spirit and the other that separates heaven and earth. To negotiate the first is to overcome death. “The soul is integrated by its association with a single body.” [51] Before the individual could attempt the more advanced exercises, he would bind his soul by an oath to return to his body—he had little desire for nirvana,[52] and transmigration was not an acceptable solution. Next, to make the joining of heaven and earth it is essential to make the first union permanent, for we cannot live forever on this afflicted globe, and heaven is far away. All of this is apparent in the Egyptian funerary literature.

So how do we span the gulf? The space must be filled or replaced by a third element that (1) brings the two opposites together and (2) bonds them permanently. The supplying of this bond, incidentally, is the sum and substance of alchemy.[53] Where can such an entity be found? It is a singularity emerging from the coincidentia oppositorum, something resembling the miracle of creation by which one forms “substance out of chaos and made nonexistence into existence.” [54] It must be something that seems both real and unreal at the same time: “carve great pillars from air that cannot be grasped.” [55] It is as mysterious as the interface between past and future, which is actually nonexistent and yet is everything we have! The idea is nowhere better represented than in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, Facsimile 2, figure 2. We are asked to imagine the twelve boundaries of space, each extending to infinity as “transmission lines, through which creative energy flows into the universe from twelve diagonal paths in the tree of life. . . . These infinities are the interface between the physical and the transcendental.” [56]

“Transmission lines,” “paths,” “interface” —Dr. Kaplan gives us a long list of that thing “in the middle” which brings mind and matter together. But is it real? Can one actually carry out such an operation? Like the Egyptian crossing of the great gulf, the Kabbala teaches that the attempt is exceedingly difficult and fraught with terrible risk and danger. Still, it is the only answer, and to keep the whole thing real, the operation deals only with elements whose reality is beyond question. The proof of the solution, according to the Sefer Yetzirah, is the undeniable reality of all component parts and structures: (1) The universe is real, (2) the mind or consciousness of the individual is real, and (3) the thing that connects them must also be real—it is the year with its undoubted recurrence and revival of life.[57] Is this too far-fetched? “When a person is in a normal state of consciousness, he may be able to form mental images, but they are weak, transient, and blurred by mental static”; however, those images “formed in a meditative state appear solid, substantial, and real.” [58] There are two principles that put the achievement within range of the adept’s powers; they are polarity and hierarchy.

Polarity

Wisdom (חכמה, ḥokmāh) and understanding (בינה , bînāh) are opposite poles; what brings them together is the נשמה (nĕšāmāh), the individual soul which stands between them (fig. 62). [59] Wisdom is nonverbal thought understanding is its verbalization. They are represented by male and female respectively. “The male represents unchanneled creative force. This can only be brought into fruition when delineated, enclosed, and channeled by the female womb.” [60] This concept is clearly set forth in figures 7, 5, and 6 of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. The repeated formula is to “ ‘Understand with Wisdom, and to be wise with Understanding.’ This involves a deliberate oscillation between Understanding and Wisdom, between verbal Binah consciousness, and nonverbal Chakhmah consciousness,” [61] which can only be brought to rest when “the place in the middle becomes the focus of spiritual tension, . . . the ‘unifying covenant.’ ” [62] The formlessness and void of Genesis, Tohu and Bohu, are respectively “pure substance” and “pure information not related to any substance.” [63] The two must be brought together. The תלי (tĕlî, “quiver,” literally “hanger” ) is “the link between the spiritual and the physical.” [64] In that capacity God is always acting as Jehovah, and the constant go-between is the רוח הקוךש (rûaḥ haqqôdeš), which Jewish scholars are understandably reluctant to translate literally, though Kaplan occasionally calls it by its name, the Holy Ghost.

Figure 62. After a long journey, the weary seeker of truth enters the “Gates of Light” and lays hold on his goal, the tree of the sephirot. “This is the gate, Divine Name, where the just will enter through them” (in Latin and Hebrew). 1516, Paulus Ricius, Portae Lucis.

				 To join the two opposites we must have an element that partakes of and bonds with both sides, like glue uniting wood and leather. The standard illustration of this in the Sefer Yetzirah is the three divisions of the circle: fire above, water below, and air in the middle, the “air that cannot be grasped,” which seems unsubstantial and yet is as real as it appears when it “enters the realm of the pillars,” the Seven Pillars of Wisdom between heaven and earth. They appear solid, substantial, and real to one “in a meditative state,” but to others they have no substance.[65] Kaplan, who was a physicist, compared the air to a weak nuclear force which “decides between the other two,” thus bringing new particles such as electrons into existence.[66] The “ ‘fire’ is Binah consciousness, where the mind itself is constantly radiating energy”; the water is “Chakhmah consciousness, where the mind can absorb spiritual energy from without”; and the air is “Ruach HaKodesh, the medium through which such spiritual energy is transmitted.” [67] For us it is the Holy Ghost which is the advocate, representative, and messenger between God and man. Even more striking is the analogy of gas as “intermediate between the containment of solid matter and total expansion of pure radiation.” [68] And yet this paradoxical theme is a physical reality. Professor Philippe Derchain recognizes the vital connecting principle of air among the Egyptians, as if the “stability of the sky was one of the visible signs of continuation of the universe,” which depended on “two aspects of the primordial principle . . . an aerial deity, whose action is manifested on the one hand in the form of the vital breath penetrating the nose like the north wind, and on the other, as a mass of air which supports the sky above the earth ‘when he extends his arms under the sky.’ ” [69]

One section of the Sefer Yetzirah is devoted to describing the polarization as the three Mothers, which “represent cause, effect, and their synthesis.” They are the foundation, and from them are born the three Fathers,” from which “space is defined; . . . then creation can take place.” [70] They represent the Hegelian trio—cause, effect, and synthesis—which is a new thing and just as real as the others. We shall see that this principle is vividly conveyed in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus. “It is Truth that binds all opposites together.” [71] The maat-feather of the Egyptians is breath and wind shared by the universe above as well as that below; [72] as in the Sefer Yetzirah, the central element is air. In the hypocephalus Amun is the one between the fiery solar realms above and the creative chthonian realms below.

The bringing together is the key of everything. The words Kaplan uses are transition, in between, binding, pipes, channels, contact, confluence, focus, unifying covenant (Latin co-venant, a bringing together), intermediate, bonding, mediation, center, fulcrum, synthesis, deciding between, transmission, intermediary, meeting, integration, interface, trough or channel, middle point, deciding one, etc. The two main ideas here are bringing together and bonding together.

Hierarchy

Hierarchy is the other dominant principle. It is gradualness. Might the intimidating gulf be crossed by easy stages? Hierarchy passes through a series of steps from one degree to another, making the transition ever more gradual, and therefore believable, as we add more and more steps. The Christian gnostics have borrowed this idea in their thirty aeons; by easy stages they bridged the gap between a God who shuns all material being as utter defilement and the very physical world which he is supposed to have created.

The many charts which the Kabbalists have left us show their love of drawing circles within circles, “nearness to the residence of God” being the measure of holiness (Fac. 2, fig. 2). Section eight of the Sefer Yetzirah lists the ten sephirot in order of their importance and appearance (fig. 63). The individual approaches his solution through 32 paths of consciousness, leading corruptible mortality ever nearer to the sublime. This principle of a gradual progression toward ever greater penetration is another conspicuous aspect of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus and his interpretation of it, where hierarchy plays a major role.

Figure 63. When God created the universe, his power was manifested in a descending series of spheres, the ten sephirot, each one less powerful, until the center where man could be created and the choice between good and evil was possible.

The Sephirot

The leap of the spark is achieved by the sephirah and requires immense effort on the part of the devotee: breathing exercises, head motions, yoga, and especially the pronouncing of the letters—the sephirot—with complete absorption and elaborate care, breathing out and facing east.[73] By manipulating the letters correctly, the Kabbalist is controlling “the most elementary forces of creation,” [74] with God’s approval, since “the Sephirot are the means with which God communicates with his creation.” [75] Sefer is a book, sifrah is a number, sippur is a story that brings them together; this brings us letters, numbers, and the story they produce. There are ten special sephirot which are basically imponderable (beli-mah), which when rendered “without reality,” as Kohler and Ginzberg do, are only abstractions that express relationships.[76] It is the twenty-two sephirot of the Hebrew alphabet which produce the material world. How? The basic idea is that letters form words which form sentences; it was by the Word that God created everything. What the sephirot convey is knowledge; “it has the connotation of joining and intercourse,” bridging the gap.[77]

Yet in themselves the sephirot have no physical qualities, they are only “the point of confluence between Wisdom and Understanding.” [78] They are both physical[79] and mental,[80] and yet they can be used to create a pumpkin, a golem, or the universe.[81] How is it done? You begin with the visible sign. Abraham tells us in the Midrash that God “wrote with his finger as with a pen of flame upon the crown of my head, the signs by which he created the heavens and earth, the seas and the rivers and the heights and the depths, and the signs which he created of the stars (mezilloth, constellations), etc. And of it he created an endless food supply, and all the secrets of the beginning, and all the glory of the sun, moon, and stars.” [82]

This combines Sefer Yetzirah, Tabula Smaragdina, and hypocephalus. Of peculiar interest is the guarantee of food supply represented in figure 6 of Facsimile 2 as the biological food chain which assures perpetual nourishment for life on earth (see p. 303, fig. 33).

But how real is all this? The main problem still mystifies. For the mathematicians, the spiritual heirs of the Kabbala, “objective reality must be replaced by the observer-created reality.” [83] “The existence of an external world with fixed properties,” writes the mathematician Morris Kline, “is really an unwarranted inference. There is no evidence that anything exists beyond impressions and ideas that belong to nothing and represent nothing.” [84] The quote is from Hume, but this is pure beli-mah. “Our mathematical theory of the physical world is not a description of phenomena as we perceive them but a bold symbolic construction.” [85] “We must face the fact that there is no universally accepted correspondence between mathematics and physical reality.” [86]

But the scientists cannot leave it there any more than the Kabbalists can. The great founders of modern mathematics believed that “there is inherent in nature a hidden harmony that reflects itself in our minds in the form of simple mathematical laws.” [87] So let us have the connection. Alas, Albert Einstein regretted that a human being “will never be able to compare his picture (of the universe) with the real mechanism and he cannot even imagine the possibility of the meaning of such a comparison.” [88] Galileo came nearest when, talking like a Kabbalist, he declares that the greatest and most inspired of all inventions was the alphabet, and that “mathematical principles are the alphabet in which God wrote the world.” [89] So the Sefer Yetzirah teaches that without words and letters “the universe would cease to exist.” [90] “Every existing thing somehow contains these linguistic elements and exists by their power.” [91] Can’t creation be done in silence? Who started this language business? It is the mystery of the Word. The Sefer Yetzirah has much to say about the seven gates, the sensory passages through which all impressions enter the mind to be digested and formulated. The only way these can be shared between individuals is through a single projector: the mouth. This doctrine meets us in the Memphite theology, which explains that “every word of God came into being through what the heart devised and the tongue commanded.” [92] The doctrine receives strong emphasis in the Pearl of Great Price; “for my works are without end, and also my words, . . . and there is no end to my works, neither to my words” (Moses 1:4, 38); the purpose of the works is to share his glory: “For behold, this is my work and my glory” —that is, to share his work and his glory with man (Moses 1:39). “The Sephirot,” we are told, “are the means through which God communicates with his creation. They are also the means by which man communicates with God.” [93] Indeed, God can only be approached through the sephirot.[94] For the rabbis, direct revelation is out; it is all borderline, the thing between. Sephirot are like dust devils, gone in an instant, vanishing before we barely perceive them. Are they real? [95]

Form and Design, the Physical Object

We do not have a copy of the Sefer Yetzirah, only a great many descriptions of it. Some of its teachings are explained by the Kabbalists in a variety of graphic designs, notably the cosmic tree, the human body as a microcosm, and various geometrical arrangements—for example, of the twenty-two sephirot.

First of all, it is quite apparent that the Sefer Yetzirah was engraved on a stone. The adept is instructed to “engrave them, carve them, permute them, and with them seal the three mothers in the universe . . . the year, and the soul, male and female.” [96] “Engrave and carve the throne of glory, the sepharim,” etc.[97] The five vowels are to be engraved on a signet ring.[98] Engraving, carving, and sealing with a signet all imply precious stones.

We must emphasize the obvious because inevitably the doctors have turned the carving and the stones into metaphors. “The initiate must first depict the letters ‘engraving’ them in his mind. Then he must ‘carve’ them out, making them fill his entire consciousness. After this, he can permute them in various ways. He can also manipulate them through numerical values and the standard ciphers.” [99]

Note: It is always made clear that Abraham himself carved the Sefer Yetzirah. So too Abraham also handed on an Egyptian document, the Book of Abraham, written “with his own hand upon papyrus.” What “written with his own hand” or “carved himself” specifies both in the Sefer Yetzirah and the Book of Abraham is that it was written by Abraham and no other—he was the author. It also applies only to the text of the Book of Abraham and not to the pictures, which were used by Abraham as an aid to the reader unfamiliar with Egyptian things and could have been copied many times and still be official.

We are dealing with stones as we contemplate the letters of the alphabet: “The high priest, looking down on his breastplate, then looked to see out of the letters engraved on the stones shone out most brightly, and then constructed the answer out of these letters.” [100] In this case it is the Urim and Thummim. All along it is not just a stone but a sapphire. The companion piece of the Sefer Yetzirah is the Sefer Raziel, which is, as we have seen, written on a sapphire, and related to the Berayta which tells of the great round sapphire under God’s throne. The holiest designation of the Sefer Yetzirah is the כתר (keter), the crown, from the root כתר (kātar), meaning to surround. “It is through the attribute of Keter or Kedem (eternity) that God encompasses all space and time.” [101] This ties it to the Tabula Smaragdina as the omega, the all-embracing circle of the cosmos and the waters of Oceanus which surround the world.[102]

The all-important גלגל (galgāl), the great circle (cf. the stone circle at Gilgal in Joshua 4:20) of the Sefer Yetzirah, is formed by sephirot: “engrave like a garden, carve them like a wall, . . . and stand each one up making a stone circle of the letter surrounding him like a wall.” [103] The Holy Spirit, the first breath, is from the כתר (keter), the direct light; and the second breath is associated with the מלכות (mālākûṭ) and is called reflected light. The analogy is that of a glassblower’s breath forming a glass vessel.[104] The proto-sephirot could not fuse into something higher. Therefore they were overwhelmed by light, called “the breaking of the vessels.” This certainly calls up the very ancient business of breaking the green glass or the gems found in the Book of the Dead 125.[105]

The Chemical Connection

The magid (preacher) says that the Sefer Yetzirah will enable one to create gold—in fact, to convert a given substance “to any matter he desires.” [106] Who is it but the alchemist who engraves, carves, permutes, weighs, and transforms the elements.[107] If the alchemist covets the Tabula Smaragdina above all else, here we find him with the Sefer Yetzirah, and what do we see in the hypocephalus but the universe (Fac. 2, figs. 1, 2, 4), the year (figs. 1, 4, 5), and the soul (fig. 7)?

In order to create, the Kabbalist, along with circle drawing, also uses prayer with a system of meditative kavvanot. “The actual text of the prayer, therefore, serves as a kind of banister onto which the Kabbalist holds as he makes his not unhazardous ascent, groping his way by the words. The kavvanot, in other words, transform the words of the prayer into holy names that serve as landmarks on the upward climb.” [108] This deserves notice because the words of the prayer as a banister strikingly parallel the rod of iron to which one clings while making his way to a full knowledge of the gospel: “clinging to the rod of iron, even until they did . . . partake of the fruit of the tree” (1 Nephi 8:24). Can we say “the sephirotic tree” (see p. 517, fig. 62)? It is quite a coincidence that “the rod of iron, which my father had seen, was the word of God” (1 Nephi 11:25).

We should not neglect the greatest alchemist of the fourteenth century, Nicolas Flamel, a good and wise man; he became an alchemist when he saw in a vision an angel holding a strange book, with the promise that Flamel would later be able to read it. The vision departed in a blaze of light. A few days later Flamel saw the very book for sale in Paris. It was bound with copper sheets and the pages were of very thin bark, probably papyrus, which would not have been recognized in Paris in 1357. This was the Book of Abraham the Jew, and Flamel was indeed able to read it since it was a Latin translation. There were drawings on every seventh page, which Flamel described; but viewed as pictures they did not fit with the text at all, and Flamel never divulged what was written in the book.[109]

Why should the sapphire be the link that binds heaven and earth? What is hard as rock but transparent as air? In the Berayta the sapphire is called “the essence of heaven.” [110] The ruach (רוח) is rendered by translators of the Sefer Yetzirah as air, wind, and spirit and is called in the Sefer Yetzirah the breath of the glassblower.[111] It is מלכיט (mālākîṭ), the jawharah khadrah (green jewel), its surface displaying both the heavens and the earth, a great green rock with the thickness of seven heavens and seven earths, concerning which the sage Luqmān told his son, “O my son! Be they as small as one grain of mustard-seed, be they hidden in a rock, or in heaven, or the earth, God will bring all things to light.” And when he had said these words, “his gall bladder burst because of his fear; and he died.” [112]

The Sefer Yetzirah is round, as might be expected of a plan of the cosmos; it is the galgal, the circle of the year, the complete round going in one direction from perfect joy (ענג, ʿōneg) to its reverse around the circle, ultimate misery (נגע, negaʿ).[113] To create anything “the initiate actually draws a circle around the objects that he wishes to form.” [114]

The Sefer Yetzirah is always described as displaying two main parts in the engraved circle, divided horizontally. They represent the world of light above and darkness below, the balance of the universe, the two mothers with a third holding the balance in the middle.[115] It is also the division of the male and the female.[116] The king as Aleph rules above over the water below. The “top is heaven created from fire,” and the lower part is chthonian—the womb, water, and earth. It is “the seal of the earth in the universe, the cold in the year, the womb, in the soul.” [117] It shows that “every desire, the one opposite the other, was made by God.” Good is the opposite of evil; each makes the other recognizable, defining its identity by the other.[118]

There is also a vertical halving expressed as the two sides of a balance with “the tongue of decree deciding between them.” [119] It is the basic division of three and seven on which the government of the universe (as of the Church) is based: “God the faithful king dominates them all . . . and all of them are bound to one another.” The vertical balancing is always between the advocate and the accuser following the analogy of the law court,[120] so familiar from the Tabula Smaragdina and the hypocephali, where the perfect balance is represented by the apes and the serpents, and the heads of the rams. By halving both up and down and to right and left, we get the quadrata, like a wall “set . . . up like a battle.” [121]

Along with this, and equally important, are the three main horizontal divisions. We are told that though there are two main divisions we must consider also the three divisions.[122] The three levels are fire and water with air between them—“water, fire, and breath (or spirit).” [123] The heat of the fire and the coldness of the water are tempered by the moistness of the common element: the air.[124] The three-part letter shin is the king with the crown on his head, providing a sun in the year and the head of the living being (soul, נפש, nepeš), male and female.[125] “There are three mothers in the world—air, fire, and water.” The fire occupies the top third, the water occupies the bottom third, and the generative principle is in the middle, “earth was created from water.” Between them comes the balancing of all, the air.[126] The Egyptians call the air Shu, and the earth is his wife Tefnut; “the control or balance between them” is signified by the maat-feather, Shu, the air, also the universal law which holds all things in equilibrium, and Mut the mother.[127]

The Sefer Yetzirah tells us how each major creation of God under the heavens is found in groups of seven. The holy palace, exactly in the center of the six directions and supporting all, is number seven. There “they make the Creator sit on his throne.” [128] From the seven doubles were formed seven planets, seven days, seven gates of the soul, masculine and feminine.[129] The seven lights of the menorah are the seven eyes on the stone (Zechariah 3:9). All this suggests the general organizing principle of the hypocephalus.

In the Joseph Smith hypocephalus the rectangular panels (figs. 16 and 17 are irregular) are closely matched in all the main hypocephali and usually number seven; there are six houses completely surrounding and sustaining the One in the middle, which upholds them all (see p. 194, fig. 16, panels 2–7 surrounding panel 1).[130] It is Aries in the universe, Nissan in the year, the right foot in the soul, male and female.[131]

Thus we see that there are significant parallels between the Sefer Yetzirah and the hypocephalus. They both deal with a description of the cosmos, God’s place in it, and how mankind can come to use the power of God. And thus by an imperceptible degree we proceed to the wonderful relationship of Alexander the Great and the hypocephalus.

^112. Thaʿlabi, Qiṣaṣ al-Anbiyāʾ; for an English version, see William M. Brinner, trans., ʿArāʾis al-Majālis fī Qiṣaṣ al-Anbiyāʾ or “Lives of the Prophets” (Leiden: Brill, 2002), 7.

^113. Kaplan, Sefer Yetzirah, 108.

^114. Ibid., 113.

^115. Sefer Yetzirah, 2:2–4 (short version).

^116. Sefer Yetzirah 3:5–7 (short version).
