13

Alexander the Great

			 It is altogether fitting, though a bit surprising, that there is a link between the hypocephalus and the most representative symbol of human aspiration, Alexander the Great. This conclusion comes not through a line of syllogism but by direct evidence on the Joseph Smith hypocephalus itself. Sheshonq was the name of the owner of this hypocephalus, and it would be hard to find a name more laden with tradition and symbols of power than that of Sheshonq. Among other things:

1. The name has been found on no other hypocephalus.

2. It has been associated with the name Sesostris, the traditional pharaoh of Abraham.

3. There is a tradition that the pharaoh Sheshonq was the son-in-law of Solomon, who built a magnificent palace for Sheshonq’s daughter.

4. It was he who transferred the treasures of the temple at Jerusalem to the temple of Heliopolis, ever the main center of attraction for eminent Hebrews and Greeks.

5. Sheshonq’s great inscription at Karnak is the only instance in which the name of Abraham is found on a pharaonic document.

What about Alexander? Sheshonq is the name by which priests, oracles, and common people of Egypt hailed him during his great campaign to conquer the world as World-conquering Sesonchosis.[1] What can possibly be the connection? It all comes down to the big problem that has faced us from the beginning; every man’s ideal, whether he denies it or not, is to live at top form forever. That was Alexander’s obsession. The only way to beat the game was to be in charge of everything. This is the idea of empire: everything we have not conquered, every unsubdued tribe on our borders, is a threat to God’s empire; the world was either ager pacatus (pacified and broken to the will of Rome) or ager hosticus (unsubmissive and rebellious), either Dar-as-Salām or Dar-al-Ḥarb, people who have submitted to the king or people who have to be converted or exterminated, either the empire of Cyrus or the taunting Queen Tomyris, who lured him to his destruction, etc.

The idea of kingship recognizes no equal, and so the safety of the realm and honor of the ruler demand that he push the border ever forward into the hostile world beyond. So Alexander had no other choice—he even sighed for more worlds to conquer. Conquering vast areas of the world was his avocation, but that was only half a victory. He still had to beat time: “To be thus,” said Macbeth, a king who had won it all, “is nothing; but to be safely thus.” [2] That was the rub. Everywhere Alexander went he sought out the most celebrated oracles and asked them the same two questions: Will I always be victorious and conquer? The answer was always yes. The second question: “How long will I live?” The answer, “Don’t ask. Your fame will be undying; what more do you want?” What he wanted was eternal life, or at least a very long life; he was ceaselessly haunted by the prophecy of his early death, proclaimed at his birth—the greater the conquest, the more painful to give it up before he could really enjoy it. The dismal repetition of the oracles spoiled everything for Alexander. He could conquer space, but he couldn’t conquer time. We have already seen how a pharaoh’s funerary text ends with ʿnḫ mἰ Rʿ nḥḥ ḥnʿ ḏ.t, “living like Re for time and eternity.” Often he asked more modestly for “millions of years.”

Power is the theme of Abraham’s affair with Pharaoh. Alexander’s problem was that of every successful mortal. Solomon’s lament makes it plain—after describing the beauties and pleasures of this life, he can only come up with “vanity of vanities; all is vanity” (Ecclesiastes 1:2). The successful businessman, all set to enjoy the sumptuous retirement, got the message: “Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee” (Luke 12:20). “Thou hast it now: king, Cawdor, Glamis, all . . . out, out, brief candle!” [3]

Power is the issue, but eternity is the goal. Pharaoh’s father Noah “blessed him with the blessings of the earth, and with the blessings of wisdom, but cursed him as pertaining to the Priesthood” (Abraham 1:26). Abraham, on the other hand, explains that the records of the eternal priesthood “running back from myself to the beginning of the creation . . . have come into my hands” (Abraham 1:28). That timeless heritage was denied Pharaoh, whose blessings were great but temporal.

The first word recognized by the experts from the Joseph Smith hypocephalus was that of the owner—Sheshonq or Shishak; it has been transliterated in more than thirty ways. In 1896, Théodule Devéria read the four lines: “O great God in Sekhem; O Great God, lord of the heaven (sky), of the earth and of the underworld . . . the Osiris S’es’enq.” [4] He identifies the party as having the same name as the “Shishak” of the Bible (1 Kings 11:40; 14:25–26; 2 Chronicles 12:2–9), noting that the name is not attested much earlier than the ninth century b.c.,[5] much too late for Abraham.

The words which Devéria left unread are found on other hypocephali and make up the prayer: “Grant life to the ba of the Osiris NN,” and only in the Joseph Smith exemplar is NN Sheshonq. Whoever this Sheshonq was, his name (the unmistakable six tall stalks of papyrus that leap out at the student) sets the Joseph Smith hypocephalus apart from all others.

Finally, it is the name that reverberates through the pages of the most widely read and popular of ancient romances—the famous History of Alexander the Great, preserved in manuscripts found all over the ancient and medieval world, in which Alexander repeatedly appears in glory under the name of Sheshonq.[6]

To explain this strange conjunction of names, Gaston Maspero argues that elements “based on the iconography of Luxor” were introduced into the Hellenistic romance to make Alexander an acceptable pharaoh to the population of Alexandria.[7] For David G. Hogarth, the story was composed in Alexandria to “affiliate” the Macedonian rulers with the real pharaohs who reigned before them.[8] One Jewish version has Alexander’s father, Philip, ruling in Egypt rather than in Macedon.[9]

The Cosmocrator

A cosmocrator is one who rules the cosmos, who has all power and virtue. There have been periods in history when the world has been afflicted by an epidemic of would-be cosmocrators. The time of Abraham was one, and the time of Alexander another (fig. 64). It is a battle of the Titans. Every cosmocrator finds himself facing various rivals at those times when the game is popular. But by definition, there can be only one true and authentic ruler of the universe. Alexander is one clearly qualified, first by a demonstration of his divine conception by Ammon, the king of the gods, and then by those qualities of character which convinced the human race of his supernal calling. But in one thing he is lacking—the man is a mortal. Like the rest of us, he is subject to pain and marked by prophecy for an early death. How can his mortality be reconciled to his divinity? To answer that he goes from oracle to oracle to the end of his life. He cannot stand the idea of dying or of being beaten by anyone. In his determined quest both he and the oracular god he consults assume the name of Sheshonq. Because of its singular appearance on the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, we are obliged to follow the name wherever it leads until something significant shows up.

Figure 64. This large gold medallion, struck more than five hundred years after his death, shows Alexander as cosmocrator, originally an astronomical term. He bears his sacred shield like his hero, Achilles, showing a portion of the Zodiac: Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, and Leo. Found at Abuqir near Alexandria, 2 1/8" in diameter. 59.1 Medallion with Alexander the Great, Anonymous, Roman, ca. a.d. 215–43, gold. Photo ©The Walters Art Museum, Baltimore.

Alexander and Ammon

Our story begins with the last true pharaoh of Egypt, Nectanebos II (360–343 b.c.), a great magician and sorcerer, fleeing from his country, which, in spite of its matchless scientific and magical achievements, had been conquered for the second time by the Persians under Artaxerxes III. The fugitive became a wanderer and a figure of romance. A famous prophetic text, the mysterious Demotic Chronicle, foretells that Nectanebos would someday return to Egypt and liberate his kingdom from the oppressor.[10] But the most celebrated story about him, which survived right through the Middle Ages, tells of how in his travels the outcast came to the court of Pella, the seat of Philip of Macedon, while that king was away campaigning, and how he consorted with the Queen Olympias in the mystical guise of the god Ammon, and so became the father of none other than Alexander the Great.[11] Arrian reports that Alexander’s own soldiers, when he pressed them too heavily on campaign, would get even by joking about his Papa Ammon.[12]

On his first meeting with Olympias, Nectanebos read her horoscope using a marvelous tablet made of gold and ivory on which the Sun, the Moon, Mercury, Mars, Venus, Jupiter, and Saturn were represented by various precious stones, as well as a horoscope made of white marble (fig. 65).[13] This tablet was used for divination by the moving of pieces representing the planets, each a different gem.[14] The most important was the emerald or smaragd, which represented the planet Mercury, as well as Thoth or Hermes.[15]

By his astrology, the wandering pharaoh predicted that Olympias would have a son by the Libyan Ammon, who would appear to her as a middle-aged, shaggy, and graying man with ram’s horns on his head.[16] Thus prepared, the queen had a dream in which Nectanebos came to her in the night in the form of Ammon of Libya, the true father of Alexander.[17] It is not strange that Alexander himself later recognized and advertised the parentage. This was a necessary prelude to ruling the world. If he would be a true and accepted king of Egypt, such a parentage was required; other rulers of the country before him had gone through the same procedure.[18] The scene of Ammon consorting with Hatshepsut’s mother, Queen Ahmose, is depicted on the walls of her famous mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri.[19] That same chamber at Luxor in which Thutmoses IV, in the guise of Ammon, consorts with Queen Mutemuau, was restored by Alexander himself twelve hundred years later, showing that the story of his own birth was no late fiction or sensationalism, but strictly in the established tradition of Egypt. Without a legitimate claim to divinity, Alexander would not only be unacceptable to the Egyptians, but to the whole oriental world of the time, which required divine credentials of a ruler.[20]

Philip was willing to accept the Ammon story, shrewdly observing that “kings . . . can do nothing against the gods!” [21] Nectanebos stayed on at court and was his son’s tutor. While they were discussing astronomy on a fieldtrip one evening, Alexander pushed his teacher into a pit. When asked why he did it, he said, “For, while not knowing the earth, you seek to study the affairs of the heavens.” [22] This recalls the story of how Thales fell into a ditch while stargazing and was mocked by a peasant girl for the same lack of common sense. Nectanebos’s fall proved fatal, and with his dying breath the last native king of Egypt told Alexander the story of how he came to Olympias “as my god [Ammon]” and so became his father.[23]

Figure 65. By consulting tables of planetary positions compiled by the Babylonians, Nectanebos moved the sun, moon, and planets to their positions at Olympias’s birth on a marble tablet like this Roman example, divided into the twelve houses of the Zodiac and the twelve animals of the Egyptian Dodecaoros system. In the center are busts of Apollo with his spiked solar crown and Phoebe with her lunar bow. Gray marble, 9 13/16" square, ¾" thick; present location unknown; reversed photo of the paper squeeze made by Daressey now in the Institut français d’archÉologie orientale.

		 The next teacher of Alexander was none other than Aristotle, who, upon being introduced to the boy, cried out, “Hail, world conqueror (cosmocrator), for you are destined to be a great emperor.” [24] And when at the age of fifteen Alexander tamed the terrible horse Bucephalus (which means “cow head,” or horned head), his royal father, Philip, deferred to him with, “Hail, Alexander, conqueror of the world (cosmocrator).” [25] Taking over the new kingdom on the death of his father, the young ruler twice petitioned for an oracle and received two visions. “called as by the voice of the divine spirit (θοπνύστης Φώνης, theopneustēs phōnes)” to go forth conquering and to conquer (see Revelation 6:2).[26]

It is not surprising that as the son of Ammon (his mother added her account to that of Nectanebos), Alexander, filled with a sense of mission, was driven by an overpowering urge to get to the shrine and center of Ammon worship for the ultimate confirmation of his cosmocratic calling.[27] Siwa was a world-famous oracle in the midst of the terrible Libyan Desert.

There were close ties between the age-old oracle of Dodona in northern Greece, the Ammon of Thebes in Egypt, and the shrine of the Libyan Ammon at the Siwa Oasis. All belong on the same prehistoric circuit, according to Herodotus.[28] “The Egyptian images of Zeus have a ram’s head; and in this, the Egyptians are imitated by the Ammonians (Libyans),” according to the father of history, and this same being was identified with the ram-headed Zeus which Greek immigrants into Cyrene brought to the Libyan desert at a place between the Nile and the Siwa Oasis (fig. 66).[29] On his way to this transplanted shrine of the ram-headed god, Alexander “was met by delegates from the Cyrenaeans, bearing a crown and splendid gifts,” which he graciously accepted.[30] Now, as is well known, the head of Alexander often appears on Greek coins and is recognizable from the fact that he is wearing ram’s horns.

The theme of our story, regardless of time, place, and characters, is the right and calling to rule the world, to be a cosmocrator. Alexander’s status is that of the most successful cosmocrator of them all. That in fact is the theme of the Pseudo-Callisthenes, in which Alexander is depicted as a truly devout, humane, idealistic, and magnanimous hero, with a sense of mission to bring light and hope to a world that had lost its bearings. The same situation is perfectly set forth in the opening verses of the Book of Abraham. Alexander was as successful as one can imagine any mortal to be in this life, for who else actually brought a whole new world civilization into being? Yet the point of the whole thing, constantly emphasized in the Pseudo-Callisthenes, Diodorus, and Plutarch, is that he is mortal after all.

Figure 66. Map showing sacred sites in Egypt, Libya, Greece, and the East. Map by Tyler Moulton.

The Visit to Ammon

What really transpired between Alexander and Ammon in the temple at Siwa has been a favorite subject of speculation among scholars. In all the main sources the story is set forth in a perfectly natural and convincing manner; the scene of Alexander confronting the oracle is one of striking verity (d’une verité saisissante), according to Maspero, who finds everything happening in strict conformity to Egyptian temple usage.[31] Classical scholars, ignoring Egyptian practices, have tended to scoff at the idea that Alexander actually went to consult the oracle for the reason that all the sources give. Thus Charles Anthon describes the Siwa caper as “an adventure resembling more of the wildness of romance than the soberness of history, and which has on this very account been regarded by some with an eye of incredulity.” [32] Ulrich Wilcken insisted that Alexander went to Siwa, not to be recognized by Ammon, but only to consult the oracle about his forthcoming campaign.[33] Friedrich Oertel doubts that Alexander really entered the shrine at all and is convinced that all accounts are fictitious, conceding only that Alexander might have received some sort of oracle, or “Quasiorakel.” [34] According to Joseph G. Milne, Alexander did not go to consult the oracle at all, and “the religious part (of the story) was either incidental or accessory.” [35]

It seems that all are missing the point; of course the accounts are fabulous, but they are perfectly true. To describe a day in the life of any person, one must make a choice of two stories. One is about what the person did, as the storyteller remembers—that is, imagines—it. The other would be a meticulous account of every exact motion, in every exact spot visited by the person, for exactly how long, how fast he went from here to there, etc. That might satisfy some wanting the true and accurate account. But all those careful details would be completely irrelevant, while everything the storied Alexander does is relevant to the human experience.

Ammon’s credit was good throughout the rest of the world; at that time the Libyan Ammon was perhaps the most celebrated of all oracles and the most widely recognized. Croesus, about to contend with Cyrus for world empire, sent delegates to all the great oracles “in Greece and in Libya” to know the situation.[36] Cambyses, having conquered Egypt, was on his way to consult the Libyan Ammon when his army disappeared in a sandstorm.[37] Alexander’s father, Philip, consulted the Delphic oracle about who would rule after him.[38] The reliable Lucan reports that in his day Ethiopians, Arabs, and Indians all reverenced Jupiter-Hammon at the only grove in all of Libya, where the multitudes of people would flock for the oracle of the Horned Jove.[39]

The classical writers were fond of mentioning Ammon’s horns (Lucian makes fun of them) and especially of associating him with the constellation of the Ram, not only as the leader of the year but also as Aries, the leader of the hosts. It was the horned Ammon who led the migrating hoards to unknown lands, brought the first sheep into Egypt, and saved its people by discovering the sacred spring of Siwa.[40] In short, Ammon is the cosmocrator. As the beardless horned god on the coins, the model followed by Alexander, he is the Libyan Dionysus, the Agathos Daimon, Ammon-Dionysos, Arsaphes = Ḥrἰ-š=f, the Son of Osiris. Thus Ramses II calls himself “Her-shef, king of Egypt” at Abu Simbel, the benefactor of the human race, the great culture hero leading his people along the “Way of Horus” into Egypt.[41]

Jan Zandee notes that in the famous Admonitions of Ipuwer [42] “it is impossible to tell whether the text conceives of Ammon as the heavenly king or the pharaoh as the representative of Ammon, since Egypt itself was identifying with the cosmos,” and at any rate “the king was a center in which certain divine power was concentrated.” [43] No less than Ammon, the son of Ammon must also wear Ammon’s horns as “Dionysus, the model followed by Alexander and the other Ptolemies.” [44] It was the particular concern of usurpers to secure this certification for themselves, as Richard Lepsius has pointed out. Amenophis I was first to appear in the guise which became standard after Amenophis III; Ramses II worships himself in that form at Abu Simbel.[45]

We regard this as a pagan aberration, and that is exactly what it was. The lofty morals and ethics and great philosophical wisdom of the ancients are, by their own confession, futile and vain because they cannot see beyond this world. Alexander is a perfect example of this, his glorious career one long cry of despair.

The Foredoomed Prince

We considered above the Egyptians’ absolute insistence on a real and physical resurrection; nothing else would do. So also with Alexander; the oracles all promised him immortality in history, but that was not what he wanted—what good is a dead cosmocrator? The premonition of an early death went back to the omen of the serpent’s egg given to Philip—the serpent issued from the egg, crawled about the room, and then returned to the egg, signifying Alexander’s short-lived journeys and demise.[46]

What the Libyan Ammon told Alexander was that if he wanted ageless youth, he should build a noteworthy city a little above the island of Proteus (Pharos).[47] Alexander gave orders to plot the circumference of the city, and when they took meal to mark the outline, various species of birds picked up the meal and flew off in all directions. Soothsayers interpreted this omen as meaning that the city of Alexandria would feed the world and that the men born in it would travel throughout the entire world.[48]

The Ascension of Alexander

Alexander’s city, which was to make him immortal, was laid out on a cosmic plan, and at the center was a huge altar dedicated “to whatever god you are, who . . . watch[es] over the endless earth.” [49] As Alexander was in the act of making the initiatory sacrifice, a giant eagle swooped down and snatched the meat from the altar and flew with it to another altar, a prehistoric shrine dating from the time of the first fathers, where stood two obelisks (like the two pillars of Heliopolis) bearing the inscription “I, Sesonchousis, king of Egypt and conqueror of the world (cosmocrator), built and dedicated this to Sarapis, the first god revealed to this land.” [50]

Alexander, facing the god at the altar, cried out, “Great Sarapis, give me a sign if you are the god of this land.” So the god appeared to him in a dream, telling him that the eagle was a sign: “Wasn’t it evident to you that I am the guardian and protecting god of all?” [51] As to the big question, “How am I destined to die?” the reply was, “It is griefless and good and honorable that mortal man not know in advance what the time of the end of his life will be. . . . It should seem good to you not to know your destiny.” [52] This desperate question of Alexander, like a cry of distress, is the prayer for life from the lion couch in Facsimile 1, as well as on the hypocephali.[53] By way of reply, the god takes him by the hand and bears him aloft to an exceeding great mountain—it is our old ascension motif.[54]

The purpose of taking him there is to show him, as Abraham was shown, that his name would become immortal: the city of Alexandria is unfolded to his view in all its glory, as the Holy City was to the ascended prophets.[55] He is told that many will worship him in that place where “by dying and yet not dying, you shall come to me . . . you shall be revered as having become a god” [56]—this is the peculiar status of the typical ascension hero. He is shown a chart with the numbers and dimensions setting forth the greatness of the gods, and upon awakening recognizes that it was the great Sarapis, the god of all things, and ordered another great altar inscribed with lines from the Iliad identifying Sarapis with the son of Kronos.[57] “Then the god departed,” [58] as he does after showing the heavens to Abraham in the early Christian writings. Alexander continued his journey into Egypt, where he was met in every city by local prophets bearing their own gods and hailing him, Alexander, as “the New Sheshonq, the cosmocrator!” [59]

Arriving at the primordial capital of Memphis, he was crowned and enthroned in the temple of Ptah the creator, the Khnum of the ram’s horns, and while in the temple he noticed a statue of black stone, bearing the inscription on its base, “This king who fled shall return to Egypt, not having grown old, but rejuvenated. And he shall make your enemies, the Persians, subject to you.” [60] The priests explained to Alexander, when he asked them, that “this is the last king, Nectanebos,” whereupon the hero threw his arms around the statue, exclaiming, “This is my father, and I am his son.” [61] The world already knew that Ammon was his father, and this clinched it with the Egyptians. He was indeed the New Sheshonq—Seonchosis junior, as Valerius calls him.[62]

But the man who claimed to rule Egypt at that time was Darius III, who also considered himself both son of Ammon and cosmocrator. To Alexander he sent a letter: “I, king of kings, kinsman of the gods, who share the throne of the sun god Mithra, and rise with the sun, Darius, myself a god, give my servant Alexander these orders. I advise you to withdraw and return to your parents, who are in servitude to me, to remain on the lap of your mother, Olympias.” [63] This is the tone of a cosmocrator, not rancorous or peevish and only mildly contemptuous, above the affairs of mere mortals, treating Alexander as a naughty little boy, which is how he really thought of him. “At your age,” the king of kings continues, “one still requires training and nursing and lap-feeding.” [64] So he sends to the little delinquent a whip to teach him discipline and a ball to play with the other kids, instead of going around like a gang leader terrorizing the settlements.[65]

It is routine for every would-be cosmocrator to characterize his rival as a terrorist, disrupting the peace he has bestowed on human society.[66] Using Bible language, Darius says his own hosts are as numberless as the sands and, “in case you haven’t collected enough loot to pay off your fellow terrorists,” he sends Alexander a third gift, a chest of gold.[67] And so the contest is on—the classic confrontation of the two cosmocrators in a world which, by very definition, can have only one. It follows that the one claiming the title must necessarily denounce the other as a sacrilegious fraud and as such, the two pretenders formally address each other. Exactly such an exchange of insults took place a thousand years later between a Roman emperor and another Persian—Justinian and Chosroes.[68] It is noteworthy that in these cases each claimant depicts his rival as a mere terrorist and describes himself as the savior of civilization.

Alexander entered into the game with zest. The gold, he replied, was a sign of tribute due him by the Persian, the whip was for him to thrash the barbarians, and the ball put the universe into his grasp. “I took the ball,” he wrote, “to mean that I shall master the world and hold it in my power—for the world is ball-shaped, a sphere” —thus giving a lesson to Darius as to who was really the educated adult and anticipating that unfailing symbol of the cosmocrator east and west, the imperial globe or Erdapfel.[69]

To this Darius replies with a letter of rebuke boasting that he is “the king of kings, great god Darius . . . which even the gods honor and have determined and decreed to be the sharer of their throne [and] is to you alone unknown. . . . You have found an unrulable land and proclaimed yourself king; . . . (you have) gathered men like yourself as partners in piracy. . . . I have written you to come and do obeisance to Darius.” [70] After a daring personal reconnaissance visit to the very tent of Darius, Alexander assumed the grand Persian manner of state for himself. Standing in the midst of his army “as Aramazd in heaven,” he encouraged them to follow him into battle against Darius. As they marched forth, Alexander rode in front of them on Bucephalus, “and no one could come near him.” [71] So it is we continually find the princes of this world, lacking the priesthood, would feign pretend to have it by staging marvelous theatrical special effects—the Book of Abraham comes right to the point; the best Pharaoh could do was to go on “seeking earnestly to imitate that order established by the fathers” (Abraham 1:26).

In the edict announcing his succession to the rule of Darius, Alexander addresses his new subjects as “King Alexander, son of the divine king, Ammon.” [72] So he was duly recognized by all as an “equal . . . of the Olympian gods” and as one who had “become Darius.” But he remembered the lesson of Darius: “I renounce the honor of being equal to the gods. For I am a mortal man and must fear this sort of thing.” [73]

Soon came a letter to Alexander from another cosmocrator, the Indian monarch Porus, who wrote saying: “I am invincible. For I am king not only of men, but of the gods, too.” He attributed Alexander’s defeat of Darius to “luck” and berates him, saying, “You think you are all powerful, when in fact you are weak,” and the duel was on again.[74] A real duel it was. Alexander challenged the giant Porus, who was five cubits tall, saying, “let you and me alone fight together for the rule of the Indians.” [75] Dispatching his adversary by a lucky stroke, he claimed the kingdom.[76] Then he turned to the more agreeable pursuit of exchanging wisdom with the Brahmins, and after a discussion on the subjects of life and death which so fascinated him, the all-powerful king, duly impressed by their wisdom, asked them patronizingly, “Ask me for whatever you like.” The answer of the Brahmins to the presumptuous question of their visitor was even a better put-down than Diogenes’ famous reply to Alexander—it was the terrible question: they replied, “Give us immortality, that we might not die” —the one thing that they knew would cut him down to size. He could only answer, “I do not have that power, for even I am a mortal man.” [77]

Leaving India, Alexander went west through the terrible desert of Gedrosia, and in a letter to Aristotle, describes how, at the western end, he came to a marble stele or column bearing the inscription: “Sesonchousis, ruler of the world (cosmocrator), set up this monument for those who sail through the Red Sea.” [78] Like Abraham, the mysterious hero performed benefactions for his fellowman all along his victorious route; this column marked the limits of the earlier Sheshonq’s world conquest, which Alexander had now surpassed as had been prophesied by the Libyan Ammon.

Crossing over to Africa, the hero approached the realm of the famous Queen Candace of Meroe on the upper Nile. He sent a courteous letter ahead, requesting her to bring to the border an ancient image of Ammon, a strong supporter of Candace in war, so that he could make sacrifices to it.[79] When he met her in her palace, he saw her “godly face,” and she seemed to him “that he was seeing his own mother, Olympias.” [80]

The visit has much in common with stories of other cosmocrators cut down to size by sensible women. Most famous is Solomon and the Queen of Sheba, the Arabic Bilqis, who turns the tables and outwits him, even as the Shulamite humiliates him. Then there is Cyrus and Tomyris, Sisera and Jael, Holofernes and Judith, and other tales in which the queen teaches the overambitious cosmocrator a lesson in humility. “No matter how smart a man appears to be,” Candace tells her guest, “there may be another wiser and more intelligent than he.” [81]

And now to the point of our story. The queen's son, Candaules, told Alexander the location of a cave, which he said was frequented by the gods. With a few men Alexander entered the cave and found an antechamber filled with a glowing mist, illuminated by stars which glittered on the ceiling.[82] There he saw “some reclining figures, whose eyes were shining like beams of light.” One of them spoke to him: “Glad greetings, Alexander. Do you know who I am?” “No, my Lord,” replied Alexander. He answered, “I am Sesonchousis, the world-conquering king who has joined the ranks of the gods. But I was not as fortunate as you, for you will have an immortal name even after death.” [83] One “who has joined the ranks of the gods” forcibly recalls Virgil’s picture of his patron Augustus, described in his Fourth Eclogue as the first Roman cosmocrator, permixtus heroas, et ipse videbitur illis—mingling with the heroes and visibly one of them.

Sesonchousis then tells Alexander to enter into another chamber where he will “see the creator and overseer of the entire universe.” [84] Thus invited, the king passed from the lion-couch room to the celestial room, where he sees a “fire-bright haze and, seated on a throne, . . . the lord Sarapis.” [85] Alexander instantly recognized him and says, “I saw you near the land of the Libyans seated on a throne, and now I see you here once again.” [86] Budge pointed out that the image of the Libyan Ammon which Alexander saw at Siwa was not a statue, but a disk with the ram-headed god in the middle of it, certainly suggesting a figure like the hypocephalus.[87] Standing by Alexander’s side Sesonchousis explains: “He is seen everywhere, though he is fixed in one spot; just as the sky is seen everywhere though it is set fast at a certain height in the firmament.” [88] To this one too, Alexander addresses his perennial question: “Lord, God, how many years shall I live?” Whereupon Sesonchousis, who is standing by him says, “It is well for mortal man not to know when he is to die... Still the city you are building will be celebrated and famous to all men, . . . and you shall live there upon dying and not dying, for the city which you are building shall be your grave.” [89] Upon passing out of the cave Alexander was met by a delegation of Satraps, who “crowned him king, and gave him his royal robe.” [90] This is the third station in these mystic rites.

At the greatest Egyptian temples are found what the French archaeologists designated the “groupe dogmatique” or the “chambres mystérieuses.” [91] Alexander himself had his own shrine built in the temple complex of Ammonophis at Luxor.[92] There is such a suite in the temple at Opet,[93] as in the Pyramid of Unas,[94] and most spectacularly on the ceiling of the Osiris chapel on the roof of the temple of Denderah (see pp. 310–11, fig. 35).[95] As Maxence de Rochemonteix describes one such complex: “Such is the symbolic rule which prevailed in a general way in the conception of the plan of the groupe dogmatique in the Egyptian temples. . . . The decoration of the three rooms . . . corresponds well to the three regions of the East, the Zenith, and the West . . . arranged,” as Rochemonteix puts it, “as three stations of the divine epic.” [96] It suggests the well-known declension of the sun as Re in the morning, Ammon at noon, and Atum in the evening. This is the sacrifice-and-renewal motif of the Book of Abraham, depicted in our hypocephalus drama with three scenes representing the episodes of the three chambers in the same order in the three facsimiles of the Book of Abraham.[97]

The purpose of the temple complex at Opet is to celebrate the birth, death, and resurrection of Osiris. The most important room is the second chamber in the northern court of the temple that is called “the chamber of triumph.” “It represents the western horizon, and it is here that the sun, after its daily journey, will disappear below the horizon. The god is supposed to die, and the chamber thus becomes the tomb where he will lie; but he only dies to be reborn; he only falls under the blows of his enemies to triumph over them with great glory.” [98] This is the situation presented in the Book of the Dead chapter 78 and spell 312 of the Coffin Texts.

It is the same at the temple of Hathor at Denderah. “The roof of the inner temple also has two parallel sets of rooms on its eastern and western sides which function as chapels dedicated to the death and resurrection of Osiris.” [99] One of these chapels contained the famous Zodiac (now in the Louvre), portrayed on the ceiling, which is a vast chart of the starry heavens showing the constellations of the Hellenistic period. It is disproportionately low, giving one the sensation of actually passing through or mingling with the heavens.[100]

Leaving the overwhelming circle of the heavens, one passes from here through a low, narrow passage—suggesting the birth passages of megalithic monuments throughout the world—to emerge on an open court, the walls of which are adorned with a splendid frieze depicting a coronation scene with all the gods lined up to do obeisance to the one on the throne.[101] This recalls the final incident in Alexander’s tour, when he too was given the crown and robe by the Satraps as he emerged from the Zodiac room, having passed through the heavens.[102] From the moment Alexander emerges from the cave to be hailed with a crown and robe, he was accepted as the pharaoh of all Egypt. It also recalls our Facsimile 3, Abraham delivered from the bier, taken through the heavens, and finally placed on the king’s own throne.

Besides the shrine of Ammon at Siwa, there was another great Ammon complex—that of the el-Kharga Oasis further to the north and only four days’ journey from the river (see p. 542, fig. 66). There it was that Darius I (521–481 b.c.), the great cosmocrator of his time, confirmed his rule over Egypt by erecting the most splendid of his works, a temple of Ammon, with later additions by Nectanebos II, who, according to the legend, begot Alexander. There were also additions made during the Ptolemaic period.[103]

Facsimile 2, Figures 8–11: The Text

Standing alone, the name of Sheshonq did not tell us much, but, as associations accumulate far and wide, our wonder increases. Let us turn now to the panel that contains the name of Sheshonq in the Joseph Smith hypocephalus; we discover there important details not found on any of the other hypocephali.

The four lines read, “O God of sleeping ones from the time of the creation (sp tpy). O Mighty God, Lord of heaven and earth, of the hereafter, and of his great waters. May the soul (ba) of the Osiris Shishaq (Sheshonq) be granted life.” “The sleeping ones” is a euphemism for the dead. This, as noted, is the well-known prayer found on the rims and corresponding panels of other hypocephali. It is like the prayer which Alexander, as Sheshonq, uttered to Sheshonq; it is like his and every other Osiris’s oft-repeated petition for more life. If this Sheshonq turns out to be at least a priest (for such he certainly was) or high priest, the name would still point our minds to Abraham because of his dealings with Sheshonq, which we shall soon consider.

The figure in the cave whom Alexander first met identified himself as Sesonchousis. He in turn introduced him to a figure reclining on a couch, like the couch in Facsimile 1. The great god addressed here was Sarapis, the lord of the universe.[104] In some manuscripts of the Canon of Jerome there is a note to Sesonchosis saying that “Siparis was the father of Sesonchosis, king of Egypt. Certain people say that after his death, he was proclaimed a god by the Egyptians and given the name of Sarapis.” [105] Sarapis was the god of Alexandria and the Ptolemaic kingdom and is usually thought to be derived from the Egyptian Wsἰr Ḥp (Osiris-Apis).[106] In Pseudo-Callisthenes, Alexander, as we have seen, after being introduced to Sarapis by Sesonchousis, asks how long he would live.[107] So too the owner of the Joseph Smith hypocephalus, also named Sheshonq, prays to be granted life. So here, as in the cenotaph of Seti I, in the 78th chapter of the Book of the Dead, the 312th spell of the Coffin Texts, on the rim of many hypocephali and on many, many other occasions, we find a figure supine on a couch, and a request for life.

Which Sheshonq?

Who were the real historical Sheshonqs, and why was that strange name chosen to designate both the mythical first pharaoh and the legendary last pharaoh? The most celebrated of the name was Sheshonq I, first king and founder of the Twenty-second “Sheshonqid” Dynasty, whose “foreign origin,” as Alan H. Gardiner puts it, “was betrayed by such barbarous names as Shoshenq, Osorkon, and Takeloth,” and he states that “they were evidently closely akin to those Libyans whom Merneptah and Ramesses III had repelled with such difficulty.” [108] But for many years it was debated whether they were of African or Asiatic origin.

Over a hundred years ago Daniel H. Haigh connected the name of Susinak with Elam.[109] Pierre Montet later agreed with W. M. Flinders Petrie, finding that “the name of Sheshonq strikingly recalls the god of Suza, Shushinaq.” [110] The partiality of the Sheshonqids to the god Seth in opposition to the priest of Ammon at Thebes, who had dominated the Twenty-first Dynasty, attests to their Asiatic origin, according to Montet.[111]

But the consensus of most recent histories of ancient Egypt is that the Twenty-second Dynasty is of Libyan origin.[112] But the connection with the Sea People brings in the European and Minoan invaders.[113] This takes us right into the Minoan world and the heroes of Homer. The long inscription of Merneptah tells how that king “destroyed the Meshwesh forever and stopped the Libyans, taking captive the children of their miserable chief.” [114] Some see in the Rebu the blond Cyrenian Libyans.[115]

The point of all this is that Sheshonq seems to be everywhere, ranging the vast deserts of Africa, settled in his oracular oases, or equally at home in the no less vast expanses of Asia. The name is spread just as freely through time, denoting both the first and the last kings of Egypt. How the name persists is seen in Jerome, who equates Sesac to Babylon, and in a Kabbalistic gematria, in which “for Babel we read, changing the order of the letters, Sesch.” [116] Can it be mere coincidence that the only times Israel and Egypt are rivals for world power is when a Sheshonq is involved? The tradition is that a Sheshonq “was first to rule all of Egypt after Horus, the son of Isis and Osiris,” nor was he inferior to his father.[117] Dicaearchus said it was Sesonchosis, or Sesostris (according to one), that “was the first man to discover mounting horses. Others [say] that Oros [Horus] did these things, not Sesonchosis.”[118]

By Greek times stories about several different pharaohs had been combined in a legendary figure called Sesostris/Sesoosis. His recorded exploits included those not only of the historical Sesostris (Senwosret), but also Sheshonq, Ramses II, and several others. Sesoosis is in all things a first-class cosmocrator. Diodorus found no agreement among ancient writers, Egyptian or Greek, about Sesoosis, yet he reports that Hephaestus (Ptah) appeared to Sesoosis’s father in a dream and said he would have a son who would rule the entire world.[119] Consistent with aspirations to world empire, Sesoosis/Sheshonq was also the founder of the warrior caste and cult, of the worship of the gods, and of the study of the stars.[120]

The confusion between Sesostris and Sheshonq seems to have arisen because of similarities between them. As Sheshonq founded the Twenty-second Dynasty, so Sesostris founded the Twelfth, thought by many to be the greatest of all dynasties. Tradition required that he be the founder of the dynasty, even though he followed Ammenemes I.[121] And we are assured that it was not Sheshonq, but Sesostris who invented horseback riding and conquered all the world as ”King of Kings and Despot of Despots.“[122] He was, says a Byzantine writer, “the king of the Assyrians, that famous Sesostris who yoked kings to his chariot and called himself cosmocrator.” [123]

And so those two heroes were constantly confused. When Manetho reports that it was a thousand years before Sheshonq founded the Twenty-second Dynasty that another Sheshonq (Sesonchosis) founded the great Twelfth Dynasty,[124] Josephus insists that Manetho had got his names mixed up, writing Sheshonq for Sesostris.[125] As Kurt Sethe pointed out, since each name is written in a variety of ways and they sound a lot alike to begin with, it is not surprising that they are constantly getting mixed up.[126] Thus Hercules, as the world benefactor, is named Shu, Shos, or Sosis.[127] Isis herself is called Saosis.[128] Sethos I was confused with Sethosis, Sesoosis, and Sesostris, the last being identified with both Sesostris of the Nineteenth Dynasty and Senwosret III of the Twelfth Dynasty.[129] Justin Martyr borrows the entire Sasychis story of Diodorus 1.94–95, usually attributed to Sesostris under the name of Sesonchosis, rendered Sesonchis by Manetho (and Africanus) and Sesongchosis by Eusebius, all of these being, according to Eduard Meyer, none other than our biblical Sheshonq I.[130] Theopompus insists that Sesongchosis, the first king of Egypt, was actually Sesostris.[131]

Sesostris-Sheshonq

Nowhere do we find a more striking tendency for two names widely separated in time to meet and fuse so stubbornly as those of Sesostris and Sheshonq. Right at the beginning, Jean-François Champollion was so struck by the resemblance of the deeds of Ramses II to those attributed to Sesostris by the classical writers that he did not hesitate to identify the two as one and the same person.[132] But since Manetho puts Sesostris in the Twelfth Dynasty and Ramses belongs in the Nineteenth, there must be some other link between them. Diodorus reports that the Egyptian priests and poets differed as widely among themselves about the identity of Sheshonq as the Greeks did, and Diodorus’s own Seoosis is often identified with Sesostris III. A son of Sesostris-Seoosis burned the licentious women at “a place called the Red Clay,” according to Herodotus, and the holy field by Diodorus[133]—from which it is perfectly clear that the Egyptians may have called the place Deseret. The king on the lion couch is spectacularly depicted as Sheshonq III in his tomb with a cryptogrammatic inscription, which is repeated in the tombs of Seti I and Ramses VI, who anticipated the Sheshonquids in restoring the barbaric archaic rites.[134]

The Sheshonqids were if anything even more pious than Sesostris. Sheshonq I gave the priesthood of Amun at Thebes to his sons (though the genealogical chart of the first prophets of Amun at Karnak begins with his own name).[135] The family monopolized the high priestly offices.[136] It was claimed that the phoenix then first came to Heliopolis,[137] where we see him conspicuously at home on the rim of our hypocephalus. It was the super-pious Sheshonqs III and IV who revived the drowning of the Apis-bull on days fixed by mystic numbers.[138] The claim to divine authority was a positive obsession with these kings who constantly jockeyed for power with the old priesthood of Thebes.[139] And during their rule “the two halves of the country continued to vacillate between friendship and enmity,” [140] even as the alien Abraham and Pharaoh do. The son of Sheshonq I describes his reign “by mythic parallels from archaic times,” comparing himself to Horus when, as a high priest of Amun, he received the approval of the oracle of Thebes.[141] So where do we stand? Champollion’s intuition that Ramses II and Sesostris may be the same is justified by Gerald A. Wainwright’s observation that stories cluster around Ramses II “whether under the names of Aegyptus, Sesostris, Sesoosis, or ‘Sethos-who-was-also-Ramesses.’ ” [142]

It was at Avaris that Ramses II, Sesostris, and Sheshonq made their presence felt. There, according to the excavator, the Nineteenth Dynasty usurps the Twelfth.[143] But then it was Ramses’ turn when the Sheshonquids took over, supplanted, and even destroyed his works.[144] The last of the Sheshonquids was replaced by Necho I at Athribis.[145] This Necho is in turn identified with that Rikajon from Sinear who seized the throne of Egypt and took Sarah away from Abraham.[146]

The Hyphenated Hero

What has caused Sesostris and Sheshonq to be confused and identified even more than their sibilant names are the great conquests in Africa and Asia, in which each is credited with the exploits of the other, marking them both as true cosmocrators. Thus while Sheshonq gets credit for the real conquests of Sesostris in the far south, Sesostris is celebrated for expeditions to the north and east, which Sheshonq made but Sesostris never did. Noting this, Sethe goes so far as to hyphenate the name Sesostris-Sesonchosis.[147]

Consider how many Sheshonqs we have, all doing the same thing!

1. A Sheshonq is the first king of Egypt.

2. Another Sheshonq is the founder of its greatest dynasty.

3. Another brought about its greatest revival.

4. Six hundred years later yet another restores the kingdom and adopts the name of Sheshonq by instruction from Ammon.

5. Moreover, when Darius I, whose empire was the greatest of all, conquered Egypt and mounted the throne as pharaoh he took the name of Sesostris. Diodorus says the great lawgivers of Egypt were Mnevis, Sasychis, Sesoosis, Bocchoris, Amasis, and Darius; it was Darius who “learned . . . about the greatness of soul of the ancient kings (of Egypt) and . . . imitated their manner of life . . . [and] at his death was accorded equal honors with the ancient kings of Egypt.” [148]

6. When Alexander drove out Darius’s successor, he in turn took the name of Sheshonq.

7. Alexander’s successor, Ptolemy I, inaugurating the new line of pharaohs, also took the name of Sesostris, because Alexander had already preempted Sheshonq.

Thus the names Sesoosis, Sesonchosis, Sesostris, etc., reverberate down the ages in the model followed by Nectanebos and Ptolemy, the immediate predecessor and successor respectively of Alexander the Great.[149]

Sheshonq and Sesostris always have a foot in both continents. Upon becoming pharaoh, Sheshonq I, whatever his background, immediately undertook the reconquest of all the vast expanses of Asia which had once been under Egyptian sway.[150] That empire had reached its maximum extent under Sesostris, and according to Pseudo-Callisthenes it was Sheshonq whose conquests were finally surpassed only by the second Sheshonq, Alexander. One had to rule both realms to be a cosmocrator, and the situation is clearly reflected in the pharaoh of the Book of Abraham, whose rule is both Egyptian and “Chaldean.”

And to the point, Manetho says that Abraham’s pharaoh was Sesostris but calls him Sesonchosis, according to Sethe.[151] Both figures were noted for their exceeding piety, both revived the old religion, including human sacrifice, even of the pharaoh! [152] This again puts us into the Abraham picture. I have discussed at length the attempt to sacrifice Abraham as a substitute for pharaoh on the altar.[153] Wainwright published a study on “the attempted sacrifice of Sesostris,” following Herodotus’s account. It tells how when Sesostris returned to his kingdom after world conquest, his brother, ambitious to seize the throne, invited him to a banquet; wood was piled around the house for a conflagration. To escape the flames, and in agreement with his wife, Sesostris placed two of his children on the blazing barrier and so with the rest escaped, using them as a bridge.[154]

Actually this strange episode is a standard cosmocrator motif. When Croesus was beaten by his rival cosmocrator, Cyrus, he built himself a huge funeral pyre upon which he sat enthroned, but at the last moment, by the prayers of his rival, he was miraculously rescued by rain; then he joined Cyrus in his world conquest as his wise adviser.[155] Another story by Herodotus is in the same tradition. This time it was Pheros (Pheros being the alternate name of Sheshonq!), the son of Sesostris, who attempted to control the elements, for which he was smitten with blindness, the only cure to which must be supplied by a chaste virgin, all other applicants being consigned to the flames.[156] Wainwright finds all these stories connected,[157] and we have already connected them with the account in the first chapter in the Book of Abraham, where a reluctant Abraham is subjected to the fanatical rites revived by a pharaoh who rules in both Egypt and Asia.

The Sheshonqids and the Lotus

The Book of Abraham emphasizes the completely ritual nature of what Abraham had to go through, and that special emphasis is placed on the lotus as symbolic of the land of Egypt. It is an interesting coincidence that it is in the dynasty of Sheshonq that the lotus received special attention; under that king, chalices with lotuses “commemorate the accession, or a claim to the kingship when the claim is not strong, or a Sed-festival.” The symbol of the infant sun on the lotus at Hermopolis on one of the chalices “is connected . . . with some event in the reign of Sheshonq I.” [158] The lotus occurs on all three facsimiles in the Book of Abraham, where it is explained by Joseph Smith as denoting Abraham in Egypt. It was under Sheshonq I that chalices with the infant sun on the lotus figured prominently in “the great New Year’s mystery drama of the sun-god.” [159] Though “from an early period we find it (the lotus) closely associated with Egyptian gods and kings,” it is especially in the great religious revival of the Sheshonquids that it comes to our attention.[160]

Abraham in Stone

In his great relief on the wall at Karnak, Sheshonq I treats us to the only appearance in the whole corpus of Egyptian writing of the name of Abraham. In 1904 James H. Breasted commented on lines 71–72 of this inscription: p ḥw-q-rw ἰ-b-r-m or פחקל אברם (p-ḥql ʾbrm), which can be “nothing else than ‘The Field of Abram’ ” (see pp. 184–85, fig. 15). [161] We already have the name of Jacob on the list of Thuthmose III, and probably also that of Joseph.[162] We might, therefore, expect to find the name of Abraham, especially at this time.[163] This identification with Abraham is accepted by some [164] but rejected by others.[165] “Decades ago, in the arrogant manner of Old Testament scholarship of that day,” writes Kenneth Kitchen, “Wellhausen dismissed the list of Shishak as historically worthless, saying, ‘He could simply have reproduced an older list of one his predecessors.’ ” [166]

The most famous of all Sheshonq I’s exploits was the taking of Jerusalem and Solomon’s temple. Solomon is the only Hebrew cosmocrator, and Sheshonq is his rival. Each bided his time, the pharaoh feigning friendship with the strong kings of Israel even while he moved in on their territory.[167] Though scholars routinely dismissed the Karnak inscription as a gross exaggeration, the Abi Ba’al inscription from Byblos shows that the kings of that state recognized Egyptian rule.[168] Even if one considers it only a recognition of friendship, as Gardiner does, still “the discovery at Megiddo of a fragment mentioning Shōshenḳ” shows that he was there too (fig. 67; see map on p. 542, fig. 66).[169]

Figure 67. The only surviving fragment of a monumental stele features the name “Sheshonq, Beloved of Amun.” Hastily carved from local stone in Megiddo, it proclaimed his influence in the area, ca. 930 b.c. Israel Museum.

			 				 Sheshonq kept steady pressure on supporting Yekimilk, the usurper against Itoba’al in Byblos, and giving asylum to Jeroboam against his father Solomon. At the Egyptian court, Hadad, the Arab, was elevated by Queen Tachpenes, the great-aunt of Sheshonq I, and went back to Arabia to make trouble for Solomon.[170] Solomon was dependent on Egyptian power and received support from Sheshonq by the gift of the important city of Gezer. But five years after the death of Solomon, Sheshonq I “thought it a good opportunity to make his claim in Palestine unquestionable,” and with Libyan troops, he sacked Jerusalem (1 Kings 14:25). It was the riches of this plunder that restored the fortunes of the Egyptian empire.[171] While Solomon was still alive, Jeroboam had fled to the court of Egypt, and the invasion was in support of his claim.[172]

W.M. Flinders Petries notes that after “Solomon died, he (Sheshonq) intervened and overran Judea, probably in the name of his nephew, the child of Pharaoh’s daughter . . . it was Karamat’s son who again invaded Judea . . . in the days of Asa.” [173] In all of this, “Shishak’s queen, Karamat, was the central figure.” [174]

There is no confirmation for any of this in the Egyptian record; we do not even know which pharaohs were involved, according to Gardiner.[175] All of this could be little more than coincidence as far as Abraham is concerned, were it not for another name more closely bound to Sheshonq than any other in the dynasty and even more inseparably bound to the name and fortunes of Abraham, which is the subject of the next chapter.

14

Nimrod

			 By far the name most frequently associated with Abraham in Jewish legends is that of his alter ego, reverse image, and mortal enemy Nimrod.

The Name

Though Georges Legrain found the name of Nimrod quite common in the Near East,[1] its origin has always been a favorite subject of speculation. There has never been any agreement as to who the first historical Nimrod was—was he Nin-marad, King of Marad, an old semi-mythical king, as Emil Kraeling and William F. Albright thought,[2] or was he Marduk, the eponymy of the Second Dynasty of Isin? [3] Or is it the famous Tukulti-Ninurta I (1235–1198 b.c.),[4] or Jacob of Serug’s Bar Nemre, or Baal Nemre of ancient Namara in Northern Haran? [5] The small army of candidates makes one thing certain—that we are going back to very early times and to a time of the great migrations.[6]

The trouble is that, though it is agreed that Babylonia is “the land of Nimrod” (Micah 5:6), there is no mention of the name in any cuneiform document, and every attempt to connect it to a Mesopotamian divinity has failed. “Everything remains pure hypothesis without factual foundation.” [7] Karl Preisendanz concludes that “a satisfactory explanation of the whole Nimrod problem appears in total absence of any inscriptional evidence for Nimrod to be at present impossible.” [8] The name turns up all over the Near East, especially in the north, the Haran country. Abraham’s dealings with Nimrod take the hero all over—to Khadr and Cutha in southern Mesopotamia, to Bithynia and Hathra and the Kurdish mountains of Armenia.[9]

In identifying Nimrod, Frederick J. Foakes-Jackson concluded: “Every writer seems to take a different view of him: Pinches . . . identifies him with the god Merodach. Sayce . . . says he was not a son of Cush, but the founder of a Kassite Dynasty and a Semite; whilst Cheyne transports him from Babylonia and settles him among the Jerahmeelites in Southern Palestine!” [10] Some equate him to Belus, the founder of Babylon, hence as Bel-Marduk or Marduk.[11] To Christian writers he was Titan (see Genesis 10:8–9), the giant who imprisoned Chronos and his wife.[12] A Jewish tradition even identifies him with Zarathustra, to whom Abraham is said to have taught astronomy.[13] It has long been the custom to identify Nimrod with Amraphel as is done in the Book of Jasher.[14] Whether it was the “Amraphel of the Khatti-Mitani-Kossaer invasion” or someone else,[15] it is as Amraphel that “‘Nimrod the City Builder’ has stolen Hammurabi’s laurels.” [16] And it was Amraphel who led the five kings against Abraham (Genesis 14:1, 9).

Nimrod and Abraham

“Of this man,” wrote one of the earliest and most thorough students of Nimrod, “there are nearly as many names and titles, as there are mythic or ancient romances in existence.” [17] Why does such a problematic and elusive figure as Nimrod deserve so much attention? How does it concern us? He is the most important character next to Abraham himself in the voluminous Abraham literature. In legend the two are inseparable. He is often called Abraham’s counterpart or alter image. Like Abraham, Nimrod is a representative figure. Everything about him is outsized: he is always the giant, the Titan, larger than life, totally dedicated to his own greatness, contemptuous of all others including God, recognizing no rule but his own; he is the would-be cosmocrator, ruler of the universe, the arrogant pretender,[18] which makes him the archrival and mirror image of Abraham, with everything in reverse.

The story is that Nimrod began his rule well, as a just and modest young man. Even after “the sons of Cham made Nembroth a prince over them,” he lived at peace with the people of Shem and Japheth; “while Noah was yet alive . . . they lived at one with each other and the earth was at peace.” [19] It was power that corrupted the youthful Nimrod. Here a brief digression is in order.

The Garment of Nimrod

According to Rabbi Eliezer, the coats of skin which god made for Adam and Eve “were with Noah in the ark, and when they went forth from the ark, Ham, the son of Noah, brought them forth with him, and gave them as an inheritance to Nimrod. When he put them on, all the beasts, animals, and birds, when they saw the coats, came and prostrated themselves before him. The sons of men thought that this (was due) to the power of his might; therefore, they made him king over themselves.” [20] Consequently he ordered the city and tower to be built. There are many versions of the garment story.[21] Divine power always went with the garment. Whenever the youthful Nimrod, age twenty, put on the garment, “God gave him might and power, . . . and the animals, the cattle and birds, came and fell down before him, for they thought he was the king because of his mighty power, and they also made him their king.” [22] Another account has it that Nimrod had the garment not from Ham but from his own father Cush, who loved Nimrod as the child of his old age “and gave him a skin garment which God had made for Adam when he went out of the Garden of Eden.” The garment passed from Adam to Enoch, to Methuselah, to Noah, from whom it was stolen by Ham as they were leaving the ark. Ham gave it to his firstborn Cush who hid it when Nimrod was born and later gave it to him.[23] “Nimrod became strong when he put on the garments, . . . and he hunted the animals and he built altars, and he offered upon them animals before the Lord.” [24]

The Cosmoplanes

That makes Nimrod the first cosmoplanes, “the one who leads the world astray, who made all the world to rebel against God,” [25] who found himself in open rebellion against the kingdom of heaven. “They cast off the kingdom of heaven from themselves, and appointed Nimrod king over themselves, a slave son of a slave.” [26] This is where the Book of Abraham comes in.

Nimrod and his people found a great valley to the east and founded the city of Shinar where all the people and tongues gathered, bringing him their tribute; there he set up gods of wood and stone and “taught all his people evil ways. . . . They threw off the reign of heaven and made Nimrod their king.” [27] And so we read it in the Book of Abraham: “My fathers, having turned from their righteousness, and from the holy commandments which the Lord their God had given them, unto the worshiping of the gods of the heathen, utterly refused to hearken to my voice; For their hearts were set to do evil, and were wholly turned to the god of Elkenah . . . and the god of Pharaoh, king of Egypt; Therefore they turned their hearts to the sacrifice of the heathen in offering up their children unto these dumb idols, and hearkened not unto my voice, but endeavored to take away my life” (Abraham 1:5–7).

Some see in Nimrod the great hunter “an allegory concerning human affairs” [28]—a state of constant warfare. It was Satan’s rule of blood and horror, and Nimrod was his front man until he was sent down to hell. He never really ruled but was simply doing Satan’s bidding. He is, says Ka’ab al-Akbar, “the disciple of the Devil who instructs him in a way by which you can kill the God of Abraham in the heaven where He resides.” [29] So the Devil, not Nimrod, is the archetype of the quintessential Asiatic cosmocrator of which Nimrod is far and away the prime example. But he is by no means the only one. We may think of Abraham as a real person and Nimrod as a myth; but they were both very real, for a good deal of world history is made up of the exploits and pretensions of men who would conquer the world.

Nimrod is often equated with Orion—rude, violent, insolent, and destined for a fall.[30] As Orion, the Jews identified him with the rebel angel Shemhazai and even with the rebellious Samael—that is, Satan.[31] It was Esau, another mighty hunter, who killed Nimrod and took the stolen garment from him, with which he bought a mess of pottage.[32] Other pretenders followed his example, and Attila even took the name of Nimrod for himself.[33] This faking of the garment marks Nimrod as the great liar, a liar from the beginning, “the Father of Lies.” He is a type of antichrist, the deceiver of the world who for a time “dazzled mankind with his splendor and audacity.” [34] And we are never allowed to forget that he is everything that Abraham is not.

Abraham at the age of fifty (some say it was twenty) had no ambition for himself, and he rebuked Nimrod for failing to provide righteous leadership: “I thought you would lead your subjects in the right way, but you would not.” In his anger, Nimrod summoned a great council, and the entire nation assembled in the great year-rite to behold the sacrificing of Abraham—back to the situation in Abraham chapter 1. The king’s wise men, upon seeing Abraham, identified him as the true cosmocrator, “The man at whose birth one great star swallowed up four other stars.” They had warned Nimrod of that at his birth and now told him that Terah had failed to sacrifice the newborn Abraham but had substituted another child in his place. Terah, terrified, in order to appease Nimrod offers to make another sacrifice, offering his sons Haran and Abraham to the flames. But what happened? Haran was burned to ashes, along with twelve other victims, and Abraham was saved again—again the theme of the substitute sacrifice as an atonement. The king and his court all bowed down to Abraham when he was delivered from the pyre, but Abraham told them not to worship him but the true God.[35]

Many are the stories of the mortal rivalry of Abraham and Nimrod with the rule of the universe as the issue. The man has three names, according to the Midrash: Cush, Nimrod, and Amraphel (neatly bracketed by Africa and Asia), and in another version Amraphel and the five kings fought against Abraham, who was victorious with the aid of the star Jupiter. They placed him upon a bema in the plain Sava, where they once tried to sacrifice him and hailed him as their ruler, but he rebuked them, telling them “there is only one king of the world [that is, cosmocrator], and that is God.” [36]

The Cosmocrats

As we saw in the previous chapter, the cosmocrator is one who rules the whole world by his power and authority. He is the מלך העולם (melek hā-ʿôlām), the pacator orbis. Many a king claimed the office, but none were up to it. When the Emperor Augustus took over the rule of the Roman Empire, their number one poet declared, “He now rules a world at peace with the virtues of the Fathers.” [37] His rule is always virtuous, which means that anyone who defies him deserves nothing but instant extinction. The list of real cosmocrators is a short one. Though many have aspired to the glory, few have obtained it. Such giants are so much alike in their manners and their methods that their names and identities have often been interchanged and confused in legend and even in history. The lists vary but the same familiar names turn up in all of them.

The oldest lists seem to favor a round number of ten, including both good and bad cosmocrators. Numbers one to ten were:

1. The Lord, who rules heaven and earth, created the world and ruled from on high.

2. Nimrod, who ruled the entire world because the people had been weakened by the flood.

3. Joseph, son of Jacob. He ruled in second place after Pharaoh for forty years.

4. Solomon—all the world brought him gifts.

5. Ahab, king of Israel.

6. Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon.

7. Cyrus of Persia.

8. Alexander the Great.

9. The Messiah.

10. The Lord takes over again.[38]

It was common to match good ones against bad ones. The Clementine writings arrange them in pairs, each real and righteous cosmocrator being opposed by a renowned rival and pretender, as Cain versus Abel, Noah versus the Giants, Pharaoh versus Abraham, Isaac versus the Philistines, Jacob versus Esau, Moses versus the magicians of Pharaoh, the Son of Man versus the Tempter, Peter versus Simon Magus, the missionaries of the gospel versus all the world, and finally Christ versus the antichrist.[39] In the Asatir’s list of ten, “Nimrod was the first king over the whole world after God, and further on among the next seven we find Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus, and, very significantly, Alexander the Great. The ninth king will be the Messiah, and after him the rule will return again to God.” [40] Thaʿlabi lists four cosmocrators, two believing ones, Solomon and Alexander the Great, and two infidels, Nimrod and Nebuchadnezzar.[41]

There is a long list of second-string cosmocrators—Darius, Xerxes, some deities such as the Persian Mithra, the Egyptian Sarapis, and the Babylonian Tukulti-Ninurta, often identified by scholars with Nimrod but who loved to identify himself with the epic hero Gilgamesh.[42] In Egypt, inscriptions of the Emperor Caracalla proclaim him by the title of cosmocrator, to which both his infinite arrogance and culminating madness entitle him.[43] But “of the Eastern potentates of antiquity, three have an abiding hold on the imagination—Nimrod, the mighty Hunter before the Lord, Solomon, and Alexander the Great.” [44] Before noting how these three are each tightly bound to the name of Sheshonq, we should indulge in a moralizing fantasy.

The wars the cosmocrator waged were all obligatory wars with the sole purpose of bringing peace to the world under his unchallenged rule, which extends to the ends of the earth (imperium Oceano, famam qui terminet astris),[45] and there is none to molest or make afraid. Vopiscus noted that soon there would be no need of soldiers.[46] Naturally the only alternative to the cosmocrator’s rule is chaos.[47] The cosmocrator is often depicted on the walls of palaces and temples engaged in single combat with monster lions, bulls, dragons, serpents, and giants, all representing evil, from Tiamat to the antichrist. The outer walls of the Egyptian temples are covered surprisingly with battle scenes; what they are is assurance that the king will keep the forces of chaos and outer darkness away from the sacred precinct.[48]

The strange title of Eisler’s great work, ΙΗΣΟϒΣ ΒΑΣΙΛΕϒΣ Οϒ ΒΑΣΙΛΕϒΣΑΣ (Jesus the King Who Never Ruled), announces the important but quite neglected fact that every dispensation is under the auspices of one who holds the true kingship and priesthood. But such have never exercised world dominion. The rightful rulers have always been given a bad time, thanks to the machinations of the real cosmocrator, who is Satan. From Eden to the present, the Devil is always left in possession of the field: “Hereafter I will not talk much with you: for the prince of this world cometh, and hath nothing in me” (John 14:30). It was he who prompted Nimrod to challenge God, who tempted Adam to disobey so that he would take orders from him, who led the human race astray by putting his own candidates up against the authentic ones. It was he who ordered Christ to worship him, promising him all the kingdoms of this world, which only a cosmocrator has the power to bestow. In the Muslim tradition the Imâm, or true ruler, of each age is confronted by a “Jabbâr: That cruel impious king, the adversary of the Imâm in each cycle, such as a Nimrod and Abraham, Pharaoh and Moses.” [49] Herod was to Christ, according to Thaʿlabi’s informant, as Nimrod was to Abraham.[50] In Canaanite mythology Perseus’s “conquest of ‘Typhon’ is comparable to that of Seth by Horus, with whom Min was identified.” [51] The titles which the diadochs (successors of Alexander) and the Roman emperors gave themselves loudly proclaim their rank as cosmocrators, saviors of the world, pious, benevolent and saintly,[52] while the unconverted viewed them as nothing less than evil Babylon. Antiochus IV Epiphanes and Philip Arrhidaeus both went mad and suffered the fate of Nimrod, as reflected in that strange and archaic story of the eagle, the egg, the plucking out of the eye, etc.[53]

The fatal flaw in the cosmocrator’s claims was his mortality; being mortals, would-be cosmocrators had to depend heavily on special effects to be convincing.[54] Above all, what worried them was the problem that so exercised Alexander. As mortals they knew that their days were numbered, and that set limits to their kingdom. But a true cosmocrator had not only to rule all the four quarters of the world, but his had to be an enduring office—he is necessarily αἰωνόβιος (aiōnobios), living for aeons—immortal, and, as we have seen, it was precisely this point which sent Alexander to oracles all over the world with the same question. All that could reassure him against the ravages of time was the ever-recurring cycle of rebirth and resurrection on which the king had first claim. It was the mystery of kingship in which the king succeeds himself in all times and all places as was so movingly proclaimed by Sheshonq to Alexander. This brings us back to our hypocephalus, Facsimile 2.

Back to Sheshonq

A remarkable aspect of Wainwright’s old barbaric royal mumming is the way the examples cluster around the names of Sesostris and Sheshonq, while the most conspicuous character in the Abraham version is of course his great antagonist, Nimrod. In the impressive list of Egyptian monarchs marked for ceremonial death by the priests of the old religion, the most famous stories are those of Sesostris, Amasis, Bocchoris, and Busiris, all related.[55] We have treated the Hercules and Busiris story as the best example of what happened to Abraham substituting for the royal sacrifice.[56] For the classical writers this Sesostris was the prime example of a universal divine ruler—“about him stories gathered as about no other ruler in ancient history with the exception of Alexander the Great,” wrote C. H. Oldfather.[57] James H. Breasted noted that “in Greek times Sesostris had long since become but a legendary figure which cannot be identified with any particular king,” though “some of the deeds of Ramses II were possibly also interwoven into the Greek legend of Sesostris.” [58] The important thing is that ancient and modern scholars alike have regularly identified Sesostris as the pharaoh of Abraham, whom they make contemporary with Sesostris I, II, and III, and the name Ibrm is found on a hieratic ostracon that belonged either to Sesostris I or Ramses II.[59] Relevant to our Abraham study is the finding of a considerable number of objects with the name of Sesostris in Palestine.[60] In the famous Execration Texts, much studied as a clue to the date and identity of Abraham, the names of Sesostris I and II dominate.[61]

One strange and unique legend common to Sesostris and Abraham deserves attention. It is the story of how the ruler saved his own life by sacrificing his sons in the flame. In the Egyptian version, when Sesostris returned in triumph from one of his world conquests, his brother, coveting the kingdom for himself, set fire to the house in which Sesostris and his family were sleeping. By having his two sons form a human bridge by lying on the flaming barrier of burning walls, Sesostris and the rest were able to escape.[62] In the Abraham version, Haran, Abraham’s brother, is consumed by the fire in which Abraham was to be burned.[63] Before that we are told the infant Abraham escaped death at the hands of Nimrod when by subterfuge another child was offered in his place.[64]

The female version of the royal sacrifice was Nitocris, who cast herself into a room full of embers.[65] It seems that the motif of the royal sacrifice follows us everywhere, and there is no better guide to what was going on than the Book of Abraham. By diligent search Joseph Smith might have put his version together from classical sources, but what would that have to do with Abraham, of all people? Sesostris was succeeded by his son Pheron, who figures in what seems to be something of a standard scenario in which the recurrent themes are the presumptuous king, his blinding, the wounding of the waters, the virtuous woman who restores him, the unvirtuous women consigned to the flames, and the healing and thanksgiving.[66] Eugène Lefébure saw in the story of Pheron a representation of the decadence that followed the rule of Sesostris, which he equates with the rule of Ramses II.[67]

The name of Pheron, thought by some to be simply a reading of Pharaoh, also turns up as Pharos, Panos, Puntos, and Pharethones. It was Pharethones, according to Josephus, who unwittingly took Sarah away from Abraham.[68] According to Bar Hebraeus, it was not Pharethones but Pânôs who took Sarah.[69] In yet other accounts, it was Pharethones who was taught astrology by Abraham, who stayed twenty years in his court.[70]

The idea is that Abraham’s host was just called Pharaoh, and the Jewish tradition was that the pharaoh of Abraham was indeed the first to bear that name.[71] Since “there has never been any agreement among scholars as to which king was the pharaoh of Abraham,” [72] simply to call him Pharaoh is the safest thing. This is conspicuously the case in the Book of Abraham where no other name or title occurs.
